questions.stem: Among the following, the true statement regarding TYPES OF ATAXIA. Q: Which statement regarding types of ataxia is TRUE?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Depressed or absent ankle jerk is seen in sensory ataxia
choices.explanation: Sensory ataxia results from loss of proprioceptive input (dorsal columns / peripheral nerves). Because the afferent limb of the reflex arc is interrupted, deep tendon reflexes such as the ankle jerk are depressed or absent, and there is a positive Romberg sign and impaired vibration/position sense.

choices.label: B
choices.text: In vestibular ataxia, dysarthria is an important sign
choices.explanation: Dysarthria is a feature of CEREBELLAR ataxia, not vestibular. Vestibular ataxia presents with vertigo, nystagmus and a tendency to fall toward the lesion.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Nystagmus is seen in cases of sensory ataxia
choices.explanation: Nystagmus is a cerebellar/vestibular sign. Sensory ataxia does not cause nystagmus; it causes a stamping gait worse in the dark and absent reflexes.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Impaired vibration and position sense is seen in vestibular ataxia
choices.explanation: Impaired vibration and position sense is the hallmark of SENSORY (dorsal column) ataxia, not vestibular ataxia.

questions.keyPoint: Absent ankle jerk + positive Romberg + loss of vibration/proprioception = sensory ataxia; nystagmus and dysarthria point to cerebellar/vestibular causes.

rows: Stamping gait worse in dark, positive Romberg
Loss of proprioception/vibration, absent ankle jerk
B12, nerve conduction, dorsal column imaging
Sensory ataxia = depressed/absent reflexes + positive Romberg

questions.stem: A 36-YEAR-OLD FEMALE presented with GRADUAL LOSS OF VISION in the RIGHT EYE over one week. She had been admitted a few months ago with SPASTIC WEAKNESS IN HER LOWER LIMBS. T2 MRI of the brain revealed MULTIPLE HYPERINTENSE LESIONS IN THE PERIVENTRICULAR AREA, and ESR was NORMAL. Q: Which of the following is TRUE?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Steroid pulse therapy is a useful initial therapeutic approach
choices.explanation: This is actually TRUE in management of an acute MS relapse, but it is not the option highlighted; the marked answer addresses pupillary reflexes. High-dose IV methylprednisolone does shorten relapse duration.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Pupillary reflexes are expected to be normal
choices.explanation: In optic neuritis the affected eye develops a RELATIVE AFFERENT PUPILLARY DEFECT (Marcus Gunn pupil): on the swinging-light test the affected pupil paradoxically dilates. So pupillary reflexes are NOT normal — making this statement the one the question tests as the abnormality marked; per the file key this option is highlighted. Note: standard teaching says an RAPD is present, so the pupillary light reflex is abnormal.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Screening for vasculitis has no value
choices.explanation: ESR is normal here and the clinical picture (relapsing CNS lesions disseminated in time and space) is MS; vasculitis screening is reasonable to exclude mimics but the picture is classic for MS.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Behcet's disease is the most likely diagnosis
choices.explanation: Behcet's typically presents with oral/genital ulcers and uveitis. The relapsing CNS picture with periventricular T2 lesions is multiple sclerosis.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Antibiotic therapy is the treatment of choice in the above case
choices.explanation: There is no infection; antibiotics are not indicated. Acute MS relapse is treated with high-dose IV corticosteroids.

questions.keyPoint: Optic neuritis in young woman with periventricular white-matter lesions and prior spastic paraparesis = MS; expect a relative afferent pupillary defect (Marcus Gunn pupil).

rows: Painful monocular visual loss, prior spastic paraparesis, periventricular T2 lesions
Dissemination in time and space; RAPD on exam
MRI brain/cord, CSF oligoclonal bands, VEP
IV methylprednisolone for acute relapse; DMTs (beta-interferon) for prevention
Optic neuritis produces a relative afferent pupillary defect

flag.note: FILE ANSWER vs STANDARD TEACHING — File says: B Standard (Davidson's Principles & Practice of Medicine; Oxford Handbook of Clinical Medicine): Optic neuritis classically causes a RELATIVE AFFERENT PUPILLARY DEFECT, so the pupillary light reflex is NOT normal. If the question stem is 'which is TRUE', the textbook-true statement is A (steroid pulse therapy is useful). Exam advice: Match the file key (B) if graded against it; standard MCQs expect that steroid pulse therapy is the true useful statement (A) and that the pupil is abnormal (RAPD).
flag.source: Study guide flag (file key vs standard teaching)

questions.stem: Patient with OBSTRUCTIVE SLEEP APNOEA SYNDROME. Q: Which of the following treatments should be started for Sleep Apnoea Syndrome?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: OSA is treated with POSITIVE airway pressure, not negative. Negative pressure would worsen airway collapse.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: BiPAP/IPAP is reserved for specific scenarios (e.g., concurrent hypoventilation); first-line for OSA is continuous positive pressure (CPAP).

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Nasal CPAP is the standard, most effective therapy for moderate-severe OSA. Continuous positive pressure splints the upper airway open during sleep, preventing collapse, improving daytime somnolence and reducing cardiovascular risk.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: OSA does not require intubation; CPAP is delivered non-invasively via a nasal/face mask.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Neither intermittent nor negative pressure is used; OSA needs continuous POSITIVE pressure.

questions.keyPoint: First-line therapy for moderate-to-severe obstructive sleep apnoea is nasal CPAP.

rows: Snoring, witnessed apnoeas, daytime somnolence, obesity
Nasal CPAP is first-line for OSA

questions.stem: Statements regarding MULTIPLE SCLEROSIS (MS). Q: All the following are true regarding Multiple Sclerosis (MS) EXCEPT:

choices.label: A
choices.text: Immune-mediated pathogenesis in a genetically susceptible patient triggered by environmental factors is an accepted theory
choices.explanation: This is TRUE — MS is an immune-mediated demyelinating disease in genetically susceptible individuals with environmental triggers (low vitamin D, EBV, smoking).

choices.label: B
choices.text: Betaferon therapy is important to delay progression in relapsing-remitting disease
choices.explanation: This is TRUE — beta-interferon (Betaferon) is a disease-modifying therapy that reduces relapse rate in relapsing-remitting MS.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Vertigo tends to be peripheral in type
choices.explanation: This is the FALSE statement (the exception). In MS, vertigo is CENTRAL in origin (brainstem/cerebellar demyelinating plaques), not peripheral. Central vertigo features include accompanying brainstem signs and direction-changing nystagmus.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Oral therapy (Fingolimod) is a promising line of therapy
choices.explanation: This is TRUE — fingolimod is an oral immunomodulator (sphingosine-1-phosphate receptor modulator) used in relapsing forms of MS.

choices.label: E
choices.text: MRI findings are essential in following up the disease process
choices.explanation: This is TRUE — MRI is central to diagnosis (dissemination in time/space) and monitoring of MS.

questions.keyPoint: In MS, vertigo is central (brainstem) in origin, not peripheral.

rows: Relapsing CNS deficits disseminated in time and space
McDonald criteria, MRI, CSF oligoclonal bands
Relapse: IV steroids; DMTs: interferon, fingolimod
MS vertigo is central, not peripheral

questions.stem: Definition of DYSTONIA. Q: Dystonia is defined as:

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Slow distal writhing movements describe ATHETOSIS, not dystonia.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Involuntary muscle contraction leading to abnormal posture
choices.explanation: Dystonia is a movement disorder characterised by sustained or repetitive involuntary muscle contractions that produce twisting, repetitive movements or abnormal/fixed postures.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Jerky involuntary movements describe CHOREA, not dystonia.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: A resting involuntary movement describes a resting TREMOR (e.g., parkinsonian tremor), not dystonia.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Rhythmic contraction and relaxation around a joint
choices.explanation: Rhythmic oscillatory movement around a joint describes TREMOR, not dystonia.

questions.keyPoint: Dystonia = sustained/repetitive involuntary muscle contraction causing abnormal posturing.

rows: Co-contraction of agonist and antagonist muscles (basal ganglia dysfunction)
Clinical; consider Wilson's/genetic causes if young
Botulinum toxin, anticholinergics, deep brain stimulation
Dystonia = involuntary contraction → abnormal posture (vs chorea = jerky, athetosis = slow writhing)

questions.stem: Features of ANTERIOR SPINAL ARTERY THROMBOSIS. Q: The following are features of anterior spinal artery thrombosis EXCEPT:

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: This is TRUE — anterior spinal artery infarction causes dissociated sensory loss (loss of pain/temperature with preserved vibration/proprioception).

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: This is TRUE — a sensory level (for pain/temperature) is found below the level of the infarct.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: This is TRUE — corticospinal tract involvement produces motor weakness/paraparesis below the lesion.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: This is TRUE — autonomic tracts are affected, causing bladder/bowel sphincter disturbance.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Loss of vibration and position sense
choices.explanation: This is the EXCEPTION (false). The anterior spinal artery supplies the anterior two-thirds of the cord, SPARING the dorsal columns. Therefore vibration and proprioception are PRESERVED — their loss is NOT a feature.

questions.keyPoint: Anterior spinal artery syndrome spares the dorsal columns — vibration and proprioception are preserved.

rows: Para/quadriparesis, loss of pain/temperature, sphincter disturbance, preserved dorsal columns
MRI spine showing anterior cord infarct
MRI, vascular risk assessment, aortic imaging

questions.stem: First line of management of VASCULAR DEMENTIA. Q: The first line of management of vascular dementia is:

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Shunting treats normal-pressure hydrocephalus, not vascular dementia.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: SSRIs treat coexisting depression but are not the primary management of vascular dementia.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Vascular dementia arises from cumulative cerebrovascular insults. The cornerstone of management is controlling vascular risk factors — chiefly HYPERTENSION, plus diabetes, hyperlipidaemia and smoking cessation — to prevent further ischaemic damage.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Cholinesterase inhibitors are first-line in ALZHEIMER'S disease; they have limited efficacy in vascular dementia.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Atypical antipsychotics are used only for severe behavioural symptoms and carry stroke risk; not first-line.

questions.keyPoint: Vascular dementia is managed primarily by aggressive control of vascular risk factors, especially hypertension.

rows: Stepwise cognitive decline, focal signs, vascular risk factors

questions.stem: A 30-YEAR-OLD MAN presents with ACUTE RIGHT-SIDED LOWER MOTOR NEURON FACIAL WEAKNESS. Q: Which of the following is NOT correct regarding idiopathic (Bell's) palsy and its differentials?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Presence of a vesicular rash at the external auditory meatus is consistent with herpes zoster infection
choices.explanation: This is CORRECT — vesicles in the ear canal indicate Ramsay Hunt syndrome (herpes zoster oticus).

choices.label: B
choices.text: A course of steroids can be given within the first 2 weeks of onset
choices.explanation: This is CORRECT — early oral corticosteroids improve outcomes in Bell's palsy.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Loss of sensation in the right side of the face is consistent with the idiopathic type
choices.explanation: This is the FALSE statement (the answer). Bell's palsy affects the facial (VII) nerve, which controls MOTOR function of the face, not sensation. True sensory loss over the face is NOT a feature of idiopathic Bell's palsy and instead suggests trigeminal nerve involvement or another lesion.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Viral etiology should be suspected as a common cause of Bell's palsy
choices.explanation: This is CORRECT — HSV reactivation is implicated in the aetiology of Bell's palsy.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Additional right-sided abducent nerve palsy suggests a cerebellopontine angle lesion
choices.explanation: This is CORRECT — combined VI and VII involvement points to a pontine/cerebellopontine angle lesion rather than idiopathic Bell's palsy.

questions.keyPoint: Bell's palsy is a LMN facial (motor) palsy; sensory facial loss is NOT a feature and points to another diagnosis.

rows: Acute unilateral facial weakness including forehead, no sensory loss
Early oral steroids +/- antivirals, eye protection
Bell's palsy is purely motor; facial sensory loss is not seen

questions.stem: GUILLAIN-BARRE SYNDROME (GBS). Q: In Guillain-Barre Syndrome (GBS), which of the following is true?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Lymphocytes (>500 cells) with increased protein are the main CSF abnormality
choices.explanation: GBS shows albuminocytologic dissociation — RAISED PROTEIN with NORMAL/low cell count (<10 cells). A high lymphocyte count (>500) would suggest infection/inflammation, not GBS.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The cardinal features are flaccid areflexic weakness
choices.explanation: GBS classically presents with progressive ASCENDING FLACCID weakness and AREFLEXIA (loss of deep tendon reflexes), often following an infectious trigger (e.g., Campylobacter jejuni).

choices.label: C
choices.text: The central nervous system is affected in a scattered manner
choices.explanation: GBS is a disorder of the PERIPHERAL nervous system (immune attack on peripheral nerve myelin/axons); scattered CNS lesions describe MS.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Extreme ages, history of diarrheal illness, and axonal affection are good prognostic signs
choices.explanation: These are POOR prognostic features — extremes of age, preceding diarrhoeal (axonal/AMAN) illness and axonal degeneration predict worse recovery.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Urine retention and sensory level are common features
choices.explanation: A sensory level and prominent sphincter dysfunction suggest a SPINAL CORD lesion, not GBS; in GBS sensory signs are mild and a true sensory level is absent.

questions.keyPoint: GBS = ascending flaccid areflexic weakness with albuminocytologic dissociation in CSF.

rows: LP (high protein, normal cells), nerve conduction, spirometry
IVIG or plasma exchange, supportive ventilation
Cardinal feature = flaccid areflexic ascending weakness

questions.stem: Statements about PARKINSON'S DISEASE. Q: In Parkinson's disease:

choices.label: A
choices.text: The weakness is distal in nature
choices.explanation: Parkinson's causes bradykinesia and rigidity, not true weakness; there is no distal weakness pattern.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Kayser-Fleischer ring in young is characteristic of punch-drunk syndrome
choices.explanation: Kayser-Fleischer rings are characteristic of WILSON'S disease, not punch-drunk (chronic traumatic) encephalopathy.

choices.label: C
choices.text: L-dopa therapy is the cornerstone in the management
choices.explanation: Levodopa (usually combined with carbidopa/benserazide) is the most effective drug and the cornerstone of symptomatic treatment in idiopathic Parkinson's disease, replenishing dopamine in the nigrostriatal pathway.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Young age group is usually affected
choices.explanation: Parkinson's disease typically presents in OLDER adults (onset usually in later adulthood), not the young.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Reflexes are NORMAL or slightly reduced in Parkinson's; brisk/exaggerated reflexes suggest an UMN/parkinson-plus process.

questions.keyPoint: Levodopa is the cornerstone of symptomatic management of Parkinson's disease.

rows: Bradykinesia, rigidity, resting tremor, postural instability
L-dopa is the cornerstone of treatment

questions.stem: Aetiology of ALZHEIMER'S DISEASE. Q: Regarding the aetiology of Alzheimer's disease:

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Familial early-onset Alzheimer's is AUTOSOMAL DOMINANT (APP, PSEN1, PSEN2 mutations), not recessive.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The gene for presenilin-1 is on chromosome 19
choices.explanation: PRESENILIN-1 is on chromosome 14; chromosome 19 carries the APOE risk gene.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The presenilin-2 gene is on chromosome 14
choices.explanation: PRESENILIN-2 is on chromosome 1; presenilin-1 is on chromosome 14.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Females are affected twice as commonly as males
choices.explanation: Alzheimer's disease shows a female predominance (roughly 2:1), especially in advanced age. Most familial early-onset cases follow an autosomal dominant pattern and trauma is a contributing risk factor.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Head trauma IS a recognised risk factor for Alzheimer's disease, so this statement is false.

questions.keyPoint: Alzheimer's disease is about twice as common in women; familial forms are autosomal dominant.

rows: Progressive memory loss, amyloid plaques, neurofibrillary tangles
Clinical + cognitive testing, exclude reversible causes
Female:male ~2:1; familial forms autosomal dominant (APP/PSEN1 chr14/PSEN2 chr1)

questions.stem: SELEGILINE classification. Q: Selegiline is:

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Selegiline is not an antibiotic; it is an antiparkinsonian agent.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Selegiline is a monoamine oxidase inhibitor, specifically MAO-B at low doses. It reduces dopamine breakdown and is used in Parkinson's disease management; at higher doses it loses MAO-B selectivity.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: It is not a tricyclic antidepressant.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: It is not a selective serotonin reuptake inhibitor; combining it with SSRIs risks serotonin syndrome.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: It is not a serotonin-noradrenaline reuptake inhibitor.

questions.keyPoint: Selegiline is a selective MAO-B inhibitor used in Parkinson's disease.

rows: Inhibits dopamine breakdown in the brain

questions.stem: Clinical TEST used to diagnose MYASTHENIA GRAVIS. Q: What is the name of the clinical test used to diagnose Myasthenia gravis?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: MRI brain is not used to diagnose MG; MRI/CT chest is used to look for thymoma.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: This is the option MARKED in the file (red box). EMG/repetitive nerve stimulation is a confirmatory electrophysiological test for MG (decremental response). NOTE: the page's own explanatory text describes the Tensilon (edrophonium) test, which is the classic bedside CLINICAL test (option C) - see flag.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: The Tensilon (edrophonium) test is the classic bedside CLINICAL test for MG (transient improvement in strength), and the page's note describes it - so by standard teaching C is the expected answer. However the file highlights B, hence the flag. Use C for standard MCQs.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Evoked potentials assess CNS conduction pathways (e.g., VEP in MS), not the neuromuscular junction.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: CT thymus screens for thymoma/thymic hyperplasia but is not the clinical confirmatory test for the diagnosis itself.

questions.keyPoint: The Tensilon (edrophonium) test transiently improves muscle strength and is the classic clinical test for MG.

rows: Fatigable weakness, ptosis, diplopia, worse at end of day
Anti-AChR antibodies, repetitive nerve stimulation/SFEMG, Tensilon test
AChR/MuSK antibodies, EMG, CT chest for thymoma
Tensilon (edrophonium) test = clinical bedside test for MG

flag.note: FILE ANSWER vs STANDARD TEACHING — File says: B Standard (Davidson's; Oxford Handbook of Clinical Medicine): The bedside CLINICAL test for myasthenia gravis is the Tensilon (edrophonium) test (option C); the explanatory note on the page itself describes the Tensilon test. EMG is an electrophysiological investigation, not the named clinical test. Exam advice: Match the file key (B/EMG) if graded against it, but standard MCQs expect C (Tensilon/edrophonium test); the page's own note supports C.
flag.source: Study guide flag (file key vs standard teaching)

questions.stem: Most common manifestation of MUSCLE WEAKNESS in MYASTHENIA GRAVIS. Q: The most common manifestation of muscle weakness in Myasthenia gravis is:

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Diplopia is very common and often accompanies ptosis, but ptosis is the single most common/early presenting feature of ocular MG.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Proximal limb (and bulbar) weakness occurs in generalised MG but is not the most common initial manifestation; ocular signs predominate at onset.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Dysphagia reflects bulbar involvement and occurs later/less commonly than ocular signs.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Fatigability is the underlying characteristic of MG, but the most common discrete clinical manifestation/presentation is ptosis (an ocular sign).

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Ptosis is one of the most common and earliest manifestations of MG, frequently accompanied by diplopia due to extraocular muscle involvement. Ocular symptoms are the presenting feature in the majority of patients.

questions.keyPoint: Ptosis (with diplopia) is the most common/earliest manifestation of myasthenia gravis.

rows: Fatigable ptosis, diplopia, worse with activity
Ptosis is the most common presenting sign

questions.stem: DUCHENNE MUSCULAR DYSTROPHY (DMD). Q: In Duchenne Muscular Dystrophy:

choices.label: A
choices.text: Age of onset is around 10 years of age
choices.explanation: DMD presents EARLY, usually before age 5 with delayed motor milestones; onset at 10 is too late and suggests Becker dystrophy.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Cardiac assessment should be done frequently early in the disease
choices.explanation: DILATED CARDIOMYOPATHY is a common and serious complication of DMD, so routine/early cardiac surveillance (ECG, echocardiography) is essential even before overt cardiac symptoms appear.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Creatine kinase (CPK) is MARKEDLY elevated (often 10-100x normal) in DMD, not mildly.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: DMD is X-LINKED RECESSIVE (dystrophin gene), affecting boys; it is not autosomal.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Life expectancy is more than 35 years of age
choices.explanation: Although improved, life expectancy in DMD generally does not extend much beyond the third decade without advanced interventions.

questions.keyPoint: Cardiomyopathy is a major DMD complication; perform early/regular cardiac assessment.

rows: X-linked, onset <5y, calf pseudohypertrophy, Gower sign
Markedly raised CK, dystrophin gene mutation

questions.stem: The CELL BODIES of SEROTONIN-RELEASING NEURONS. Q: The cell bodies of serotonin-releasing neurons are located in an area of the brain known as the:

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: The basal forebrain contains CHOLINERGIC neurons (e.g., nucleus basalis of Meynert), not the main serotonergic cell bodies.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Serotonin (5-HT) is synthesised predominantly in the RAPHE NUCLEI of the brainstem. These neurons project widely throughout the brain and regulate mood, sleep, appetite and other functions.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: The frontal cortex receives serotonergic projections but does not contain the cell bodies.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: The cingulate cortex is a projection target, not the source of serotonergic neurons.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: The locus ceruleus is the main source of NORADRENERGIC (noradrenaline) neurons, not serotonergic.

questions.keyPoint: Serotonergic neuron cell bodies reside in the brainstem raphe nuclei (locus ceruleus = noradrenaline).

rows: Raphe nuclei = serotonin; locus ceruleus = noradrenaline

questions.stem: CSF FINDINGS in a patient with VIRAL ENCEPHALITIS. Q: CSF findings in a patient with viral encephalitis typically include:

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: A positive Gram stain indicates BACTERIAL infection, not viral encephalitis.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: In viral encephalitis protein is normal or only mildly elevated; markedly high protein (>100 mg/dL) suggests bacterial or TB meningitis.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Viral encephalitis characteristically shows a LYMPHOCYTIC pleocytosis (mild-to-moderate), with normal/slightly reduced glucose and mildly elevated protein.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Glucose is typically NORMAL in viral encephalitis; markedly low glucose points to bacterial/TB/fungal infection.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Opening pressure is usually normal or only mildly raised in viral encephalitis, not characteristically elevated.

questions.keyPoint: Viral encephalitis CSF = lymphocytic pleocytosis, normal glucose, mildly raised protein.

rows: Fever, altered mental state, seizures (HSV: temporal)
LP with viral PCR, MRI brain, EEG

questions.stem: DIABETIC NEUROPATHY. Q: In diabetic neuropathy, all of the following are true EXCEPT:

choices.label: A
choices.text: Mononeuritis multiplex has a vascular basis
choices.explanation: This is TRUE — mononeuritis multiplex in diabetes results from ischaemic injury to individual nerves (vasa nervorum), a vascular mechanism.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Glove-and-stocking sensory loss and absent ankle jerks are common presentations
choices.explanation: This is TRUE — distal symmetric diabetic polyneuropathy classically causes glove-and-stocking sensory loss with depressed/absent ankle jerks.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Diabetic amyotrophy (proximal motor neuropathy) is often asymmetrical in presentation
choices.explanation: This is TRUE — diabetic amyotrophy tends to be asymmetrical and involves the proximal lower limb (hip girdle).

choices.label: D
choices.text: Amitriptyline is useful to control pain
choices.explanation: This is TRUE — amitriptyline (and other agents) is used for painful diabetic neuropathy.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Diabetic polyneuropathy presents with proximal weakness
choices.explanation: This is the FALSE statement (the exception). Diabetic polyneuropathy is a DISTAL, length-dependent, predominantly SENSORY neuropathy (glove-and-stocking); proximal weakness is not its typical presentation (that pattern fits amyotrophy).

questions.keyPoint: Diabetic polyneuropathy is distal and sensory (glove-and-stocking); it does not present with proximal weakness.

rows: Distal symmetric glove-and-stocking sensory loss, absent ankle jerks
HbA1c, NCS, exclude other causes HIGH-YIELD SUMMARY
It is distal/sensory, not proximal motor

questions.stem: Common causes of DELIRIUM IN THE ELDERLY. Q: One of the most common causes of delirium in the elderly is:

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Acute hypoxia can precipitate delirium but is not THE single most common cause; polypharmacy is more frequent. (Note: marked in file as a common distractor.)

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Substance/alcohol issues cause delirium but are not the leading cause in the elderly population.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Polypharmacy is one of the most common causes of delirium in the elderly. Older adults often take multiple medications, increasing the risk of adverse drug interactions and side effects; benzodiazepines, anticholinergics and opioids are common culprits.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Accidental poisoning is uncommon as a routine cause of elderly delirium compared with medications.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Alcohol withdrawal can cause delirium but is less common than medication-related (polypharmacy) causes in the elderly.

questions.keyPoint: Polypharmacy is among the most common, reversible causes of delirium in older adults.

rows: Treat cause, stop offending drugs, supportive
Polypharmacy is a leading reversible cause End block 1

flag.note: FILE ANSWER vs STANDARD TEACHING — File says: C Standard (Oxford Handbook of Clinical Medicine; Davidson's): Polypharmacy/drugs is indeed a leading cause of delirium in the elderly (option C, highlighted green). Option A (acute hypoxia) carries a red mark in the file as a common distractor, but the correct/green answer is C. Exam advice: Answer C (polypharmacy) — matches the green highlight and standard teaching.
flag.source: Study guide flag (file key vs standard teaching)

questions.stem: Which of these is NOT SPECIFIC to the cerebellum. Q: One of these is not specific to cerebellum:

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Nystagmus is a recognised cerebellar sign (gaze-evoked nystagmus).

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Dysdiadochokinesia (impaired rapid alternating movements) is a specific cerebellar sign.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: The rebound phenomenon is a specific cerebellar sign of impaired check.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: An unsteady gait is NON-specific — it occurs in sensory ataxia, vestibular disease, weakness and many other conditions, not only cerebellar disease.

questions.keyPoint: Unsteady gait is non-specific; dysdiadochokinesis, nystagmus and rebound are specific cerebellar signs.

rows: DANISH signs (dysdiadochokinesia, ataxia, nystagmus, intention tremor, slurred speech, hypotonia)
Unsteady gait is non-specific, not cerebellum-specific

questions.stem: Feature usually NOT SEEN IN SYNCOPE versus seizure disorder. Q: The following feature is usually not seen in syncope versus seizure disorder:

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: A prodrome/aura (lightheadedness, sweating) can occur before syncope, so it is seen in syncope too.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Lateral tongue biting is characteristic of a generalised tonic-clonic SEIZURE and is typically NOT seen in syncope — it helps distinguish a seizure from a faint.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Absence of headache after the attack
choices.explanation: Lack of post-event headache fits syncope (rapid recovery), so it can be seen in syncope.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: No content / not the answer.

questions.keyPoint: Lateral tongue biting points to seizure, not syncope.

rows: Seizure: tongue bite, post-ictal confusion; syncope: rapid recovery, pallor

questions.stem: Statement about PARKINSON DISEASE. Q: In Parkinson disease:

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Parkinson's is a disease of older adults, not the young.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: No content.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: No content.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Levodopa (with a peripheral decarboxylase inhibitor) is the most effective drug and main symptomatic treatment of idiopathic Parkinson's disease.

questions.keyPoint: L-dopa is the main (most effective) treatment of Parkinson's disease.

questions.stem: Symptom NOT SEEN in PSEUDOBULBAR PALSY. Q: Which symptom is not seen in pseudobulbar palsy?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Pseudobulbar palsy is an UPPER motor neuron lesion of the lower cranial nerves; fasciculations are a LOWER motor neuron sign (seen in BULBAR palsy), so they are NOT seen in pseudobulbar palsy.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Brisk reflexes are expected in pseudobulbar (UMN) palsy.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Increased tone (spasticity) is an UMN feature seen in pseudobulbar palsy.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: An upgoing plantar (Babinski) is an UMN sign present in pseudobulbar palsy.

questions.keyPoint: Pseudobulbar (UMN) palsy: spastic tongue, brisk jaw jerk, emotional lability — NO fasciculations (those are bulbar/LMN).

rows: Spastic dysarthria, brisk jaw jerk, emotional lability, no wasting/fasciculation
No fasciculations (UMN); fasciculations = bulbar/LMN palsy End block 2

questions.stem: A 26-YEAR-OLD with sudden UNSTEADINESS and INABILITY TO WALK for 12 hours, with CLUMSINESS when he uses his hand to handle objects. Q: Where is the site of lesion?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: A cortical lesion would produce contralateral weakness/sensory or higher-function deficits, not pure limb incoordination.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Acute ataxia with limb clumsiness/incoordination (intention tremor, past-pointing) localises to the CEREBELLUM. Cerebellar lesions impair coordination of voluntary movement on the same side.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Sensory ataxia worsens in the dark and has a positive Romberg with proprioceptive loss; the clumsiness of handling objects here fits cerebellar dysfunction.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Basal ganglia lesions cause movement disorders (parkinsonism, chorea), not ataxic limb incoordination.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: No content.

questions.keyPoint: Acute limb incoordination + gait ataxia = cerebellar lesion.

rows: Limb incoordination + gait ataxia localises to cerebellum

questions.stem: Patient with WEAKNESS on the RIGHT SIDE and APHASIA. Q: Which artery is damaged?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Right MCA would cause LEFT-sided weakness and (in left-dominant patients) preserve language; aphasia indicates left hemisphere.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Lacunar (small-vessel) strokes cause pure motor/sensory syndromes WITHOUT cortical signs such as aphasia.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Right-sided weakness plus APHASIA indicates a dominant (usually left) hemisphere cortical stroke. The left MCA supplies the motor cortex (controlling the right body) and the language areas (Broca/Wernicke).

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: No content.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: No content.

questions.keyPoint: Right hemiparesis + aphasia = left (dominant) MCA territory stroke.

rows: Right face/arm>leg weakness, aphasia, right hemianopia

questions.stem: Patient with EPILEPSY had RECURRENT ATTACKS of SHORT DURATION (30-60 seconds) with LIP SMACKING, AUTOMATISMS, and POST-ICTAL CONFUSION; cannot remember the episode. Q: What is the type of epilepsy?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: A focal aware (simple partial) seizure preserves awareness; here the patient cannot remember and is confused, so awareness is impaired.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Lip-smacking automatisms, impaired awareness and post-ictal confusion are characteristic of a FOCAL seizure with IMPAIRED AWARENESS (complex partial), typically temporal lobe in origin.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Absence seizures are brief blank stares in children with NO post-ictal confusion and immediate recovery; automatisms with post-ictal confusion point to focal seizures.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: No content.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: No content.

questions.keyPoint: Automatisms (lip-smacking) + impaired awareness + post-ictal confusion = focal impaired-awareness (temporal lobe) seizure.

rows: Aura, automatisms, impaired awareness, post-ictal confusion
Automatisms + post-ictal confusion = focal impaired awareness

questions.stem: Patient ATE A HEAVY MEAL then suddenly became PARALYSED with LOW SERUM POTASSIUM. Q: What is the diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: No content.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: No content.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Episodic flaccid paralysis triggered by a carbohydrate-rich meal (insulin drives potassium into cells) with documented HYPOKALAEMIA is classic for hypokalaemic periodic paralysis (a channelopathy), often associated with thyrotoxicosis in Asians.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: No content.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: No content.

questions.keyPoint: Carbohydrate meal → flaccid paralysis with low K+ = hypokalaemic periodic paralysis.

rows: Episodic flaccid weakness after carbohydrate/rest, low serum K+
Low K+ during attack; consider thyrotoxicosis
Potassium replacement, treat thyrotoxicosis, avoid triggers HIGH-YIELD SUMMARY
Carb meal + low K+ + paralysis = hypoK periodic paralysis

questions.stem: A FEMALE who is GOOD IN THE MORNING and gets WORSE AT NIGHT. Q: What is the diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: No content.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Fatigable weakness that is mild in the morning and WORSENS through the day/with activity is the hallmark of MYASTHENIA GRAVIS (antibodies against the post-synaptic acetylcholine receptor).

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: No content.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: In Lambert-Eaton, weakness IMPROVES with repeated activity (post-exercise facilitation) and is associated with small-cell lung cancer — the opposite diurnal/activity pattern.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: No content.

questions.keyPoint: Worsening weakness through the day (fatigability) = myasthenia gravis; improvement with exercise = Lambert-Eaton.

rows: Fatigable weakness worse in evening, ptosis, diplopia
Fatigability worse at night = MG End block 1

questions.stem: Patient came suddenly with the WORST HEADACHE OF HER LIFE and MENINGISM. Q: Best initial imaging?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Thunderclap headache with meningism suggests subarachnoid haemorrhage. The best INITIAL investigation is a NON-CONTRAST CT of the head, which is highly sensitive for acute blood within the first 6-24 hours.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: MRI is less available/slower and not the first-line acute test for SAH.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Skull X-ray has no role in detecting intracranial haemorrhage.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: LP (for xanthochromia) is done only AFTER a negative CT, and not before 12 hours; it is not the initial imaging.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: No content.

questions.keyPoint: Thunderclap headache → non-contrast CT head first; LP only if CT negative.

rows: Non-contrast CT; LP (xanthochromia) if CT negative
Non-contrast CT is the best initial test

questions.stem: MCDONALD CRITERIA for MS DIAGNOSIS. Q: McDonald criteria for MS diagnosis depends on:

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: No content.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: No content.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: The McDonald criteria diagnose MS by demonstrating lesions DISSEMINATED IN TIME (new lesions at different time points) and IN SPACE (different CNS locations), supported by MRI and CSF findings.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: No content.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: No content.

questions.keyPoint: McDonald criteria = dissemination in time AND space.

rows: Lesions disseminated in time and space
MRI + clinical attacks; CSF oligoclonal bands support

questions.stem: LUMBAR PUNCTURE in a patient with MS. Q: LP of patient with MS will show:

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: CSF protein is normal or only slightly raised in MS; markedly high protein suggests GBS or infection.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: CSF glucose is normal in MS; it is not raised.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Oligoclonal band not matched with serum
choices.explanation: MS shows intrathecal IgG synthesis — CSF-restricted OLIGOCLONAL BANDS that are NOT present in matched serum, indicating local CNS antibody production.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: No content.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: No content.

questions.keyPoint: MS CSF = oligoclonal bands present in CSF but NOT in matched serum.

rows: Oligoclonal bands in CSF not matched in serum

questions.stem: A YOUNG FEMALE with PAINLESS RIGHT-SIDED BLURRED VISION that WORSENS WITH EYE MOVEMENT; FUNDOSCOPIC EXAMINATION WAS NORMAL (retrobulbar optic neuritis). Q: What would you expect to find on the next investigation?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Retrobulbar optic neuritis (vision worse on eye movement, normal fundus because inflammation is behind the disc) characteristically produces a RELATIVE AFFERENT PUPILLARY DEFECT (Marcus Gunn pupil) on the swinging-light test.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: A central scotoma occurs but the specific expected sign on the pupil exam is an RAPD; field testing is not the 'next investigation' clue intended here.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Perimetry may show a scotoma but RAPD is the classic pupillary finding in optic neuritis.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: The fundus is normal in retrobulbar neuritis; haemorrhage is not expected.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: No content.

questions.keyPoint: Retrobulbar optic neuritis: vision worse on eye movement, normal fundus, RAPD present.

rows: Painful monocular blurring worse on eye movement, normal fundus, RAPD
Optic neuritis → relative afferent pupillary defect

questions.stem: MOST MODIFIABLE RISK FACTOR of ISCHAEMIC STROKE. Q: The most modifiable risk factor of ischaemic stroke is:

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: HYPERTENSION is the single most important MODIFIABLE risk factor for ischaemic (and haemorrhagic) stroke; treating it markedly reduces stroke risk.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Smoking is an important modifiable risk factor but confers less population-attributable stroke risk than hypertension.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Diabetes increases stroke risk but is a weaker contributor than hypertension.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: No content.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: No content.

questions.keyPoint: Hypertension is the most important modifiable risk factor for stroke.

rows: Hypertension = most important modifiable risk factor End block 5

questions.stem: A 30-YEAR-OLD FEMALE presents with ATAXIA and NYSTAGMUS; she says she had VISUAL LOSS 3 YEARS AGO that RESPONDED WELL TO INTRAVENOUS MEDICATIONS. Q: What is the diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Two CNS events disseminated in time (optic neuritis 3 years ago that responded to IV steroids) and space (current cerebellar ataxia/nystagmus) in a young woman = MULTIPLE SCLEROSIS.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: NMO causes severe optic neuritis and longitudinally extensive transverse myelitis with aquaporin-4 antibodies; the relapsing brainstem/cerebellar picture here fits MS better.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Optic neuritis alone is a single event; the additional cerebellar signs years later indicate dissemination = MS.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: ADEM is typically a monophasic post-infectious demyelination in children, not a relapsing course over years.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Acute necrotising haemorrhagic encephalomyelitis is a fulminant monophasic illness, not this relapsing picture.

questions.keyPoint: Optic neuritis years earlier + new cerebellar signs = MS (dissemination in time and space).

rows: Relapsing CNS deficits (optic neuritis, cerebellar) disseminated in time/space
McDonald criteria, MRI, CSF oligoclonal bands
Two events in time and space = MS

questions.stem: A 20-YEAR-OLD FEMALE presents with UNILATERAL THROBBING STRONG HEADACHE FOR 2 HOURS with NAUSEA & VOMITING, PRECEDED BY VISIONS OF FLASHING LIGHT. Q: What is the diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Cluster headache is severe unilateral periorbital pain with autonomic features (lacrimation, rhinorrhoea), typically in men, lasting 15-180 min — not throbbing with visual aura.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Unilateral THROBBING headache with nausea/vomiting preceded by a visual AURA (flashing lights/scintillations) is classic MIGRAINE WITH AURA, typical in young women.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Tension headache is bilateral, band-like, non-throbbing, without aura or vomiting.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Raised ICP headache is worse in the morning/on lying down with papilloedema, not a brief aura-preceded throbbing attack.

questions.keyPoint: Throbbing unilateral headache + nausea + visual aura = migraine with aura.

rows: Unilateral throbbing headache, nausea/vomiting, photophobia, visual aura
Clinical; imaging only if red flags
Visual aura + throbbing headache = migraine with aura

questions.stem: For the previous case (MIGRAINE WITH AURA in a 20-year-old female). Q: What prophylactic treatment should be given?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Paracetamol is an acute analgesic, not a prophylactic agent for migraine.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Carbamazepine is used for trigeminal neuralgia/epilepsy, not migraine prophylaxis.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: PROPRANOLOL, a beta-blocker, is a first-line PROPHYLACTIC agent for migraine, reducing the frequency and severity of attacks.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Verapamil is used for CLUSTER headache prophylaxis, not migraine.

questions.keyPoint: Propranolol is first-line migraine prophylaxis (verapamil = cluster prophylaxis).

rows: Propranolol = first-line migraine prophylaxis End block 6

questions.stem: Regarding EEG WAVES. Q: Which of the following statements is TRUE?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Theta and Delta waves are considered normal findings in awake adult patients
choices.explanation: Theta and delta are SLOW waves; in an awake adult they are abnormal. Delta is normal only in deep (slow-wave) sleep and in infants.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Alpha waves are the characteristic waves of a child during deep sleep
choices.explanation: Alpha (8-13 Hz) is the characteristic rhythm of a relaxed awake adult with eyes closed, not of a child in deep sleep (that is delta).

choices.label: C
choices.text: Beta waves are the normal dominant rhythm in active, alert adults
choices.explanation: BETA waves (>13 Hz, fast/low-amplitude) are the normal dominant rhythm during active mental concentration and alert wakefulness/eyes open.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The presence of Theta and Delta waves during sleep in an adult is considered abnormal
choices.explanation: Theta and delta during SLEEP in an adult are NORMAL (slow-wave sleep); they are abnormal only when present in the awake adult.

questions.keyPoint: Beta = alert/active; Alpha = relaxed eyes-closed; Theta/Delta = sleep (delta normal in deep sleep, abnormal when awake).

rows: Beta>13Hz alert; Alpha 8-13Hz relaxed; Theta 4-7Hz; Delta<4Hz deep sleep
Beta is the dominant rhythm of the alert adult

questions.stem: Clinical features of CEREBELLAR DYSFUNCTION. Q: All of the following are non-specific clinical features of cerebellar dysfunction EXCEPT (i.e., which is NON-specific):

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Dysdiadochokinesia is a SPECIFIC cerebellar sign (impaired rapid alternating movements).

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Hypotonia is a NON-SPECIFIC feature — reduced tone occurs in many conditions (LMN lesions, acute UMN/spinal shock, etc.), whereas dysdiadochokinesia, intention tremor and nystagmus are specific cerebellar signs.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Intention tremor (worse on approaching a target) is a specific cerebellar sign.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Gaze-evoked nystagmus is a recognised cerebellar sign.

questions.keyPoint: Hypotonia is non-specific; dysdiadochokinesis, intention tremor and nystagmus are specific cerebellar signs.

rows: DANISH: dysdiadochokinesia, ataxia, nystagmus, intention tremor, slurred speech, hypotonia
Hypotonia is non-specific among cerebellar features

questions.stem: GREY MATTER distribution in CNS structures. Q: Gray matter is present in all of the following structures EXCEPT:

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: The cerebral cortex is grey matter (neuronal cell bodies).

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: The INTERNAL CAPSULE is composed of WHITE MATTER (myelinated ascending/descending fibre tracts), so it contains no grey matter — making it the exception.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: The thalamus is a deep grey matter nucleus.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: The basal ganglia are deep grey matter nuclei.

questions.keyPoint: The internal capsule is white matter (fibre tracts); cortex, thalamus and basal ganglia are grey matter.

rows: Grey = cell bodies (cortex, nuclei); white = myelinated tracts

questions.stem: Clinical features of a LESION/DYSFUNCTION in the BASAL GANGLIA. Q: Which of the following clinical features is most characteristic of a lesion or dysfunction in the basal ganglia?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Sensory loss reflects thalamic/parietal or sensory pathway lesions, not basal ganglia disease.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Spasticity (velocity-dependent hypertonia) is an UPPER MOTOR NEURON/corticospinal sign, not basal ganglia.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: RIGIDITY (lead-pipe/cogwheel, increased tone throughout the range) is characteristic of basal ganglia (extrapyramidal) dysfunction, e.g., Parkinson's disease, along with bradykinesia and tremor.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Hyperreflexia is an UMN/pyramidal sign, not extrapyramidal.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Hyporeflexia indicates LMN/peripheral nerve lesions, not basal ganglia disease.

questions.keyPoint: Rigidity (not spasticity) is the characteristic extrapyramidal/basal ganglia tone abnormality.

rows: Rigidity is the basal ganglia tone sign; spasticity is pyramidal

questions.stem: UPPER MOTOR NEURON (UMN) LESIONS. Q: Upper Motor Neuron (UMN) lesions are most commonly associated with damage to which of the following structures?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Cerebellar damage causes ataxia/incoordination, not UMN signs.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Spinal cord lesions can produce UMN signs below the level, but the UMN/corticospinal tract ORIGINATES in the cerebral (motor) cortex; the question asks the most common structure of origin.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Upper motor neurons originate in the primary motor CEREBRAL CORTEX and travel via the corticospinal (pyramidal) tract; damage here (e.g., stroke) produces UMN signs — spasticity, hyperreflexia, upgoing plantars.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Basal ganglia lesions cause extrapyramidal signs (rigidity, movement disorders), not classic UMN signs.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: The medulla contains the pyramidal decussation but is not the most common origin/site associated with UMN lesions.

questions.keyPoint: Upper motor neurons arise in the cerebral (motor) cortex; UMN signs = spasticity, hyperreflexia, Babinski.

rows: Spasticity, hyperreflexia, upgoing plantar, no wasting/fasciculation
Damage to corticospinal tract from motor cortex
UMN originates in cerebral cortex Get ahead Neuro

questions.stem: A 68-YEAR-OLD WOMAN presents to her GP with HEADACHES and VISUAL DISTURBANCE. She has noticed that she gets an ITCHY RASH when she gets out of a HOT BATH. She has a RUDDY COMPLEXION and a PALPABLE SPLEEN. Bloods reveal a RAISED PACKED RED CELL VOLUME with a RAISED RED CELL MASS, RAISED WHITE CELL COUNT and THROMBOCYTOSIS. Q: What is the most likely diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: CML causes a very high white cell count with a left shift and massive splenomegaly, but not the raised red cell mass and aquagenic pruritus described here.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Lymphoma causes lymphadenopathy and B symptoms, not a raised red cell mass with aquagenic pruritus.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Migraine does not cause polycythaemia, splenomegaly or itching after a hot bath.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Raised RED CELL MASS with raised WCC and platelets, ruddy complexion, splenomegaly and aquagenic pruritus (itching after a hot bath) are classic for POLYCYTHAEMIA RUBRA VERA (a JAK2-positive myeloproliferative disorder). Hyperviscosity causes headache and visual disturbance.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: The pruritus is aquagenic (caused by water/heat, histamine release), not a soap allergy, and it does not explain the haematological findings.

questions.keyPoint: Aquagenic pruritus + raised red cell mass + splenomegaly = polycythaemia rubra vera (check JAK2).

rows: Ruddy face, aquagenic pruritus, splenomegaly, raised red cell mass/WCC/platelets HIGH-YIELD SUMMARY
Raised red cell mass, JAK2 V617F mutation
Itch after hot bath + high red cell mass = PRV

questions.stem: MRI FINDINGS in MULTIPLE SCLEROSIS. Q: Which of the following best describes the MRI findings in multiple sclerosis?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: MS lesions are predominantly WHITE matter (periventricular), not cortical grey matter.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Longitudinally extensive transverse myelitis (more than three spinal segments)
choices.explanation: LETM over >3 segments is characteristic of NEUROMYELITIS OPTICA (aquaporin-4), not typical MS (whose cord lesions are short).

choices.label: C
choices.text: Periventricular white matter lesions matching the clinical picture
choices.explanation: MS lesions are periventricular white matter, but they do NOT necessarily correlate with the clinical picture (clinico-radiological dissociation).

choices.label: D
choices.text: Periventricular white matter lesions not necessarily matching the clinical picture
choices.explanation: MS produces PERIVENTRICULAR WHITE MATTER plaques (e.g., Dawson's fingers) that are disseminated in time/space and do NOT necessarily correlate with the current clinical symptoms — the classic clinico-radiological dissociation.

choices.label: E
choices.text: White matter lesions exclusively in the cerebellum and brainstem
choices.explanation: MS lesions are widespread (periventricular, juxtacortical, infratentorial, cord), not exclusively cerebellar/brainstem.

questions.keyPoint: MS MRI = periventricular white-matter lesions disseminated in space, not necessarily matching symptoms.

rows: Periventricular white matter plaques, Dawson's fingers

questions.stem: A 60-YEAR-OLD MAN with RECURRENT DIZZY SPELLS for 4 months that occur WHEN HE MOVES HIS HEAD. Attacks are NOT associated with deafness or tinnitus, and neurological examination is entirely normal. Diagnosis of BENIGN PAROXYSMAL POSITIONAL VERTIGO is favoured. Q: Which description of findings on Hallpike's manoeuvre would confirm this diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Delayed onset (a few seconds) torsional nystagmus on descent facing both sides
choices.explanation: BPPV nystagmus is unilateral (positional, on the affected side), not bilateral on both sides.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Delayed onset (a few seconds) torsional nystagmus on descent facing one side only
choices.explanation: BPPV gives a characteristic Hallpike response: a LATENCY of a few seconds, then TORSIONAL nystagmus on descent toward the AFFECTED side only, which is fatigable and adaptable.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Immediate torsional nystagmus on descent facing both sides
choices.explanation: Immediate, non-fatiguing, bilateral nystagmus suggests a central cause, not peripheral BPPV.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Immediate torsional nystagmus on descent facing one side only
choices.explanation: BPPV nystagmus has a LATENCY (delayed onset); immediate onset argues against BPPV.

choices.label: E
choices.text: No nystagmus on descent facing either side
choices.explanation: A confirmatory Hallpike must provoke nystagmus; absence does not confirm BPPV.

questions.keyPoint: BPPV Hallpike: latency, fatigable torsional nystagmus toward the affected side only.

rows: Brief positional vertigo, no deafness/tinnitus, normal exam
Latency + fatigable torsional nystagmus on one side

questions.stem: A 35-YEAR-OLD HOMELESS MAN presents in a STATE OF UNCONSCIOUSNESS. He was fitting when the ambulance crew got to him 20 minutes ago; a friend estimated he had started fitting around 15 minutes before. He is a KNOWN EPILEPTIC and you find a PACK OF PHENYTOIN on him. He looks DISHEVELLED and SMELLS OF ALCOHOL. BP 170/95, temp 37.9 C, quiet systolic murmur. Q: Which of the following investigations will be most useful at this stage?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: CT may be done later to look for a structural cause, but it is not the most useful immediate test in a known epileptic with phenytoin.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: The murmur is incidental; echo does not address the cause of the status epilepticus now.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: EEG is useful for non-convulsive status but is not the most immediately useful test here.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) of the brain
choices.explanation: MRI is impractical/slow in an actively fitting unstable patient.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: In a known epileptic carrying phenytoin who presents in status, SUB-THERAPEUTIC PHENYTOIN LEVELS (poor compliance) is the most likely precipitant, so measuring the phenytoin level is the most useful investigation at this stage.

questions.keyPoint: Known epileptic in status carrying phenytoin → check the (likely subtherapeutic) phenytoin level.

rows: >5 min continuous seizure, known epileptic, non-compliance

questions.stem: An 83-YEAR-OLD MAN diagnosed with Parkinson's disease 3 years ago has been treated with LEVODOPA (L-DOPA). He initially responded well but has become INCREASINGLY STIFF and has FALLEN MORE in the last 4 months DESPITE NO INTERCURRENT ILLNESS OR CHANGE in L-DOPA therapy. Q: Which is the best management option?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: This represents disease progression with reduced response (end-of-dose wearing off). Adding a DOPAMINE AGONIST (e.g., ropinirole) augments dopaminergic stimulation and smooths motor response without simply escalating levodopa toxicity.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Add a peripheral dopamine antagonist (e.g. domperidone)
choices.explanation: Domperidone treats dopaminergic nausea; it would not improve the motor decline.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Reducing levodopa would worsen rigidity and bradykinesia.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Escalating levodopa alone increases the risk of dyskinesias and motor fluctuations; adding an adjunct is preferred.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Stopping levodopa abruptly risks a parkinsonian crisis (neuroleptic-malignant-like syndrome).

questions.keyPoint: Worsening Parkinson's despite stable levodopa → add a dopamine agonist as an adjunct.

rows: Increasing rigidity and falls despite stable levodopa

questions.stem: A 35-YEAR-OLD MAN with a 2-day history of RIGHT-SIDED FACIAL WEAKNESS, otherwise fit, NO PRECEDING INFECTION. Middle ear normal, salivary glands not enlarged, no other cranial nerves affected. The FOREHEAD IS NOT SPARED. Limb examination and routine investigations all normal. Q: Which represents the most reasonable management plan?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Aspirin, dipyridamole, a statin and an angiotensin-converting enzyme (ACE) inhibitor
choices.explanation: This is secondary stroke prevention; forehead involvement indicates a LMN (Bell's) palsy, not a cortical stroke (which spares the forehead).

choices.label: B
choices.text: Penicillin-based antibiotic therapy and antiviral therapy
choices.explanation: There is no infection; routine antibiotics are not indicated, and eye protection/steroids are missing.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Steroids help but, alone, omit essential eye protection (and, where indicated, antivirals).

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Antibiotics are unnecessary here, and eye protection is omitted.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Steroids, antiviral therapy and eye protection
choices.explanation: Bell's palsy (LMN VII palsy, forehead NOT spared) is best managed with early oral STEROIDS, consideration of ANTIVIRALS, and crucially EYE PROTECTION (lubricants/taping) to prevent exposure keratopathy from incomplete eye closure.

questions.keyPoint: Bell's palsy: steroids + antivirals + eye protection (forehead NOT spared = LMN).

rows: Acute LMN facial weakness, forehead involved, no other deficits

questions.stem: A 60-YEAR-OLD MAN with TOTAL VISUAL LOSS in his LEFT EYE developing over 1 day. He had a HEADACHE ON THE LEFT SIDE in the 5 days preceding, CONSTANT AND SEVERE, WORSE ON TALKING OR EATING (jaw claudication). LETHARGIC for 2 months, shoulder aches. RELATIVE AFFERENT PUPILLARY DEFECT; OPTIC DISC PALE AND SWOLLEN. Q: Which investigation would confirm the most likely diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: CT does not diagnose giant cell arteritis.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Inflammatory markers (C-reactive protein (CRP), erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR))
choices.explanation: ESR/CRP are usually raised and support the diagnosis, but they are not CONFIRMATORY; biopsy is.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: LP has no role in diagnosing GCA.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: The picture (jaw claudication, scalp/temporal headache, PMR symptoms, anterior ischaemic optic neuropathy with RAPD) is GIANT CELL (temporal) ARTERITIS. TEMPORAL ARTERY BIOPSY (showing granulomatous arteritis) is the confirmatory/diagnostic test (start steroids immediately, do not delay).

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: VEP assesses optic nerve conduction (e.g., in MS) and does not confirm GCA.

questions.keyPoint: GCA confirmed by temporal artery biopsy; start high-dose steroids immediately to save vision.

rows: Temporal headache, jaw claudication, visual loss, PMR, raised ESR
Temporal artery biopsy (skip lesions); raised ESR/CRP
Biopsy confirms; steroids first, don't delay

questions.stem: A 75-YEAR-OLD MAN recently diagnosed with Parkinson's disease started on LEVODOPA (L-DOPA), which helped his tremor and bradykinesia. In the past week he had a FALL SHORTLY AFTER GETTING OUT OF HIS CHAIR, during which he briefly BLACKED OUT and afterwards was SWEATING AND FELT COLD. Q: Which side effect most likely explains the fall?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Dyskinesias are involuntary movements, not a cause of syncope/blackout on standing.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Hallucinations are a dopaminergic side effect but do not cause a postural blackout.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Nausea is common with levodopa but does not explain syncope on standing.

choices.label: D
choices.text: None, he actually has multiple system atrophy and not Parkinson's disease
choices.explanation: MSA causes autonomic failure, but the scenario fits a recognised levodopa side effect (postural hypotension); diagnosing MSA is not the best single answer here.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Blacking out shortly after standing, with sweating and feeling cold, is classic for POSTURAL (orthostatic) HYPOTENSION — a recognised dopaminergic (levodopa) side effect causing a drop in BP on standing.

questions.keyPoint: Levodopa causes postural hypotension → syncope/falls on standing.

rows: Blackout/fall on standing, sweating, feeling cold

questions.stem: A 52-YEAR-OLD MAN with HYPERTHYROIDISM, VITILIGO and a 30 PACK/YEAR SMOKING history presents with ACUTE CLUMSINESS OF HIS RIGHT HAND. NORMAL CRANIAL NERVES; the only abnormal feature is PAST POINTING AND DYSDIADOCHOKINESIS in the RIGHT HAND. DWI MRI shows a SMALL RIGHT-SIDED CEREBELLAR INFARCT. Q: Which investigation is UNLIKELY to be helpful?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: The cerebellum is supplied by the POSTERIOR (vertebrobasilar) circulation, not the carotid (anterior) circulation. A CAROTID DOPPLER is therefore UNLIKELY to be helpful for a cerebellar (posterior circulation) infarct.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: ECG is useful to look for AF/cardiac source of embolism.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: ESR helps screen for vasculitis as a stroke cause in a younger patient.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: FBC screens for polycythaemia/thrombocytosis contributing to stroke.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: MRA images the vertebrobasilar vessels supplying the cerebellum and is helpful.

questions.keyPoint: Cerebellum = posterior circulation; carotid Doppler (anterior circulation) is unhelpful for cerebellar infarct.

rows: Carotid Doppler is irrelevant for posterior circulation stroke

questions.stem: Findings on LUMBAR PUNCTURE used to suggest MULTIPLE SCLEROSIS (given a correlative clinical picture). Q: Which finding on lumbar puncture is most commonly used to suggest a diagnosis of multiple sclerosis?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Anti-MBP is non-specific and not the standard diagnostic CSF marker for MS.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: CSF-restricted OLIGOCLONAL BANDS on electrophoresis (present in CSF but not in serum) indicate intrathecal IgG synthesis and are the characteristic CSF finding supporting a diagnosis of MS.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: RBCs in CSF suggest haemorrhage or a traumatic tap, not MS.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: A mild lymphocytosis can occur but is non-specific; oligoclonal bands are the key marker.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Protein is usually normal or only mildly raised in MS; markedly raised protein suggests GBS/infection.

questions.keyPoint: CSF oligoclonal bands (not in serum) are the characteristic LP finding in MS.

rows: CSF oligoclonal IgG bands not matched in serum
Oligoclonal bands = key CSF marker of MS

questions.stem: A 40-YEAR-OLD MAN presents after 8 hours of SEVERE FRONTAL HEADACHE, PHOTOPHOBIA and TWO EPISODES OF VOMITING. GCS 13, TEMP 38.5 C, BP 95/65, PULSE 105 bpm, PHOTOPHOBIC and NECK STIFFNESS, no focal deficit. He has been drinking 8-10 cans of lager per night since job loss 7 months ago, recently coughing, feeling unwell, lives at home, not lost weight. Q: What are you most concerned that this might be?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Listeria is a concern at extremes of age, in pregnancy and immunocompromise; in a non-elderly adult the most common community pathogen is pneumococcus.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Meningococcal disease classically has a non-blanching purpuric rash; possible, but the most common cause of community bacterial meningitis in this adult is pneumococcus.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Streptococcus pneumoniae is the MOST COMMON cause of community-acquired bacterial meningitis in adults; alcohol excess (immunocompromise) and a preceding respiratory illness further raise the risk. The acute picture with fever, photophobia, neck stiffness and reduced GCS fits bacterial meningitis.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: TB meningitis is more subacute/chronic with a slower course, not an 8-hour acute presentation.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Viral meningitis is usually milder with preserved consciousness; reduced GCS, hypotension and tachycardia point to bacterial disease.

questions.keyPoint: Pneumococcus is the commonest cause of community-acquired bacterial meningitis in adults; alcoholism increases risk.

rows: Acute fever, headache, photophobia, neck stiffness, reduced GCS
LP (after CT if indicated): high neutrophils, low glucose, high protein
Blood cultures, LP, CT if focal signs
Empirical ceftriaxone + dexamethasone (+ amoxicillin if Listeria risk)

questions.stem: A 24-YEAR-OLD MAN with two episodes of INVOLUNTARY CLONIC BEATING OF THE RIGHT ARM followed by LOSS OF CONSCIOUSNESS with URINARY AND FAECAL INCONTINENCE, headache and confusion for hours. Diagnosis of FOCAL MOTOR SEIZURES with SECONDARY GENERALISATION. Started on VALPROATE. Q: Which of the following would you NOT tell him (as it is untrue)?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Computed tomography (CT)/magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) is indicated to look for a cause
choices.explanation: This is TRUE — focal-onset seizures warrant brain imaging to look for a structural cause.

choices.label: B
choices.text: No driving for 1 year after the last seizure
choices.explanation: This is TRUE — driving regulations require a seizure-free period (commonly 1 year) after the last seizure.

choices.label: C
choices.text: On valproate, blood tests will need to be monitored for liver enzymes and full blood count
choices.explanation: This is TRUE — valproate can cause hepatotoxicity and thrombocytopenia, so LFTs and FBC are monitored.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: This is the UNTRUE statement. Gum (gingival) hypertrophy is a side effect of PHENYTOIN, not sodium valproate. Valproate causes weight gain, tremor, hair loss, hepatotoxicity and is teratogenic.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: This is TRUE — alopecia/hair loss is a recognised valproate side effect.

questions.keyPoint: Gum hypertrophy = phenytoin; valproate causes weight gain, tremor, alopecia, hepatotoxicity, teratogenicity.

rows: Focal clonic arm jerking → generalised seizure with incontinence
Sodium valproate (avoid in women of childbearing potential)
Gum hypertrophy is phenytoin, not valproate

questions.stem: A 65-YEAR-OLD WOMAN with TIREDNESS, SHORTNESS OF BREATH and LOSS OF SENSATION IN HER FEET. She is VISIBLY PALE, has MILD JAUNDICE and GLOSSITIS. There is LOSS OF JOINT POSITION SENSE AND VIBRATION DISTALLY, with EXTENSOR PLANTAR REFLEXES, ABSENT ANKLE JERKS and BRISK KNEE JERKS bilaterally. Q: What is the most likely cause for her symptoms?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Sickle cell disease presents earlier with painful crises, not this combined cord/peripheral picture with megaloblastic features.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: G6PD deficiency causes episodic haemolysis with oxidative triggers, not subacute combined degeneration.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Heart failure does not cause dorsal column/corticospinal signs, glossitis or jaundice.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Uraemia can cause neuropathy but not this classic mixed UMN/dorsal column picture with megaloblastic anaemia.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Pallor, mild jaundice and glossitis (megaloblastic anaemia) with LOSS OF VIBRATION/PROPRIOCEPTION, EXTENSOR PLANTARS plus ABSENT ANKLE JERKS and BRISK KNEE JERKS = SUBACUTE COMBINED DEGENERATION of the cord from VITAMIN B12 DEFICIENCY (mixed dorsal column + corticospinal + peripheral neuropathy).

questions.keyPoint: Megaloblastic anaemia + dorsal column loss + extensor plantars with absent ankle jerks = B12 deficiency (SACD).

rows: Megaloblastic anaemia, glossitis, dorsal column loss, mixed UMN/LMN signs
Low B12, macrocytosis, intrinsic factor antibodies
FBC/film, B12, intrinsic factor antibodies HIGH-YIELD SUMMARY
Absent ankle jerks + brisk knee jerks + extensor plantars = SACD

questions.stem: Eligibility for IV recombinant tissue plasminogen activator (tPA) in ISCHAEMIC STROKE. Q: Which of the following patients is eligible for thrombolysis with intravenous recombinant tissue plasminogen activator (tPA)?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 2 hours post onset, BP 150/80 mmHg, GCS 11, MRI shows infarct
choices.explanation: A markedly reduced GCS (11) is a relative contraindication; severe stroke/depressed consciousness argues against thrombolysis.

choices.label: B
choices.text: 2 hours post onset, BP 160/90 mmHg, GCS 15, MRI shows haemorrhage
choices.explanation: Haemorrhage is an ABSOLUTE contraindication to thrombolysis.

choices.label: C
choices.text: 2 hours post onset, BP 160/95 mmHg, GCS 15, MRI shows infarct
choices.explanation: This patient is within the time window, has a confirmed ISCHAEMIC infarct (not haemorrhage), preserved GCS 15, and a BP below the 185/110 threshold — so they are ELIGIBLE for IV tPA.

choices.label: D
choices.text: 2 hours post onset, BP 195/115 mmHg, GCS 15, MRI shows infarct
choices.explanation: BP >185/110 mmHg is a contraindication until lowered; 195/115 currently excludes thrombolysis.

choices.label: E
choices.text: 7 hours post onset, BP 135/80 mmHg, GCS 15, MRI shows infarct
choices.explanation: 7 hours is beyond the standard thrombolysis time window.

questions.keyPoint: tPA needs: ischaemic infarct, within time window, BP <185/110, no haemorrhage.

rows: Confirmed ischaemia, within window, controlled BP
IV alteplase if eligible; control BP <185/110

questions.stem: Statements about SUBARACHNOID HAEMORRHAGE (SAH). Q: Which of the following statements about subarachnoid haemorrhage is NOT true?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: This is TRUE — SAH can produce focal deficits from associated parenchymal involvement or vasospasm.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Lumbar puncture (LP) immediately on admission is likely to show xanthochromia
choices.explanation: This is the FALSE statement. XANTHOCHROMIA (yellow CSF from bilirubin) takes about 12 hours to develop after the bleed, so an IMMEDIATE LP would NOT reliably show it — LP should be delayed at least 12 hours after symptom onset.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Subarachnoid haemorrhage may present as a new onset of seizures
choices.explanation: This is TRUE — SAH can present with seizures.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Subarachnoid haemorrhages may be provoked by exertion or coitus
choices.explanation: This is TRUE — aneurysmal SAH is classically precipitated by exertion or sexual activity.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Unenhanced CT within 48 hours is 95% sensitive
choices.explanation: This is TRUE — early non-contrast CT is highly sensitive (~95%+) in the first hours-to-days for SAH.

questions.keyPoint: Xanthochromia takes ~12 hours to form; LP for SAH must be delayed at least 12 hours.

rows: Thunderclap headache, exertional onset, seizures, focal signs
Non-contrast CT; delayed LP (xanthochromia) if CT negative
CT head, LP after 12h, CT angiography
Xanthochromia needs ~12h; don't do immediate LP

questions.stem: The FOUR CARDINAL SIGNS of PARKINSON'S DISEASE. Q: Which of the following best describes the four cardinal signs of Parkinson's disease?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Bradykinesia, mask-like facies, rigidity, and tremor ~10-12 Hz
choices.explanation: The resting tremor is ~3-5 Hz, not 10-12 Hz, and mask-like facies is a feature of bradykinesia rather than a separate cardinal sign.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Bradykinesia, micrographia, rigidity, and tremor ~3-4 Hz
choices.explanation: Micrographia is a manifestation, but the fourth cardinal sign is POSTURAL INSTABILITY, not micrographia.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Bradykinesia, postural hypotension, rigidity, and tremor ~3-4 Hz
choices.explanation: Postural hypotension is autonomic (often drug-related/MSA), not one of the four cardinal motor signs (postural instability is).

choices.label: D
choices.text: Bradykinesia, postural instability, rigidity, and tremor ~3-4 Hz
choices.explanation: The four cardinal motor signs of Parkinson's disease are BRADYKINESIA, RIGIDITY, RESTING TREMOR (~3-5 Hz, classically 4-6 Hz) and POSTURAL INSTABILITY (TRAP).

choices.label: E
choices.text: Bradykinesia, postural instability, rigidity, and tremor ~10-12 Hz
choices.explanation: The frequency is wrong — parkinsonian resting tremor is slow (~3-5 Hz), not 10-12 Hz (which would be physiological/enhanced).

questions.keyPoint: Parkinson's TRAP: Tremor (3-5 Hz resting), Rigidity, Akinesia/bradykinesia, Postural instability.

rows: Bradykinesia, rigidity, resting tremor 3-5 Hz, postural instability
Four cardinal signs (TRAP); tremor is slow (~3-5 Hz)

questions.stem: A 78-YEAR-OLD WOMAN, recently FEELING UNDER THE WEATHER and LOSING SOME WEIGHT, develops a SEVERE LEFT-SIDED HEADACHE with JAW PAIN ON EATING and BLURRED VISION ON THE LEFT SIDE. Q: Which investigation is needed to give a definitive diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: CT head does not diagnose giant cell arteritis.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: ESR supports the diagnosis (usually markedly raised) but is not definitive.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) of the head
choices.explanation: MRI does not confirm GCA.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Jaw claudication, temporal headache, systemic symptoms and visual disturbance in an elderly woman = GIANT CELL ARTERITIS. TEMPORAL ARTERY BIOPSY (granulomatous arteritis with giant cells) is the definitive diagnostic test (start steroids without delay).

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Temporal artery ultrasound (halo sign) is supportive but biopsy is the definitive/gold-standard test here.

questions.keyPoint: Temporal artery biopsy is the definitive test for giant cell arteritis; treat with steroids immediately.

rows: Elderly, temporal headache, jaw claudication, visual symptoms, raised ESR

questions.stem: A 53-YEAR-OLD MAN with LOWER BACK PAIN and SCIATICA into the RIGHT LEG for the past year, presenting with a 2-DAY HISTORY OF LEG WEAKNESS AND SEVERE PAIN. Q: Which sign does NOT make you think of cauda equina syndrome?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: This DOES fit cauda equina — urinary retention with overflow/distended bladder is a key feature.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: This DOES fit — reduced anal sphincter tone reflects sacral root (LMN) involvement.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: This DOES fit — cauda equina is a LMN lesion, so ankle reflexes are reduced/absent.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: This DOES fit — saddle (perianal) anaesthesia is a hallmark of cauda equina syndrome.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: UPGOING PLANTARS (Babinski) are an UPPER motor neuron sign. Cauda equina syndrome is a LOWER motor neuron lesion (compressed lumbosacral nerve roots), so plantars are DOWNGOING/flexor; upgoing plantars would NOT fit and suggest a cord (UMN) lesion instead.

questions.keyPoint: Cauda equina is LMN: areflexia, saddle anaesthesia, retention; upgoing plantars (UMN) do NOT fit.

rows: Back pain, bilateral sciatica, saddle anaesthesia, retention, reduced anal tone
It is LMN — plantars are downgoing, not upgoing

questions.stem: A 27-YEAR-OLD WOMAN (investment banker) with DIFFICULTY WALKING and a TREMOR IN THE RIGHT HAND over the last 4 days. 5 MONTHS AGO she had PAIN AND BLURRING IN THE LEFT EYE for ~10 days (put down to sinusitis) that returned to normal. Grandfather had Parkinson's. Tremor in the right hand WORSE ON APPROACHING THE TARGET, ABSENT AT REST; DECREASED LIGHT TOUCH BILATERALLY and LOSS OF VIBRATION/PROPRIOCEPTION TO THE ANKLES. Q: What are you most concerned is the diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: ADEM is typically a monophasic post-infectious illness, not a relapsing course with a separate optic event months earlier.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: The tremor is an INTENTION tremor (worse approaching target, absent at rest) = cerebellar/MS, opposite to the RESTING tremor of Parkinson's.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: GBS causes an acute areflexic ascending weakness, not relapsing CNS signs with an intention tremor and prior optic neuritis.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: A young woman with a prior episode of optic neuritis (painful left eye blurring) and now cerebellar (intention tremor) and dorsal column signs = two CNS events DISSEMINATED IN TIME AND SPACE = MULTIPLE SCLEROSIS.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: The objective intention tremor, sensory loss and prior optic neuritis are organic, not functional.

questions.keyPoint: Prior optic neuritis + new cerebellar/sensory signs in a young woman = MS (dissemination in time and space).
