rows: Optic neuritis history, intention tremor, dorsal column signs
McDonald criteria, MRI, CSF oligoclonal bands
Two CNS events in time and space = MS

questions.stem: A 33-YEAR-OLD WOMAN with MULTIPLE SCLEROSIS has PAINFUL SPASMS and DISABLING SPASTICITY IN HER LEFT LEG. Q: Which of the following medications is most likely to help?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: BACLOFEN, a GABA-B agonist, is a first-line antispasticity agent that reduces painful spasms and spasticity in MS (alternatives: tizanidine, gabapentin, botulinum toxin).

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Lactulose is a laxative; it does not treat spasticity.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Modafinil is used for MS-related fatigue, not spasticity.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Oxybutynin treats neurogenic bladder/urinary urgency, not muscle spasticity.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Propranolol treats tremor/migraine, not spasticity.

questions.keyPoint: Baclofen is first-line for spasticity in MS.

questions.stem: A 65-YEAR-OLD WOMAN with LEFT-SIDED WEAKNESS of sudden onset. Type II diabetic, smokes 20/day, high BP. Power in the LEFT ARM is 0 throughout, LEFT LEG power 3 at the hip and knee and 4 at the foot. Reflexes reduced throughout, sensation absent in the arm and reduced in the leg. Unable to see on her LEFT-HAND SIDE; LOWER HALF OF LEFT FACE WEAK; DYSPHASIA. Q: Which vascular territory is affected?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: ACA affects the contralateral LEG more than the arm; this patient has arm>leg weakness on the LEFT, so a left ACA would not explain left-sided arm-predominant weakness.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: ACA causes leg-predominant weakness, but the arm is more affected here, pointing to MCA rather than ACA.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: A 'right carotid artery' label is non-specific; the cortical pattern (face/arm>leg, hemianopia, dysphasia) localises to the right MCA territory specifically.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: LEFT face and arm>leg weakness, LEFT homonymous hemianopia and dysphasia indicate a large RIGHT MCA territory infarct (contralateral face/arm-predominant deficit with cortical signs).

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: PCA mainly causes an isolated contralateral homonymous hemianopia, not the dense face/arm weakness seen here.

questions.keyPoint: Contralateral face/arm>leg weakness + hemianopia + dysphasia = MCA territory (here right MCA).

rows: Left face/arm>leg weakness, left hemianopia, dysphasia
Imaging, ECG, carotid Doppler, glucose HIGH-YIELD SUMMARY

questions.stem: A 35-YEAR-OLD HOMELESS MAN is UNCONSCIOUS AND FITTING; he has been FITTING NOW FOR 35 MINUTES. He SMELLS OF ALCOHOL and is DISHEVELLED, has an adequate airway, is breathing 10 L/min O2, pulse and BP normal, and CAPILLARY GLUCOSE IS NORMAL. Q: What should be your immediate management be?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: 2 mg is an inadequate dose; benzodiazepines should be given at an effective seizure-terminating dose.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Diazepam 2 mg intravenously and Pabrinex intravenously
choices.explanation: Pabrinex (thiamine) is appropriate in an alcoholic, but 2 mg diazepam is sub-therapeutic.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Lorazepam 4 mg intravenously and Pabrinex intravenously
choices.explanation: In status epilepticus, IV LORAZEPAM (e.g., 4 mg) is the first-line benzodiazepine, and in an alcoholic/malnourished patient PABRINEX (thiamine) is co-administered to prevent Wernicke's encephalopathy. Glucose is normal, so dextrose alone is not needed.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: 8 mg exceeds the standard initial lorazepam dose and omits thiamine in an alcoholic patient.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Lorazepam 8 mg intravenously and Pabrinex intravenously
choices.explanation: 8 mg is an excessive single dose; the standard first dose is around 4 mg with thiamine.

questions.keyPoint: Status epilepticus: IV lorazepam first-line; give Pabrinex (thiamine) in alcoholics.

rows: IV lorazepam + thiamine (Pabrinex); phenytoin if persists
Lorazepam + thiamine in the alcoholic with status

questions.stem: Diseases most commonly responsible for MISDIAGNOSIS of PARKINSON'S DISEASE. Q: Which of the following diseases is the most common reason for misdiagnosis of Parkinson's disease?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Corticobasal degeneration is a rarer Parkinson-plus syndrome; it is not the commonest reason for misdiagnosis.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: ESSENTIAL (familial) TREMOR is the most common condition misdiagnosed as Parkinson's. It is a POSTURAL/ACTION tremor (improves with alcohol, often bilateral, family history), in contrast to the asymmetric RESTING tremor of Parkinson's.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: PSP (vertical gaze palsy, early falls) is a Parkinson-plus syndrome but less common as a misdiagnosis source than essential tremor.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Chronic traumatic (boxer's) encephalopathy is uncommon and not the leading cause of misdiagnosis.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Wilson's is rare and affects the young with Kayser-Fleischer rings; not the commonest misdiagnosis.

questions.keyPoint: Essential (action/postural) tremor is the commonest mimic of Parkinson's disease.

rows: Bilateral postural/action tremor, family history, improves with alcohol
Clinical (contrast with resting tremor of PD)
Essential tremor is the commonest misdiagnosis of PD

questions.stem: A 55-YEAR-OLD WOMAN with MILD COGNITIVE IMPAIRMENT and DISINHIBITION without significant mood change. She also has FALLS and URINARY INCONTINENCE, no major past medical history, never smoked/drank. No cranial nerve or arm defects; the LEGS show INCREASED TONE, UPGOING PLANTARS and CLONUS BILATERALLY with some WEAKNESS OF HIP AND KNEE FLEXION. Q: Which of the following diagnoses may explain the picture?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Alzheimer's presents with progressive amnestic dementia, not the gait/incontinence/dementia triad with UMN leg signs.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: BIH/IIH causes headache and papilloedema in young obese women, not this dementia/gait/incontinence triad.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: DLB features fluctuating cognition, visual hallucinations and parkinsonism, not the classic NPH triad with bilateral UMN leg signs.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: The triad of GAIT APRAXIA/falls, URINARY INCONTINENCE and DEMENTIA ('wet, wobbly, wacky') with bilateral leg UMN signs is classic for NORMAL PRESSURE HYDROCEPHALUS — a potentially reversible cause treated with CSF shunting.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Vascular dementia causes stepwise decline with focal vascular signs; the specific gait-incontinence-dementia triad points to NPH.

questions.keyPoint: Dementia + gait disturbance + urinary incontinence ('wet, wobbly, wacky') = normal pressure hydrocephalus.

questions.stem: A 32-YEAR-OLD WOMAN with TINGLING and NUMBNESS AROUND HER MOUTH for 1 week. Occasionally develops PAINFUL CARPAL SPASM. Past medical history of GRAVES DISEASE for which she recently had SUBTOTAL THYROIDECTOMY. Q: What is the most likely diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Hypercalcaemia causes 'stones, bones, groans, moans' (constipation, polyuria), not perioral tingling and carpal spasm.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Hypermagnesaemia causes reduced reflexes and weakness, not tetany.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Perioral paraesthesia and CARPAL SPASM (carpopedal/tetany, Trousseau/Chvostek signs) after thyroid surgery indicate HYPOCALCAEMIA from inadvertent removal/damage of the PARATHYROID glands during thyroidectomy.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Hypophosphataemia causes muscle weakness/rhabdomyolysis, not classic tetany after thyroidectomy.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Hypothyroidism causes fatigue and slowed reflexes, not acute tetany; the surgical clue points to parathyroid-related hypocalcaemia.

questions.keyPoint: Tetany/perioral tingling/carpal spasm after thyroidectomy = hypocalcaemia (parathyroid injury).

rows: Perioral tingling, carpal spasm, Chvostek/Trousseau signs

questions.stem: A 75-YEAR-OLD WOMAN with WEIGHT LOSS, ANOREXIA and FATIGUE. Lost 2 stone in 6 months, investigated for presumed DYSPHAGIA (nothing found). REDUCED WALKING ABILITY AND SHOULDER PAIN; struggles to STAND UP OUT OF THE CHAIR; brushing the back of her hair is difficult; TENDER SHOULDERS TO PALPATION. ESR 60 mm/hour, CRP 30 mmol/L. Q: What is the most likely diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Fibromyalgia causes widespread pain with tender points but normal inflammatory markers; ESR/CRP are raised here.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: IBM causes distal/asymmetric weakness in older men with normal/mildly raised CK, not this proximal stiffness with high ESR.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: MG causes fatigable weakness without raised inflammatory markers or true pain/tenderness.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Elderly patient with PROXIMAL SHOULDER/HIP GIRDLE PAIN AND STIFFNESS, difficulty rising from a chair and raising the arms, systemic symptoms and a RAISED ESR/CRP is classic for POLYMYALGIA RHEUMATICA (associated with giant cell arteritis; responds dramatically to low-dose steroids).

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Polymyositis causes painless proximal WEAKNESS with markedly RAISED CK; PMR causes proximal PAIN/STIFFNESS with normal CK and high ESR.

questions.keyPoint: Elderly + proximal girdle pain/stiffness + high ESR = polymyalgia rheumatica (rapid steroid response).

rows: Proximal shoulder/hip girdle pain and stiffness, raised ESR/CRP
Clinical + raised ESR; dramatic steroid response

questions.stem: CRANIAL NERVES affected by pathology in the CAVERNOUS SINUS. Q: Which of the following cranial nerves would be affected by pathology in the cavernous sinus?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: The optic nerve (II) does not pass through the cavernous sinus.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: This omits IV and VI and overstates V (only V1 and V2 pass through).

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: This omits the ophthalmic (V1) division of the trigeminal nerve, which also traverses the cavernous sinus.

choices.label: D
choices.text: III, IV, VI and ophthalmic division of V
choices.explanation: The CAVERNOUS SINUS transmits cranial nerves III (oculomotor), IV (trochlear), VI (abducens) and the OPHTHALMIC (V1) (and maxillary V2) divisions of the trigeminal, along with the internal carotid artery. Lesions cause a painful ophthalmoplegia.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: This omits IV and is imprecise about which trigeminal divisions are involved.

questions.keyPoint: Cavernous sinus contents: CN III, IV, V1 (+V2), VI and the internal carotid artery.

rows: CN III, IV, V1/V2, VI + internal carotid artery
Contains III, IV, VI and ophthalmic (V1) division of V

questions.stem: A 19-YEAR-OLD FEMALE university student with PROBLEMS IN BOTH ARMS and DIFFICULTY WALKING. Difficulty writing/typing ~6 months. SMALL BURNS AND BLISTERS ON HANDS (can't remember getting them). Fallen twice walking up the stairs. MILD OCCIPITAL HEADACHES increasing in frequency. LOSS OF PINPRICK SENSATION throughout the arms and on the back to around the T3 LEVEL. SMALL MUSCLE WASTING and LOSS OF POWER in the arms; REFLEXES HARD TO ELICIT/ABSENT. Legs mild symmetrical weakness, knee reflexes brisk, plantars equivocal. Q: What is the most likely diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Cervical spondylosis is degenerative and occurs in older adults, not a teenager, and does not give a suspended cape-like dissociated sensory loss.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: MS does not classically produce a suspended dissociated (pinprick) sensory loss with painless burns and LMN wasting in the hands.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Painless burns, muscle wasting and a dermatomal dissociated sensory level are objective organic findings.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: A young patient with a CAPE-LIKE/SUSPENDED DISSOCIATED SENSORY LOSS (loss of pain/temperature, sparing of touch), PAINLESS BURNS, LMN WASTING/AREFLEXIA in the arms (anterior horn) plus UMN signs in the legs, and occipital headaches (often Chiari-related) is classic for SYRINGOMYELIA (a central cervical cord cavity).

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Transverse myelitis is acute/subacute with a clear sensory level and no painless burns or chronic hand wasting over months.

questions.keyPoint: Cape-like dissociated sensory loss + painless burns + hand wasting in a young patient = syringomyelia.

rows: Suspended dissociated sensory loss (pain/temp), painless burns, hand wasting, UMN legs
Dissociated sensory loss + painless burns = syringomyelia

questions.stem: A 74-YEAR-OLD WOMAN with ACUTE RIGHT-SIDED HEMIPARESIS and a LEFT MCA INFARCT on DWI MRI (FIRST STROKE). Past medical history unremarkable, BP normal, ECG SINUS RHYTHM with occasional ventricular ectopics, normal cholesterol/glucose, CAROTID DOPPLER 85% STENOSIS OF THE LEFT CAROTID. Q: Which of the following treatments will NOT benefit her?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: BP lowering with an ACE inhibitor benefits secondary stroke prevention.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Antiplatelet therapy benefits secondary prevention of non-cardioembolic ischaemic stroke.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: A symptomatic 85% (>70%) carotid stenosis benefits from carotid endarterectomy.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Statins benefit secondary prevention regardless of baseline cholesterol.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: This is a NON-cardioembolic (large-artery, carotid-related) stroke in SINUS RHYTHM. WARFARIN/anticoagulation provides NO benefit (and added bleeding risk) in the absence of atrial fibrillation; antiplatelets are preferred.

questions.keyPoint: Anticoagulation does not benefit non-cardioembolic stroke in sinus rhythm; use antiplatelets + carotid surgery.

rows: Antiplatelet, statin, ACE inhibitor, carotid endarterectomy
Warfarin only helps cardioembolic stroke (e.g., AF)

questions.stem: A 28-YEAR-OLD AFRO-CARIBBEAN PREGNANT WOMAN with a 2-MONTH HISTORY OF TINGLING IN HER FEET AND DIFFICULTY WALKING progressing to barely able to walk. Came to Britain 3 years ago from West Africa, 2-year-old son, lives in a flat, no significant PMH other than diarrhoeal illnesses in Africa (HIV NEGATIVE). THIN, CONJUNCTIVA PALE. No cranial nerve abnormalities/no abnormalities on the arms. Legs: INCREASED TONE, MARKED WEAKNESS GRADE 3-4 worse in ankles, KNEE JERKS BRISK, ANKLE JERKS HARD TO ELICIT/ABSENT, PLANTARS UPGOING; LOSS OF LIGHT TOUCH to just above the ankles, ABSENT PROPRIOCEPTION AND VIBRATION in the big toe joints. Q: Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: There is no diabetes here, and a pure peripheral neuropathy would not give brisk knee jerks/upgoing plantars (UMN signs).

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: GBS is acute, areflexic and predominantly motor; the dorsal column loss with mixed UMN/LMN signs over 2 months fits a cord process, not GBS.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: She is HIV negative, and the dorsal column + corticospinal pattern points to combined cord degeneration.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Subacute combined degeneration of the cord
choices.explanation: Pallor (megaloblastic anaemia) with combined DORSAL COLUMN loss (vibration/proprioception), CORTICOSPINAL signs (brisk knee jerks, upgoing plantars) AND a peripheral neuropathy (absent ankle jerks) is SUBACUTE COMBINED DEGENERATION from VITAMIN B12 deficiency (common in poor diet/pregnancy).

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Tabes (tertiary syphilis) gives dorsal column loss and areflexia but not the corticospinal/UMN leg signs of SACD, and the megaloblastic features point to B12 deficiency.

questions.keyPoint: Pallor + dorsal column loss + brisk knees/upgoing plantars + absent ankle jerks = SACD (B12 deficiency).

rows: Megaloblastic anaemia, dorsal column loss, mixed UMN/LMN signs
Brisk knees + absent ankles + upgoing plantars = SACD

questions.stem: A 55-YEAR-OLD MAN with a 4-MONTH HISTORY OF HEADACHES that USUALLY OCCUR IN THE MORNINGS and are BECOMING MORE FREQUENT AND SEVERE. Q: Which of the following would suggest increased intracranial pressure?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Jaw claudication suggests giant cell arteritis, not raised ICP.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Kernig's sign indicates meningeal irritation (meningitis/SAH), not raised ICP per se.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Low opening pressure on lumbar puncture
choices.explanation: Raised ICP gives a HIGH opening pressure; low pressure suggests the opposite (e.g., CSF leak).

choices.label: D
choices.text: Relief of symptoms when lying down
choices.explanation: Raised-ICP headache is WORSE on lying down/in the morning and on coughing/straining; relief on lying suggests low-pressure (CSF leak) headache.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: TRANSIENT BILATERAL VISUAL LOSS (visual obscurations) from papilloedema, along with morning headache worse on coughing/straining, is a hallmark of RAISED INTRACRANIAL PRESSURE.

questions.keyPoint: Transient visual obscurations + morning headache worse on straining = raised ICP (papilloedema).

rows: Morning headache, worse on straining, transient visual obscurations, papilloedema
CT/MRI brain; LP only after imaging

questions.stem: A 44-YEAR-OLD WOMAN (office worker) with a 2-MONTH HISTORY OF PAIN IN THE RIGHT HAND AND THUMB WEAKNESS. Pains SHOOT INTO THE FIRST THREE FINGERS, progressed despite ergonomic keyboards. Weak thumb; FIRST THREE FINGERS FEEL NUMB. WASTING OF THE THENAR EMINENCE, WEAKNESS OF THUMB ABDUCTION, and REDUCED SENSATION in the THUMB, INDEX, MIDDLE FINGER AND LATERAL HALF OF THE FOURTH FINGER. Q: Which nerve or nerve root is affected?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: C6 radiculopathy affects the thumb/index and biceps reflex but does not cause isolated thenar wasting with a median sensory distribution sparing the radial dorsum.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: C8 supplies the small muscles of the hand more broadly and the little finger; the deficit here is specifically median.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Thenar wasting, weak thumb ABDUCTION (abductor pollicis brevis) and sensory loss over the thumb, index, middle and lateral half of the ring finger = MEDIAN NERVE compression at the wrist (CARPAL TUNNEL SYNDROME).

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Radial nerve lesions cause wrist drop and dorsal sensory loss, not thenar wasting and palmar median sensory loss.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Ulnar nerve lesions affect the little/medial ring finger and hypothenar/interossei, not the thenar eminence and lateral 3.5 digits.

questions.keyPoint: Thenar wasting + lateral 3.5-digit sensory loss + weak thumb abduction = median nerve (carpal tunnel).

rows: Lateral 3.5-digit numbness, thenar wasting, weak thumb abduction, nocturnal symptoms
Splinting, steroid injection, carpal tunnel release
Median nerve = lateral 3.5 digits + thenar muscles

questions.stem: INVESTIGATION FINDINGS in MYASTHENIA GRAVIS. Q: Which of the following investigation findings would you NOT expect in myasthenia gravis?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: This IS expected — repetitive nerve stimulation shows a DECREMENTAL response in MG (post-synaptic receptor loss).

choices.label: B
choices.text: Hyperplasia of the thymus gland or thymoma on computed tomography (CT) of the chest
choices.explanation: This IS expected — thymic hyperplasia or thymoma is commonly associated with MG.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: This IS expected — anti-AChR antibodies are positive in most generalised MG.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: RIMMED VACUOLES on muscle biopsy are characteristic of INCLUSION BODY MYOSITIS, NOT myasthenia gravis. MG is a disorder of the neuromuscular JUNCTION, not the muscle fibre itself, so this finding would NOT be expected.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: This IS expected — edrophonium (Tensilon) transiently improves weakness in MG.

questions.keyPoint: Rimmed vacuoles = inclusion body myositis, not myasthenia gravis.

rows: Decremental RNS, anti-AChR antibodies, thymic abnormality, positive Tensilon
Rimmed vacuoles belong to inclusion body myositis

questions.stem: SYMPTOMS of COMPLEX PARTIAL SEIZURES. Q: Which of the following symptoms would NOT be caused by complex partial seizures?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: This CAN occur — deja-vu is a classic temporal lobe (psychic) aura of complex partial seizures.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Fumbling or rubbing, slightly odd limb movements
choices.explanation: This CAN occur — automatisms (fumbling, lip-smacking) are typical of complex partial seizures.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Shooting/electric shock-like pains in the limbs
choices.explanation: SHOOTING/ELECTRIC-SHOCK pains in the limbs are NEUROPATHIC/radicular phenomena, NOT a feature of complex partial seizures. Seizure symptoms are positive phenomena (auras, automatisms, altered awareness), not lancinating limb pains.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: This CAN occur — olfactory/gustatory hallucinations are temporal lobe (uncinate) auras.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: This CAN occur — impaired awareness defines a complex partial (focal impaired-awareness) seizure.

questions.keyPoint: Lancinating limb pains are not a feature of complex partial seizures (those are neuropathic).

rows: Shooting limb pains are neuropathic, not seizure

questions.stem: A 70-YEAR-OLD WOMAN with a BACKGROUND OF BOWEL CANCER (resected 2 years ago), presenting with a 3-WEEK HISTORY OF BACK PAIN, PROGRESSIVE CLUMSINESS AND HEAVINESS OF THE LEGS WITH PARAESTHESIA. Arms normal, but WEAKNESS THROUGHOUT THE LEGS, BRISK REFLEXES, UPGOING PLANTARS and DIMINISHED SENSATION BILATERALLY UP TO THE BELLY BUTTON (umbilicus). Q: Where is the lesion likely to be?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: A C5 cord lesion would involve the arms; here the arms are normal and the sensory level is at the umbilicus.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: An L2 lesion gives a lower (groin) level and tends to cause LMN/cauda equina signs, not a T10 sensory level with UMN signs.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: T8 corresponds to a level above the umbilicus (lower costal margin); the level here is precisely the umbilicus = T10.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: The UMBILICUS is the T10 DERMATOME. A sensory level at the umbilicus with UMN leg signs (brisk reflexes, upgoing plantars) localises the cord-compressing (metastatic) lesion to T10.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: T12 corresponds to a level just above the inguinal ligament, below the umbilicus; the umbilical level is T10.

questions.keyPoint: Sensory level at the umbilicus = T10 (nipple = T4, xiphisternum = T7).

rows: Back pain, progressive leg weakness, UMN signs, T10 sensory level

questions.stem: A 35-YEAR-OLD WOMAN with WEAKNESS AND PARAESTHESIA for 2 weeks, starting with UNUSUAL SENSATIONS IN FEET AND HANDS, then FEET DRAGGING when she walked, then unable to get out of her chair and arm weakness. DIARRHOEA AND ABDOMINAL CRAMPS 4 weeks ago. WEAKNESS THROUGHOUT LEGS AND ARMS IN A PATCHY DISTRIBUTION (grades 2-4), DIMINISHED REFLEXES, reduced sensation in the feet up to mid-shin. Q: Which investigation finding is suggestive of the likely diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Multifocal decreased motor conduction block only
choices.explanation: GBS shows reduced conduction velocity/demyelination, not just isolated block; this option is incomplete.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Multifocal decreased motor conduction speed with/without conduction block
choices.explanation: The picture (post-Campylobacter diarrhoea, ascending areflexic weakness) is GUILLAIN-BARRE SYNDROME. Nerve conduction studies show MULTIFOCAL DEMYELINATION — reduced motor conduction velocity with or without conduction block — the classic finding in AIDP.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Oligoclonal bands suggest MS (CNS), not GBS, which shows albuminocytologic dissociation.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Positive antiviral or antibacterial antibodies to campylobacter
choices.explanation: Campylobacter is a common trigger, but serology supports the trigger rather than directly confirming the neuropathy; NCS is the suggestive diagnostic finding.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: GBS CSF shows raised protein with NORMAL cell count (albuminocytologic dissociation); raised lymphocytes suggest infection.

questions.keyPoint: GBS NCS = multifocal demyelination (reduced motor conduction velocity +/- conduction block).

rows: Post-infectious ascending areflexic weakness, sensory symptoms

questions.stem: A LUMBAR PUNCTURE in a patient with HEADACHE: CELLS 460/mm3 (normal <4/mm3), PREDOMINANT CELL TYPE LYMPHOCYTES, GLUCOSE 1.3 mmol/L (LOW; normal 2-9), PROTEIN 1200 mg/L (HIGH; normal 200-400). INDIA INK STAINING AFTER CULTURE IS NEGATIVE. Q: Which one of the following are these findings consistent with?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Pyogenic bacterial meningitis shows a NEUTROPHIL predominance, not lymphocytes.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Cryptococcus would be suggested by a POSITIVE India ink stain; here India ink is NEGATIVE.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Malignant (carcinomatous) meningitis can lower glucose but is identified by malignant cells on cytology; the lymphocytic, low-glucose, high-protein, India-ink-negative picture is classic for TB.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: A LYMPHOCYTIC pleocytosis with LOW GLUCOSE and HIGH PROTEIN, and a NEGATIVE India ink (excluding cryptococcus), is the classic CSF picture of TUBERCULOUS MENINGITIS.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Viral meningitis is lymphocytic but with NORMAL glucose and only mildly raised protein; the markedly low glucose here points to TB.

questions.keyPoint: Lymphocytes + LOW glucose + HIGH protein + India ink negative = tuberculous meningitis.

rows: Subacute headache, lymphocytic CSF, low glucose, high protein
LP, CSF TB PCR, CT/MRI (basal meningeal enhancement)
Lymphocytic + low glucose + high protein = TB meningitis

questions.stem: A 55-YEAR-OLD MAN with 6 MONTHS OF PROGRESSIVE WEAKNESS IN THE ARMS AND LEGS. He has CHOKED a few times recently on SWALLOWING LIQUIDS. He is a SMOKER, no significant PMH, British, not travelled widely. Cranial nerves: PALATE DOES NOT RISE much on saying 'aah' on either side (bulbar). WASTING OF THE DORSAL INTEROSSEOUS MUSCLES AND FASCICULATIONS in the right forearm and left deltoid; diffuse weakness (grade 4) with reflexes present/brisk; NO SENSORY DEFICIT. Legs: FASCICULATIONS in the thighs, proximal weakness on the left, BRISK KNEE JERKS and UPGOING PLANTAR REFLEXES; no sensory deficit. Q: Which of the following diagnoses is most likely?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Cervical spondylosis does not cause bulbar signs or widespread fasciculations, and usually has sensory involvement.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Possible but unlikely here (low risk, British, no risk factors); idiopathic MND is far more likely with this classic picture.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: COMBINED UMN signs (brisk reflexes, upgoing plantars) AND LMN signs (wasting, FASCICULATIONS) in multiple regions, PLUS BULBAR involvement, with NO SENSORY DEFICIT, is the hallmark of MOTOR NEURONE DISEASE (amyotrophic lateral sclerosis).

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: MS gives UMN and sensory signs but does NOT cause LMN wasting/fasciculations; pure motor mixed UMN/LMN signs point to MND.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Syringomyelia causes a dissociated SENSORY loss; the complete absence of sensory signs argues against it and for MND.

questions.keyPoint: Mixed UMN + LMN signs in multiple regions with bulbar involvement and NO sensory loss = motor neurone disease.

rows: Mixed UMN/LMN signs, fasciculations, bulbar signs, no sensory loss
UMN + LMN signs with NO sensory loss = MND Ischemic stroke

questions.stem: A 79-YEAR-OLD MAN admitted with LEFT HEMIPARESIS. CT reveals a MIDDLE CEREBRAL ARTERY INFARCT. Q: What is his most significant risk factor for stroke?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: HYPERTENSION is the single most significant (and modifiable) risk factor for stroke; the INTERSTROKE study showed it carries the largest population-attributable risk among the modifiable factors.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Smoking is an important risk factor but contributes less attributable stroke risk than hypertension.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Family history is non-modifiable and a weaker contributor than hypertension.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Diabetes increases stroke risk but to a lesser degree than hypertension.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Dyslipidaemia contributes to atherosclerosis but is a less significant single risk factor than hypertension.

questions.keyPoint: Hypertension is the most significant modifiable risk factor for stroke.

questions.stem: A 71-YEAR-OLD RIGHT-HANDED MAN brought in by ambulance at 17:50 after a COLLAPSE. His wife found him on the floor unable to move his right arm or leg and unable to speak; her call to the ambulance was logged at 17:30. PMH: well-controlled hypertension, ISCHAEMIC HEART DISEASE and ATRIAL FIBRILLATION for which he is on WARFARIN. HERNIA REPAIR 3 MONTHS AGO; brother had a 'bleed in the brain' at 67. Q: What is the absolute contraindication to thrombolysis in this male?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: A relative's haemorrhagic stroke is not a contraindication to thrombolysis.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Surgery 3 months ago is outside the typical recent-surgery window (usually within 2 weeks); it is not an absolute contraindication here.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Because he was FOUND on the floor with an UNKNOWN/UNWITNESSED time of onset, it is impossible to confirm he is within the thrombolysis window. UNKNOWN onset time is the ABSOLUTE contraindication here — the last-known-well time was not established.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: It is not yet known whether this is ischaemic or haemorrhagic; a haemorrhage would be a contraindication, but it has not been confirmed.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Warfarin is a relative contraindication (depends on INR) but is not the single absolute one given the uncertain stroke type and unknown onset.

questions.keyPoint: Unknown/unwitnessed onset (found down) precludes thrombolysis — time of onset is the absolute barrier.

rows: Unwitnessed onset, found down, unknown last-well time
CT to differentiate ischaemia vs haemorrhage
Thrombolysis only if onset confirmed within window

questions.stem: A 77-YEAR-OLD WOMAN admitted to hospital with a URINARY TRACT INFECTION. She receives antibiotics and is responding well. On the FOURTH DAY she is eating her lunch when she suddenly DROPS HER FORK. She calls for the nurse, who notices the LEFT SIDE OF HER FACE IS DROOPING. Q: What is the best next course of action?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Urgent imaging is needed soon, but the IMMEDIATE priority while she is eating is to protect the airway from aspiration.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Thrombolysis is considered after imaging confirms ischaemia and excludes contraindications, not as the immediate first action.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: MRI is slower and not the immediate priority during an acute event while she is eating.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Aspirin is given only after haemorrhage is excluded on imaging, not before.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: She has just had an acute stroke WHILE EATING; the immediate, simple, life-saving step is to make her NIL BY MOUTH to prevent aspiration pneumonia before formal swallow assessment, then arrange urgent imaging.

questions.keyPoint: Acute stroke while eating: make NIL BY MOUTH first to prevent aspiration, then image urgently.

rows: Sudden facial droop and dropping objects while eating
Urgent CT head after airway protection
Nil by mouth, then thrombolysis if eligible
Protect the airway (NBM) before imaging if actively eating

questions.stem: On observation, a patient has a LEFT FACIAL DROOP. On closer examination his LEFT NASOLABIAL FOLD IS FLATTENED, the LEFT CORNER OF HIS MOUTH DROOPS, and he is UNABLE TO KEEP HIS CHEEKS PUFFED OUT. EYE CLOSURE IS ONLY SLIGHTLY WEAKER compared to the right, and his FOREHEAD WRINKLES when asked to look up high (forehead spared). Q: What is the diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: FOREHEAD SPARING with lower-face weakness indicates an UPPER MOTOR NEURON facial palsy. The forehead has bilateral cortical innervation, so a contralateral (RIGHT) cortical/MCA lesion causes left LOWER facial weakness while sparing the forehead.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: A parotid tumour compresses the facial nerve trunk causing a LOWER motor neuron palsy (whole side including forehead), not forehead sparing.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: A LEFT capsular lesion would cause a RIGHT-sided facial weakness; this patient has LEFT facial weakness, indicating a right-sided lesion.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Bell's palsy is a LMN palsy that affects the whole half of the face INCLUDING the forehead; here the forehead is spared.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: A CPA lesion produces a LMN facial palsy (forehead involved) often with VIII signs, not forehead sparing.

questions.keyPoint: Forehead sparing = UMN facial palsy → contralateral cortical lesion (here right MCA).

rows: Upper motor neuron facial palsy (cortical stroke)
Forehead spared = UMN (contralateral cortex); forehead involved = LMN

questions.stem: A patient admitted with a STROKE. On examination of her VISUAL FIELDS, she is UNABLE TO SEE IN THE RIGHT LOWER QUADRANT of her field. Q: Where is the lesion?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: A chiasmal lesion causes a BITEMPORAL hemianopia, not an isolated right lower quadrantanopia.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: A RIGHT LOWER QUADRANTANOPIA results from a lesion of the contralateral (LEFT) SUPERIOR optic radiation, which passes through the PARIETAL lobe ('PITS': Parietal-Inferior field defect... contralateral lower quadrant). A left parietal lesion produces a right inferior quadrantanopia.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: A right temporal lesion damages the inferior radiation (Meyer's loop) causing a LEFT SUPERIOR quadrantanopia, not a right lower defect.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: A RIGHT radiation lesion affects the LEFT visual field, not the right.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: An optic nerve lesion causes MONOCULAR visual loss, not a homonymous quadrantanopia.

questions.keyPoint: Right lower quadrantanopia = left PARIETAL (superior radiation) lesion (parietal=lower, temporal=upper).

rows: Homonymous quadrantanopia maps to the contralateral optic radiation
Parietal radiation = inferior field; temporal (Meyer's loop) = superior field

questions.stem: A 74-YEAR-OLD MAN with a WITNESSED SUDDEN ONSET of APHASIA AND RIGHT HEMIPARESIS 1 HOUR AGO. History of hypertension; meds hydrochlorothiazide and metoprolol. On exam: BP 178/94, pulse 80/min regular, NONFLUENT APHASIA, RIGHT PRONATOR DRIFT, RIGHT LEG DRIFT, EXTENSOR PLANTAR RESPONSE ON THE RIGHT. ECG sinus rhythm. CT head within 1 hour reveals EARLY ISCHEMIC CHANGES. Q: Which of the following is the best treatment?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Aspirin is given (usually after 24 h if thrombolysed, or immediately if not) but is inferior to thrombolysis in an eligible patient presenting within 1 hour.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Acute anticoagulation with heparin is not beneficial in acute ischaemic stroke and increases bleeding risk.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: BP 178/94 is below the 185/110 threshold and does not require lowering before thrombolysis; labetalol alone does not treat the stroke.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Witnessed onset within 1 hour, ischaemic changes (no haemorrhage) on CT, and BP below 185/110 make him ELIGIBLE for IV tPA — the best treatment to maximise reperfusion within the time window.

questions.keyPoint: Witnessed onset within window + ischaemic CT + BP <185/110 → give IV tPA.

rows: Witnessed sudden deficit within 4.5 h, ischaemic CT HIGH-YIELD SUMMARY

questions.stem: A 65-YEAR-OLD MAN with RIGHT-SIDED WEAKNESS AND EXPRESSIVE APHASIA that began suddenly 2 HOURS AGO. History of osteoarthritis, gout and hypertension; NO recent head trauma or surgery. Meds lisinopril, allopurinol, acetaminophen. Alert, BP 164/90, pulse 66, DENSE RIGHT HEMIPARESIS. CBC, platelets, PT, glucose and ECG are normal. CT HEAD WITHOUT IV CONTRAST IS NORMAL. Q: What is the next best step?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Carotid Doppler is part of secondary-prevention workup but is not the immediate next step in a patient eligible for acute reperfusion.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Acute heparin is not indicated in acute ischaemic stroke and raises bleeding risk.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Discuss with patient and his family the risks and benefits of intravenous recombinant tissue-type plasminogen activator
choices.explanation: Within 2 hours of onset, with a normal (non-haemorrhagic) CT and no contraindications, the next step is to DISCUSS THE RISKS AND BENEFITS OF IV tPA with the patient/family and proceed with thrombolysis — informed consent is part of acute stroke care.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Aspirin is appropriate only if thrombolysis is not given/contraindicated; here the patient is a thrombolysis candidate.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: CT has already excluded haemorrhage; obtaining MRI would delay time-critical thrombolysis.

questions.keyPoint: Eligible acute stroke within window: discuss risks/benefits of IV tPA and treat — don't delay.

rows: Onset within window, normal CT, no contraindications
Consent and give tPA; don't delay with MRI

questions.stem: A 68-YEAR-OLD MAN with hypertension and coronary artery disease presents with RIGHT-SIDED WEAKNESS, SENSORY LOSS and EXPRESSIVE APHASIA. Symptoms began 6 HOURS PRIOR to arrival. Neuroimaging is shown. In the ED his BLOOD PRESSURE IS PERSISTENTLY 180/95. Q: Which of the following is the best next step in management of this patient's blood pressure?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Aggressive BP lowering can worsen perfusion of the ischaemic penumbra; nitroprusside is not appropriate here.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Administer IV clonidine 0.1 mg po until the blood pressure drops below 140/90
choices.explanation: Lowering BP to <140/90 acutely is excessive and risks extending the infarct.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: This is an acute ischaemic stroke OUTSIDE the thrombolysis window (6 hours) and the BP (180/95) is below the 220/120 treatment threshold for non-thrombolysed patients. Permissive hypertension maintains penumbral perfusion, so the best step is to OBSERVE (do not lower) the BP.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Mannitol is for raised intracranial pressure/oedema, not for routine BP management in acute stroke.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Labetalol is reserved for BP >220/120 (or >185/110 if thrombolysing); at 180/95 in a non-thrombolysed patient, treatment is unnecessary.

questions.keyPoint: In acute ischaemic stroke (not for tPA), permit hypertension up to 220/120 — observe, don't lower.

rows: Permissive hypertension; treat only if >220/120 (or >185/110 if tPA)
Infarct extension if BP lowered too much
Don't lower BP unless >220/120 in non-thrombolysed stroke

questions.stem: A 55-YEAR-OLD DIABETIC WOMAN suddenly develops WEAKNESS OF THE LEFT SIDE OF HER FACE as well as her RIGHT ARM AND LEG. She also has DIPLOPIA ON LEFT LATERAL GAZE. Q: Where is the responsible lesion?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: A single hemispheric lesion causes contralateral face AND limb weakness on the SAME side, not crossed (left face/right limb) findings.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: A left hemispheric lesion would cause right face and right limb weakness, not left facial weakness with right limb weakness.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: The crossed pattern (LEFT face, RIGHT body) localises to the LEFT pons (ipsilateral facial nerve/abducens nucleus + contralateral corticospinal tract), not the right brainstem.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: CROSSED HEMIPLEGIA — IPSILATERAL (left) cranial nerve signs (left facial weakness, left lateral gaze diplopia from VI/horizontal gaze involvement) with CONTRALATERAL (right) limb weakness — localises to the LEFT PONS (e.g., Millard-Gubler syndrome).

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: An MLF lesion causes internuclear ophthalmoplegia (impaired adduction), not crossed face/limb hemiplegia.

questions.keyPoint: Crossed signs (ipsilateral cranial nerve + contralateral limb weakness) localise to the brainstem (here left pons).

questions.stem: A 73-YEAR-OLD WOMAN with SUDDEN-ONSET RIGHT-SIDED WEAKNESS. 18-year history of TYPE 2 DIABETES (metformin, recent HbA1c 7.8), HYPERTENSION and osteoarthritis. Alert and oriented, BP 172/88, afebrile, SPEECH AND MENTATION NORMAL. RIGHT FACIAL DROOP WITH SPARING OF THE FOREHEAD, RIGHT ARM AND RIGHT LEG STRENGTH 3/5, right Babinski equivocal, SENSORY EXAMINATION NORMAL BILATERALLY. (Match the most likely mechanism of disease.) Q: Which is the most likely mechanism of disease?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Large-vessel stroke due to cardiogenic embolism
choices.explanation: There is no AF/cardiac source and no cortical signs (speech/sensation normal); a pure motor deficit is not cardioembolic cortical stroke.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Large-vessel stroke due to atherosclerotic middle cerebral artery (MCA) occlusion
choices.explanation: A large MCA occlusion would cause cortical signs (aphasia, neglect, sensory loss), which are absent here.

choices.label: C
choices.text: CNS vasculitis due to giant cell arteritis
choices.explanation: GCA does not typically cause a pure motor lacunar syndrome; there are no GCA features.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: A PURE MOTOR hemiparesis (face + arm + leg equally) with NORMAL speech, mentation and SENSATION, in a long-standing diabetic/hypertensive, is a classic LACUNAR (small-vessel) syndrome from occlusion of a deep penetrating artery (internal capsule).

choices.label: E
choices.text: Stroke due to carotid artery dissection
choices.explanation: Dissection typically occurs in younger patients with neck pain/Horner's; not this small-vessel pure-motor picture.

choices.label: F
choices.text: Haemorrhagic stroke due to uncontrolled hypertension
choices.explanation: An alert patient with a pure motor syndrome and no headache fits ischaemic lacunar disease; the explanation confirms a small-vessel infarct.

questions.keyPoint: Pure motor hemiparesis with no cortical signs in a diabetic/hypertensive = lacunar (small-vessel) stroke.

rows: Pure motor hemiparesis, no cortical signs, diabetes/hypertension
CT/MRI showing deep small infarct (internal capsule)
Pure motor + no cortical signs = lacunar stroke

questions.stem: A 52-YEAR-OLD HISPANIC MAN with LEFT-SIDED WEAKNESS AND DIFFICULTY WITH SPEECH. No medications; mild exertional dyspnoea for 2-3 months. Neurological symptoms came on SUDDENLY WITHOUT WARNING ('a bolt out of the blue'), no previous weakness/visual loss. BP 136/74, PULSE IRREGULAR AT 92/min, cardiac exam IRREGULAR RHYTHM and a SOFT DIASTOLIC RUMBLE AT THE APEX (mitral stenosis). MODERATE RIGHT FACIAL WEAKNESS and PRONATOR DRIFT of the right hand and arm; right leg and left side normal. Heat-cold/light-touch DIMINISHED IN THE RIGHT FACE AND ARM; SPEECH HALTING AND NONFLUENT, unable to name simple objects. (Match the most likely mechanism of disease.) Q: Which is the most likely mechanism of disease?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Large-vessel stroke due to cardiogenic embolism
choices.explanation: An IRREGULAR pulse (atrial fibrillation) with a DIASTOLIC MURMUR (mitral stenosis), abrupt 'bolt-out-of-the-blue' onset, and CORTICAL signs (nonfluent aphasia, cortical sensory loss, face/arm involvement) point to a CARDIOEMBOLIC large-vessel stroke. Mitral stenosis/AF is a classic embolic source.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Large-vessel stroke due to atherosclerotic middle cerebral artery (MCA) occlusion
choices.explanation: Atherosclerotic occlusion is plausible, but the AF + mitral stenosis make a cardiac embolic source the most likely mechanism.

choices.label: C
choices.text: CNS vasculitis due to giant cell arteritis
choices.explanation: No GCA features; the cardiac findings indicate embolism.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Lacunar strokes cause pure motor/sensory syndromes WITHOUT cortical aphasia; this patient has cortical signs.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Stroke due to carotid artery dissection
choices.explanation: No neck pain/Horner's; the irregular pulse and murmur point to a cardiac embolic source.

choices.label: F
choices.text: Haemorrhagic stroke due to uncontrolled hypertension
choices.explanation: BP is normal (136/74) and the picture is embolic ischaemia, not hypertensive haemorrhage.

questions.keyPoint: AF + mitral stenosis + abrupt cortical deficit = cardioembolic large-vessel stroke.

rows: AF/mitral stenosis, abrupt onset, cortical signs (aphasia)
AF/valve disease → cardioembolic cortical stroke; anticoagulate

questions.stem: A 60-YEAR-OLD MAN with VISUAL PROBLEMS AND DIZZINESS. The dizziness started suddenly; he SEES THE ROOM SPINNING AROUND and has been BUMPING INTO THINGS. BP 159/91, HR 72. On examination there is NYSTAGMUS AND DYSDIADOCHOKINESIA. Q: Where is his stroke?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Temporal lobe lesions affect memory/language or cause a superior quadrantanopia, not vertigo with cerebellar signs.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Parietal lesions cause sensory/visuospatial deficits, not vertigo and dysdiadochokinesia.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: As above, parietal lesions do not produce vertigo with cerebellar signs.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: The anterior (carotid) circulation supplies the cerebral hemispheres; vertigo, nystagmus and ataxia are POSTERIOR circulation features.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: VERTIGO, NYSTAGMUS and DYSDIADOCHOKINESIA (cerebellar incoordination) localise to the POSTERIOR (vertebrobasilar) circulation, which supplies the brainstem and cerebellum.

questions.keyPoint: Vertigo + nystagmus + cerebellar signs = posterior (vertebrobasilar) circulation stroke.

questions.stem: A 78-YEAR-OLD RIGHT-HANDED MALE COLLAPSES and is brought to A&E. He seems to FOLLOW CLEAR ONE-STEP COMMANDS but gets very frustrated as he CANNOT ANSWER QUESTIONS. He is UNABLE TO LIFT HIS RIGHT HAND OR LEG, has an IRREGULARLY IRREGULAR PULSE, BP 149/87. Takes only aspirin and frusemide. Q: What is the most likely diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Right-sided weakness with APHASIA (cannot answer questions = a CORTICAL language deficit) in a right-handed patient localises to the dominant LEFT CORTEX. The irregularly irregular pulse (AF) makes a cardioembolic LEFT CORTICAL INFARCT most likely.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: A right capsular infarct would cause LEFT-sided weakness without aphasia; this patient has right weakness and aphasia.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Possible, but AF on aspirin favours an embolic ISCHAEMIC cortical infarct; CT is needed to differentiate, and the most likely diagnosis is infarct.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: A pure capsular (lacunar-territory) lesion would not cause cortical aphasia; aphasia indicates cortical involvement.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Brainstem lesions cause crossed signs/coma and cranial nerve palsies, not isolated right hemiparesis with aphasia.

questions.keyPoint: Aphasia indicates CORTICAL involvement; right weakness + aphasia + AF = left cortical (embolic) infarct.

rows: Anticoagulation for AF after excluding bleed

questions.stem: A 49-YEAR-OLD MAN with SUDDEN ONSET, PAINLESS UNILATERAL VISUAL LOSS LASTING ABOUT A MINUTE, which he describes as 'A BLACK CURTAIN COMING DOWN'. BP 158/90, HR 73. There is an AUDIBLE BRUIT ON AUSCULTATION OF HIS NECK. PMH insignificant other than a deep vein thrombosis of the right leg 10 years ago. Q: The most likely diagnosis is:

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Central retinal vein occlusion causes PERSISTENT (not transient) visual loss with retinal haemorrhages, not a transient 'curtain'.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Central retinal artery occlusion causes SUDDEN PERMANENT monocular loss; amaurosis fugax is the TRANSIENT, self-resolving form.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: TRANSIENT painless monocular visual loss ('a black curtain descending') lasting about a minute with a CAROTID BRUIT is AMAUROSIS FUGAX — a transient retinal ischaemic event usually from carotid atheroembolism (a form of TIA).

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Optic neuritis causes subacute PAINFUL visual loss over hours-days in younger patients, not a transient one-minute curtain.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Acute angle-closure glaucoma causes a PAINFUL red eye with halos and a fixed mid-dilated pupil, not painless transient loss.

questions.keyPoint: Transient painless monocular 'curtain' visual loss + carotid bruit = amaurosis fugax (carotid emboli).

rows: Transient painless monocular curtain visual loss, carotid bruit
Antiplatelet, statin, carotid endarterectomy if stenosed
Curtain visual loss = amaurosis fugax (think carotid)

questions.stem: A patient is UNABLE TO MOVE HIS RIGHT ARM OR LEG. When asked to smile, the LEFT SIDE OF HIS MOUTH DROOPS. Q: Where is the lesion?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: A left cortical lesion would cause RIGHT face and RIGHT limb weakness on the same side, not crossed (left face / right limb) findings.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: A right cortical lesion would cause LEFT face and LEFT limb weakness; this patient has right limb weakness, so a single cortical lesion cannot explain crossed signs.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: CROSSED SIGNS — IPSILATERAL (left) facial weakness with CONTRALATERAL (right) limb weakness — localise to the LEFT BRAINSTEM (ipsilateral facial nucleus/nerve plus the corticospinal tract before its decussation, affecting the opposite limbs).

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: A right brainstem lesion would give right facial weakness with left limb weakness; here the face is LEFT and limbs are RIGHT, indicating the left brainstem.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: A cervical cord lesion does not produce a facial palsy (the face is supplied above the cord).

questions.keyPoint: Ipsilateral face + contralateral limb weakness = brainstem lesion (here left).

rows: Ipsilateral facial weakness, contralateral limb weakness
Crossed face/limb signs = brainstem Hemorrhagic stroke

questions.stem: A 19-YEAR-OLD MAN, GCS 12, was DOING PUSH-UPS when he complained of SUDDEN-ONSET SEVERE HEADACHE AND COLLAPSED. Q: What would you expect on his CT?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: A biconvex (lens-shaped) haematoma is an EXTRADURAL haemorrhage (middle meningeal artery), typically after trauma, not exertional thunderclap headache.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Midline shift is a non-specific mass-effect sign and not the characteristic primary appearance of acute SAH.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: A crescent-shaped (concave) collection crossing suture lines is a SUBDURAL haematoma, often in the elderly/alcoholics, not exertional SAH.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Blood along the sulci and fissures
choices.explanation: Exertional thunderclap headache with collapse is SUBARACHNOID HAEMORRHAGE. On CT, blood tracks within the SUBARACHNOID SPACE — along the SULCI, FISSURES and basal cisterns (e.g., star-shaped blood in the suprasellar cistern).

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Intraventricular blood can accompany SAH but is not the primary/characteristic distribution; subarachnoid blood along sulci/fissures is the hallmark.

questions.keyPoint: SAH on CT = blood in the subarachnoid space (sulci, fissures, basal cisterns).

rows: Exertional thunderclap headache, collapse, reduced GCS
Non-contrast CT (subarachnoid blood); LP if CT negative

questions.stem: A 52-YEAR-OLD MAN with a 2-HOUR HISTORY OF SEVERE, SUDDEN-ONSET GENERALIZED HEADACHE, nausea and one episode of vomiting; symptoms began while watching television. SIX DAYS AGO he had a SEVERE HEADACHE THAT RESOLVED WITHOUT TREATMENT (sentinel headache). Hypertension and hyperlipidaemia; smoked 2 packs/day for 30 years; meds lisinopril/HCTZ/simvastatin. Temp 38.1, pulse 82, resp 16, BP 162/98. Pupils equal/round/reactive, FUNDOSCOPY NO PAPILLOEDEMA, cranial nerves II-XII intact, no focal deficits, finger-to-nose and heel-to-shin normal. CT HEAD WITHOUT IV CONTRAST IS NORMAL. Q: Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Delaying 24 hours risks a catastrophic rebleed; if SAH is suspected with a negative CT, an LP should be done now (after the appropriate interval).

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: With a thunderclap headache and a sentinel headache (high suspicion of SAH) but a NORMAL CT (CT sensitivity falls after the first hours), the next step is a LUMBAR PUNCTURE to look for XANTHOCHROMIA / red cells (performed at least 12 hours after onset).

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: This treats cluster headache; it does not investigate a possible SAH and could be dangerous.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Aggressive BP lowering is not the priority; ruling out SAH with LP is.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Obtain an MRI scan of the head
choices.explanation: MRI is not more sensitive than CT for acute SAH and would delay the definitive LP.

questions.keyPoint: Thunderclap headache + negative CT → lumbar puncture for xanthochromia to exclude SAH.

rows: CT then LP; CT angiography if positive
Negative CT does not exclude SAH — do LP

questions.stem: A 62-YEAR-OLD MAN brought to the ED 40 MINUTES after his wife noticed the RIGHT SIDE OF HIS FACE WAS DROOPING during breakfast, with DIFFICULTY PUTTING ON HIS SHIRT AND SHOES. Type 2 diabetes, hypertension, hypercholesterolaemia; meds metformin/enalapril/atorvastatin; smoked 1 pack/day 35 years, one glass of wine daily. Alert and oriented, temp 37, pulse 99, BP 170/100, equal reactive pupils, RIGHT FACIAL DROOP, MUSCLE STRENGTH DECREASED IN THE RIGHT UPPER AND LOWER EXTREMITIES, sensation intact, RIGHT EXTENSOR PLANTAR, SPEECH DYSARTHRIC, BRUIT ON THE LEFT NECK. Fundoscopy normal; CBC, coagulation and serum concentrations normal. Q: Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: In any acute stroke presentation the FIRST-LINE investigation is a NON-CONTRAST CT OF THE HEAD to distinguish ISCHAEMIC from HAEMORRHAGIC stroke, which determines whether thrombolysis is safe and guides further management.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: MRI is more sensitive for early ischaemia but is slower/less available; CT is the immediate first test to exclude haemorrhage.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Carotid Doppler is part of later secondary-prevention workup, not the immediate next step.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Angiography is invasive and not the initial test in acute stroke.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: LP is not indicated for acute focal stroke; it is used when SAH is suspected with a negative CT.

questions.keyPoint: Acute stroke: non-contrast CT head first to differentiate ischaemic vs haemorrhagic.

rows: Acute focal deficit, need to exclude haemorrhage
CT head first, then further workup
CT head is the immediate first investigation

questions.stem: TWO DAYS after admission for an ISCHEMIC STROKE OF THE LEFT MIDDLE CEREBRAL ARTERY, a 74-YEAR-OLD WOMAN is evaluated for WORSENING CONFUSION. She received IV ALTEPLASE and was admitted to ICU; 24 hours after admission she started ANTIPLATELET therapy and was already able to speak in full sentences with slightly improved muscle function. This morning she IS CONFUSED, pulse 112, resp 12/min, BP 120/64, DOES NOT RESPOND TO VERBAL COMMANDS BUT WITHDRAWS APPROPRIATELY TO PAINFUL STIMULI, equal reactive pupils. Q: Which of the following is the most likely cause of this patient's symptoms?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Neurological DETERIORATION (worsening confusion, reduced responsiveness) within 24-36 hours of an ischaemic stroke treated with ALTEPLASE plus antiplatelet therapy is most likely HAEMORRHAGIC TRANSFORMATION of the infarct — a recognised thrombolysis complication.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Vasospasm is a complication of subarachnoid haemorrhage (days 4-14), not of a treated ischaemic MCA stroke.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Possible, but after thrombolysis + antiplatelets the more likely cause of acute deterioration is haemorrhagic transformation.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Uncal herniation would cause a fixed, dilated (blown) pupil and a deteriorating GCS; her pupils are equal and reactive.

questions.keyPoint: Deterioration after thrombolysis for ischaemic stroke = haemorrhagic transformation.

rows: Stop antithrombotics, reverse coagulopathy, neurosurgery review

questions.stem: A 67-YEAR-OLD WOMAN with SEVERE HEADACHE and LEFT-SIDED WEAKNESS AND NUMBNESS for 2 hours. Hypertension, atrial fibrillation, type 2 diabetes; meds enalapril/amlodipine/insulin/WARFARIN. Oriented, temp 36.8, pulse 90 irregular, BP 165/94. LEFTWARD AND DOWNWARD DEVIATION OF THE EYES, PUPILS MIOTIC AND NONREACTIVE, MUSCLE STRENGTH 2/5 ON THE LEFT and 5/5 ON THE RIGHT, deep tendon reflexes decreased on the left, SENSATION DECREASED ON THE LEFT and normal on the right and face. PT 51 seconds (INR 4.3). Q: Haemorrhage into which of the following locations is the most likely cause of this patient's symptoms?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Cerebellar haemorrhage causes ataxia, vomiting and gaze palsy toward the side of the lesion, not contralateral hemisensory/hemimotor loss with downward eye deviation.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Putaminal haemorrhage classically deviates the eyes TOWARD the lesion (away from the weak side) and is predominantly MOTOR; the prominent sensory loss and downward-deviated miotic pupils favour thalamus.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Pontine haemorrhage causes pinpoint pupils with COMA and quadriparesis, not an oriented patient with hemisensory loss.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: A RIGHT THALAMIC haemorrhage causes CONTRALATERAL (left) hemisensory loss > weakness, with characteristic eyes deviated DOWN and inward ('peering at the nose'/wrong-way eyes) and small, poorly reactive pupils. Warfarin (INR 4.3) precipitated the bleed.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: A frontal lobe bleed causes contralateral weakness/behavioural change but not the classic thalamic ocular signs and dense hemisensory loss.

questions.keyPoint: Hemisensory loss + eyes deviated down-and-in + miotic poorly reactive pupils = thalamic haemorrhage.

rows: Contralateral hemisensory loss, downward eye deviation, miotic pupils
Reverse anticoagulation, BP control, neurosurgery review

questions.stem: ONE DAY after a LEFT CAROTID ENDARTERECTOMY, a 63-YEAR-OLD MAN has a SEVERE HEADACHE (9/10) and nausea. He had 80% STENOSIS in the left carotid and received heparin before surgery, with 2 TIAs 4 months ago. Type 2 diabetes, hypercholesterolaemia; smoked 1 pack/day 40 years; meds lisinopril/metformin/sitagliptin/aspirin. Temp 37.3, PULSE 111, BP 180/110, CONFUSED and oriented only to person, PUPILS SLUGGISH, RIGHT FACIAL DROOP, MUSCLE STRENGTH DECREASED IN THE RIGHT EXTREMITIES, deep tendon reflexes 3+ on the right, surgical incision clean, CT head shown. Q: Which of the following is the strongest predisposing factor for this patient's condition?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: High-grade stenosis predisposes to cerebral hyperperfusion after revascularisation, but uncontrolled HYPERTENSION is the strongest modifiable predisposing factor for the resulting intracerebral haemorrhage.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Antiplatelets add bleeding risk but are not the strongest predisposing factor for cerebral hyperperfusion haemorrhage.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Heparin contributes to bleeding risk but the dominant driver of hyperperfusion injury is poorly controlled blood pressure.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: This is CEREBRAL HYPERPERFUSION SYNDROME causing intracerebral haemorrhage after endarterectomy. The previously hypoperfused brain cannot autoregulate; UNCONTROLLED HYPERTENSION (BP 180/110) is the STRONGEST predisposing factor, driving oedema and haemorrhage.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Smoking is a vascular risk factor but not the immediate strongest predisposing factor for post-endarterectomy hyperperfusion haemorrhage.

questions.keyPoint: Uncontrolled hypertension is the strongest predisposing factor for post-endarterectomy hyperperfusion ICH.

rows: Ipsilateral headache, seizures, focal deficit days after CEA, very high BP
Strict BP control prevents hyperperfusion injury

questions.stem: A previously healthy 42-YEAR-OLD WOMAN with SUDDEN ONSET OF SEVERE OCCIPITAL HEADACHE DURING DEFECATION 8 HOURS AGO, followed by TWO EPISODES OF VOMITING. The headache reached MAXIMUM INTENSITY WITHIN SECONDS and she has never had a headache like this. NO neck stiffness or neurologic symptoms. Mother and two sisters have migraine. Temp 37, BP 148/88, pulse 90, resp 20. Significant distress from pain; NO meningismus, papilloedema or focal neurologic signs. Q: Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

choices.label: A
choices.text: CT angiography of the head and neck
choices.explanation: CT angiography is used to localise an aneurysm AFTER SAH is confirmed, not as the first diagnostic step.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: LP is the next step only if the initial CT is negative; a non-contrast CT must be obtained first.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: A thunderclap headache (maximal in seconds), worst-ever, precipitated by straining (defecation), is SAH until proven otherwise. The first step is an urgent NON-CONTRAST CT OF THE HEAD, which is highly sensitive in the first 6-24 hours.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Treating this as migraine could mask a life-threatening SAH; imaging must come first.

questions.keyPoint: Thunderclap headache → urgent non-contrast CT head first (then LP if negative).

rows: CT then LP if negative; CT angiography to find aneurysm
CT head is the first investigation for thunderclap headache

questions.topic: SAH with negative CT - lumbar puncture (MKSAP)
questions.stem: A 73-YEAR-OLD RETIRED WOMAN evaluated 6 HOURS AFTER a SUDDEN EXPLOSIVE ONSET OF SEVERE HEADACHE. Hypertension controlled by diet and exercise; no relevant family history; no allergies; takes no over-the-counter medications. No obvious distress; temp normal, BP 179/108, pulse 119, resp 14. NO meningismus, normal level of consciousness, no focal abnormalities. LABORATORY STUDIES AND A CT SCAN OF THE HEAD WITHOUT CONTRAST ARE NORMAL. Q: Which of the following is the most appropriate next management step?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: An explosive thunderclap headache with a NORMAL CT still requires exclusion of SAH; the next step is a LUMBAR PUNCTURE (looking for xanthochromia/red cells), as CT sensitivity declines after the first hours and a negative CT does not exclude SAH.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: MRI is not more sensitive than CT for acute SAH and would delay the definitive LP.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Observation risks missing a sentinel SAH bleed; LP is required after a negative CT.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Empirical migraine treatment is inappropriate before SAH is excluded by LP.

questions.keyPoint: Thunderclap headache with normal CT → lumbar puncture to exclude SAH.

rows: CT then LP; CT angiography if confirmed
Normal CT does not exclude SAH — perform LP

questions.stem: A 35-YEAR-OLD PREVIOUSLY HEALTHY WOMAN SUDDENLY DEVELOPS A SEVERE HEADACHE WHILE LIFTING WEIGHTS. A minute later she has a TRANSIENT LOSS OF CONSCIOUSNESS; she awakes with VOMITING and a CONTINUED HEADACHE described as 'THE WORST HEADACHE OF MY LIFE'. Uncomfortable and vomits during examination. BP 140/85, pulse 100/min, resp 18/min, temp 36.8. THERE IS NECK STIFFNESS. Careful cranial nerve and deep tendon reflex testing is otherwise normal. Q: Which of the following is the best next step in evaluation?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Exertional thunderclap headache ('worst of my life') with transient LOC and neck stiffness is SAH until proven otherwise. The best next step is a NON-CONTRAST CT OF THE HEAD, highly sensitive for acute subarachnoid blood (LP if CT negative).

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Contrast is unnecessary and can obscure acute blood; a non-contrast CT is the correct first test for suspected SAH.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Angiography localises an aneurysm AFTER SAH is confirmed; it is not the first step.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: A Holter assesses arrhythmia and has no role in acute thunderclap headache evaluation.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: LP is done only AFTER a negative CT; performing it first risks missing imaging-detectable bleed and is hazardous if there is raised ICP/mass.

questions.keyPoint: Worst-ever exertional headache + neck stiffness → non-contrast CT head first (LP only if CT negative).

rows: Exertional thunderclap headache, transient LOC, neck stiffness
Non-contrast CT head; LP if negative
Non-contrast CT first; LP if CT negative Myopathies

questions.stem: A 45-YEAR-OLD WOMAN presents to her physician with a 4-week history of gradually increasing LIMB WEAKNESS. She first noticed difficulty climbing stairs, then problems rising from a chair and, finally, lifting her arms ABOVE SHOULDER LEVEL. Aside from some difficulty swallowing, she has NO ocular, bulbar, or sphincter problems and NO sensory complaints. Family history is NEGATIVE for neurological disease. Examination reveals significant PROXIMAL LIMB AND NECK MUSCLE WEAKNESS with MINIMAL ATROPHY, normal sensory findings, and normal deep tendon reflexes. Affected muscles are slightly tender; THERE IS NO SKIN RASH. Q: What is the likely pathogenesis of her condition?

choices.label: A
choices.text: B- and T-cell mediated attack against muscle autoantigens
choices.explanation: Symmetrical proximal weakness over weeks, dysphagia, tender muscles without a skin rash and a negative family history point to polymyositis, an idiopathic inflammatory myopathy. Pathogenesis is autoimmune: CD8+ cytotoxic T cells invade and destroy non-necrotic muscle fibres expressing MHC class I, with a contribution from B-cell/antibody mechanisms (e.g. anti-Jo-1). The absence of a heliotrope/Gottron rash distinguishes it from dermatomyositis.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Anterior horn cell degeneration in the spinal cord
choices.explanation: This describes motor neuron disease (ALS), which causes asymmetric weakness with fasciculations and upper motor neuron signs (hyperreflexia), not symmetric proximal weakness with normal reflexes.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Antibodies to the acetylcholine receptor at the neuromuscular junction
choices.explanation: This is myasthenia gravis, which characteristically causes fatigable weakness with ocular/bulbar involvement (ptosis, diplopia), not tender muscles.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Vasculitis with focal nerve and muscle ischemia
choices.explanation: Vasculitic neuropathy typically produces asymmetric mononeuritis multiplex with sensory loss, not symmetric painless proximal myopathy.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Abnormal trinucleotide repeat in the DMPK gene
choices.explanation: This is myotonic dystrophy, an inherited disorder with distal weakness, myotonia and a positive family history, not an acute acquired proximal myopathy.

questions.keyPoint: Polymyositis = symmetric proximal weakness, tender muscles, elevated CK, NO rash; mediated by CD8+ T cells attacking MHC-I-expressing muscle fibres (endomysial infiltration).

rows: Symmetric proximal weakness, dysphagia, tender muscles, no rash
Clinical + elevated CK, positive myositis antibodies (anti-Jo-1)
CK, EMG (fibrillations), muscle biopsy (endomysial CD8+ infiltrate)
Aspiration, interstitial lung disease, respiratory weakness
CD8+ T-cell endomysial attack on MHC-I+ fibres; no rash (vs dermatomyositis)

questions.stem: A 42-YEAR-OLD WOMAN comes to the physician because of a 2-MONTH history of progressive MUSCULAR WEAKNESS. She has had difficulty climbing stairs, getting up from chairs, and brushing her hair. Her vital signs are within normal limits. Muscle strength is 2/5 with FLEXION OF THE HIPS and 3/5 with ABDUCTION OF THE SHOULDERS. She is unable to stand up from her chair without the use of her arms for support. Laboratory studies show ELEVATIONS in LEUKOCYTE COUNT, ERYTHROCYTE SEDIMENTATION RATE, and CREATINE KINASE concentration. Histological evaluation of a biopsy specimen of the DELTOID muscle is most likely to show which of the following? Q: Histological evaluation of a biopsy specimen of the deltoid muscle is most likely to show which of the following?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Sarcolemmal MHC-I overexpression with CD8+ lymphocytic infiltration
choices.explanation: Subacute symmetric proximal weakness with elevated CK, ESR and leukocytosis is polymyositis. The biopsy hallmark is endomysial CD8+ cytotoxic T-lymphocytes surrounding and invading non-necrotic muscle fibres that aberrantly overexpress MHC class I on the sarcolemma. This cell-mediated injury distinguishes polymyositis from the perifascicular/perivascular CD4+/B-cell pattern of dermatomyositis.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Relative atrophy of type II muscle fibers with hypertrophy of type I muscle fiber
choices.explanation: Type II fibre atrophy is seen in disuse, steroid myopathy and ageing, not in an inflammatory myopathy with elevated CK and ESR.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Perimysial inflammation with perivascular CD4+ T lymphocytic infiltration
choices.explanation: This perimysial/perivascular CD4+ and B-cell pattern with perifascicular atrophy is dermatomyositis, which would have a skin rash.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Muscle fiber necrosis with rare inflammatory cells
choices.explanation: Sparse inflammation with prominent necrosis suggests necrotizing autoimmune myopathy (e.g. statin- or anti-HMGCR-associated), not the dense endomysial CD8+ infiltrate of polymyositis.

questions.keyPoint: Polymyositis biopsy: endomysial CD8+ T cells invading MHC-I-overexpressing non-necrotic fibres. Dermatomyositis = perimysial/perivascular CD4+, perifascicular atrophy + rash.

rows: Symmetric proximal weakness, raised CK/ESR, no rash

questions.stem: A 43-YEAR-OLD WOMAN comes to the physician because of a 2-MONTH history of progressive muscle pain and stiffness that WORSENS WITH EXERCISE. She also has DIFFICULTY climbing stairs, getting out of chairs, and putting things on shelves or in cupboards. She has had a normal MENSTRUAL period, FEELS COLD all the time, and has a 9-kg (20-lb) WEIGHT GAIN during the past year. She has HYPERTENSION controlled with amlodipine. She has cool clammy skin. Her PULSE is 80/MIN and her BLOOD PRESSURE is 138/70 mm Hg. Physical examination shows weakness of the proximal muscle groups. She has DELAYED TENDON REFLEX RELAXATION, with a mounding of the muscle surface occurring just before relaxation. CREATINE KINASE level is 3120 U/L. Q: Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: EMG is non-specific and would not establish the underlying endocrine cause suggested by cold intolerance, weight gain and delayed reflex relaxation.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: The acquired adult presentation with hypothyroid features points to an endocrine cause, not an inherited dystrophy.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Cold intolerance, weight gain, constipation-type features, delayed ("hung-up") deep tendon reflex relaxation and a markedly elevated CK in a proximal myopathy are classic for hypothyroid myopathy. Measuring TSH (with free T4) confirms hypothyroidism; treating with levothyroxine reverses the myopathy and normalises CK.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Temporal arteritis presents with headache, jaw claudication and high ESR in the elderly, not cold intolerance and weight gain with raised CK.

questions.keyPoint: Proximal myopathy + raised CK + cold intolerance, weight gain and DELAYED reflex relaxation = hypothyroid myopathy. Check TSH first.

rows: Proximal weakness, raised CK, cold intolerance, weight gain, hung-up reflexes
Serum TSH (raised) + free T4 (low) HIGH-YIELD SUMMARY
Delayed (hung-up) ankle reflex + high CK; check TSH

questions.topic: Becker muscular dystrophy / in-frame dystrophin deletion
questions.stem: A 16-YEAR-OLD BOY is brought to the physician by his parents because of PROGRESSIVE difficulty walking and trouble climbing stairs over the past 2 years. He is still able to walk but tires easily. He has NO trouble with his arms. Family history shows that a maternal uncle had similar symptoms. Serum CREATINE KINASE concentration is 540 U/L. Genetic analysis shows an IN-FRAME DELETION in the dystrophin gene. Q: This patient is at greatest risk of dying from which of the following conditions?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: The cardiac risk in dystrophinopathy is a primary cardiomyopathy, not accelerated atherosclerotic coronary disease.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Respiratory failure is the usual cause of death in severe Duchenne dystrophy; in milder Becker dystrophy, cardiac dilated cardiomyopathy predominates as the lethal complication.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: An in-frame dystrophin deletion produces a partially functional protein = Becker muscular dystrophy (later onset, slower progression, still ambulant in teens, X-linked with maternal-uncle history, raised CK). Dystrophin deficiency in cardiac muscle causes a progressive dilated cardiomyopathy, and congestive heart failure is the leading cause of death in Becker dystrophy because skeletal disease is comparatively mild.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: PE is not a characteristic mode of death in muscular dystrophy.

questions.keyPoint: IN-FRAME dystrophin deletion = Becker (milder, ambulant later); death usually from DILATED CARDIOMYOPATHY / CHF. Out-of-frame deletion = Duchenne (death from respiratory failure).

rows: X-linked, slowly progressive proximal weakness, ambulant into teens/adult, raised CK
In-frame deletion = Becker; dies of dilated cardiomyopathy

questions.stem: A 56-YEAR-OLD WOMAN comes to the physician because of a 3-MONTH history of progressive weakness. She has NO history of serious illness and takes NO medications. Her vital signs are within normal limits. Physical examination shows a VIOLACEOUS RASH OVER HER EYELIDS. Muscle strength is 4/5 at the shoulders and hips but normal elsewhere. Q: This patient is at greatest risk for which of the following conditions?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Although dermatomyositis raises overall cancer risk, the strongest associations are with solid adenocarcinomas (ovarian, lung, GI, breast), not Hodgkin lymphoma specifically.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: The heliotrope (violaceous periorbital) rash with proximal weakness is dermatomyositis, a paraneoplastic condition. Adult dermatomyositis carries a markedly increased risk of occult malignancy; in women, OVARIAN adenocarcinoma is one of the most strongly associated cancers (along with lung, breast and GI). Age-appropriate cancer screening is mandatory.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Small-cell (oat cell) lung cancer is classically linked to Lambert-Eaton syndrome; lung cancer is associated with dermatomyositis but ovarian carcinoma is the better answer in this woman.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Pheochromocytoma is not associated with dermatomyositis and presents with episodic hypertension, palpitations and sweating.

questions.keyPoint: Heliotrope rash + proximal weakness = dermatomyositis = paraneoplastic; screen for malignancy. In women, ovarian adenocarcinoma is a key association.

rows: Heliotrope rash, Gottron papules, proximal weakness
Occult malignancy (ovarian, lung, GI, breast), ILD
Dermatomyositis is paraneoplastic; ovarian Ca in women

questions.topic: Duchenne muscular dystrophy - initial diagnostic step
questions.stem: A 3-YEAR-OLD BOY is brought to the physician by his parents because of a 6-MONTH history of WORSENING MOBILITY issues. During this period, his parents noticed that he had occasional falls and increasing difficulties climbing stairs and running. The boy had a NORMAL DEVELOPMENT up until then; he was able to walk by the age of 15 months. There is NO personal or family history of serious illness. He takes NO medications and immunizations are up-to-date. He is at the 10th percentile for height and 25th percentile for weight. Vital signs are within normal limits. Musculoskeletal examination shows ENLARGED CALF MUSCLES BILATERALLY. Deep tendon reflexes are 1+ on the lower extremities and 2+ on the upper extremities. He has a WADDLING GAIT and when asked to get up from the floor, he SUPPORTS HIMSELF WITH HIS HANDS ON HIS LEGS to get up in an upright position (GOWERS SIGN). Q: Which of the following is the most appropriate initial step in diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Muscle biopsy (showing fibrofatty replacement and absent dystrophin) confirms Duchenne but is invasive and is not the FIRST step; a markedly raised CK is checked before considering biopsy/genetics.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Dystrophin gene (DMD) analysis is the definitive confirmatory test, but the cheapest, least invasive INITIAL screen is serum CK, which is grossly elevated and prompts genetic testing.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: A boy of about 3 years with delayed motor milestones, proximal weakness, waddling gait, Gowers sign and calf pseudohypertrophy has Duchenne muscular dystrophy. Serum CK is markedly elevated (often >10-100x normal) and is the appropriate, inexpensive, non-invasive INITIAL test; a high CK then directs confirmatory DMD genetic analysis (and, if needed, muscle biopsy).

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: EMG shows a non-specific myopathic pattern and is uncomfortable; it is not the first-line diagnostic step in suspected Duchenne dystrophy.

questions.keyPoint: Suspected Duchenne (boy ~3-5 yr, Gowers sign, calf pseudohypertrophy): serum CK is the FIRST test (grossly raised), then DMD gene analysis confirms.

rows: Boy ~3 yr, delayed walking, Gowers sign, calf pseudohypertrophy, waddling gait
Markedly raised CK then DMD genetic analysis
Serum CK first; genetics; biopsy if needed
Check serum CK FIRST in suspected DMD

questions.stem: A 47-YEAR-OLD WOMAN comes to the physician because of PROGRESSIVE MUSCLE WEAKNESS for 5 MONTHS.
