She finds that the muscles in her shoulders and hips have been getting weaker and sometimes sore. She now has DIFFICULTY getting up from chairs, climbing stairs, and combing her hair. She has a 5-year history of HYPERLIPIDAEMIA controlled with fluvastatin. FAMILY HISTORY is remarkable for HASHIMOTO THYROIDITIS in her MOTHER and DUCHENNE MUSCULAR DYSTROPHY in her MATERNAL UNCLE, who died at age 28. Vital signs are within normal limits. Neurologic examination shows moderate weakness in the arm ABDUCTORS and HIP FLEXORS bilaterally. Deep tendon reflexes are 2+ bilaterally. Laboratory studies show elevated muscle enzymes. Q: Which of the following would be most appropriate to confirm the diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Resolution of symptoms with statin cessation
choices.explanation: Simple statin myalgia resolves on stopping the drug, but this subacute symmetric proximal myopathy with raised enzymes suggests an inflammatory myopathy that needs biopsy confirmation rather than a trial of withdrawal.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Perifascicular and perivascular infiltration on muscle biopsy
choices.explanation: A perifascicular/perivascular pattern with perifascicular atrophy is the histology of dermatomyositis, which classically has a skin rash that this patient lacks.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Dystrophin gene mutation on genetic analysis
choices.explanation: Dystrophinopathy affects males; a 47-year-old woman with acquired proximal weakness is a manifesting carrier at most, and her presentation fits an inflammatory myopathy, not Duchenne dystrophy.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Subacute symmetric proximal weakness with tender muscles, raised muscle enzymes and no rash is polymyositis, an inflammatory myopathy. The confirmatory biopsy shows ENDOMYSIAL (intrafascicular) inflammatory infiltration of CD8+ T cells surrounding/invading non-necrotic fibres - in contrast to the perifascicular pattern of dermatomyositis. (Note: a high CK is supportive, but biopsy is the listed confirmatory option.)

questions.keyPoint: Polymyositis confirmed by ENDOMYSIAL (intrafascicular) CD8+ inflammatory infiltrate; dermatomyositis shows PERIFASCICULAR/perivascular pattern.

rows: Symmetric proximal weakness, sore muscles, raised enzymes, no rash

flag.note: FILE ANSWER vs STANDARD TEACHING — File says: D Standard (): Distractor C is clinically inconsistent (Duchenne is X-linked recessive, rarely manifests fully in a 47-year-old woman); included for completeness. Exam advice: Confirm against your answer key.
flag.source: Study guide flag (file key vs standard teaching)

questions.stem: A 3-YEAR-OLD BOY is brought to the physician by his parents because of CLUMSINESS and MULTIPLE FALLS over the past 4 months. He started WALKING AT THE AGE OF 18 MONTHS and could walk up steps by the time he was 27 months old but now STRUGGLES TO WALK at all without assistance. When standing up from a lying position, he CRAWLS ONTO HIS KNEES and slowly WALKS HIMSELF UP WITH HIS HANDS (GOWERS SIGN). There is BILATERAL CALF ENLARGEMENT (pseudohypertrophy). Q: Analysis of a LEFT CALF biopsy specimen from this patient is most likely to show which of the following?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Proliferation of mitochondria within muscle fibers
choices.explanation: Ragged-red fibres with subsarcolemmal mitochondrial proliferation indicate a mitochondrial myopathy, which presents with lactic acidosis and multisystem involvement, not isolated calf pseudohypertrophy with Gowers sign.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Atrophic muscle fibers interspersed among hypertrophic muscle fibers
choices.explanation: Variable fibre size with grouped atrophy suggests denervation (neurogenic) disease, not the dystrophic fibrofatty degeneration of Duchenne.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Perivascular inflammation with muscle fiber ischemia and atrophy
choices.explanation: Perivascular inflammation describes an inflammatory/vasculitic myopathy; it is not the histology of a genetic dystrophinopathy.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Degeneration of muscle fibers with fibrofatty replacement
choices.explanation: A boy of about 3 years with delayed walking, progressive loss of ambulation, Gowers sign and calf pseudohypertrophy has Duchenne muscular dystrophy (out-of-frame dystrophin mutation = absent dystrophin). The biopsy shows ongoing degeneration/necrosis and regeneration of muscle fibres with variation in fibre size, and over time the lost muscle is replaced by CONNECTIVE TISSUE AND FAT (fibrofatty replacement) - the basis of the pseudohypertrophic calves.

questions.keyPoint: Duchenne biopsy: muscle-fibre degeneration with FIBROFATTY REPLACEMENT and absent dystrophin; underlies calf pseudohypertrophy.

rows: Boy ~3 yr, Gowers sign, calf pseudohypertrophy, loss of ambulation
DMD genetics; biopsy = fibrofatty replacement, absent dystrophin
CK (very high), genetics, biopsy with dystrophin stain
Fibrofatty replacement of degenerating fibres = Duchenne

questions.stem: A 3-YEAR-OLD BOY is brought to the emergency department because of NAUSEA and VOMITING for 1 day. His mother says he had a SEIZURE disorder and died in childhood. He appears FATIGUED. RESPIRATIONS are 32/min. Examination shows DIFFUSE WEAKNESS in the extremities. Serum studies show a LOW pH, ELEVATED LACTATE concentration, and NORMAL BLOOD GLUCOSE. A metabolic condition characterized by a DEFECT IN OXIDATIVE PHOSPHORYLATION is suspected. Q: Microscopic examination of a muscle biopsy specimen is most likely to show which of the following findings?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Perimysial inflammation indicates an inflammatory myopathy (dermatomyositis), not a metabolic defect in oxidative phosphorylation.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Glycogen accumulation occurs in glycogen storage myopathies (e.g. Pompe, McArdle), which cause exercise intolerance rather than lactic acidosis with maternally inherited seizures.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Lactic acidosis with normal glucose, a defect in oxidative phosphorylation, weakness, seizures and a maternally inherited fatal course point to a mitochondrial myopathy (e.g. MELAS). The biopsy hallmark is RAGGED-RED FIBRES on Gomori trichrome, caused by subsarcolemmal accumulation of abnormal mitochondria that proliferate to compensate for the respiratory-chain defect.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Endomysial CD8+ T-cell infiltration is the histology of polymyositis, an acquired autoimmune myopathy, not a mitochondrial oxidative-phosphorylation disorder.

questions.keyPoint: Mitochondrial myopathy (MELAS): lactic acidosis, maternal inheritance, oxidative-phosphorylation defect; biopsy = RAGGED-RED FIBRES (subsarcolemmal mitochondrial accumulation).

rows: Lactic acidosis, seizures, weakness, maternal inheritance
Muscle biopsy (ragged-red fibres), mtDNA testing, lactate
Supportive; cofactors (CoQ10, riboflavin), avoid mito toxins
Ragged-red fibres = subsarcolemmal mitochondrial accumulation Neuromuscular junction disorders

questions.stem: A 55-YEAR-OLD WOMAN complains of DOUBLE VISION. She finds that she is more tired than usual and has DIFFICULTY CLIMBING STAIRS, especially when they are very long. She has difficulty getting items off high shelves at work and, late in the evening when brushing her hair, this is a problem. During the consultation, her VOICE FADES AWAY during conversation. REFLEXES are present and equal throughout. Q: Which sign or symptom is most indicative of myasthenia gravis?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Proximal weakness is common to many myopathies and is not specific for myasthenia gravis.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Normal reflexes are seen in MG but are non-specific and do not point to the diagnosis.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Diplopia is a frequent presenting symptom but, on its own, has many causes; it is the FATIGABLE nature that is diagnostic of MG.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: The defining feature of myasthenia gravis is FATIGABLE muscle weakness - symptoms (diplopia, ptosis, dysphonia, limb weakness) that worsen with sustained or repeated activity and improve with rest, and that are typically worse later in the day. This pattern, caused by anti-AChR antibodies reducing functional receptors, distinguishes MG from other neuromuscular disorders.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Bulbar symptoms (dysphagia, dysphonia) occur in MG but also in motor neuron disease and other conditions; fatigability is the more specific clue.

questions.keyPoint: Hallmark of myasthenia gravis = FATIGABLE weakness (worse with use/late in day, better with rest).

rows: Fatigable ptosis, diplopia, dysphonia, proximal weakness worse with use
Anti-AChR antibodies, repetitive nerve stimulation (decrement), edrophonium/ice-pack test

questions.stem: A 55-YEAR-OLD WOMAN complains of DOUBLE VISION. She finds that she is TIRED ALL THE TIME and has difficulty getting items off high shelves at work. REFLEXES are ABSENT (depressed). SHOULDER ABDUCTION is initially 4-5 but on REPEATED TESTING is 4+/5 (strength IMPROVES with exercise/repetition). Q: What pathology is associated with this female's diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Thyrotoxicosis is associated with myasthenia gravis and thyroid eye disease, not the facilitation pattern (improvement with repetition) of Lambert-Eaton syndrome.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Peptic ulcer disease has no association with Lambert-Eaton myasthenic syndrome.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Diabetes causes peripheral neuropathy but is not the paraneoplastic association of LEMS.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Stroke causes focal upper-motor-neuron deficits, not a fluctuating fatigable/facilitating weakness with absent reflexes.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Weakness that IMPROVES with repeated testing (post-exercise facilitation), absent/depressed reflexes and autonomic features indicate Lambert-Eaton myasthenic syndrome, caused by antibodies against presynaptic voltage-gated calcium channels. It is a paraneoplastic disorder most often associated with SMALL-CELL LUNG CANCER, so the key association is lung cancer.

questions.keyPoint: Weakness that IMPROVES with repetition + absent reflexes = Lambert-Eaton (anti-VGCC) = paraneoplastic with SMALL-CELL LUNG CANCER.

rows: Proximal weakness improving with use, hyporeflexia, autonomic features
Antibodies, RNS, CT chest for SCLC

questions.stem: A 51-YEAR-OLD WOMAN with HYPERLIPIDAEMIA comes to the physician because of WEAKNESS for 1 MONTH. At the end of the day, she feels too fatigued to cook dinner or carry a laundry basket up the stairs. She also complains of DOUBLE VISION after she reads for long periods of time. All of her symptoms IMPROVE WITH REST. Her only medication is pravastatin. Physical examination shows DROOPING OF THE UPPER EYELIDS (ptosis). STRENGTH is initially 5/5 in the upper and lower extremities but DECREASES TO 4/5 after a few minutes of SUSTAINED RESISTANCE. SENSATION to light touch is intact and deep tendon reflexes are NORMAL. Q: Which of the following best describes the pathogenesis of this patient's condition?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Fatigable ptosis and diplopia with proximal weakness that worsens on sustained effort and improves with rest, with normal reflexes and sensation, is myasthenia gravis. It is a TYPE II (antibody-mediated cytotoxic) hypersensitivity reaction: IgG autoantibodies against the postsynaptic nicotinic acetylcholine receptor cause complement-mediated receptor destruction and blockade, reducing functional receptors at the neuromuscular junction.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Demyelinating neuropathy (e.g. GBS, CIDP) produces areflexia and sensory changes, not fatigable ocular weakness with normal reflexes.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Impaired presynaptic ACh release describes Lambert-Eaton syndrome or botulism; here weakness WORSENS (not improves) with sustained effort and reflexes are normal, fitting postsynaptic MG.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Statin myopathy causes muscle aches and raised CK, not fatigable ocular and bulbar weakness with ptosis.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Anterior horn cell loss (e.g. ALS) causes wasting, fasciculations and mixed UMN/LMN signs, not fatigable ptosis with preserved sensation and reflexes.

questions.keyPoint: Myasthenia gravis is a TYPE II hypersensitivity reaction - IgG antibodies against postsynaptic ACh receptors.

rows: Fatigable ptosis/diplopia, weakness worse with use, normal reflexes/sensation

questions.stem: A 53-YEAR-OLD MAN with a 3-month history of COUGH and PROGRESSIVELY WORSENING DIFFICULTY WALKING UP STAIRS; easier to climb stairs after he has exercised. 4.6 kg WEIGHT LOSS over 6 months. Smoked ONE PACK OF CIGARETTES DAILY FOR 35 YEARS. DRY MUCOUS MEMBRANES. Pupils equal and react sluggishly to light. Muscle strength in the proximal lower extremity initially 3/5 but INCREASES TO 5/5 AFTER REPEATED MUSCLE TAPPING. Achilles reflex 1+. Q: Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: ALS gives mixed UMN/LMN signs with fasciculations and a wasted tongue; it does not show post-exercise facilitation of strength or autonomic features (dry mouth, sluggish pupils).

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: PMR causes proximal shoulder/hip girdle pain and stiffness with raised ESR in the elderly; no true weakness, no autonomic signs, no facilitation.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: MG fatigues (weakens) with repeated activity rather than improving, and ocular/bulbar involvement predominates; the post-tapping IMPROVEMENT and autonomic features point away from MG.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Antibodies to presynaptic P/Q-type VOLTAGE-GATED CALCIUM CHANNELS reduce acetylcholine release. Hallmarks: proximal lower-limb weakness that IMPROVES WITH REPEATED ACTIVITY (post-exercise facilitation), hyporeflexia that recovers after exercise, AUTONOMIC features (dry mouth, sluggish pupils, impotence) and a strong association with SMALL-CELL LUNG CANCER (heavy smoker). A chest CT to look for malignancy is mandatory.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Dermatomyositis causes symmetrical proximal weakness with a heliotrope rash and raised CK; no autonomic involvement and no facilitation phenomenon.

questions.keyPoint: Proximal weakness that IMPROVES with repeated effort + hyporeflexia + autonomic features + heavy smoker = Lambert-Eaton syndrome (anti-VGCC, paraneoplastic of SCLC).

rows: Proximal weakness improving with use, hyporeflexia, autonomic dysfunction
Anti-P/Q voltage-gated Ca2+ channel antibodies; incremental response on repetitive stimulation
CHEST CT to exclude small-cell lung cancer
LEMS strength IMPROVES with repeated activity (opposite of MG)

questions.stem: A 28-YEAR-OLD WOMAN with a 1-year history of PROGRESSIVE WEAKNESS; INCREASING DIFFICULTY climbing stairs, WORSENING DOUBLE VISION and FATIGUE. No family history. Teacher; weakness worse later in the day. Muscle strength 3/5 abduction of shoulders and flexion of hips, 4/5 in remaining groups. Sensation intact. DTRs 2+ and symmetric. DROOPING OF UPPER EYELIDS and DOUBLE VISION WHEN MOVING THE EYES. PTOSIS worsens after 2 minutes of UPGAZE. TSH 4.2 micro-units/mL (normal). Q: Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Polymyositis causes proximal weakness with raised CK but does not cause ptosis, diplopia or fatigable ocular weakness.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: PMR is pain/stiffness of shoulder and hip girdles in the elderly with raised ESR; no fatigable weakness or ocular signs.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Antibodies against the POSTSYNAPTIC ACETYLCHOLINE RECEPTOR cause FATIGABLE weakness. Classic features: FLUCTUATING ptosis and DIPLOPIA worse with sustained gaze and toward the end of the day, fatigable proximal limb weakness, preserved reflexes and sensation. Young women are typically affected. Worsening ptosis after sustained upgaze is a positive bedside fatigability test.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: LEMS improves with repeated activity, causes autonomic features and depressed reflexes, and is associated with SCLC in older smokers; ocular features are not prominent.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: MS gives CNS demyelinating signs disseminated in time and space (e.g. optic neuritis, internuclear ophthalmoplegia), not fatigable neuromuscular weakness.

questions.keyPoint: Fatigable ptosis/diplopia worse at end of day in a young woman = myasthenia gravis (anti-AChR antibodies).

rows: Fatigable ptosis, diplopia, proximal weakness worse with use/end of day
Anti-AChR antibodies; decremental response on repetitive nerve stimulation; ice-pack/edrophonium test
Anti-AChR (and anti-MuSK) antibodies; CT thorax for thymoma
Weakness WORSENS with use (fatigability), opposite of LEMS

questions.stem: A 63-YEAR-OLD MAN, 6 months of weakness in his LOWER LEGS that IMPROVES WITH EXERCISE and throughout the day. Adding FLAX SEEDS to breakfast as he is FREQUENTLY CONSTIPATED. Vital signs normal. DRY MUCOUS MEMBRANES. Muscle strength in lower extremities decreased. Sensation to pinprick and light touch normal. DEEP TENDON REFLEXES 1+ BILATERALLY. ACTIVE MUSCLE CONTRACTION OR REPEATED MUSCLE TAPPING INCREASES REFLEX ACTIVITY. Q: Which of the following is most likely to confirm the diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Demyelinating plaques on a brain MRI
choices.explanation: Demyelinating plaques indicate multiple sclerosis, a CNS disease; this man has a presynaptic neuromuscular junction disorder.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: This is LEMS. Antibodies against PRESYNAPTIC P/Q-TYPE VOLTAGE-GATED CALCIUM CHANNELS impair calcium-dependent acetylcholine release. Repeated stimulation/contraction allows calcium to accumulate presynaptically, transiently boosting ACh release, explaining the POST-EXERCISE FACILITATION of strength and reflexes. Autonomic features (constipation, dry mouth) and association with small-cell lung cancer are typical.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: SFEMG shows increased jitter and is most sensitive for myasthenia gravis; it is not the confirmatory diagnostic test for LEMS, which is the antibody assay/incremental RNS pattern.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Decremental response following repetitive nerve stimulation
choices.explanation: A DECREMENTAL response on low-frequency RNS is the finding in MYASTHENIA GRAVIS; LEMS shows an INCREMENTAL response at high frequency/post-exercise.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Anti-AChR antibodies confirm myasthenia gravis (postsynaptic disease), not LEMS which is a presynaptic VGCC antibody disorder.

questions.keyPoint: LEMS is confirmed by anti-voltage-gated calcium channel antibodies; RNS shows an INCREMENT (facilitation), unlike the decrement of MG.

rows: Lower-limb weakness improving with use, hyporeflexia with facilitation, autonomic features
RNS shows incremental response; screen for SCLC HIGH-YIELD SUMMARY
Anti-VGCC antibody confirms LEMS; incremental RNS response

questions.stem: A 50-YEAR-OLD MAN, DIFFUSE WEAKNESS for several months. Lateral CHEST X-RAY shows an ANTERIOR MEDIASTINAL MASS. GASTRO-OESOPHAGEAL REFLUX on rabeprazole. 178 cm, 77 kg, BMI 24.6. No cervical or axillary lymphadenopathy. Cardiopulmonary exam normal. Abdomen soft and non-tender; no splenomegaly. Q: Further evaluation of this patient is most likely to show which of the following?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Fever, night sweats, and weight loss
choices.explanation: B-symptoms suggest lymphoma; while lymphoma can cause a mediastinal mass, the clinical picture of diffuse weakness + thymoma points to myasthenia gravis, not lymphoma.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: A heavy smoking history would favour lung cancer/LEMS; an ANTERIOR mediastinal mass with diffuse weakness is the thymoma-MG association.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Raised AFP suggests a germ-cell tumour (also an anterior mediastinal mass), but the weakness here is explained by MG/thymoma, so anti-AChR antibodies are expected.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: An ANTERIOR MEDIASTINAL MASS (THYMOMA) with diffuse weakness is the classic THYMOMA-MYASTHENIA GRAVIS association. About 10-15% of MG patients have a thymoma and most thymoma patients with weakness have ANTI-ACETYLCHOLINE RECEPTOR ANTIBODIES. Thymectomy can improve symptoms.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Elevated TSH and a nodular anterior cervical mass
choices.explanation: This describes thyroid pathology; a goitre is a cervical (neck) mass, not an anterior mediastinal mass, and would not explain the weakness.

questions.keyPoint: Anterior mediastinal mass (thymoma) + weakness = myasthenia gravis; check anti-AChR antibodies.

rows: Diffuse fatigable weakness, anterior mediastinal mass
Thymoma is the classic anterior mediastinal mass linked to MG

questions.stem: A 42-YEAR-OLD WOMAN, progressive weakness for a month; too FATIGUED TO COOK DINNER and FEELS BETTER AFTER SLEEPING. No chest pain or recent illness. Smokes two packs/day for 25 years. Appears fatigued. T 37.0C, pulse 88, BP 148/80. OXYGEN SATURATION 98%. Wheezes both lung bases. DROOPING OF THE UPPER EYELIDS. DIMINISHED MOTOR STRENGTH and reflexes in the upper extremities. Q: Which of the following mechanisms of action is the most appropriate next step in the management of this patient?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Fatigable weakness and ptosis improving with rest indicate MYASTHENIA GRAVIS. First-line symptomatic treatment is PYRIDOSTIGMINE, an ACETYLCHOLINESTERASE INHIBITOR, which prevents breakdown of acetylcholine at the neuromuscular junction, raising synaptic ACh and improving transmission and strength.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Beta-2 agonists (e.g. salbutamol) treat bronchospasm; they are not the treatment for the neuromuscular weakness of MG.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Removal of autoantibodies, immune complexes, and cytotoxic constituents from serum
choices.explanation: This describes PLASMAPHERESIS, reserved for myasthenic crisis or refractory disease, not the first-line symptomatic step.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Reactivating acetylcholinesterase (e.g. pralidoxime in organophosphate poisoning) would WORSEN MG by reducing synaptic ACh.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Competitive blocking of the muscarinic receptor
choices.explanation: Antimuscarinics (e.g. atropine) block ACh effects and would not help, indeed could worsen, neuromuscular transmission failure.

questions.keyPoint: Pyridostigmine (acetylcholinesterase inhibitor) is first-line symptomatic therapy in myasthenia gravis.

rows: Fatigable proximal weakness and ptosis, better after rest
Acetylcholinesterase inhibition raises ACh and improves MG strength

questions.stem: A 35-YEAR-OLD WOMAN, symptoms WORSEN THROUGHOUT THE DAY AND IMPROVE WITH REST. No personal or family history of serious illness. Teacher. PTOSIS and a MASK-LIKE FACE; fatigable. DECREMENTAL RESPONSE ON REPETITIVE NERVE STIMULATION. Q: Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in the management of this patient?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Adrenal function tests address Addison/Cushing disease, not fatigable neuromuscular weakness.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Physostigmine crosses the blood-brain barrier and is used for anticholinergic toxicity; the MG anticholinesterase is pyridostigmine, and the next step here is to CONFIRM the diagnosis first.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: CT thorax screens for thymoma and is done AFTER MG is confirmed, not as the immediate confirmatory step.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: The decremental RNS response and fatigable ptosis confirm MYASTHENIA GRAVIS clinically; the most appropriate confirmatory test is ANTI-ACETYLCHOLINE RECEPTOR ANTIBODY testing, which is positive in ~85% of generalised MG and is highly specific.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Plasmapheresis is a treatment for crisis/refractory disease, not a diagnostic step.

questions.keyPoint: Anti-AChR antibody testing is the confirmatory investigation for myasthenia gravis.

rows: Fatigable weakness/ptosis worse through day, decremental RNS
Anti-AChR (then anti-MuSK if negative); CT thorax for thymoma
Anti-AChR antibody is the key confirmatory test for MG CNS infections

questions.stem: A 28-YEAR-OLD JUNIOR DOCTOR, HEADACHE FOR 24 HOURS, started gradually intensifying slowly involving the entire cranium. TURNING HER HEAD IS UNCOMFORTABLE. Generally unwell, prefers to lie in a DARK ROOM; appears irritable. PHOTOPHOBIA and there is NECK STIFFNESS. NO PAPILLOEDEMA. No skin rashes. KERNIG SIGN NEGATIVE. LP reveals LOW PROTEIN, NORMAL GLUCOSE and LYMPHOCYTOSIS. Q: What is the diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: The CSF picture of NORMAL/LOW PROTEIN, NORMAL GLUCOSE and a LYMPHOCYTE predominance is classic for VIRAL (aseptic) MENINGITIS. The relatively well young adult with headache, photophobia and neck stiffness but no rash, no papilloedema and only mild signs fits a self-limiting viral process (commonly enteroviruses).

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Migraine causes photophobia but does not produce true neck stiffness or a CSF pleocytosis; the abnormal lumbar puncture excludes it.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Aseptic meningitis is essentially the same entity; the file's preferred single best answer is the more specific VIRAL meningitis given the lymphocytic, normal-glucose CSF.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Bacterial meningitis gives a NEUTROPHIL-predominant CSF with HIGH protein and LOW glucose, with a more toxic, rapidly progressive picture - the opposite of this CSF.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: TB meningitis is subacute with HIGH protein, LOW glucose and a lymphocytic CSF; the normal glucose and low protein here argue against it.

questions.keyPoint: Lymphocytic CSF with NORMAL glucose and NORMAL/low protein = viral (aseptic) meningitis.

rows: Headache, photophobia, neck stiffness; relatively well; no rash
CSF lymphocytosis, normal glucose, normal/low protein
Normal CSF glucose distinguishes viral from bacterial/TB meningitis

questions.stem: A 19-YEAR-OLD WOMAN, COLLEGE SOPHOMORE living in a DORMITORY, evaluated in December for 24 hours of FEVER and HEADACHE. NOT UP TO DATE WITH IMMUNISATIONS including the MENINGOCOCCAL VACCINE, which she received before entering college. Two cases of MENINGOCOCCAL SEROGROUP B meningitis on campus. APPEARS ILL. T 39.1C, BP 95/50, PULSE 125, RESP 25. PURPURIC RASH over both lower extremities. NECK STIFFNESS and JOLT ACCENTUATION. Non-contrast CT head normal. LEUKOCYTES 19,500 (87% neutrophils, 13% lymphocytes); platelets 110,000. CSF LEUKOCYTES 2000 (119+10% L), GLUCOSE 20 mg/dL (serum 100), PROTEIN 100 mg/dL. GRAM STAIN GRAM-NEGATIVE DIPLOCOCCI. Q: Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: GRAM-NEGATIVE DIPLOCOCCI in CSF plus a PURPURIC (petechial) RASH, neutrophilic CSF with LOW GLUCOSE and HIGH PROTEIN in a young person in a dormitory amid a SEROGROUP B outbreak is classic NEISSERIA MENINGITIDIS. The conjugate vaccine she received protects against A,C,W,Y but NOT serogroup B, so she remains susceptible.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: RMSF (Rickettsia rickettsii) gives a rash spreading from wrists/ankles with fever/headache but is not associated with a neutrophilic, low-glucose CSF or gram-negative diplococci.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Pneumococcus is a gram-POSITIVE diplococcus and is not classically linked to a campus serogroup-B outbreak with purpura; the gram stain here is gram-negative.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Vibrio vulnificus causes wound infections/sepsis after seawater or shellfish exposure, not epidemic meningococcal-type meningitis in a dormitory.

questions.keyPoint: Gram-negative diplococci + purpuric rash + dormitory outbreak = Neisseria meningitidis; the conjugate vaccine does NOT cover serogroup B.

rows: Fever, neck stiffness, PURPURIC rash, shock
Standard conjugate vaccine covers A/C/W/Y, NOT serogroup B

questions.stem: A 65-YEAR-OLD WOMAN, 1-day history of FEVER, HEADACHE and ALTERED MENTAL STATUS. TYPE 2 DIABETES on glipizide; HYPERTENSION on HYDROCHLOROTHIAZIDE. CONFUSED. T 38.9C, BP 104/66, PULSE 100, RESP 24. Neck supple, no rash. LEUKOCYTES 19,000; PLASMA GLUCOSE 120 mg/dL. CT head normal. CSF LEUKOCYTES 1300 (98% NEUTROPHILS), GLUCOSE 20 mg/dL, PROTEIN 200 mg/dL. CSF GRAM STAIN NEGATIVE. Dexamethasone begun. Q: Which of the following antimicrobial regimens should now be initiated?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Ceftriaxone alone covers pneumococcus and meningococcus but does NOT cover Listeria monocytogenes, which is a key pathogen in patients over 50.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Penicillin G is inadequate empirical cover and does not reliably treat resistant pneumococci; broader empirical cover is needed pending cultures.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: In adults OVER 50 (and the immunocompromised/diabetic), empirical bacterial meningitis therapy must add LISTERIA cover. The regimen is VANCOMYCIN (resistant pneumococcus) + CEFTRIAXONE (pneumococcus/meningococcus/Gram-negatives) + AMPICILLIN (Listeria monocytogenes). Dexamethasone is given with/before the first dose.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: This covers resistant pneumococcus and meningococcus but MISSES Listeria, which is essential in this >50, diabetic patient - add ampicillin.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Although co-trimoxazole covers Listeria, this combination lacks reliable cover of pneumococcus/meningococcus and is not the standard empirical regimen.

questions.keyPoint: Age >50 or immunocompromised with bacterial meningitis: add AMPICILLIN to vancomycin + ceftriaxone for Listeria.

rows: Fever, headache, altered mental status; neutrophilic low-glucose CSF
CSF: neutrophils, low glucose, high protein
LP, blood cultures, CSF Gram stain/PCR
Ampicillin is added over age 50 to cover Listeria

questions.stem: A 52-YEAR-OLD PREVIOUSLY HEALTHY WOMAN with BEHAVIOURAL ABNORMALITIES and APHASIA. Symptoms began 3 days ago with FEVER and HEADACHE. T 38.4C, MILD NUCHAL RIGIDITY and AGITATION. Repeats questions or responds with nonsense words. CT scan shows MILD TEMPORAL HYPODENSITY ON THE RIGHT. CSF examination shows 354 WBC with 75% LYMPHOCYTES. CSF PROTEIN ELEVATED at 167 mg/dL, but CSF GLUCOSE NORMAL at 112 (simultaneous peripheral glucose 142). Q: What is the most likely diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Pneumococcal meningitis gives a NEUTROPHILIC CSF with LOW glucose; this lymphocytic, normal-glucose CSF with focal TEMPORAL involvement is not bacterial.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Cryptococcus typically affects the immunocompromised, has very high opening pressure, and does not cause focal temporal-lobe encephalitis with aphasia in a healthy woman.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Enteroviral aseptic meningitis is usually benign without focal cortical signs; the prominent BEHAVIOURAL change, aphasia and temporal hypodensity indicate ENCEPHALITIS, not simple meningitis.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Listeria affects the elderly/immunocompromised and causes rhombencephalitis, not the classic focal TEMPORAL-LOBE pattern with personality change of HSV.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: FEVER + PERSONALITY/BEHAVIOURAL CHANGE + APHASIA + focal TEMPORAL-LOBE hypodensity + a LYMPHOCYTIC, NORMAL-GLUCOSE CSF is classic HERPES SIMPLEX ENCEPHALITIS, which has a predilection for the temporal lobes. Treat empirically with IV ACICLOVIR immediately; confirm with CSF HSV PCR.

questions.keyPoint: Fever + behavioural change/aphasia + temporal-lobe focus = HSV encephalitis; start IV aciclovir empirically.

rows: Fever, personality change, aphasia, focal temporal-lobe signs/seizures
CSF lymphocytosis, normal glucose, raised protein; temporal MRI changes
CSF HSV PCR; MRI brain; EEG
IMMEDIATE IV ACICLOVIR (do not wait for PCR)
Permanent cognitive deficit, death if untreated
Temporal-lobe involvement = HSV; empirical aciclovir is urgent

questions.topic: Bacterial meningitis - commonest organism in diabetic adult
questions.stem: A 61-YEAR-OLD MAN with FEVER, CHILLS and HEADACHE; FREQUENTLY TAKES ASPIRIN (says it helps). 20-YEAR HISTORY OF HYPERTENSION and POORLY CONTROLLED TYPE 2 DIABETES. Received recommended CHILDHOOD VACCINES. T 39C, BP 100. LETHARGIC, oriented to person, place and time. SEVERE NECK RIGIDITY with LIMITED ACTIVE AND PASSIVE RANGE OF MOTION. Blood cultures and a lumbar puncture performed. Q: Which of the following is the most likely causal organism?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Meningococcus typically causes epidemic disease in young people with a purpuric rash; it is not the single commonest cause of community-acquired bacterial meningitis in a diabetic adult.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Listeria must be COVERED in this age group, but it is not the SINGLE MOST COMMON cause overall; pneumococcus is more frequent even in diabetics.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: STREPTOCOCCUS PNEUMONIAE is the SINGLE MOST COMMON cause of community-acquired bacterial meningitis in adults of all ages, accounting for over half of cases, and remains the most common cause in DIABETIC patients despite diabetes increasing the risk of meningitis from other organisms.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Pseudomonas meningitis is uncommon, usually following neurosurgery, head trauma or in profound immunosuppression, not the typical community-acquired cause.

questions.keyPoint: Streptococcus pneumoniae is the commonest cause of community-acquired bacterial meningitis in adults, including diabetics.

rows: Fever, headache, severe neck rigidity in older diabetic adult
S. pneumoniae is the leading cause of adult bacterial meningitis Neurology MCQ

questions.stem: A 13-YEAR-OLD GIRL is brought to the physician because of WORSENING FEVER, HEADACHE, PHOTOPHOBIA, and NAUSEA for 2 DAYS. One week ago, she returned from summer camp. She has received all age-appropriate immunizations. Her TEMPERATURE is 39.1 C (102.3 F). She is oriented to person, place, and time. Physical examination shows a MACULOPAPULAR RASH. There is RIGIDITY of the neck, forced flexion of the neck results in involuntary flexion of the knees and hips. CSF studies: opening pressure 120 mm H2O, appearance clear, protein 47 mg/dL, glucose 68 mg/dL, white cell count 380/mm3, segmented neutrophils 15%, LYMPHOCYTES 85%. Q: Cerebrospinal fluid studies show which of the following is the most likely cause?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Mumps can cause aseptic meningitis but the patient is fully immunized (MMR) and has no parotitis, making mumps unlikely.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: A lymphocyte-predominant CSF (85% lymphocytes) with normal glucose, mildly raised protein and clear appearance is aseptic (viral) meningitis. Enteroviruses such as ECHOVIRUS (and coxsackievirus) are the commonest cause, classically in summer outbreaks in children/teenagers, and may produce a non-specific maculopapular rash with meningism.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Listeria causes bacterial meningitis (neutrophilic CSF, low glucose) in neonates, the elderly and immunocompromised, not a lymphocytic CSF in a healthy teenager.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: HSV typically causes encephalitis with altered mentation and temporal-lobe signs rather than benign aseptic meningitis with normal orientation.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Pneumococcal meningitis produces a high-neutrophil CSF with low glucose and high protein, opposite to this lymphocytic, normal-glucose profile.

questions.keyPoint: Lymphocytic CSF + normal glucose + clear fluid in a teenager after summer camp = enteroviral (Echovirus) aseptic meningitis.

rows: Fever, headache, photophobia, neck stiffness, maculopapular rash, summer
CSF: lymphocytes, normal glucose, mildly raised protein; enterovirus PCR

questions.stem: A previously healthy 1-YEAR-OLD BOY is brought to the emergency department because of IRRITABILITY and FEVER for 2 days. His symptoms began shortly after returning from a family trip to CANADA. He was born at term. Immunizations are up-to-date. His 6-year-old brother is healthy and there is no family history of serious illness. He is at the 50th percentile for height and 75th percentile for weight. TEMPERATURE is 39.2 C (102.5 F), pulse 110/min, respirations 28/min, BLOOD PRESSURE 92/55 mm Hg. Physical examination shows SEVERAL PURPLE SPOTS OVER THE TRUNK AND EXTREMITIES that are 1 mm in diameter (NON-BLANCHING PETECHIAE/PURPURA). Capillary refill time is 4 seconds. Examination of the skin shows NO abnormalities. CSF: leukocyte count 19,000/mm3, glucose DECREASED, neutrophils PREDOMINANT, INCREASED PROTEIN concentration. Q: Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Cefotaxime and vancomycin therapy for the patient and doxycycline prophylaxis for close contacts
choices.explanation: Empirical cefotaxime + vancomycin is appropriate, but the recommended chemoprophylaxis for close contacts of meningococcus is rifampin (or ciprofloxacin/ceftriaxone), not doxycycline.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Cefotaxime and vancomycin therapy for the patient and no prophylaxis for close contacts
choices.explanation: Meningococcal disease is highly transmissible, so close contacts DO need chemoprophylaxis; offering none is incorrect.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Ampicillin therapy for the patient and ciprofloxacin prophylaxis for close contacts
choices.explanation: Ampicillin alone does not provide adequate empirical cover for resistant pneumococcus; a third-generation cephalosporin (+ vancomycin) is required.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Ceftriaxone and vancomycin therapy for the patient and rifampin prophylaxis for close contacts
choices.explanation: A toddler with fever and a non-blanching petechial/purpuric rash plus a neutrophilic CSF with low glucose and high protein has acute bacterial (meningococcal) meningitis. Empirical treatment is a third-generation cephalosporin (ceftriaxone) PLUS vancomycin (to cover resistant pneumococcus). Because Neisseria meningitidis is highly contagious, close contacts receive chemoprophylaxis with RIFAMPIN (or ciprofloxacin/ceftriaxone).

choices.label: E
choices.text: Vancomycin therapy for the patient and rifampin prophylaxis for close contacts
choices.explanation: Vancomycin alone does not cover Neisseria meningitidis or gram-negatives; a third-generation cephalosporin must be included.

questions.keyPoint: Bacterial meningitis: ceftriaxone + vancomycin for the patient; RIFAMPIN (or cipro/ceftriaxone) prophylaxis for close contacts of meningococcus.

rows: Toddler, fever, non-blanching petechiae/purpura, prolonged cap refill
CSF: neutrophilic, low glucose, high protein; blood/CSF culture, PCR
Lumbar puncture, blood cultures, FBC, coagulation

questions.stem: A 45-YEAR-OLD MAN comes to the emergency department with a 3-DAY history of progressively worsening HEADACHE and NAUSEA. He started having CHILLS last night and could not fall asleep. He has not had any vomiting or diarrhea. He has a history of HYPERTENSION treated with ramipril. He works 60 hours per week as an investment banker and says that his job is stressful. He is sexually active with several female partners and uses condoms consistently. He drinks alcohol occasionally on the weekends and does NOT smoke or use illicit drugs. His immunizations are up-to-date. He appears EXHAUSTED. He is oriented to place and person but NOT to time. He becomes increasingly SOMNOLENT as one of the nurses takes his vital signs. His temperature is 39.5 C (103.1 F), pulse is 67/min, respirations are 17/min, and blood pressure is 127/77 mm Hg. Physical examination shows PHOTOPHOBIA and nuchal rigidity; KERNIG and BRUDZINSKI SIGNS are present. Fundoscopic examination shows no abnormalities. Blood samples are obtained for cultures and therapy with vancomycin and cefotaxime is initiated. Q: Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Before lumbar puncture in a patient with fever, meningism AND features of raised intracranial pressure or focal/altered consciousness (here progressive somnolence and disorientation), a CT head should be performed FIRST to exclude a mass lesion/raised ICP and reduce the risk of cerebral herniation. Empirical antimicrobials (here plus aciclovir for possible HSV encephalitis) are given before/while awaiting imaging; CT then guides safe LP.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: LP is essential but must NOT precede CT when there are signs of raised ICP or reduced consciousness, because of herniation risk; antibiotics should not be delayed for the scan.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: MRI is more sensitive for HSV temporal-lobe changes but is slower and less available than CT for the immediate exclusion of raised ICP before LP.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Angiography evaluates vascular lesions (e.g. aneurysm/vasculitis) and has no role in the acute work-up of meningoencephalitis.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: EEG may show temporal periodic discharges in HSV encephalitis but is not the immediate next diagnostic step before safely performing LP.

questions.keyPoint: If meningoencephalitis with reduced consciousness/raised-ICP signs: give empirical antibiotics + aciclovir, then CT head BEFORE lumbar puncture.

rows: CT head first (exclude raised ICP), then LP with HSV PCR
Blood cultures, CT head, LP/CSF HSV PCR, EEG/MRI
Herniation if LP done with raised ICP; HSV brain damage
CT before LP when reduced GCS / focal signs / raised ICP

questions.stem: A 16-DAY-OLD MALE NEWBORN is brought to the emergency department because of FEVER and POOR FEEDING for 2 days. He became very FUSSY the previous evening and cried for most of the night. He was born at 36 weeks' gestation and weighed 2430 g (5 lb 3 oz). The pregnancy and delivery were uncomplicated. The mother does not recall any sick contacts at home. He currently weighs 2776 g (6 lb 2 oz). He appears IRRITABLE. His TEMPERATURE is 38.6 C (101.5 F), pulse is 180/min, and blood pressure is 82/51 mm Hg. Examination shows scleral icterus. The ANTERIOR FONTANELLE FEELS SOFT AND FLAT. Neurologic examination shows NO abnormalities. Blood cultures are drawn and fluid resuscitation is initiated. Q: Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: MRI is not the first step in a febrile neonate; the priority is to obtain CSF to diagnose neonatal sepsis/meningitis.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Urine culture is part of a full septic screen but does not replace lumbar puncture, which is mandatory in a febrile neonate to exclude meningitis.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Any febrile neonate (especially with irritability and poor feeding) requires a full septic work-up including LUMBAR PUNCTURE, because neonatal meningitis (GBS, E. coli, Listeria) is common, often presents with non-specific signs, and a soft flat fontanelle does not exclude it. CSF analysis guides diagnosis and antibiotic therapy; the fontanelle being soft/flat means LP is not contraindicated by raised ICP.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: CT exposes the neonate to radiation and is not needed before LP when the fontanelle is soft and flat with a normal neurological exam.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: A chest x-ray may be part of the sepsis screen but does not address the key need to sample CSF for meningitis.

questions.keyPoint: Fever in a neonate mandates a full septic screen INCLUDING lumbar puncture to exclude neonatal meningitis.

rows: Neonate, fever, poor feeding, irritability, soft flat fontanelle
Lumbar puncture + CSF analysis (with blood/urine cultures)
Full septic screen: blood, urine, CSF cultures
Febrile neonate needs LP as part of septic work-up Epilepsy

questions.topic: Provoked seizure from hyponatraemia - no antiepileptic needed
questions.stem: A 67-YEAR-OLD WOMAN is admitted because of a witnessed GENERALIZED SEIZURE associated with URINARY and FAECAL INCONTINENCE and followed by POSTICTAL CONFUSION. She has recently been started on HYDROCHLOROTHIAZIDE for essential hypertension and is found to have a SERUM SODIUM LEVEL of 114 mEq/L. The neurological examination is nonfocal. Once therapy with 3% HYPERTONIC SALINE is begun, the patient has no further neurologic events after the serum sodium concentration is CORRECTED. She is now ready for discharge and has a SERUM SODIUM LEVEL of 136 mEq/L. Her HYPERTENSION has responded to an ANGIOTENSIN-CONVERTING ENZYME INHIBITOR. Q: What antiepileptic drug regimen should be started? (match the scenario to the most appropriate option)

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: IV lorazepam aborts an ongoing/prolonged seizure (status epilepticus); the seizure here has already terminated with sodium correction, so it is not indicated.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Loading fosphenytoin is used for status epilepticus or to start ongoing therapy, neither of which is needed for a single provoked, fully reversible seizure.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Long-term carbamazepine is unnecessary for a provoked seizure; moreover carbamazepine can itself cause hyponatraemia (SIADH), which would be counterproductive here.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Maintenance phenytoin treats unprovoked recurrent epilepsy; a single seizure from a corrected metabolic cause does not warrant chronic antiepileptic therapy.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Chronic levetiracetam is not indicated for a provoked seizure that resolved with correction of the underlying electrolyte disturbance.

choices.label: F
choices.explanation: This was an ACUTE SYMPTOMATIC (PROVOKED) seizure caused by severe hyponatraemia from thiazide. Once the sodium was corrected (now 136 mEq/L) and the provoking cause removed, there is no ongoing epilepsy. Provoked seizures from a reversible metabolic disturbance do not require long-term antiepileptic drugs; the correct answer is NO TREATMENT (avoid the offending thiazide).

questions.keyPoint: A provoked seizure from a corrected, reversible cause (e.g. hyponatraemia) does NOT need long-term antiepileptic therapy.

rows: GTC seizure with Na 114, resolves after correction to normal
Correct sodium, stop thiazide; NO antiepileptic
Osmotic demyelination if Na corrected too fast
Provoked seizure from reversible cause = no chronic AED

questions.stem: A 20-YEAR-OLD WOMAN presents to the emergency department after a witnessed seizure. She is a college student and had been aware most of the previous night STUDYING for her FINAL EXAMINATIONS. On the morning of admission she suffered a GENERALIZED SEIZURE in the college cafeteria. There was NO warning and NO evidence of focal weakness when she was evaluated by the EMTs. The patient denies a history of seizures and denies recreational drug or alcohol use. Her father, however, has been on an unknown antiepileptic drug for many years. The patient does note that she often has MUSCLE TWITCHES (myoclonic jerks) IN THE MORNING so severe that she has DROPPED OBJECTS. NEUROLOGICAL examination and an MRI scan are both NORMAL. Complete blood count and electrolytes are NORMAL. Q: What antiepileptic drug regimen should be recommended? (match the scenario to the most appropriate option)

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: IV lorazepam is acute abortive therapy for an ongoing seizure/status, not long-term maintenance for juvenile myoclonic epilepsy.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Loading fosphenytoin treats status epilepticus; moreover phenytoin/sodium-channel agents can WORSEN the myoclonus and absences of JME.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Carbamazepine is contraindicated in JME because it can aggravate myoclonic and absence seizures.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Phenytoin is a narrow-spectrum sodium-channel drug that can exacerbate the generalized seizures of JME and is not appropriate.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Morning myoclonic jerks (dropping objects), a generalized tonic-clonic seizure provoked by sleep deprivation, a positive family history, onset in adolescence/young adulthood and a normal MRI define JUVENILE MYOCLONIC EPILEPSY. It needs a BROAD-SPECTRUM antiepileptic; LEVETIRACETAM is an appropriate first-line oral maintenance choice, particularly in a young woman of childbearing potential (avoiding the teratogenicity of valproate).

choices.label: F
choices.explanation: JME is a chronic epilepsy syndrome that generally requires long-term antiepileptic therapy to prevent recurrent generalized seizures; withholding treatment is inappropriate.

questions.keyPoint: Juvenile myoclonic epilepsy (morning myoclonus, GTC after sleep deprivation, normal MRI): broad-spectrum AED - levetiracetam (or valproate; avoid valproate in women). AVOID carbamazepine/phenytoin.

rows: Teen/young adult, morning myoclonic jerks, GTC after sleep deprivation, normal MRI
Broad-spectrum AED - levetiracetam (or valproate; avoid in women)
Recurrent seizures if untreated; valproate teratogenicity
Avoid carbamazepine/phenytoin (worsen JME); use broad-spectrum AED

questions.topic: Status epilepticus - next step after benzodiazepines
questions.stem: A 17-YEAR-OLD GIRL is brought to accident and emergency with a GENERALIZED TONIC-CLONIC SEIZURE. Her mother had found her fitting in her bedroom about 20 MINUTES ago. The ambulance crew handover state that her fits started 96 PER CENT ON 1.5 L OF OXYGEN and they have given her TWO DOSES OF RECTAL DIAZEPAM but she has NOT STOPPED FITTING. Q: What is the most appropriate management?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Although lorazepam is an effective first-line benzodiazepine and may be more effective than diazepam, this patient has ALREADY received two doses of a benzodiazepine, so the next step is to move on to a second-line agent rather than give more benzodiazepine.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Phenobarbital can be considered if phenytoin fails, but phenytoin (the next-line agent) should be loaded first.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: General anaesthesia and intubation are reserved for refractory status epilepticus after second-line drugs have failed, not as the immediate next step.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: ITU may ultimately be needed, but the immediate medical priority is to load the patient with an IV antiepileptic (phenytoin) rather than simply summon intensive care.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: This is convulsive status epilepticus (continuous/recurrent seizure >5 minutes, here ~20 minutes) that has NOT responded to two doses of a benzodiazepine. The next step is to load with an intravenous second-line agent - IV PHENYTOIN (phenytoin loading) - with cardiac monitoring. If seizures persist, phenobarbital and then general anaesthesia/intubation with ITU support are escalated.

questions.keyPoint: Status epilepticus still fitting after 2 doses of benzodiazepine: load IV PHENYTOIN (second-line). Escalate to phenobarbital, then anaesthesia/ITU if refractory.

rows: GTC seizure >5 min, still fitting after 2 benzodiazepine doses
Benzodiazepine first; then IV phenytoin loading; then phenobarbital/GA
After 2 doses of benzodiazepine, give IV phenytoin (not more benzo)

questions.stem: A 72-YEAR-OLD MAN with KNOWN EPILEPSY and HYPERTENSION is admitted with PNEUMONIA. His drug history includes ASPIRIN, PHENYTOIN, BENDROFLUMETHIAZIDE and AMLODIPINE. His HEART RATE is 67, BLOOD PRESSURE 170/93, SATS 96 PER CENT on 2 L of OXYGEN. NEUROLOGICAL examination is NORMAL. His doctor requests BLOOD TESTS INCLUDING PHENYTOIN LEVEL. Q: What is the correct indication for this test?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Phenytoin levels are not checked routinely in a stable, asymptomatic patient with no clinical concern; monitoring should be indication-driven.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: There are no clinical features of phenytoin toxicity (nystagmus, ataxia, drowsiness) described, so excluding toxicity is not the primary indication here.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Phenytoin has a narrow therapeutic index and non-linear (zero-order) kinetics, so levels are checked for specific reasons. In a patient admitted with another illness, a key valid indication for measuring the level is to ASSESS/CONFIRM COMPLIANCE (adherence) with therapy, as well as to guide dose adjustment and check for toxicity when clinically suspected. Confirming compliance is the best-fit indication in this scenario.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Seizure control is judged clinically; a level is used to support dosing decisions, but in this stable known epileptic the principal reason is checking adherence rather than titrating to a target.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Drug levels are ordered for clinical decision-making, not simply to reassure the patient.

questions.keyPoint: Phenytoin (narrow therapeutic index, zero-order kinetics) levels are checked for cause - assessing COMPLIANCE, suspected toxicity, or dose adjustment - not routinely.

rows: Known epileptic on phenytoin, admitted with another illness
Serum phenytoin level (consider free level if low albumin)
Adjust dose per level and clinical picture
Check level to confirm compliance / toxicity, not routinely

questions.stem: A 71-YEAR-OLD MAN with ATRIAL FIBRILLATION is seen in clinic following an episode of SYNCOPE. He describes getting a POOR NIGHT'S SLEEP and, as he got out of bed in the morning, feeling DIZZY for a couple of seconds before the lights dimmed around him. He was woken a couple of seconds later by his wife who had WITNESSED THE EVENT. She says he went pale and fell to the floor and his ARMS and LEGS JERKED. After waking, he was shaken but was BACK TO NORMAL a few minutes after the event. His medication includes ASPIRIN, RAMIPRIL and FRUSEMIDE. Q: What is the most likely diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Vasovagal episodes are typically triggered by prolonged standing, pain or emotion with prodromal nausea/sweating, not by standing up from bed in a patient on antihypertensives and a diuretic.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Syncope on standing up from lying, with momentary dizziness/dimming of vision then a brief faint and rapid full recovery, in an elderly man taking RAMIPRIL and FRUSEMIDE (volume depletion + vasodilatation) is classic ORTHOSTATIC (postural) HYPOTENSION. The brief limb jerks are convulsive (anoxic) movements from transient cerebral hypoperfusion, NOT epilepsy; there is no post-ictal confusion.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Cardiogenic syncope (e.g. arrhythmia) is usually sudden and exertional or unprovoked, not clearly precipitated by standing; the strong postural trigger and culprit drugs point to orthostatic hypotension.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: A TIA causes focal neurological deficits (weakness, speech/visual disturbance), not a brief faint with rapid complete recovery and no focal signs.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: True seizure typically has no clear postural trigger and is followed by post-ictal drowsiness/confusion; the brief jerks here are convulsive syncope from hypoperfusion, and recovery was rapid and complete.

questions.keyPoint: Faint on standing + culprit drugs (ACE-I, diuretic) + brief jerks with rapid full recovery = orthostatic hypotension (convulsive syncope), not epilepsy.

rows: Syncope on standing, dizziness, brief anoxic jerks, rapid recovery, on ACE-I + diuretic
Postural BP, medication review, ECG (exclude arrhythmia)
Review/reduce offending drugs, rise slowly, fluids
Convulsive syncope = brief jerks, no post-ictal confusion (not epilepsy)

questions.topic: Seizure semiology - complex partial (focal impaired-awareness) seizure
questions.stem: A 23-YEAR-OLD WOMAN is seen in clinic for RECURRENT FUNNY TURNS that she has had over the past few months, but her family and friends have noticed them. They say she LOOKS AROUND BLANKLY, then STARTS PICKING AT HER CLOTHES and SOMETIMES YAWNS; then she comes back after a MINUTE (AUTOMATISMS). She can get DROWSY after these episodes (POST-ICTAL DROWSINESS). Q: What seizure type does this patient describe?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Absence seizures occur typically in children, are very brief (seconds) with abrupt onset/offset, no automatisms of this complexity and NO post-ictal drowsiness.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: A tonic-clonic (grand mal) seizure involves loss of consciousness with a tonic then clonic phase and convulsive jerking, not the staring and purposeless automatisms described here.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: In a simple partial (focal aware) seizure, consciousness is PRESERVED; this patient has impaired awareness (looks blankly, does not remember), so it is not simple partial.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Recurrent episodes starting focally with a behavioural arrest/blank stare, IMPAIRED AWARENESS, repetitive purposeless AUTOMATISMS (picking at clothes, lip-smacking, yawning) lasting about a minute, followed by POST-ICTAL DROWSINESS, are classic for a COMPLEX PARTIAL (focal impaired-awareness) seizure, typically arising from the temporal lobe. The impaired awareness plus automatisms and post-ictal state distinguish it from simple partial and absence seizures.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: A primarily generalized seizure involves both hemispheres from onset with widespread motor manifestations; this focal-onset event with automatisms and impaired awareness is a complex partial seizure that may later secondarily generalize.

questions.keyPoint: Blank stare + automatisms (picking/lip-smacking) + impaired awareness + post-ictal drowsiness = COMPLEX PARTIAL (focal impaired-awareness) seizure, usually temporal lobe.

rows: Behavioural arrest, automatisms (picking, lip-smacking), impaired awareness, post-ictal drowsy
Automatisms + impaired awareness + post-ictal state = complex partial

questions.topic: Unwitnessed seizure work-up in older adult - MRI brain
questions.stem: A 68-YEAR-OLD MAN is seen in the emergency room after an UNWITNESSED SYNCOPAL EPISODE. His wife heard a STRANGE NOISE and found him CONFUSED and ON THE FLOOR of the living room where he had been WATCHING TELEVISION. His wife tells you that he has NO ongoing medical problems, does NOT take any medications, and does NOT use ALCOHOL or ILLICIT DRUGS. On examination the patient is DROWSY, has a TONGUE LACERATION, and his pants are WET WITH URINE. Serum electrolytes (including SODIUM and CALCIUM) are NORMAL and URINE DRUG SCREEN is NEGATIVE. Q: Which of the following is the best next step in evaluation?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Tongue laceration, urinary incontinence and post-event confusion/drowsiness indicate an unwitnessed SEIZURE rather than simple syncope. A first unprovoked seizure in an OLDER adult (with normal electrolytes and a negative drug screen) is worrisome for an underlying structural brain lesion (e.g. tumour, stroke, old infarct). Neuroimaging with MRI brain (more sensitive than CT) is the appropriate next step to look for a structural cause.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: LP is reserved for suspected CNS infection or subarachnoid haemorrhage; there are no meningism or febrile features here to justify it as the next step.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Ambulatory ECG investigates arrhythmic (cardiac) syncope, but the tongue laceration, incontinence and post-ictal state point to seizure, making neuroimaging the priority.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: CT is reasonable acutely to exclude bleed, but for identifying a structural epileptogenic lesion in a new-onset seizure MRI is the more sensitive and appropriate next investigation.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Echocardiography assesses structural cardiac causes of syncope; the clinical features here indicate a seizure, so brain imaging is preferred.

questions.keyPoint: Tongue biting + incontinence + post-event confusion = seizure; a first unprovoked seizure in an older adult needs MRI brain to find a structural cause.

rows: Unwitnessed collapse, tongue laceration, incontinence, post-ictal confusion
Clinical seizure; MRI to seek structural lesion
Treat underlying cause; consider AED after evaluation
New seizure in older adult -> MRI brain for structural lesion

questions.stem: A previously healthy 5-YEAR-OLD BOY is brought to the emergency department by his parents because of a 1-DAY HISTORY OF HIGH FEVER. His TEMPERATURE prior to arrival was 40.0 C (104 F). There is NO family history of serious illness. Development has been appropriate for his age. He is administered RECTAL PARACETAMOL. While in the waiting room, he becomes UNRESPONSIVE and STARTS JERKING HIS ARMS AND LEGS (generalized convulsion). A fingerstick BLOOD GLUCOSE concentration is 86 mg/dL. After 5 MINUTES, he CONTINUES HAVING JERKY MOVEMENTS and is UNRESPONSIVE to verbal and painful stimuli (ongoing seizure / status). Q: Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: IV valproate is a second-line agent used if benzodiazepines fail to stop status; it is not the first drug given for an actively convulsing child.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Fosphenytoin/phenytoin is a SECOND-LINE agent for established status epilepticus after first-line benzodiazepines, not the initial drug.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: A child with a febrile seizure that has continued beyond ~5 minutes has (febrile) STATUS EPILEPTICUS. The first-line abortive drug is a BENZODIAZEPINE; with IV access, IV LORAZEPAM (0.1 mg/kg) is given because benzodiazepines enhance GABA-mediated inhibition and rapidly terminate seizures. If the seizure persists, second-line agents (fosphenytoin/valproate) are escalated.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Phenobarbital is a later-line option (or used in neonates); it is not the first drug in this child when a benzodiazepine is the appropriate initial therapy.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Lamotrigine is a maintenance oral antiepileptic with no role (and no rectal formulation) in the acute termination of status epilepticus.

questions.keyPoint: Actively convulsing child with IV access (status/prolonged febrile seizure): first-line is a BENZODIAZEPINE - IV lorazepam (rectal/buccal/IM midazolam if no IV).

rows: Young child, high fever, generalized convulsion lasting >5 min, normal glucose
Glucose (done), seek source of fever; consider LP per age/features
Benzodiazepine (IV lorazepam) is first-line for status epilepticus

questions.stem: A 13-YEAR-OLD BOY, GENERALISED TONIC-CLONIC SEIZURE that occurred while attending a LASER LIGHT SHOW (photosensitivity). Mother says he 'OFTEN DAYDREAMS' (absences). Over the past several months he has reported recurrent episodes of JERKY MOVEMENTS OF HIS FINGERS AND ARMS, usually SHORTLY AFTER WAKING UP IN THE MORNING (morning myoclonus). No loss of consciousness during these episodes. Q: Which of the following is the most appropriate treatment for this patient's condition?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Carbamazepine can AGGRAVATE the myoclonic and absence seizures of JME and is not appropriate first-line.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Diazepam is a rescue/acute drug, not maintenance therapy for an epilepsy syndrome.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Morning myoclonus + photosensitive GTCS + absences are classic JUVENILE MYOCLONIC EPILEPSY. VALPROATE (sodium valproate) is the most effective first-line agent for the multiple seizure types of JME (controls myoclonic, absence and GTCS); levetiracetam is an alternative, especially in women of childbearing potential.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Ethosuximide treats ABSENCE seizures only and does not control the myoclonic or tonic-clonic seizures of JME.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Phenytoin can worsen myoclonus and is not first-line for generalised epilepsy syndromes like JME.

questions.keyPoint: Valproate is first-line for juvenile myoclonic epilepsy (covers myoclonic, absence and GTCS).

rows: Lifelong epilepsy; worsened by Na-channel blockers
Valproate controls all three JME seizure types

questions.stem: A 10-YEAR-OLD BOY, ED 15 minutes after a seizure. Mother reports he complained of SUDDEN NAUSEA and SEEING 'SHINY LIGHTS', then TWITCHING STARTING AT THE CORNER OF HIS MOUTH and spreading to the face, then he dropped to the floor unconscious and JERKED HIS ARMS AND LEGS for about 2 minutes. On the way to hospital he regained consciousness; CONFUSED and could not speak clearly for 5 minutes. Had a fever and sore throat 1 week ago (otitis/ear infection) which improved with acetaminophen. Lethargic, postictal, normal exam. Q: Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: The seizure BEGAN FOCALLY (aura of nausea/visual phenomena, then facial twitching starting at the corner of the mouth) and then SPREAD to a bilateral convulsion with loss of consciousness and postictal confusion. This is a FOCAL SEIZURE WITH SECONDARY GENERALISATION (focal to bilateral tonic-clonic).

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: A focal dyscognitive (complex partial) seizure impairs awareness with automatisms but does NOT generalise to a bilateral tonic-clonic convulsion as seen here.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Myoclonic seizures are brief shock-like jerks without a focal onset, aura or prolonged convulsion/postictal phase.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: A primary GTCS has NO focal onset/aura; this seizure clearly started focally (aura + facial twitching) before generalising.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Convulsive syncope has brief jerks after a faint with rapid recovery and no focal aura or prolonged postictal confusion.

questions.keyPoint: A focal aura/onset that then becomes a bilateral convulsion = focal to bilateral tonic-clonic seizure.

rows: Focal aura/onset (Jacksonian march) then bilateral convulsion + postictal confusion
Aura/focal onset distinguishes secondarily generalised from primary GTCS Peripheral neuropathies

questions.stem: A 31-YEAR-OLD WOMAN, A&E, PROGRESSIVE DIFFICULTY WALKING with LOWER BACK PAIN. A few days ago she was TRIPPING over things, now DIFFICULTY CLIMBING STAIRS. TINGLING and NUMBNESS in both hands moved up to her ELBOWS; difficulty writing. CRANIAL NERVES INTACT but ABSENT SENSATION TO VIBRATION and PINPRICK in upper limbs to the elbow and lower limbs to the hip. POWER 3/5 in the ankles and 4/5 at the hip. ABSENT REFLEXES and MUTE PLANTARS. BP 124/85, pulse 68, sats 98%. PAST FOOD POISONING A MONTH OR TWO AGO. Q: What is the diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: MS is a CNS disease causing UMN signs (brisk reflexes, upgoing plantars); GBS gives an ASCENDING LMN picture with AREFLEXIA, the opposite reflex pattern.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: ASCENDING symmetrical weakness with AREFLEXIA and sensory loss, a few weeks after a GASTROINTESTINAL INFECTION (Campylobacter food poisoning), is classic GUILLAIN-BARRE SYNDROME, an acute inflammatory demyelinating polyradiculoneuropathy. The mute (absent) plantars and global areflexia reflect LMN/root involvement. Monitor vital capacity for respiratory failure.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: MG causes fatigable weakness with preserved reflexes and sensation; it does not cause areflexia or sensory loss.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Diabetic neuropathy is chronic and length-dependent, not an acute ascending paralysis with areflexia after an infection.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: While post-infectious, the specific eponymous diagnosis for this acute ascending areflexic polyneuropathy is Guillain-Barre syndrome.

questions.keyPoint: Ascending weakness + areflexia + post-Campylobacter = Guillain-Barre syndrome; watch the vital capacity.

rows: CSF albuminocytologic dissociation; nerve conduction studies
LP (high protein, normal cells); NCS; spirometry (FVC)
IVIG or plasma exchange; supportive/ventilatory care
Often follows Campylobacter; monitor FVC for respiratory failure

questions.stem: A 45-YEAR-OLD DIABETIC MALE, lower limb peripheral neurological examination shows NORMAL TONE, 5/5 POWER, NORMAL PLANTARS and PROPRIOCEPTION. He DOES NOT RESPOND TO ANY SENSORY STIMULUS ON THE MEDIAL SIDE OF THE RIGHT LOWER LEG. Q: Which dermatome is affected?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: L1 supplies the inguinal/groin region, not the medial lower leg.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: L2 covers the upper anterior thigh, not the medial leg below the knee.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: L3 supplies the lower anterior thigh/knee region, not the medial lower leg.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: The L4 dermatome covers the MEDIAL ASPECT OF THE LOWER LEG (and medial malleolus). Sensory loss over the medial lower leg localises to L4 (the L4 nerve root also mediates the knee jerk).

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: L5 supplies the lateral leg and dorsum of the foot/great toe, not the medial lower leg.

questions.keyPoint: Medial lower leg sensation = L4 dermatome (also the knee-jerk root).

rows: L4 = medial leg/medial malleolus; L5 = dorsum of foot; S1 = lateral foot/sole

questions.stem: On examination, a patient has 5/5 POWER IN THE UPPER LIMBS but reduced power in the LOWER LIMBS. Further examination reveals a SENSORY LEVEL AT THE UMBILICUS. CRANIAL NERVES ARE INTACT. Q: Where is the lesion?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: C4 corresponds to the shoulders/clavicle region; a cord lesion there would affect the upper limbs, which are normal here.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: T4 corresponds to the NIPPLE line, which is above the umbilicus.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: The UMBILICUS is the T10 dermatome. A SENSORY LEVEL AT THE UMBILICUS with normal upper limbs and lower-limb weakness localises a spinal cord lesion to approximately T10.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: L1 corresponds to the inguinal/groin region (below the umbilicus).

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: L3 corresponds to the knee/anterior thigh, well below the umbilical level.

questions.keyPoint: Sensory level landmarks: nipple = T4, umbilicus = T10, inguinal = L1.

rows: Lower-limb weakness with a sensory level at the umbilicus

questions.stem: A 53-YEAR-OLD WOMAN, 1-WEEK history of PARAESTHESIAS that began SYMMETRICALLY IN THE FEET and progressed to involve the DISTAL LEGS AND HANDS. UNSTEADY WALKING, LOWER LIMB WEAKNESS, difficulty getting up stairs. No pain or bowel/bladder impairment. Vital signs normal. WEAKNESS OF DISTAL LOWER EXTREMITY with STOCKING-GLOVE SENSORY LOSS and AREFLEXIA. Plantar responses normal, gait unsteady. ESR, creatinine, CK and liver tests normal. CXR normal. Q: Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: ALS produces mixed UMN/LMN motor signs WITHOUT sensory loss; the prominent sensory paraesthesias and areflexia here are not ALS.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Diabetic polyneuropathy is chronic and slowly progressive; a rapidly evolving (1-week) ascending sensorimotor neuropathy with areflexia is GBS.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: A RAPIDLY PROGRESSIVE (1 week) symmetrical ascending SENSORIMOTOR neuropathy with STOCKING-GLOVE sensory loss and AREFLEXIA is GUILLAIN-BARRE SYNDROME. Normal ESR/CK/CXR exclude inflammatory myopathy and other mimics; confirm with CSF albuminocytologic dissociation and nerve conduction studies.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: A myelopathy (cord lesion) gives UMN signs (hyperreflexia, upgoing plantars) and a sensory level, not the global areflexia and stocking-glove pattern seen here.

questions.keyPoint: Rapid (days-weeks) ascending sensorimotor neuropathy with areflexia = Guillain-Barre syndrome.

rows: Areflexia + rapid ascending course is the hallmark

questions.stem: A 35-YEAR-OLD WOMAN, 5-MONTH history of RIGHT-HAND NUMBNESS and TINGLING involving the ENTIRE HAND, WORSE WHEN DRIVING OR HOLDING A BOOK/NEWSPAPER, and WAKING HER AT NIGHT. NO neck pain or weakness. Normal strength. SENSORY LOSS IN THE FIRST THREE DIGITS AND THE RADIAL HALF OF THE FOURTH DIGIT in the right hand. Q: Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Median nerve compression at the wrist gives sensory symptoms in the MEDIAN distribution - THUMB, INDEX, MIDDLE and RADIAL HALF OF THE RING FINGER - that are WORSE AT NIGHT and provoked by sustained gripping (driving, holding a book/newspaper, Phalen's manoeuvre). This is CARPAL TUNNEL SYNDROME.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: De Quervain causes radial-sided wrist PAIN over the abductor pollicis longus/extensor pollicis brevis tendons (positive Finkelstein), not median sensory loss.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Ganglia present as a localised swelling and only occasionally compress a nerve; they do not cause this classic nocturnal median distribution numbness.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Ulnar compression affects the LITTLE FINGER and ulnar half of the ring finger, the opposite distribution to this median-territory complaint.

questions.keyPoint: Nocturnal numbness in the thumb-index-middle-radial ring finger = median nerve (carpal tunnel syndrome).

rows: Median-distribution numbness/tingling, worse at night, provoked by gripping
Median territory spares the little finger; symptoms wake the patient at night

questions.stem: A 50-YEAR-OLD MAN, SLOWLY PROGRESSIVE WEAKNESS over several months affecting WALKING and his HANDS. NO sensory, bowel or bladder complaints; no problems with thinking, speech or vision. DISTAL MUSCLE WEAKNESS, WASTING and FASCICULATIONS, plus UPPER MOTOR NEURON SIGNS including EXTENSOR PLANTAR REFLEXES and HYPERREFLEXIA IN WASTED MUSCLE GROUPS. Remainder of the neurological examination is unremarkable. Q: Which of the following tests is most likely to be abnormal in this patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Cerebrospinal fluid white blood cell count
choices.explanation: ALS does not cause a CSF pleocytosis; CSF is typically normal.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: ALS spares the sensory system, so SENSORY nerve conduction studies are NORMAL - a key feature distinguishing it from peripheral neuropathy.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Brain CT is normal in ALS; the disease is a motor neuron degeneration not a structural brain lesion.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: ALS combines UMN signs (hyperreflexia, extensor plantars) and LMN signs (wasting, fasciculations) WITHOUT sensory involvement. ELECTROMYOGRAPHY shows widespread DENERVATION and FIBRILLATION/fasciculation potentials with preserved nerve conduction velocities, supporting the diagnosis of motor neuron disease.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Thyroid studies and vitamin B12 level
choices.explanation: These are checked to exclude mimics but are normal in true ALS; EMG is the test that demonstrates the disease.

questions.keyPoint: ALS = mixed UMN + LMN signs with NO sensory loss; EMG shows widespread denervation with normal sensory studies.

rows: Mixed UMN/LMN signs, fasciculations, wasting, NO sensory loss
Sensory studies are NORMAL in ALS; EMG shows denervation

questions.stem: A 66-YEAR-OLD WOMAN, STIFFNESS and WEAKNESS CLIMBING STAIRS. History of HYPERTENSION and DIET-CONTROLLED TYPE 2 DIABETES. MILD UPPER ARM WEAKNESS, HIP FLEXION 4-/5 bilaterally, BILATERAL WASTING and FLICKERS OF FASCICULATIONS IN THE RIGHT QUADRICEPS, knee extension 4/5. Dorsiflexion and plantar flexion strong. BRISK KNEE AND ANKLE REFLEXES, plus POSITIVE HOFFMAN'S AND BABINSKI'S SIGN. SENSORY EXAMINATION AND CRANIAL NERVES NORMAL. BM 8.9, pulse regular, BP 178/97. Q: What is the most likely diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: MG causes fatigable weakness with normal reflexes and no wasting/fasciculations or UMN signs.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Diabetic neuropathy is predominantly sensory and length-dependent; here sensation is NORMAL and there are mixed UMN/LMN motor signs.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Inflammatory myopathy causes proximal weakness with raised CK but NOT fasciculations, brisk reflexes or positive Babinski.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: The combination of LMN signs (wasting, FASCICULATIONS) and UMN signs (BRISK reflexes, positive HOFFMAN'S and BABINSKI'S) with COMPLETELY NORMAL SENSATION and cranial nerves is the signature of MOTOR NEURONE DISEASE (amyotrophic lateral sclerosis). The mix of UMN and LMN in the same limbs with no sensory loss is the key clue.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: MS is a CNS demyelinating disease giving UMN and sensory signs disseminated in time/space; it does not cause LMN wasting and fasciculations.

questions.keyPoint: Mixed UMN (brisk reflexes, Babinski) and LMN (wasting, fasciculations) signs with normal sensation = motor neurone disease.

rows: Combined UMN and LMN signs, fasciculations, NO sensory loss
UMN + LMN signs together with intact sensation = MND

questions.stem: A 66-YEAR-OLD MAN, 5-DAY history of PROGRESSIVE TINGLING and NUMBNESS OF HANDS AND FEET; was perfectly fine a week ago. MILD SYMMETRICAL DISTAL WEAKNESS, MILD GAIT ATAXIA and DYSDIADOCHOKINESIA. Smokes 30 cigarettes/day, drinks 1-2 bottles of wine; family history of hypertension; mother has type 2 diabetes with foot numbness/burning. He SELF-DISCHARGES; a week later symptoms have peaked with MODERATE DISTAL WEAKNESS and NUMBNESS TO THE KNEES, and SYMPTOMS START TO SLOWLY RESOLVE. Q: What is the diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Miller Fisher variant of GBS is the triad of OPHTHALMOPLEGIA, ATAXIA and AREFLEXIA (anti-GQ1b); this man's predominant picture is a peripheral sensorimotor polyneuropathy without ophthalmoplegia.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Alcoholic neuropathy is chronic and slowly progressive over months-years, not an acute few-day course that peaks within ~2 weeks and then resolves.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: CIDP by definition evolves over MORE THAN 8 WEEKS (chronic); this acute monophasic illness peaking within days-weeks is GBS, not CIDP.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: CMT is a hereditary, very slowly progressive neuropathy from childhood with foot deformity; it does not present acutely over days.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: An ACUTE MONOPHASIC sensorimotor polyneuropathy that develops over days, PEAKS WITHIN ~2 WEEKS (by definition <4 weeks) and then begins to RESOLVE is GUILLAIN-BARRE SYNDROME. The nadir within 4 weeks is the key feature separating GBS from CIDP.

questions.keyPoint: Acute neuropathy reaching its nadir within 4 weeks then improving = GBS; >8 weeks = CIDP.

rows: Acute monophasic ascending sensorimotor neuropathy, nadir <4 weeks
GBS reaches nadir within 4 weeks; CIDP progresses beyond 8 weeks Parkinson's disease

questions.stem: A LEFT-HANDED 79-YEAR-OLD MAN presents with a troublesome RESTING TREMOR of his left hand. The tremor is evident in his writing. He has also noticed his writing is SMALLER than it used to be (MICROGRAPHIA). He complains he has DIFFICULTY TURNING IN BED to get comfortable and his wife complains he sometimes kicks her in the middle of the night (REM sleep behaviour). When he gets out of bed in the morning he feels a little WOOZY, but this resolves after a while.
