On examination, he blinks about three times a minute and his face does not show much emotion (HYPOMIMIA). GLABELLAR TAP IS POSITIVE. He has a slow, SHUFFLING GAIT. He has difficulty stopping, starting and turning. He holds his feet slightly apart to steady himself. When you pull him backwards, he is unable to right himself and stumbles back (POSTURAL INSTABILITY). Q: Which of the signs and symptoms is NOT commonly associated with parkinsonism?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: The Parkinsonian gait is typically narrow-based and shuffling, NOT broad-based. Postural instability is in fact a feature of advanced PD (the patient fails the pull test). The trick here is the question asks which is NOT commonly associated; postural instability IS associated, but the option that is genuinely not parkinsonian is the broad-based gait. NOTE: the file marks A as the answer in error-prone wording; the explanation in the file states the parkinsonian gait is narrow-based, so the truly non-parkinsonian feature is a broad-based gait.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: REM sleep behaviour disorder is a recognised premotor/associated feature of PD; the patient kicking his wife at night reflects this, so it IS associated.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Hypomimia (masked facies, reduced blinking) is a classic parkinsonian sign, so it IS associated.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Broad-based gait is a CEREBELLAR sign, not parkinsonian (PD gait is narrow-based/shuffling); by standard teaching this is the option NOT associated with parkinsonism.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Autonomic dysfunction (orthostatic hypotension, constipation) is common in PD and Parkinson-plus syndromes, so it IS associated.

questions.keyPoint: PD gait is narrow-based and shuffling; a broad-based gait points to cerebellar disease, not parkinsonism.

rows: Resting tremor, rigidity, bradykinesia, postural instability
PD gait is shuffling/festinating, not broad-based

flag.note: FILE ANSWER vs STANDARD TEACHING — File says: A Standard (Davidson's Principles & Practice of Medicine; Bailey & Love): Broad-based gait (D) is the feature NOT associated with parkinsonism; it is cerebellar. PD gait is narrow-based and shuffling. Exam advice: Match the file key (A) if graded against it; standard MCQs expect D (broad-based gait).
flag.source: Study guide flag (file key vs standard teaching)

questions.stem: A 47-YEAR-OLD DENTIST consults because of TREMOR which is interfering with his work. The tremor has come on gradually over several years and seems more prominent AFTER THE INGESTION OF CAFFEINE; he notices that, in the EVENING after work, an ALCOHOLIC BEVERAGE WILL DECREASE THE TREMOR. No one in his family has a similar tremor. He is otherwise healthy and takes no medications. On examination his vital signs are normal. Except for the tremor, his neurological examination is normal; in particular there is no focal weakness, rigidity, or bradykinesia. When he holds out his arms and extends his fingers, you detect a RAPID FINE TREMOR OF BOTH HANDS; the tremor goes away when he rests his arms at his side (ACTION/POSTURAL TREMOR). Q: What is the best next step in the management of this patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: MRI scan to visualize the basal ganglia
choices.explanation: Essential tremor is a clinical diagnosis; structural imaging of the basal ganglia is not indicated. MRI would be appropriate if there were focal or atypical features.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Electromyogram and nerve conduction studies to more fully characterize the tremor
choices.explanation: EMG/NCS assess peripheral nerve/muscle disorders, not a central postural tremor. Not helpful here.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: The clinical picture (symmetric postural/action tremor improved by alcohol, worsened by caffeine, no parkinsonian features) is classic ESSENTIAL TREMOR. First-line therapy is a non-selective beta-blocker (propranolol) or primidone. A therapeutic trial is the appropriate next step.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Primidone is also an effective first-line agent for essential tremor, but propranolol is typically the preferred first trial; primidone is an acceptable alternative, especially at higher doses.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Neurology referral to rule out motor neuron disease
choices.explanation: MND causes weakness/fasciculations, not an isolated symmetric action tremor; referral is unnecessary.

questions.keyPoint: Essential tremor: bilateral action/postural tremor, improves with alcohol, worsens with caffeine; first-line treatment is propranolol or primidone.

rows: Bilateral postural/action tremor, alcohol-responsive, positive family history often
Alcohol relieves essential tremor; propranolol is first-line

flag.note: FILE ANSWER vs STANDARD TEACHING — File says: (not marked in file) Standard (Davidson's; Katzung Pharmacology): Therapeutic trial of propranolol (C) - essential tremor is treated first-line with a beta-blocker; the dentist needs symptom control for fine motor work. Exam advice: Claude-derived - confirm against your answer key. Either C (propranolol) or D (primidone) is acceptable as first-line.
flag.source: Study guide flag (file key vs standard teaching)

questions.stem: A 74-YEAR-OLD WOMAN consults because of TREMOR and difficulty completing her daily tasks on time. She has hypertension and takes hydrochlorothiazide 25 mg every morning. She does not smoke and uses alcohol infrequently. On examination, her BP is 126/84; her vital signs are otherwise unremarkable. Eye movements are normal as are her reflexes and motor strength. She MOVES SLOWLY; her timed up-and-go (TUG) test TAKES 24 SECONDS (normal 10 seconds). She has a SLOW RESTING TREMOR with a frequency of about 3 per second; the tremor is more prominent on the right than the left. The TREMOR DECREASES WITH INTENTIONAL MOVEMENT. Her handwriting has deteriorated and is small and crabbed. Q: Which therapy is most likely to improve her functional disabilities?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Switching her antihypertensive to propranolol 20 mg po bid
choices.explanation: Propranolol treats essential tremor, not the resting tremor/bradykinesia of PD. It would not improve her parkinsonian disability.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Anticholinergics help tremor in younger PD patients but are poorly tolerated and risky in the elderly (confusion); not the best choice for functional improvement here.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: A benzodiazepine does not treat PD and risks sedation/falls in an elderly woman.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Ropinirole beginning at a dose of 0.25 mg tid
choices.explanation: A dopamine agonist is an option in younger patients, but in an elderly patient with significant functional disability, levodopa is more effective and better tolerated.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Carbidopa/levodopa beginning at a dose of one-half of a 25 mg/100 mg tablet tid
choices.explanation: She has Parkinson's disease (asymmetric resting tremor, bradykinesia, micrographia, slow TUG). Levodopa/carbidopa is the MOST EFFECTIVE drug for controlling motor symptoms and is preferred in elderly patients with substantial functional disability.

questions.keyPoint: Levodopa/carbidopa is the most effective treatment for PD motor symptoms and is preferred in elderly patients with significant disability.

rows: Asymmetric resting tremor, bradykinesia, rigidity, micrographia
Motor fluctuations, dyskinesias with chronic levodopa
Levodopa is the most effective drug; preferred in elderly/disabled

questions.stem: A 59-YEAR-OLD WOMAN comes to the physician because of PROGRESSIVELY WORSENING COORDINATION AND INVOLUNTARY MOVEMENTS in her left hand for the past 6 months. Her husband also reports that she has been WITHDRAWN AND APATHETIC during this period. She is oriented to time, place, and person. Examination shows a BIMANUAL, RHYTHMIC, LOW-FREQUENCY TREMOR that is more prominent in the left hand. There is normal range of motion in the arms and legs; active movements are very slow (BRADYKINESIA). Muscle strength is normal, and there is INCREASED RESISTANCE TO PASSIVE FLEXION AND EXTENSION in the limbs (RIGIDITY). She walks with a SHUFFLING GAIT and takes small steps. Q: Which of the following is the most likely underlying cause of this patient's symptoms?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Accumulation of neurotoxic metabolites secondary to hepatocyte damage
choices.explanation: This describes hepatic encephalopathy/Wilson's; would have liver dysfunction and a different tremor (asterixis/wing-beating), not this picture.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Copper accumulation due to mutations in hepatocyte copper-transporting ATPase
choices.explanation: Describes Wilson's disease; typically presents in younger patients with Kayser-Fleischer rings and liver disease, not a 59-year-old with classic PD.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Neuronal degeneration due to alpha-synuclein protein misfolding
choices.explanation: This is Parkinson's disease. The pathological hallmark is degeneration of dopaminergic neurons in the substantia nigra with intracytoplasmic LEWY BODIES, which are aggregates of misfolded ALPHA-SYNUCLEIN protein. This causes the resting tremor, rigidity, bradykinesia, and shuffling gait.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Cerebellar ischemia due to chronic hypertension
choices.explanation: Cerebellar lesions cause an intention tremor and ataxia, not a resting tremor with rigidity and bradykinesia.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Proliferation of beta-adrenergic receptors from excessive circulating T4
choices.explanation: Hyperthyroidism causes a fine postural tremor, not bradykinesia/rigidity/shuffling gait.

questions.keyPoint: Parkinson's disease results from loss of nigral dopaminergic neurons; Lewy bodies are misfolded alpha-synuclein aggregates.

rows: Resting tremor, rigidity, bradykinesia, shuffling gait, apathy
Lewy bodies = misfolded alpha-synuclein in substantia nigra

questions.stem: A 67-YEAR-OLD MAN comes to the physician because of a worsening TREMOR that began 1 year ago. The tremor affects his LEFT HAND and IMPROVES when he uses his hand to complete a task. He also reports feeling STIFFER throughout the day, and he has FALLEN TWICE in the past year. No change in cognition or mood. Not had difficulty sleeping, but his wife says he would KICK AND PUNCH WHILE DREAMING for almost a decade (REM sleep behaviour). He drinks two cans of beer daily. Takes no medications. Appears well nourished. Vital signs within normal limits. The patient maintains a BLANK STARE throughout the visit. Q: Further evaluation is most likely to show which of the following?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: This is Parkinson's disease (asymmetric resting tremor that improves with action, rigidity, falls, masked facies, REM sleep behaviour disorder). BRADYKINESIA manifests as progressive REDUCTION IN AMPLITUDE and speed of repetitive movements such as foot tapping, finger tapping, or opening/closing the hand.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Startle myoclonus suggests prion disease (CJD), not PD.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Chorea is seen in Huntington's disease, not Parkinson's disease.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: He has clear parkinsonian signs; the exam will not be normal.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Gaze nystagmus suggests cerebellar/vestibular pathology or intoxication, not idiopathic PD.

questions.keyPoint: Bradykinesia in PD = progressive decrease in amplitude and speed of repetitive movements (e.g., foot/finger tapping).

rows: Asymmetric rest tremor, rigidity, bradykinesia, RBD, masked facies, falls
Bradykinesia confirmed on repetitive movement testing
Bradykinesia = decremental amplitude on repetitive tapping

questions.stem: A 47-YEAR-OLD MAN is brought to the office by his wife because of UNUSUAL BEHAVIOR for the past 2 weeks. His wife reports that the patient spent a large sum of money at the local casino and bought a new car without consulting her. He has been sleeping poorly. He says, 'This is very unusual for him.' Yesterday, he also tried to FLIRT WITH HIS WIFE'S BEST FRIEND (HYPERSEXUALITY). His medications are PROPRANOLOL, ROPINIROLE, and budesonide nasal spray. He does not smoke cigarettes, drink alcohol, or use illicit drugs. He is 178 cm tall, weighs 82 kg; BMI 26 kg/m2. Vital signs within normal limits. When asked about his mood, the patient replies, 'I feel fine. I just had to buy that car; she's such a beauty. I want to get out of here to take her for a ride!' Remainder of the examination shows no abnormalities. Q: Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: He is not a danger to himself or others; hospitalization is not warranted for a medication side effect that resolves on dose reduction.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Antipsychotics are not the treatment; this is a drug-induced impulse control disorder, not primary psychosis, and dopamine-blockers can worsen the underlying PD.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Dopamine agonists such as ropinirole commonly cause IMPULSE CONTROL DISORDERS (pathological gambling, hypersexuality, compulsive shopping). The appropriate management is to REDUCE OR TAPER THE DOPAMINE AGONIST, after which symptoms usually resolve.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: CBT does not address the pharmacological cause; the disorder resolves on lowering the dopamine agonist.

questions.keyPoint: Dopamine agonists (ropinirole, pramipexole) cause impulse control disorders; manage by reducing/tapering the agonist.

rows: Dopamine agonists cause impulse control disorders

questions.stem: A 76-YEAR-OLD WOMAN with Parkinson disease comes to the physician with worsening TREMOR, SHUFFLING GAIT, and UNSTEADINESS despite taking LEVODOPA/CARBIDOPA combination medication three times daily. Neurologic examination shows a high frequency resting tremor of both hands, markedly slowed finger- and toe-tapping, and rigidity of the upper extremities. Her gait is slow, with shortened stride length. SELEGILINE is added to her medication regimen. Q: Which of the following is the most likely mechanism of action of this drug?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Blockade of central methylation of levodopa
choices.explanation: This describes COMT inhibitors (entacapone/tolcapone), which block methylation (O-methylation) of levodopa, not selegiline.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Increased release of dopamine from central neurons
choices.explanation: This is the mechanism of amantadine, not selegiline.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Inhibition of central oxidative deamination of dopamine
choices.explanation: Selegiline is a MONOAMINE OXIDASE-B (MAO-B) INHIBITOR. It blocks the oxidative deamination of dopamine in the CNS, increasing dopamine availability and prolonging its action. It is used as an adjunct or for early/mild PD.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Decreased peripheral conversion of levodopa to dopamine
choices.explanation: This is the action of carbidopa (a peripheral dopa-decarboxylase inhibitor), not selegiline.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Decreased peripheral conversion of levodopa to dopamine
choices.explanation: Duplicate distractor describing carbidopa; not selegiline's central MAO-B mechanism.

questions.keyPoint: Selegiline is an MAO-B inhibitor; it blocks central oxidative deamination of dopamine.

rows: Selegiline = MAO-B inhibitor (blocks dopamine breakdown centrally)

questions.topic: First-line PD therapy in younger patient
questions.stem: A 55-YEAR-OLD WOMAN comes to the physician because of a 6-MONTH HISTORY of worsening INVOLUNTARY MOVEMENT of the left hand. She also reports that it takes her LONGER THAN USUAL TO GET UP from a chair. Her symptoms are worse when she feels STRESSED at work. She has no history of serious illness and takes no medications. Neurological examination shows DIFFICULTY INITIATING MOVEMENT and a TREMOR in the left hand AT REST. The tremor DECREASES when the patient is asked to draw a circle (resting tremor abolished by voluntary action). Q: Which of the following is the most appropriate pharmacotherapy?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: A benzodiazepine does not treat the bradykinesia/resting tremor of PD.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Methimazole treats hyperthyroidism; irrelevant here.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Propranolol treats essential (action) tremor, but this is a RESTING tremor of PD, so it would not help.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: An anticholinergic can help PD tremor in younger patients but does not address bradykinesia, and a non-ergot dopamine agonist is the typical first-line choice in patients under 65.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: This is early Parkinson's disease (asymmetric resting tremor abolished by action, bradykinesia) in a relatively YOUNG patient (<65). A NON-ERGOT DOPAMINE AGONIST such as PRAMIPEXOLE (or ropinirole) is the typical first-line treatment in younger patients to delay levodopa-related motor complications.

questions.keyPoint: In younger PD patients (<65), a non-ergot dopamine agonist (pramipexole/ropinirole) is typical first-line to delay levodopa complications.

rows: Pramipexole/ropinirole (non-ergot dopamine agonist) first-line in young
Dopamine agonist first-line in young PD; levodopa in elderly

questions.stem: A 58-YEAR-OLD WOMAN comes to the physician because of a 6-MONTH HISTORY of DIFFICULTY WALKING, clumsiness of her arms and legs, and SLURRED SPEECH. Physical examination shows MASKED FACIES, slow, SHUFFLING GAIT. When her ankles are passively flexed, there is INVOLUNTARY, JERKY RESISTANCE (cogwheel rigidity). Treatment is initiated with a combination of LEVODOPA AND CARBIDOPA. Q: The addition of carbidopa is most likely to decrease the risk of which of the following potential adverse drug effects?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Carbidopa does not specifically reduce tremor as an adverse effect; the tremor is a disease feature, not a side effect.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Carbidopa inhibits PERIPHERAL dopa-decarboxylase, reducing the peripheral conversion of levodopa to dopamine. This MITIGATES PERIPHERAL SIDE EFFECTS such as ORTHOSTATIC HYPOTENSION, nausea, and vomiting, while allowing more levodopa to reach the CNS.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Hallucinations are a central effect of dopaminergic therapy (CNS), not reduced by carbidopa, which acts peripherally.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Dyskinesia is a central effect of chronic levodopa; carbidopa does not prevent it (and by increasing central levodopa may contribute).

questions.keyPoint: Carbidopa blocks peripheral dopa-decarboxylase, reducing peripheral side effects (orthostatic hypotension, nausea, vomiting).

rows: Masked facies, shuffling gait, cogwheel rigidity
Carbidopa reduces peripheral side effects of levodopa

questions.stem: A 72-YEAR-OLD WOMAN comes to the physician because she is SEEING THINGS THAT SHE KNOWS ARE NOT THERE. Sometimes she sees a DOG in her kitchen and at other times she sees a STRANGER in her garden, both of which no one else can see (VISUAL HALLUCINATIONS). She also reports a LACK OF MOTIVATION to do daily tasks for the past week. Three years ago, she was diagnosed with PARKINSON DISEASE and was started on LEVODOPA and CARBIDOPA. Her younger brother has schizophrenia. The patient also takes LEVOTHYROXINE for hypothyroidism. She used to drink a bottle of wine every day, but she STOPPED DRINKING ALCOHOL 2 MONTHS AGO. Neurologic examination shows a mild resting tremor of the hands and bradykinesia. Her thought process is organized and logical. Q: Which of the following is the most likely underlying cause of this patient's symptoms?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: LEVODOPA, by overstimulating central D2 receptors, commonly causes VISUAL HALLUCINATIONS, especially in elderly patients on long-term/high-dose therapy. With organized thought and PD on long-standing levodopa for 3 years, her hallucinations are most likely a levodopa adverse effect.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Alcohol withdrawal hallucinations occur days after cessation, not 2 months later; she stopped alcohol 2 months ago, making this unlikely.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: New-onset schizophrenia at age 72 is very unlikely, and her thought process is organized and logical; the family history is a distractor.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Apathy is present, but isolated visual hallucinations with preserved insight and organized thought are not explained by depression.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Hypothyroidism causes fatigue/cognitive slowing, not vivid visual hallucinations.

questions.keyPoint: Visual hallucinations in an elderly PD patient on long-term levodopa are most often a levodopa adverse effect.

rows: Visual hallucinations, preserved insight, long-term levodopa, elderly
Reduce levodopa; consider quetiapine/pimavanserin if persistent
Levodopa causes visual hallucinations, especially in the elderly Multiple sclerosis

questions.stem: A 23-YEAR-OLD WOMAN complains that her RIGHT LEG has become progressively STIFF AND CLUMSY over the last couple of weeks. She is worried she has not been able to go to work for the last 4 days. On examination, TONE IS INCREASED and there is a CATCH AT THE KNEE. She has SIX BEATS OF CLONUS and an UPGOING PLANTAR. Power is reduced to 3-4/5 in the right leg flexors. There is NO SENSORY INVOLVEMENT and the rest of the neurological exam is normal other than a PALE DISC on ophthalmoscopy. On further questioning, she admits she has had TWO EPISODES OF BLURRED VISION IN HER RIGHT EYE in the last two years, each lasting a couple of weeks from which she fully recovered (dissemination in time and space). Q: What is the most appropriate initial treatment?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: NSAIDs do not treat an MS relapse.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Interferon-beta is a disease-modifying drug to PREVENT relapses, not the initial treatment of an acute relapse.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Bed rest does not shorten a relapse.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Methotrexate is not standard MS relapse therapy.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: An acute MS RELAPSE is treated with high-dose CORTICOSTEROIDS (oral or IV methylprednisolone), which speed recovery from the relapse. This patient has UMN signs and prior optic neuritis (dissemination in time and space).

questions.keyPoint: Acute MS relapses are treated with high-dose corticosteroids (oral or IV) to speed recovery.

rows: UMN limb signs, clonus, upgoing plantar, prior optic neuritis
Clinical + MRI; lesions disseminated in time and space
Steroids treat relapses; they do not change long-term outcome

questions.stem: A 42-YEAR-OLD WOMAN presents with ATAXIA. Gadolinium-enhanced MRI reveals multiple subcortical white matter lesions as well as enhancing lesions in the cerebellum and spinal cord. She is diagnosed with MS. TWO MONTHS LATER she develops OPTIC NEURITIS. Q: What feature is associated with a milder disease course?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Older age at onset is generally associated with a worse, more progressive course, not a milder one.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Cerebellar/motor presentation is associated with a worse prognosis; sensory or optic onset is more favourable.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: FEMALE GENDER is associated with a MILDER MS disease course. Other favourable prognostic features include younger age at onset, sensory or optic neuritis onset, complete recovery from relapses, and long intervals between relapses.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The interval between the two episodes of two months
choices.explanation: A short interval between relapses indicates more active disease and a worse prognosis.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: A high lesion load with enhancing/infratentorial lesions indicates more active disease, a worse prognostic sign.

questions.keyPoint: Favourable MS prognostic factors: female sex, young onset, sensory/optic onset, full recovery, long inter-relapse interval.

rows: Female sex confers a milder MS course

questions.stem: A 26-YEAR-OLD WOMAN presents for FOLLOW-UP of her MULTIPLE SCLEROSIS. She has had two SEPARATE EPISODES of OPTIC NEURITIS and recent STUTTERING PROGRESSIVE WEAKNESS in two lower extremities. She has a mild neurogenic bladder. Her symptoms have been STABLE OVER THE PAST 4 MONTHS. MRI reveals several plaques in the PERIVENTRICULAR WHITE MATTER (MRI scan shown in the following figure) and several smaller plaques in the brainstem. Q: What is the best next step in her management?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Intravenous methylprednisolone 1 g daily for 3 days
choices.explanation: For an ACUTE RELAPSE/active worsening of MS, the treatment is high-dose IV CORTICOSTEROIDS (methylprednisolone), which hasten recovery. Given recent progressive weakness and optic neuritis, this is the best next step.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Cyclophosphamide is not a standard first-line MS treatment; reserved for aggressive refractory cases.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Oral anticholinergics for the urinary incontinence and observation of the demyelinating process
choices.explanation: Anticholinergics treat the neurogenic bladder symptomatically but ignore the active demyelinating relapse that requires steroids.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Interferon-beta is disease-modifying (prevents future relapses) but does not treat the current acute relapse.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Mitoxantrone is reserved for aggressive/worsening MS; not the immediate management of an acute relapse.

questions.keyPoint: Acute MS relapse with progressive deficit = IV methylprednisolone.

rows: Recurrent optic neuritis, progressive leg weakness, periventricular plaques
IV steroids for acute relapse; DMT for prevention

questions.stem: A 30-YEAR-OLD WOMAN comes to the physician because of INCREASING WEAKNESS in both legs for 7 days. She also reports a TINGLING SENSATION in her legs that is EXACERBATED BY TAKING A HOT SHOWER (Uhthoff phenomenon). She is sexually active with one male partner and does not use condoms. Her temperature is 37.8 C (100.1 F). Physical examination shows DECREASED MUSCLE STRENGTH AND CLONUS in both lower extremities. PATELLAR REFLEX IS 4+ BILATERALLY and PLANTAR REFLEX shows an EXTENSOR RESPONSE on both sides. ABDOMINAL REFLEX IS ABSENT. An MRI of the brain is shown. Q: Which of the following findings is most likely to further support the diagnosis in this patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Positive antibody response to cardiolipin-cholesterol-lecithin antigen in the serum
choices.explanation: This is the VDRL/syphilis test; would support neurosyphilis, not MS.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Oligodendrocytes that stain positive for polyomavirus proteins on brain biopsy
choices.explanation: This describes PML (JC virus), a complication of immunosuppression, not MS itself.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Presence of immunoglobulin bands on cerebrospinal fluid immunofixation
choices.explanation: CSF OLIGOCLONAL BANDS (intrathecal IgG production not present in serum) are highly sensitive and support a diagnosis of MS. This patient has UMN signs and heat sensitivity classic for MS.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Identification of gram-positive cocci in groups on blood culture
choices.explanation: Suggests staphylococcal infection/abscess, not MS.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Detection of high-affinity antibodies to dsDNA in serum
choices.explanation: Anti-dsDNA is specific for SLE; not MS.

questions.keyPoint: CSF oligoclonal bands (intrathecal IgG) support a diagnosis of MS.

rows: Bilateral UMN leg weakness, Uhthoff phenomenon, clonus, extensor plantars
Oligoclonal bands in CSF (not serum) support MS

questions.stem: A 33-YEAR-OLD WOMAN comes to the physician for a ROUTINE HEALTH MAINTENANCE examination. She feels well. She was diagnosed with multiple sclerosis ONE YEAR AGO. She has had TWO EXACERBATIONS since then, each lasting 1 week and each requiring hospitalization for corticosteroid treatment. Her most recent exacerbation was THREE WEEKS AGO. She has had no neurologic symptoms BETWEEN exacerbations. She takes a multivitamin and a calcium supplement daily. Vital signs within normal limits. Examination, including neurologic examination, shows no abnormalities. Q: Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in pharmacotherapy?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Natalizumab is a second-line/escalation therapy (risk of PML); not the initial DMT for a patient with two relapses in mild RRMS.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Reserved for aggressive/worsening MS due to cardiotoxicity; not first-line.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Steroids treat acute relapses; she is currently well (relapse was 3 weeks ago), so steroids are not the maintenance step to prevent future relapses.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: She has relapsing-remitting MS with frequent relapses and complete recovery between them. A first-line DISEASE-MODIFYING THERAPY such as INTERFERON-BETA (or glatiramer acetate, dimethyl fumarate, teriflunomide, fingolimod) is indicated to PREVENT future relapses.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: With two relapses in a year, she warrants disease-modifying therapy rather than supportive care alone.

questions.keyPoint: Relapsing-remitting MS with frequent relapses warrants a first-line DMT (interferon beta) to prevent relapses.

rows: Two relapses in a year, full recovery between, well now
DMT (interferon beta) prevents relapses; steroids only treat acute relapse

questions.stem: A 25-YEAR-OLD WOMAN comes to the physician because of INTERMITTENT PAINFUL DOUBLE VISION for the past 3 days. Symptoms occur only when looking sideways. She has MYOPIA and has been wearing corrective lenses for 10 years. THREE DAYS AGO, she lost her balance and FELL OFF HER BIKE, for which she went to a hospital. A CT scan of the head at that time showed no abnormalities and she was released without further treatment. Her only medication is an oral contraceptive. Vital signs within normal limits. Best corrected visual acuity is 20/40 in each eye. She has an ADDUCTION DEFICIT in the right eye and HORIZONTAL NYSTAGMUS in the left eye when looking left; she has an adduction deficit in the left eye and horizontal nystagmus in the right eye when looking right. CONVERGENCE TESTING shows NO ABNORMALITIES. Fundoscopy shows BILATERAL DISC HYPEREMIA. Q: Which of the following is the most likely cause of this patient's findings?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: This describes a Chiari malformation; would not cause bilateral INO with preserved convergence.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Demyelination of the medial longitudinal fasciculus
choices.explanation: BILATERAL INTERNUCLEAR OPHTHALMOPLEGIA (adduction deficit with contralateral abducting nystagmus, preserved convergence) is caused by a lesion of the MEDIAL LONGITUDINAL FASCICULUS (MLF). In a young woman, BILATERAL INO is highly suggestive of MULTIPLE SCLEROSIS.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Myasthenia gravis can mimic any ocular palsy but produces fatigable weakness, not the specific INO pattern with preserved convergence localizing to the MLF.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: A pontine tumour could cause INO but is far less likely than demyelination in a young woman with this presentation.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Oxidative damage due to thiamine deficiency
choices.explanation: Wernicke encephalopathy causes ophthalmoplegia (often abducens palsy) with confusion/ataxia, not isolated bilateral INO in a healthy young woman.

questions.keyPoint: Bilateral INO (adduction deficit + abducting nystagmus, preserved convergence) = MLF lesion; in a young person think MS.

rows: Adduction deficit, abducting eye nystagmus, preserved convergence, bilateral
INO = MLF lesion; bilateral INO in young = MS

questions.stem: A 35-YEAR-OLD WOMAN comes to the physician for the evaluation of increasing WEAKNESS and NUMBNESS of the UPPER EXTREMITIES for 5 days. During this time, she has also had URINARY INCONTINENCE not related to sneezing or laughing. She reports that LAST SUMMER during a vacation in Mexico she had WEAKNESS AND NUMBNESS of her right lower extremity that was WORSE WHEN SHE WAS OUTSIDE (heat); she REGAINED HER STRENGTH 3 WEEKS LATER. She has no history of serious illness. She has had 10 male sexual partners in her lifetime and uses condoms inconsistently. Vital signs within normal limits. Examination shows an IMPAIRED TANDEM GAIT, mild SPASTICITY, muscle strength decreased in both upper extremities, DEEP TENDON REFLEXES 4+ BILATERALLY, ABSENT ABDOMINAL REFLEX, and decreased SENSATION TO VIBRATION AND FINE TOUCH over the upper extremities. Q: Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: B12 deficiency (subacute combined degeneration) is a differential, but the relapsing-remitting, heat-sensitive, disseminated picture points to MS, for which MRI is the diagnostic test of choice.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: ANA screens for SLE; not the most appropriate next step for this clearly demyelinating presentation.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: RPR screens for syphilis; reasonable given sexual history but not the BEST next step over MRI for suspected MS.

choices.label: D
choices.text: MRI of the brain and spine
choices.explanation: The relapsing-remitting course (right leg last summer, now upper limbs), heat sensitivity, UMN signs and bladder involvement are classic for MS. MRI OF THE BRAIN AND SPINE is the investigation of choice to demonstrate demyelinating plaques disseminated in space and time.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: CSF oligoclonal bands support MS but MRI is the more appropriate first investigation; LP is used when MRI/clinical picture is inconclusive.

questions.keyPoint: MRI of the brain and spine is the investigation of choice to confirm MS (lesions disseminated in space and time).

rows: Relapsing deficits, heat sensitivity, UMN signs, bladder involvement
MRI is the investigation of choice for MS

questions.stem: A 33-YEAR-OLD WOMAN comes to the physician because of VISION IMPAIRMENT in her RIGHT EYE for the past 2 weeks. During this period, she was UNABLE TO DISTINGUISH COLORS with her right eye. She also reports PAIN WITH EYE MOVEMENT. One year ago, she had a similar episode that affected her left eye and resolved spontaneously. No history of serious illness. She works at a library and enjoys reading, even in poor lighting conditions. Vital signs within normal limits. The PUPILS ARE EQUAL, ROUND, AND REACTIVE TO LIGHT. VISUAL ACUITY is 20/50 in the left eye, 20/100 in the right eye. With spectacles, the visual acuity is 20/20 in the left eye and 20/100 in the right eye. Slit-lamp examination shows no abnormalities. A CT scan of the head shows no abnormalities. Q: Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Open-angle glaucoma causes painless, gradual peripheral field loss, not acute painful central vision/color loss in a young woman.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: RP causes progressive night blindness and peripheral field loss, not acute painful monocular vision loss.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Acute monocular VISION LOSS with PAIN ON EYE MOVEMENT, IMPAIRED COLOR VISION (red desaturation), and a prior contralateral episode is classic OPTIC NEURITIS, which is often the first manifestation of MS.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: AMD affects elderly patients with gradual central vision loss, not a young woman with painful acute loss.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Acute angle-closure presents with a painful RED eye, halos, fixed mid-dilated pupil and raised IOP, not painless reactive pupil with color loss.

questions.keyPoint: Optic neuritis: acute painful monocular vision loss with impaired color vision; frequently the first sign of MS.

rows: Painful monocular vision loss, red desaturation, RAPD
Optic neuritis = pain on eye movement + color desaturation; first sign of MS

questions.stem: A 30-YEAR-OLD WOMAN comes to the physician for the evaluation of intermittent DIZZINESS AND DOUBLE VISION. She reports double vision whenever she LOOKS TO THE LEFT. Last summer, she had an episode of WEAKNESS in her left leg that resolved spontaneously. Ophthalmologic examination shows WEAK ADDUCTION OF THE RIGHT EYE on left lateral gaze. Attempted left lateral gaze results in HORIZONTAL NYSTAGMUS OF THE LEFT EYE. Convergence testing is NORMAL. A schematic overview of a transverse section of the midbrain is shown with labeled areas A-E. Q: The patient most likely has a lesion in which of the following labeled areas of the schematic overview of a transverse section of the midbrain?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Does not correspond to the MLF; would not produce INO.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Does not correspond to the MLF location responsible for INO.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: The findings (weak adduction of the right eye on left gaze + abducting nystagmus of the left eye, preserved convergence) are an INTERNUCLEAR OPHTHALMOPLEGIA from a lesion of the MEDIAL LONGITUDINAL FASCICULUS, which corresponds to labeled area C on the midbrain schematic.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Incorrect anatomical location for the MLF.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Incorrect anatomical location for the MLF.

questions.keyPoint: The MLF connects the CN VI nucleus to the contralateral CN III nucleus; its lesion causes INO.

rows: Adduction failure + abducting nystagmus, preserved convergence
MLF lesion = INO; young patient = MS General neurology

questions.stem: A FEMALE presents with DIPLOPIA. On closer examination, when asked to LOOK RIGHT, her LEFT EYE STAYS IN THE MIDLINE but her RIGHT EYE MOVES RIGHT AND STARTS JERKING (nystagmus) - i.e. internuclear ophthalmoplegia. Q: What is the diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: MG can mimic ocular palsies but produces fatigable weakness; the specific INO pattern (failure of adduction of one eye with abducting nystagmus of the other) localizes to the MLF, suggesting MS.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Vertigo is a symptom of imbalance, not a cause of an adduction deficit with abducting nystagmus.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Cerebellar disease causes ataxia and gaze-evoked nystagmus, not an isolated adduction failure (INO).

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: The described INTERNUCLEAR OPHTHALMOPLEGIA (failure of adduction of the left eye with abducting nystagmus of the right on right gaze) is due to an MLF lesion. In a young patient this is classically caused by MULTIPLE SCLEROSIS.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Peripheral neuropathy affects limbs, not the central MLF pathway controlling conjugate gaze.

questions.keyPoint: INO (adduction failure + abducting nystagmus) in a young patient = MS.

rows: INO localizes to MLF; MS in the young

questions.stem: Direct knowledge question. ABSENT ANKLE JERKS (a lower motor neuron sign) combined with UP-GOING PLANTARS (an upper motor neuron sign) indicate a MIXED UMN/LMN picture. Classic causes include subacute combined degeneration (B12 deficiency), Friedreich's ataxia, conus medullaris/cord lesions (cord compression), motor neurone disease, and taboparesis. Q: Which of the following is NOT a cause of absent ankle jerks and up-going plantars?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Friedreich's ataxia classically causes absent ankle jerks with extensor plantars, so it IS a cause.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Subacute combined degeneration (B12 deficiency) causes the mixed picture of absent ankle jerks and upgoing plantars, so it IS a cause.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: MS is a CNS demyelinating disease and produces UPPER MOTOR NEURON signs (hyperreflexia, upgoing plantars); it does NOT typically cause absent (LMN) ankle jerks. Therefore MS is NOT a cause of the combined absent ankle jerks + upgoing plantars.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: A lesion at the conus/lumbosacral cord can damage roots (absent ankle jerks) and the cord above (upgoing plantars), so it IS a cause.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: MND combines UMN and LMN signs and is a classic cause of mixed absent reflexes with extensor plantars, so it IS a cause.

questions.keyPoint: Causes of absent ankle jerks + upgoing plantars: SCD (B12), Friedreich's, MND, conus lesion, taboparesis - NOT MS.

rows: MS is NOT a cause (it is purely UMN)

questions.stem: A 66-YEAR-OLD MAN is evaluated in the office for a 6-MONTH HISTORY of a RESTING ARM TREMOR. He says that his writing has gotten SMALLER during this time (MICROGRAPHIA) and that he has had difficulty buttoning his dress shirts. The patient reports NO PRIOR MEDICAL PROBLEMS and is not aware of any neurologic problems in his family. He takes no medications. Results of a general medical examination are normal. Neurologic examination shows a PAUCITY OF FACIAL EXPRESSION (HYPOMIMIA). CRANIAL NERVE FUNCTION IS NORMAL. Motor examination shows normal muscle strength but mild LEFT UPPER LIMB RIGIDITY and a 5-Hz RESTING TREMOR of the right upper limb. DEEP TENDON REFLEXES ARE NORMAL, as are results of sensory examination. There is NO TREMOR OR APPENDICULAR ATAXIA. DIMINISHED ARM SWING is noted bilaterally but is worse on the right. A TREMOR IN THE RIGHT UPPER LIMB is noted during ambulation. Left upper limb ALTERNATING MOTION RATES ARE DIMINISHED. Q: Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Cervical dystonia causes abnormal neck posturing, not a resting tremor with bradykinesia and rigidity.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Essential tremor is a bilateral action/postural tremor, not a resting tremor with rigidity, micrographia, and reduced arm swing.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Huntington's causes chorea and cognitive/psychiatric decline, not asymmetric resting tremor with rigidity.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Asymmetric RESTING TREMOR (5 Hz), RIGIDITY, BRADYKINESIA (diminished alternating motion rates, reduced arm swing), MICROGRAPHIA, and HYPOMIMIA are classic for PARKINSON DISEASE. The diagnosis is clinical.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: He takes no medications, so drug-induced parkinsonism (which is usually symmetric) is excluded.

questions.keyPoint: Asymmetric resting tremor + rigidity + bradykinesia + micrographia + hypomimia = idiopathic Parkinson disease.

rows: Asymmetric resting tremor, rigidity, bradykinesia, micrographia, hypomimia
Asymmetry + resting tremor distinguishes PD from essential tremor

questions.stem: A 62-YEAR-OLD MAN presents with several weeks of EXCRUCIATING STABBING PAIN in his RIGHT CHEEK. This pain occurs several times a day, lasts for a few seconds, and is so INTENSE that he often winces or cries out. Episodes of pain can sometimes be caused by TOUCHING THE FACE, or by BLOWING ON HIS FACE (trigger zones). Q: What is the most likely diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: A carotid aneurysm does not cause brief lancinating facial pain triggered by light touch.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Migraine causes throbbing headache lasting hours, not seconds-long electric-shock facial pains.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Brief, severe, STABBING/ELECTRIC-SHOCK facial pain in a trigeminal distribution, triggered by light touch or wind to the face (TRIGGER ZONES), is classic TRIGEMINAL NEURALGIA. First-line treatment is carbamazepine.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Glossopharyngeal neuralgia causes pain in the throat/ear triggered by swallowing, not the cheek triggered by touching the face.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: A tumour would cause progressive deficits/headache, not isolated paroxysmal trigger-induced facial pain with a normal exam.

questions.keyPoint: Trigeminal neuralgia: paroxysmal electric-shock facial pain in trigeminal distribution, triggered by touch/wind; treat with carbamazepine.

rows: Brief stabbing unilateral facial pain, trigger zones
Clinical; MRI to exclude secondary cause
Carbamazepine is first-line for trigeminal neuralgia

questions.stem: A 34-YEAR-OLD WOMAN is evaluated in the office for RIGHT-SIDED FACIAL PARALYSIS that she noticed ON AWAKENING 1 HOUR AGO. She has a 10-pack-year smoking history. Personal and family medical history is noncontributory. Her only medication is a daily oral contraceptive. On physical examination, vital signs are normal. Limb strength, reflexes, and tone are normal bilaterally. Findings from a sensory examination, which included her face, are also normal. When asked to RAISE HER EYEBROWS, the patient DOES NOT ELEVATE THE RIGHT SIDE. When asked to SHUT HER EYES, she cannot close the right one but the GLOBE ROTATES UPWARD, partially covering the iris (BELL PHENOMENON). When asked to smile, the patient does not move the right side of her face. Q: Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Graves causes proptosis/lid retraction and eye movement restriction, not unilateral facial muscle paralysis including the forehead.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: A cortical (UMN) lesion spares the forehead due to bilateral cortical innervation; this patient cannot raise her eyebrow, indicating an LMN lesion.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: INVOLVEMENT OF THE FOREHEAD (inability to raise the eyebrow) plus Bell phenomenon indicates a LOWER MOTOR NEURON facial (CN VII) palsy. With acute onset, normal limbs and sensation, this is idiopathic BELL'S PALSY.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Trigeminal neuralgia causes paroxysmal facial PAIN, not facial muscle weakness.

questions.keyPoint: Forehead involvement (cannot raise eyebrow) = LMN (Bell's) palsy; forehead sparing = UMN (stroke).

rows: Acute unilateral LMN facial weakness, forehead involved, Bell phenomenon
Forehead involvement distinguishes LMN (Bell) from UMN (stroke)

questions.stem: An obese 66-YEAR-OLD MAN has had INCREASING PAIN AND TINGLING IN HIS FEET for more than 8 MONTHS. He has not seen a physician in more than 20 years. His only other symptoms are FATIGUE, BLURRY VISION, and NOCTURIA. He takes no medications. On examination, vital signs are normal; BMI is 30. Results of skin, ophthalmoscopic, cardiopulmonary, and abdominal examinations are normal. On neurologic examination, he has SENSORY LOSS in the feet and distal legs (stocking distribution). Muscle strength and reflexes are normal. Q: Which of the following tests most likely diagnoses the cause of the neurologic findings?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: CK assesses myopathy; this is a sensory neuropathy with normal strength, so CK is not diagnostic.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: The stocking-distribution sensory loss with fatigue, blurry vision, nocturia, and obesity points to UNDIAGNOSED DIABETES MELLITUS causing a distal symmetric sensory polyneuropathy. A FASTING BLOOD GLUCOSE establishes the diagnosis.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Lumbar puncture and cerebrospinal fluid analysis
choices.explanation: LP is for CNS/inflammatory neuropathies (e.g., GBS); not indicated for a chronic distal diabetic-pattern neuropathy.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Nerve biopsy is reserved for atypical/vasculitic neuropathies; unnecessary when diabetes is the obvious cause.

questions.keyPoint: Distal symmetric stocking-glove sensory neuropathy in an obese patient with polyuria/blurry vision = diabetes; check fasting glucose.

rows: Stocking sensory loss, polyuria, blurry vision, obesity
Diabetes is the commonest cause of distal symmetric polyneuropathy

questions.stem: A 64-YEAR-OLD MAN is evaluated because of WEAKNESS and DIFFICULTY WEANING FROM MECHANICAL VENTILATION. He was admitted to the ICU 2 weeks ago because of SEPTIC SHOCK (alcoholism, pneumonia, Klebsiella bacteremia). He developed respiratory failure requiring intubation and mechanical ventilation as well as ACUTE KIDNEY INJURY. His pulmonary infiltrates responded to IV antibiotics and his hypotension responded to IV norepinephrine. Now he is alert and responsive to verbal commands, is afebrile, BP 114/74, but has NOT TOLERATED several trials of weaning from the ventilator. On exam he is cooperative. CRANIAL NERVES ARE NORMAL. Muscle strength is POOR, especially in DISTAL musculature (only 2/5 strength in hands and feet, proximal 3/5). ANKLE AND KNEE REFLEXES ARE UNOBTAINABLE. Sensory examination suggests DISTAL SENSORY LOSS in the lower extremities. CK 78 (normal <140), normocytic anemia, resolving leukocytosis. Q: What is the most likely cause of this weakness?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Muscle degeneration with loss of myosin in myocytes
choices.explanation: This describes critical illness MYOPATHY; here CK is normal and there is distal sensory loss with areflexia, pointing to a neuropathy rather than myopathy.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Persistent neuromuscular blockade due to aminoglycoside use
choices.explanation: Prolonged NMJ blockade is transient and would not cause distal sensory loss; the sensory involvement indicates an axonal neuropathy.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Thiamine depletion from intravenous glucose administration
choices.explanation: Thiamine deficiency causes Wernicke/wet beriberi, not this acute distal areflexic sensorimotor picture in the ICU.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Axonal degeneration of peripheral nerves with denervation potentials in myocytes
choices.explanation: This is CRITICAL ILLNESS POLYNEUROPATHY, an AXONAL degeneration of peripheral nerves arising in sepsis/multi-organ failure. It causes distal weakness, sensory loss, and areflexia with failure to wean; EMG shows denervation potentials and CK is normal.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Demyelination of peripheral nerves from unrecognized Guillain-Barré syndrome (GBS)
choices.explanation: GBS is a demyelinating ascending paralysis usually post-infectious; the ICU/sepsis context with axonal pattern favours critical illness polyneuropathy over GBS.

questions.keyPoint: Failure to wean after sepsis/multi-organ failure with distal weakness, areflexia, normal CK = critical illness (axonal) polyneuropathy.

rows: Sepsis/MOF, failure to wean, distal weakness, areflexia, normal CK
Axonal neuropathy of sepsis = critical illness polyneuropathy

questions.stem: A 30-YEAR-OLD MAN complains of BILATERAL LEG WEAKNESS and clumsiness of fine movements of the RIGHT HAND. FIVE YEARS AGO, he had an episode of TRANSIENT VISUAL LOSS. On physical examination, he is HYPERREFLEXIC with BABINSKI SIGN in the lower extremities and CEREBELLAR DYSMETRIA with poor finger-to-nose movement on the right. When asked to look to the right, his LEFT EYE DOES NOT MOVE NORMALLY PAST THE MIDLINE; NYSTAGMUS is noted in the ABDUCTING eye (INO). A more detailed history suggests the patient has had SEVERAL EPISODES OF GAIT DIFFICULTY that have resolved spontaneously. He has no systemic symptoms of fever or weight loss. Q: Which of the following is the most appropriate next test to order?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: CSF oligoclonal bands support MS but MRI is the more appropriate first test to demonstrate disseminated demyelinating lesions.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: The relapsing-remitting course (visual loss, gait episodes), UMN signs, INO and cerebellar findings indicate MULTIPLE SCLEROSIS. MRI WITH GADOLINIUM is the most appropriate test, demonstrating active (enhancing) and old plaques disseminated in space and time.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: CSF IgG/oligoclonal bands are supportive but secondary to MRI in the diagnostic workup.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Testing for oligoclonal bands in cerebrospinal fluid
choices.explanation: Oligoclonal bands support MS but are not the BEST next test; MRI is first.

choices.label: E
choices.text: CT scan of the head with intravenous-iodinated contrast
choices.explanation: CT is insensitive for demyelinating plaques; MRI is the imaging of choice.

questions.keyPoint: MRI with gadolinium is the investigation of choice for MS (shows lesions disseminated in space and time).

rows: Relapsing deficits, INO, UMN + cerebellar signs
Gadolinium MRI demonstrates active vs old plaques

questions.stem: A 20-YEAR-OLD WOMAN presents with WEAKNESS THAT IS WORSE IN THE AFTERNOON, worse during PROLONGED ACTIVITY, and IMPROVED BY REST. When fatigued, the patient is unable to HOLD UP HER HEAD or CHEW HER FOOD. She often notes DIPLOPIA when driving home from work. On physical examination, she has NO LOSS OF REFLEXES, SENSATION, OR COORDINATION. Q: Which of the following is the likely pathogenesis of this disease?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Autoantibodies directed against the postsynaptic acetylcholine receptor causing neuromuscular transmission failure
choices.explanation: FATIGABLE WEAKNESS worse with activity and improved by rest, with ocular (diplopia), bulbar (chewing) and neck involvement and normal reflexes/sensation, is MYASTHENIA GRAVIS. It is caused by AUTOANTIBODIES against the POSTSYNAPTIC ACETYLCHOLINE RECEPTOR, impairing neuromuscular transmission.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Destruction of anterior horn cells by virus
choices.explanation: Describes poliomyelitis, which causes flaccid paralysis with areflexia, not fatigable weakness.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Progressive muscular atrophy caused by spinal degeneration
choices.explanation: This describes a motor neuron disease variant, not an NMJ disorder with fatigability.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Demyelination (MS/GBS) produces UMN or LMN deficits with sensory/reflex changes, not pure fatigable NMJ weakness.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: A glycogen storage myopathy (McArdle) causes exercise-induced cramps, not fatigable ocular/bulbar weakness.

questions.keyPoint: Myasthenia gravis = antibodies to the postsynaptic ACh receptor causing fatigable weakness.

rows: Fatigable weakness, diplopia, ptosis, bulbar/neck weakness

questions.stem: Three weeks after an UPPER RESPIRATORY ILLNESS, a 25-YEAR-OLD MAN develops WEAKNESS of his legs that progresses over several days. On physical examination he has 4/5 strength in his arms but only 2/5 in the legs bilaterally. There is NO SENSORY DEFICIT, but KNEE AND ANKLE REFLEXES CANNOT BE ELICITED (areflexia). During a 2-day observation period the weakness ASCENDS, and he begins to notice increasing weakness of the hands. He notices mild tingling, but sensory examination continues to be normal. Q: The workup of this patient is most likely to show which of the following?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Acellular spinal fluid with high protein
choices.explanation: Post-infectious ASCENDING areflexic weakness is GUILLAIN-BARRÉ SYNDROME. The classic CSF finding is ALBUMINOCYTOLOGIC DISSOCIATION: elevated protein with a normal (acellular) cell count.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Abnormal electromyogram/nerve conduction velocity (EMG/NCV) showing axonal degeneration
choices.explanation: Classic GBS (AIDP) shows DEMYELINATING changes (conduction block, prolonged latencies), not primarily axonal degeneration.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: A positive Tensilon test indicates myasthenia gravis, not GBS.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Elevated CK indicates myopathy; GBS is a neuropathy with normal CK.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Respiratory alkalosis on arterial blood gas measurement
choices.explanation: GBS respiratory failure causes hypoventilation and respiratory ACIDOSIS, not alkalosis.

questions.keyPoint: GBS shows CSF albuminocytologic dissociation (high protein, normal cells).

rows: Post-infectious ascending areflexic weakness, normal sensation
High CSF protein with normal cell count

questions.stem: A 76-YEAR-OLD WOMAN presents with NUMBNESS and MILD WEAKNESS IN THE LEGS, slowly progressive over the PAST YEAR. She has noticed NUMBNESS IN THE FINGERTIPS bilaterally. She rarely goes to the doctor and takes no medications. Neurological examination shows SENSORY LOSS TO LIGHT TOUCH DISTAL TO THE KNEES AND WRISTS in a SYMMETRIC pattern (stocking-glove). JOINT POSITION AND VIBRATORY SENSATION ARE NORMAL. ANKLE REFLEXES ARE ABSENT, and she has mild distal weakness. Q: Which of the following is the most likely abnormality on laboratory testing?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: A chronic, SYMMETRIC, distal STOCKING-GLOVE sensory polyneuropathy with absent ankle reflexes in an elderly patient is most commonly DIABETIC. The expected lab abnormality is HYPERGLYCEMIA (undiagnosed diabetes mellitus, the most common cause of distal symmetric polyneuropathy).

choices.label: B
choices.text: Macrocytic anemia with a low vitamin B12 level
choices.explanation: B12 deficiency (subacute combined degeneration) typically impairs JOINT POSITION and VIBRATION sense (dorsal columns); here these are preserved, making B12 less likely.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Oligoclonal bands indicate MS, a CNS disease, not a length-dependent peripheral neuropathy.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Hypothyroid neuropathy is possible but far less common than diabetic neuropathy as the cause of distal symmetric polyneuropathy.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Anti-AChR indicates myasthenia gravis (fatigable weakness, no sensory loss), not a sensory neuropathy.

questions.keyPoint: Distal symmetric sensory polyneuropathy with preserved proprioception/vibration is most commonly diabetic - check glucose.

rows: Symmetric stocking-glove sensory loss, absent ankle jerks, preserved proprioception
Diabetes is the commonest cause of distal symmetric polyneuropathy

questions.stem: A 58-YEAR-OLD WOMAN has a history of ALCOHOL ABUSE, CORONARY ARTERY DISEASE, and ATRIAL FIBRILLATION. Her medications include METOPROLOL, LISINOPRIL, SIMVASTATIN, WARFARIN, and OXYCODONE. THREE DAYS LATER she develops a HEADACHE, DIZZINESS, VOMITING, and HAS DIFFICULTY WALKING. On neurological examination her strength, sensation (including vibratory sensation), and reflexes are normal. She walks with an UNCOORDINATED, UNSTEADY GAIT. On testing of coordination in the upper extremities, she displays POOR FINGER-NOSE-FINGER and POOR RAPID ALTERNATING MOVEMENTS with her right upper extremity (DYSMETRIA, DYSDIADOCHOKINESIA). In the lower extremities, her HEEL-SHIN TESTING also reveals POOR COORDINATION ON THE RIGHT. Her INR is 6.5 (normal <1, therapeutic for warfarin 2.0-3.0). Q: What is the most likely cause of her neurologic findings?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: OVER-ANTICOAGULATION (INR 6.5 on warfarin) with acute headache, vomiting, ataxia and right-sided cerebellar signs (dysmetria, dysdiadochokinesia, poor heel-shin) localizes to the RIGHT CEREBELLAR HEMISPHERE. Spontaneous cerebellar HEMORRHAGE is the most likely cause.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Multiple small infarcts in the basal ganglia
choices.explanation: Basal ganglia lacunes cause motor/sensory deficits, not isolated unilateral cerebellar signs; and the over-anticoagulation favours hemorrhage.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Cerebellar degeneration due to chronic alcohol abuse
choices.explanation: Alcoholic cerebellar degeneration is chronic, symmetric (midline/gait), and progressive - not an acute unilateral onset over 3 days.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Posterior column degeneration as a result of vitamin deficiency
choices.explanation: Vibration/proprioception are NORMAL here, excluding posterior column disease.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Combined effects of oxycodone and ethanol
choices.explanation: Sedative effects cause global depression, not focal unilateral cerebellar signs.

questions.keyPoint: Acute unilateral cerebellar signs in an over-anticoagulated patient (high INR) = cerebellar hemorrhage.

rows: Headache, vomiting, ataxia, unilateral dysmetria, high INR
Reverse anticoagulation; neurosurgical evacuation if large
Warfarin over-anticoagulation predisposes to cerebellar/intracerebral hemorrhage

questions.stem: A 72-YEAR-OLD WOMAN is found UNCONSCIOUS at home by her daughter. The daughter last spoke to her mother 1 day ago, at which time her mother seemed fine. The patient has DIABETES, HYPERTENSION, ATRIAL FIBRILLATION, and CHRONIC BACK PAIN. Her medications include METFORMIN, LISINOPRIL, WARFARIN, and OXYCODONE. On examination her BLOOD PRESSURE IS 167/70, PULSE 48 beats/min, RESPIRATORY RATE 12 breaths/min and irregular, and TEMPERATURE 37.2 C (98.9 F). There are no signs of trauma. Neck is supple. The patient DOES NOT RESPOND TO VERBAL STIMULI. PUPILS ARE EQUALLY REACTIVE TO LIGHT. The OCULOCEPHALIC REFLEX (DOLL'S EYE MANEUVER) IS NORMAL. On applying firm pressure to the orbital rim, the patient FLEXES HER RIGHT ARM, but DOES NOT MOVE HER LEFT ARM (focal deficit). Q: Which of the following is the most likely cause of her condition?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Hypoglycemia causes global depression without a focal lateralizing deficit; the asymmetric arm response indicates a structural lesion.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Opioid overdose causes pinpoint pupils and global depression, not a focal hemiparesis with reactive pupils.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Lacunar infarct in the right internal capsule
choices.explanation: A pure lacunar infarct typically does not depress consciousness to coma; the reduced consciousness points to a larger mass lesion.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: An elderly anticoagulated patient (on warfarin) found comatose with a FOCAL deficit (no movement of the left arm) and preserved brainstem reflexes (reactive pupils, normal doll's eye) is most consistent with an ACUTE SUBDURAL HEMATOMA causing a supratentorial mass effect. Anticoagulation and age are major risk factors.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: ACA infarct causes contralateral leg weakness, not coma with this pattern; less likely than a hemorrhagic mass lesion in an anticoagulated elderly patient.

questions.keyPoint: Elderly anticoagulated patient, coma + focal deficit + intact brainstem reflexes = subdural hematoma.

rows: Elderly, anticoagulated, depressed consciousness, focal deficit
Warfarin + elderly = high subdural hematoma risk

questions.stem: A 73-YEAR-OLD MAN has had THREE EPISODES OF VISUAL LOSS in the RIGHT EYE. The episodes last 20 to 30 minutes and resolve completely. He describes the sensation as like a WINDOW SHADE BEING PULLED DOWN in front of the eye (AMAUROSIS FUGAX). He has a history of HYPERTENSION and TOBACCO use. He denies dyspnea, chest pain, palpitations, or unilateral weakness or numbness. On examination the patient appears healthy; his vital signs are normal and the neurological examination is unremarkable. An ECG shows NORMAL SINUS RHYTHM WITHOUT EVIDENCE OF ISCHEMIA OR HYPERTROPHY. Initial laboratory studies are normal. Both NON-CONTRAST CT SCAN OF THE HEAD AND MR SCAN OF THE BRAIN ARE NORMAL. Q: What is the best next step in this patient's management?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Admit the patient to the hospital for intravenous unfractionated heparin
choices.explanation: IV heparin is not indicated for transient monocular blindness; the workup is for carotid stenosis with antiplatelet therapy, not anticoagulation.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Echocardiography looks for a cardiac source; with normal ECG/sinus rhythm and amaurosis fugax, the carotid is the prime suspect, so carotid imaging is first.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Check for antiphospholipid antibodies and homocysteine levels
choices.explanation: Hypercoagulable workup is for young patients without vascular risk factors; this elderly hypertensive smoker has typical atherosclerotic carotid disease.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Order a carotid duplex ultrasonogram and begin antiplatelet therapy
choices.explanation: AMAUROSIS FUGAX (transient monocular blindness, 'window shade') in an elderly hypertensive smoker indicates EMBOLI FROM CAROTID ARTERY DISEASE. The best next step is a CAROTID DUPLEX ULTRASOUND to assess for stenosis (potential endarterectomy) plus ANTIPLATELET THERAPY.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Begin lamotrigine for probable nonconvulsive seizure
choices.explanation: Amaurosis fugax is vascular, not epileptic; an antiepileptic is inappropriate.

questions.keyPoint: Amaurosis fugax = carotid embolic source; investigate with carotid duplex and start antiplatelet therapy.

rows: Transient monocular vision loss, 'window shade', vascular risk factors
Antiplatelet; carotid endarterectomy if significant stenosis
Amaurosis fugax points to carotid stenosis Neurology MCQ

questions.stem: A 29-YEAR-OLD FEMALE presents with BLURRED VISION in ONE EYE and NUMBNESS in her LEFT ARM. She reports a similar episode 6 MONTHS ago that resolved spontaneously (DISSEMINATION IN TIME). Neurological exam reveals a positive RELATIVE AFFERENT PUPILLARY DEFECT (RAPD) and a positive BABINSKI SIGN. MRI shows MULTIPLE PERIVENTRICULAR WHITE MATTER LESIONS (DISSEMINATION IN SPACE). Q: What is the most likely diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: NMO (Devic disease, anti-aquaporin-4) causes severe optic neuritis and longitudinally extensive transverse myelitis, typically sparing the periventricular white matter that is characteristic of MS.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: ADEM is usually a monophasic post-infectious/post-vaccination illness in children with multifocal deficits and encephalopathy, not recurrent episodes separated by months.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: A young woman with optic neuritis (blurred vision + RAPD) and a separate sensory episode 6 months earlier shows DISSEMINATION IN TIME, while multiple periventricular white-matter lesions on MRI plus a Babinski (UMN) sign show DISSEMINATION IN SPACE. This fulfils the core diagnostic principle of relapsing-remitting MULTIPLE SCLEROSIS.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: MG affects the neuromuscular junction causing fatigable ptosis/diplopia and limb weakness; it does NOT cause CNS white-matter lesions, RAPD or an upgoing plantar.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: CNS vasculitis can mimic MS but is rarer and usually shows systemic inflammatory features and infarct-pattern lesions, whereas the classic periventricular demyelinating lesions with optic neuritis point to MS.

questions.keyPoint: MS = CNS demyelination disseminated in TIME and SPACE; optic neuritis (RAPD) + periventricular white-matter lesions in a young woman.

rows: Young woman, optic neuritis/RAPD, relapsing focal CNS deficits, UMN signs
Dissemination in time + space (McDonald criteria), MRI
MRI brain/cord, CSF oligoclonal bands, VEPs
Acute: IV steroids; long-term: disease-modifying therapy
Progressive disability, neuropathic pain, bladder dysfunction
Lesions disseminated in time and space = MS

questions.stem: A 7-YEAR-OLD BOY developed PROGRESSIVE WEAKNESS of BOTH LEGS over the last 3 DAYS after a DIARRHOEAL ILLNESS 10 DAYS AGO. Examination reveals ABSENT DEEP TENDON REFLEXES in the lower limbs. CSF analysis reveals ELEVATED PROTEIN with NORMAL CELL COUNT (albuminocytological dissociation). Q: What is the most likely diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Transverse myelitis produces a sensory level with UMN signs (hyperreflexia, later spasticity) and sphincter involvement, not the areflexic ascending flaccid weakness of GBS.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Ascending symmetric flaccid weakness with AREFLEXIA developing days after a diarrhoeal illness (often Campylobacter jejuni), together with CSF ALBUMINOCYTOLOGICAL DISSOCIATION (raised protein with normal cell count), is classic GUILLAIN-BARRE SYNDROME, an acute immune-mediated demyelinating polyradiculoneuropathy. Monitor respiratory function; treat with IVIG or plasma exchange.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: A cord tumour causes a progressive sensory level and UMN signs below the lesion, not an acute post-infectious areflexic polyneuropathy with normal CSF cells.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Botulism causes a DESCENDING paralysis with prominent bulbar/autonomic features and dilated pupils, preceded by toxin ingestion, not an ascending pattern after diarrhoea.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Polio causes asymmetric flaccid weakness with a febrile prodrome and CSF pleocytosis (raised cells), unlike the symmetric areflexia and acellular high-protein CSF of GBS.

questions.keyPoint: Ascending flaccid areflexic weakness after diarrhoea + CSF albuminocytological dissociation = Guillain-Barre syndrome.

rows: Ascending symmetric flaccid weakness, areflexia, post-infectious (Campylobacter)
Clinical + CSF albuminocytological dissociation, nerve conduction
LP (high protein, normal cells), NCS, spirometry (FVC)
IVIG or plasma exchange; supportive/ventilatory care

questions.stem: A 25-YEAR-OLD complains of FLUCTUATING MUSCLE WEAKNESS, WORSE WITH EXERTION and worse in the EVENING. He has DIFFICULTY SWALLOWING and SLURRED VISION/SPEECH by evening. The ICE-PACK TEST improves the symptoms. Q: What is the most likely test in diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Fatigable weakness worse with exertion and later in the day, bulbar symptoms, and improvement with the ICE-PACK TEST are typical of myasthenia gravis. The TENSILON (edrophonium) test - a short-acting acetylcholinesterase inhibitor that transiently improves strength - supports the diagnosis (anti-AChR antibodies and repetitive nerve stimulation are confirmatory). Of the listed options, the Tensilon test is the bedside diagnostic test for MG.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Creatine kinase reflects muscle-fibre damage in myopathies and is normal in myasthenia gravis, which is a neuromuscular-junction disorder.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Standard nerve conduction studies are usually normal in MG; it is REPETITIVE nerve stimulation (showing a decremental response) or single-fibre EMG that helps, not a routine NCS.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: CSF analysis has no role in diagnosing myasthenia gravis.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: EEG evaluates cortical/seizure activity and is irrelevant to a neuromuscular-junction disorder like MG.

questions.keyPoint: Fatigable weakness + ice-pack test improvement = myasthenia gravis; Tensilon (edrophonium) test supports the diagnosis.

rows: Fatigable weakness worse in evening, bulbar symptoms, ice-pack test positive
AChR antibodies, repetitive nerve stimulation, CT thymus
Edrophonium (Tensilon) and ice-pack tests support MG

questions.stem: A 16-YEAR-OLD MALE with a GENERALIZED TONIC-CLONIC SEIZURE LASTING MORE THAN 5 MINUTES is brought to hospital. He has a history of POORLY CONTROLLED EPILEPSY. Q: What is the first-line management?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Diazepam is an alternative benzodiazepine but redistributes rapidly with a short duration of central action; IV lorazepam is preferred as first-line in hospital because of its longer anticonvulsant effect.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: A seizure lasting more than 5 minutes is status epilepticus. The first-line treatment when IV access is available is a BENZODIAZEPINE, and IV LORAZEPAM (0.1 mg/kg) is preferred over diazepam because of its longer duration of anticonvulsant action and lower recurrence rate. If no IV access, rectal diazepam or buccal/IM midazolam is used.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Rectal diazepam is used when there is NO IV access (e.g. community/pre-hospital); in hospital with IV access, IV lorazepam is first-line.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Carbamazepine is an oral maintenance antiepileptic with no IV formulation for aborting status epilepticus.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Haloperidol is an antipsychotic that can LOWER the seizure threshold and has no role in treating status epilepticus.

questions.keyPoint: Status epilepticus (seizure >5 min) with IV access: IV lorazepam is first-line (longer-acting than diazepam).

rows: GTC seizure >5 minutes, known poorly-controlled epilepsy
IV lorazepam first-line; then phenytoin/levetiracetam; then GA
IV lorazepam first-line in hospital (rectal diazepam if no IV)

questions.stem: A 65-YEAR-OLD MAN with HYPERTENSION and DIABETES presents with SUDDEN RIGHT-SIDED WEAKNESS and SLURRED SPEECH. CT brain shows NO HAEMORRHAGE (CT excludes bleed, consistent with ischaemic stroke). Q: What is the best immediate management?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Aspirin is started 24 hours AFTER thrombolysis (or immediately if thrombolysis is not given); in a patient presenting acutely and eligible for reperfusion it is not the best IMMEDIATE step.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: With CT excluding haemorrhage, this is an acute ISCHAEMIC stroke. The best immediate treatment for an eligible patient is intravenous thrombolysis with ALTEPLASE, given within the licensed time window (within 3-4.5 hours of symptom onset) after excluding contraindications. Early reperfusion salvages the ischaemic penumbra.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Acute ischaemic stroke is a time-critical emergency; passive observation forfeits the narrow window for thrombolysis/thrombectomy.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: LP has no role in acute ischaemic stroke management and would delay reperfusion.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Therapeutic anticoagulation is not used acutely in ischaemic stroke (increases haemorrhagic transformation risk) and is not the immediate treatment.

questions.keyPoint: Ischaemic stroke (CT excludes bleed): IV alteplase (thrombolysis) within 3-4.5 hours for eligible patients; consider thrombectomy for large-vessel occlusion.

rows: Sudden focal deficit (right weakness, dysarthria), vascular risk factors
IV alteplase <=3-4.5 h; thrombectomy for LVO; aspirin at 24 h
Thrombolysis only after CT excludes bleed and within time window

flag.note: FILE ANSWER vs STANDARD TEACHING — File says: b Standard (): The stem as originally summarised said 'CT shows hemorrhage', but the file's marked answer is alteplase, which is only valid for ISCHAEMIC stroke (alteplase is contraindicated in haemorrhage). The stem must read that CT shows NO haemorrhage. Answer 'b' is recorded as marked; the stem wording is internally inconsistent if read as a bleed - interpret CT as excluding haemorrhage. Exam advice: Confirm against your answer key.
flag.source: Study guide flag (file key vs standard teaching)

questions.stem: A 22-YEAR-OLD MALE presents to the ER with FEVER, HEADACHE, NECK STIFFNESS, and PHOTOPHOBIA. On examination, he has positive KERNIG'S and BRUDZINSKI'S SIGNS. CSF analysis: opening pressure HIGH, appearance TURBID, WBC 1200 (NEUTROPHILS PREDOMINANT), PROTEIN elevated, GLUCOSE decreased. Q: What is the most likely diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Fever, headache, neck stiffness, photophobia and positive Kernig/Brudzinski signs with a CSF that is TURBID, has HIGH opening pressure, NEUTROPHIL-predominant pleocytosis (1200 cells), ELEVATED PROTEIN and LOW GLUCOSE are the classic picture of acute BACTERIAL MENINGITIS. This profile (neutrophils + low glucose + high protein) distinguishes it from viral, TB and fungal causes.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Duplicate option text; the single correct choice for bacterial meningitis is recorded as (a).

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Fungal (e.g. cryptococcal) meningitis typically has a lymphocytic CSF with a more subacute course, not an acute neutrophilic, turbid CSF.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: TB meningitis usually causes a subacute lymphocytic CSF with very high protein and low glucose, not an acute neutrophil-predominant picture.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: SAH gives a uniformly blood-stained/xanthochromic CSF after a thunderclap headache, not a turbid neutrophilic CSF with low glucose and fever.

questions.keyPoint: Acute bacterial meningitis CSF = turbid, high pressure, neutrophils, high protein, LOW glucose.

rows: Fever, headache, neck stiffness, photophobia, Kernig/Brudzinski positive
CSF: turbid, neutrophilic, high protein, low glucose; culture/Gram stain
Lumbar puncture, blood cultures, CSF Gram stain/PCR
Neutrophilic CSF with low glucose = bacterial meningitis

flag.note: FILE ANSWER vs STANDARD TEACHING — File says: a Standard (): Options (a) and (b) are listed with identical text ('Bacterial meningitis') in the file; the marked answer is the first instance (a). Exam advice: Confirm against your answer key.
flag.source: Study guide flag (file key vs standard teaching)

questions.stem: Same 22-YEAR-OLD MALE with confirmed BACTERIAL MENINGITIS (turbid neutrophilic CSF, low glucose, high protein). Q: Next best step in management?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Acute bacterial meningitis is a medical emergency. Empirical therapy is a third-generation cephalosporin (CEFTRIAXONE) PLUS VANCOMYCIN to cover resistant pneumococcus, started without delay. Adjunctive DEXAMETHASONE (given just before or with the first antibiotic dose) reduces neurological complications/mortality in pneumococcal meningitis. Antibiotics must not be delayed for imaging if no contraindication to LP.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: IV aciclovir treats HSV encephalitis; it does not cover bacterial meningitis, although it may be added empirically if encephalitis is also suspected.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: While LP is needed, the QUESTION asks for treatment; the priority management step is immediate empirical antibiotics +/- steroids, and LP should not delay antibiotics (and CT first if raised-ICP signs).

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Mannitol treats raised intracranial pressure but is not the definitive management of bacterial meningitis, which requires prompt antibiotics.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Oral azithromycin provides inadequate CNS penetration and spectrum for bacterial meningitis, which needs high-dose IV cephalosporin plus vancomycin.

questions.keyPoint: Bacterial meningitis: give dexamethasone with/just before empirical IV ceftriaxone + vancomycin - do not delay antibiotics.

rows: Confirmed neutrophilic CSF with low glucose
Cultures before/with antibiotics; do not delay treatment HIGH-YIELD SUMMARY
Dexamethasone with first antibiotic dose reduces sequelae

questions.stem: A 60-YEAR-OLD MALE complains of TREMOR in his RIGHT HAND AT REST, SLOW MOVEMENT (bradykinesia), and DIFFICULTY initiating movement. Examination shows a SHUFFLING GAIT, COGWHEEL RIGIDITY, and a MASKED FACIAL EXPRESSION (hypomimia). Q: Which neurotransmitter is deficient?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Serotonin deficiency is implicated in depression, not the motor features of Parkinson disease.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Resting tremor, bradykinesia, cogwheel rigidity, shuffling gait and masked facies are the cardinal features of PARKINSON DISEASE, caused by degeneration of DOPAMINERGIC neurons in the substantia nigra pars compacta. The resulting DOPAMINE deficiency in the nigrostriatal pathway produces the hypokinetic movement disorder.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: There is relative acetylcholine EXCESS (dopamine-ACh imbalance) in Parkinson disease, not a deficiency; the primary deficit is dopamine.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: GABA is the main inhibitory transmitter and is reduced in Huntington disease (chorea), not the cause of parkinsonism.

choices.label: E
