choices.explanation: Glutamate is the principal excitatory transmitter and is not the deficient neurotransmitter in Parkinson disease.

questions.keyPoint: Parkinson disease = loss of dopaminergic neurons in the substantia nigra -> dopamine deficiency (resting tremor, rigidity, bradykinesia).

rows: Resting tremor, bradykinesia, cogwheel rigidity, shuffling gait, masked face
Clinical (TRAP); response to levodopa supports it
Dopamine deficiency from substantia nigra degeneration

questions.stem: Same 60-YEAR-OLD MALE with PARKINSON DISEASE (resting tremor, bradykinesia, rigidity). Q: First-line treatment for motor symptoms?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: LEVODOPA combined with CARBIDOPA (a peripheral dopa-decarboxylase inhibitor that reduces peripheral conversion and side effects) is the most effective treatment for the motor symptoms of Parkinson disease, especially in older patients with significant disability. It replenishes central dopamine and gives the greatest symptomatic benefit.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Selegiline (a MAO-B inhibitor) provides only modest symptomatic benefit and is used as adjunct or in early/mild disease, not as the most effective first-line motor treatment.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Anticholinergics help tremor in younger patients but cause confusion in the elderly and do not address bradykinesia/rigidity effectively.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Amantadine gives mild benefit and is mainly used for levodopa-induced dyskinesia, not as primary motor therapy.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Dopamine agonists like bromocriptine are an alternative (often in younger patients to delay levodopa) but are less efficacious than levodopa for motor control.

questions.keyPoint: Levodopa/carbidopa is the most effective first-line treatment for Parkinsonian motor symptoms.

rows: Motor fluctuations, dyskinesias with chronic levodopa
Levodopa + carbidopa = most effective motor therapy

questions.topic: Subarachnoid haemorrhage - next step (LP if CT negative)
questions.stem: A 45-YEAR-OLD FEMALE presents with SUDDEN SEVERE HEADACHE 'WORST IN MY LIFE' (THUNDERCLAP), PHOTOPHOBIA, and a BRIEF LOSS OF CONSCIOUSNESS. CT scan shows HYPERDENSITY IN THE BASAL CISTERNS. Q: CT scan shows hyperdensity in the basal cisterns. Next step?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Lumbar puncture (if CT is negative and high suspicion)
choices.explanation: A thunderclap 'worst-ever' headache suggests subarachnoid haemorrhage. Although this CT already shows blood in the basal cisterns, the principle being tested is the diagnostic pathway: if the non-contrast CT is NEGATIVE but clinical suspicion remains high, the next step is LUMBAR PUNCTURE (after ~12 hours) to look for xanthochromia/red cells. (When CT is positive, as here, one proceeds directly to CT/catheter angiography to localise the aneurysm.) Of the listed options, LP is the recognised next diagnostic step for suspected SAH with a negative CT.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Aspirin is contraindicated in haemorrhagic stroke/SAH because it worsens bleeding.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Clipping is definitive treatment of the aneurysm but follows angiographic localisation; it is not the immediate next diagnostic step.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: MRI is less sensitive than CT for acute subarachnoid blood and is not the standard next step; CT then LP/angiography is the pathway.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: EEG has no role in the diagnosis of subarachnoid haemorrhage.

questions.keyPoint: Suspected SAH with a NEGATIVE CT: do lumbar puncture (xanthochromia after ~12 h). If CT positive, proceed to angiography.

rows: Non-contrast CT; if negative, LP for xanthochromia
Negative CT + high suspicion -> LP for xanthochromia

questions.stem: Same patient with an ANEURYSMAL SUBARACHNOID HAEMORRHAGE confirmed. Q: Definitive treatment for aneurysmal bleed?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Mannitol reduces raised intracranial pressure temporarily but does not secure the aneurysm or prevent rebleeding.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Antiepileptics may be used if seizures occur but are not the definitive treatment of the aneurysm.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Definitive treatment of a ruptured (aneurysmal) subarachnoid haemorrhage is to SECURE THE ANEURYSM early to prevent rebleeding - either by neurosurgical CLIPPING or endovascular COILING. Coiling is often preferred when anatomically suitable. Nimodipine and supportive care reduce vasospasm-related ischaemia.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Corticosteroids have no established role in treating aneurysmal SAH.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Plasmapheresis is used in antibody-mediated disorders and has no role in aneurysmal haemorrhage.

questions.keyPoint: Definitive treatment of a ruptured cerebral aneurysm = secure it by clipping or coiling (prevents rebleeding).

rows: Endovascular coiling or surgical clipping; nimodipine
Secure aneurysm by coiling/clipping to prevent rebleed

questions.topic: Giant cell (temporal) arteritis - most urgent step
questions.stem: A 72-YEAR-OLD WOMAN presents with NEW-ONSET HEADACHE, SCALP TENDERNESS, JAW CLAUDICATION, and a transient episode of VISION LOSS in one eye. ESR is 110 mm/hr. Q: What is the most urgent step?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Temporal artery biopsy confirms the diagnosis but must NOT delay treatment; steroids are started first because biopsy remains informative for some days after starting them.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: New headache with scalp tenderness, jaw claudication, transient visual loss and a markedly raised ESR in an elderly woman is GIANT CELL (temporal) ARTERITIS. Because of the risk of irreversible blindness from anterior ischaemic optic neuropathy, the most URGENT step is to start HIGH-DOSE CORTICOSTEROIDS immediately (IV methylprednisolone if visual symptoms) - before, and without waiting for, temporal artery biopsy.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: MRI brain does not diagnose or treat GCA and would dangerously delay sight-saving steroids.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Low-dose aspirin may be added as adjunct, but it is not the urgent step that prevents blindness - high-dose steroids are.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Carotid Doppler evaluates carotid stenosis, not GCA, and would delay urgent treatment.

questions.keyPoint: Suspected giant cell arteritis with visual symptoms: start HIGH-DOSE STEROIDS immediately - do not wait for biopsy (to prevent blindness).

rows: Elderly, new headache, scalp tenderness, jaw claudication, visual loss, ESR very high
Clinical + raised ESR/CRP; temporal artery biopsy (after steroids)
ESR/CRP, temporal artery biopsy, temporal artery US
Immediate high-dose corticosteroids (IV if visual loss)
Start steroids before biopsy to save vision

questions.stem: Same 72-YEAR-OLD WOMAN with suspected GIANT CELL ARTERITIS (headache, jaw claudication, raised ESR), now started on steroids. Q: Confirmatory test?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: A raised ESR supports the diagnosis but is non-specific (elevated in many inflammatory states) and does not confirm giant cell arteritis.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: TEMPORAL ARTERY BIOPSY is the diagnostic confirmatory test for giant cell arteritis, showing granulomatous inflammation with multinucleated giant cells and intimal thickening of the vessel wall. Because of skip lesions an adequate length is taken; biopsy remains informative for up to about 1-2 weeks after starting steroids, so treatment is not delayed for it.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: CRP, like ESR, is a supportive inflammatory marker but is non-specific and not confirmatory.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: CT angiography of the brain does not diagnose temporal arteritis; large-vessel involvement is better assessed with other imaging, and biopsy remains the confirmatory test.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Antinuclear antibody is used for connective tissue diseases such as lupus, not for giant cell arteritis.

questions.keyPoint: Confirmatory test for giant cell arteritis = temporal artery biopsy (granulomatous inflammation with giant cells; beware skip lesions).

rows: Elderly, headache, jaw claudication, raised ESR/CRP
Biopsy (long segment for skip lesions), temporal artery US
Biopsy is confirmatory; still positive days after steroids

questions.stem: A 30-YEAR-OLD FEMALE presents with ACUTE-ONSET UNILATERAL FACIAL WEAKNESS. She CANNOT CLOSE HER LEFT EYE and has DROOPING OF THE MOUTH with NO FOREHEAD SPARING (LMN pattern). There are NO other neurological findings. Q: Best initial treatment? (Diagnosis: peripheral facial nerve palsy - Bell's palsy)

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Antiviral alone is not first-line; corticosteroids are the key treatment, with antivirals added only as adjunct in severe cases.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Acute unilateral LOWER-motor-neuron facial weakness with NO forehead sparing and inability to close the eye, with no other neurological signs, is BELL'S PALSY (idiopathic facial nerve palsy). Best initial treatment is a course of HIGH-DOSE ORAL CORTICOSTEROIDS (prednisolone) started early (within 72 hours), which improves recovery; an antiviral (aciclovir) may be added in severe cases. Eye protection (lubrication, taping) is essential.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Imaging is reserved for atypical features or no recovery; classic Bell's palsy is a clinical diagnosis and does not need urgent MRI before treatment.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Physiotherapy/facial exercises are adjunctive; they do not replace early corticosteroids that improve outcome.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Although many recover, early steroids significantly improve the chance of complete recovery, so 'no treatment' is suboptimal.

questions.keyPoint: Bell's palsy (LMN facial palsy, no forehead sparing): early high-dose oral corticosteroids (+/- antiviral) + eye protection.

rows: Acute unilateral LMN facial weakness, no forehead sparing, cannot close eye
Early high-dose prednisolone (+/- aciclovir), eye protection
LMN palsy spares no forehead; treat early with steroids

questions.stem: A 35-YEAR-OLD FEMALE complains of BILATERAL, NON-PULSATING HEADACHE that feels like a TIGHT BAND AROUND HER HEAD. It is NOT associated with nausea, vomiting, or photophobia. She reports that it usually WORSENS DURING STRESSFUL WORKDAYS. Q: What is the most likely diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Migraine is typically unilateral, pulsating, moderate-severe and associated with nausea, photophobia and phonophobia - features absent here.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Cluster headache causes severe unilateral periorbital pain with autonomic features (lacrimation, rhinorrhoea, ptosis) in attacks, not a bilateral band-like ache.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: A BILATERAL, NON-PULSATING, band-like ('tight band') headache of mild-moderate intensity WITHOUT nausea, vomiting or photophobia, worsened by stress, is the classic description of TENSION-TYPE HEADACHE, the most common primary headache. The absence of migrainous and autonomic features confirms it.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Sinus headache features facial pressure/pain over the sinuses with nasal symptoms and is usually related to sinusitis, not a stress-related band-like headache.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Temporal arteritis occurs in older patients with scalp tenderness, jaw claudication and raised ESR, not a young woman with a stress-related bilateral ache.

questions.keyPoint: Bilateral, band-like, non-pulsating headache without nausea/photophobia, worse with stress = tension-type headache.

rows: Bilateral, band-like, non-pulsating, mild-moderate, no nausea/photophobia
Clinical; imaging only if red flags
Simple analgesia (NSAIDs), stress management; amitriptyline for prophylaxis
Band-like bilateral headache, no migrainous features

questions.stem: Same 35-YEAR-OLD FEMALE with TENSION-TYPE HEADACHE worsened by stressful workdays. Q: Best initial treatment?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Triptans are for acute migraine attacks; they are not used for tension-type headache.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: First-line management of episodic tension-type headache is simple analgesia such as an NSAID (IBUPROFEN) or paracetamol/aspirin, combined with addressing precipitants - i.e. STRESS REDUCTION and lifestyle measures (regular sleep, posture, relaxation). This treats the acute headache and tackles the underlying trigger while avoiding medication overuse.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Corticosteroids have no role in tension-type headache.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Amitriptyline is used for PROPHYLAXIS of frequent/chronic tension-type headache, not as the best INITIAL treatment for episodic headache.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: High-flow oxygen aborts CLUSTER headache attacks, not tension-type headache.

questions.keyPoint: Episodic tension-type headache: simple analgesia (ibuprofen) + stress reduction; amitriptyline is for prophylaxis of chronic cases.

rows: Episodic band-like headache triggered by stress
NSAID/simple analgesia + stress reduction; amitriptyline if chronic
Ibuprofen + stress reduction first-line; amitriptyline for prophylaxis

questions.stem: A previously healthy 2-YEAR-OLD child has a GENERALIZED TONIC-CLONIC SEIZURE lasting 2 MINUTES during an episode of FEVER (39.5 C). The child is CONSCIOUS and ALERT after the seizure. No history of seizures. No focal findings. Q: What is the most likely diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Epilepsy is recurrent UNPROVOKED seizures. A single seizure in the context of fever in a normal child is not epilepsy.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Classic simple febrile seizure: generalized, brief (<15 min), single in 24h, occurs in a febrile child aged 6 months to 5 years with no CNS infection and full recovery. Benign and self-limiting; reassurance and antipyretics.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Meningitis would show meningism, lethargy, bulging fontanelle or altered consciousness; this child is alert and well post-ictally with no such signs.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Infantile spasms (West syndrome) are flexor/extensor clusters in infants <1 year with hypsarrhythmia, not a single febrile GTC seizure.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Absence seizures are brief staring spells without convulsion or fever, typically in older children (4-10y).

questions.keyPoint: Simple febrile seizure: 6 months-5 years, generalized, <15 min, once per 24h, full recovery, no CNS infection. Management is reassurance + antipyretics; no anticonvulsants.

rows: Age 6mo-5y, fever, brief generalized seizure, rapid recovery
Identify fever source; LP only if meningitis suspected
Recurrence; very low long-term epilepsy risk
Simple febrile seizures are benign and need only reassurance

flag.note: FILE ANSWER vs STANDARD TEACHING — File says: b (Febrile seizure simple) Standard (Nelson Paediatrics; Oxford Handbook): b - matches Davidson's/Nelson criteria Exam advice: File answer correct.
flag.source: Study guide flag (file key vs standard teaching)

questions.stem: Migraine vignette (paired with the diagnosis question below). Q: Next appropriate step?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Antiepileptics (e.g. topiramate, valproate) are prophylactic options only if attacks are frequent/disabling, not the immediate next step for a typical first presentation.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: LP is reserved for suspected SAH or meningitis; not indicated for typical migraine with normal exam.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: For a clinically typical migraine with a normal neurological examination, the appropriate step is reassurance plus symptomatic treatment of the attack (analgesia/triptan); no imaging needed when there are no red flags.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: EEG has no role in migraine diagnosis or management.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Neuroimaging is only indicated with red flags (thunderclap, focal deficit, papilloedema, new headache in older age); a typical migraine with normal exam does not need MRI.

questions.keyPoint: Typical migraine with a normal neurological exam needs reassurance and symptomatic treatment, not imaging.

rows: Recurrent unilateral throbbing headache, photophobia, nausea, normal exam
None routinely; image only if red flags
No imaging needed for typical migraine with normal exam

flag.note: FILE ANSWER vs STANDARD TEACHING — File says: c (Reassurance and treat fever) Standard (Oxford Handbook of Clinical Medicine): Reassurance + symptomatic treatment is correct; the printed option oddly mentions 'treat fever' (likely a copy artefact) Exam advice: Answer letter correct; 'treat fever' wording is a typo in the option.
flag.source: Study guide flag (file key vs standard teaching)

questions.stem: A 40-YEAR-OLD presents with sudden-onset UNILATERAL SEVERE HEADACHE localized around the LEFT EYE, lasting about 1 hour, OCCURS DAILY for a week, and is ASSOCIATED WITH TEARING and NASAL CONGESTION. Q: What is the most likely diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Migraine attacks last 4-72 hours, are throbbing with photophobia/nausea, and lack the strict autonomic features and daily clustering described here.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Severe strictly unilateral periorbital pain, short attacks (15 min-3h), occurring in daily clusters, with ipsilateral autonomic features (lacrimation, nasal congestion, ptosis/miosis) is classic cluster headache. Acute treatment is high-flow oxygen and subcutaneous sumatriptan.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Sinusitis causes facial pressure with purulent rhinorrhoea and fever, not discrete severe daily attacks with cranial autonomic features.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Temporal arteritis occurs in patients >50, with scalp tenderness, jaw claudication and raised ESR, not periorbital autonomic attacks.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Tension headache is bilateral, band-like, mild-moderate, without autonomic features.

questions.keyPoint: Cluster headache: severe unilateral periorbital pain in clusters with ipsilateral autonomic features (lacrimation, rhinorrhoea, ptosis). Treat acutely with 100% oxygen + SC sumatriptan.

rows: Severe unilateral periorbital pain, daily clusters, lacrimation/nasal congestion, restlessness
MRI to exclude secondary causes if atypical
Acute: 100% O2 + SC sumatriptan; prophylaxis: verapamil
Acute cluster headache = high-flow oxygen + SC sumatriptan

flag.note: FILE ANSWER vs STANDARD TEACHING — File says: b (Cluster headache) Standard (Oxford Handbook; ICHD-3): b - correct per classic features Exam advice: File answer correct.
flag.source: Study guide flag (file key vs standard teaching)

questions.stem: Same cluster-headache patient (acute attack). Q: Best acute treatment?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Oral NSAIDs are too slow for the short, severe attacks of cluster headache.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: The evidence-based acute treatment of a cluster attack is high-flow 100% oxygen via non-rebreather plus subcutaneous (or nasal) sumatriptan; both work rapidly within the short attack window.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Steroids are used as a transitional/bridging prophylaxis, not for aborting an individual acute attack.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Paracetamol is ineffective for the intense, short-lived pain of cluster headache.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: TCAs (amitriptyline) are used for tension-type/chronic headache prophylaxis, not acute cluster attacks.

questions.keyPoint: Acute cluster headache abortive therapy = 100% oxygen + subcutaneous sumatriptan.

rows: Short severe unilateral attacks needing rapid abortive therapy

flag.note: FILE ANSWER vs STANDARD TEACHING — File says: b (Sumatriptan and 100% oxygen) Standard (Katzung; Oxford Handbook): b - correct Exam advice: File answer correct.
flag.source: Study guide flag (file key vs standard teaching)

questions.stem: A 6-YEAR-OLD boy is brought by his mother for FREQUENT BRIEF EPISODES OF STARING INTO SPACE lasting a FEW SECONDS. These happen MULTIPLE TIMES PER DAY. He does NOT RESPOND during the episode and RESUMES ACTIVITY without confusion. Q: What is the most likely diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Complex (focal impaired-awareness) seizures last longer, often have automatisms and post-ictal confusion; here recovery is immediate without confusion.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Childhood absence epilepsy: brief (few seconds) staring spells, many times daily, abrupt onset/offset with no post-ictal confusion, in a school-aged child. EEG shows 3 Hz spike-and-wave. First-line treatment is ethosuximide (or valproate).

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: ADHD inattention is continuous and the child is rousable/responsive when called; absences are discrete unresponsive episodes.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Daydreaming can be interrupted by calling the child and lacks the abrupt, stereotyped, unresponsive pattern.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Todd's paresis is transient focal weakness AFTER a focal seizure, not staring spells.

questions.keyPoint: Childhood absence seizures: brief staring spells, abrupt, no post-ictal confusion, 3 Hz spike-and-wave on EEG; treat with ethosuximide.

rows: Brief frequent staring, abrupt on/off, no post-ictal confusion, age 4-10
3 Hz spike-and-wave EEG = absence epilepsy; treat with ethosuximide

flag.note: FILE ANSWER vs STANDARD TEACHING — File says: b (Absence seizures) Standard (Nelson; Oxford Handbook): b - correct Exam advice: File answer correct.
flag.source: Study guide flag (file key vs standard teaching)

questions.stem: Same 6-year-old with suspected absence seizures. Q: What is the best initial diagnostic test?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: MRI is normal in idiopathic generalized epilepsy and is not the initial test for typical absence seizures.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: CT adds nothing for absence seizures and exposes the child to radiation.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: EEG is the key diagnostic test, showing the characteristic 3 Hz generalized spike-and-wave discharges, often provoked by hyperventilation. It confirms the diagnosis of childhood absence epilepsy.

questions.keyPoint: EEG (3 Hz spike-and-wave) is the diagnostic test for absence epilepsy; hyperventilation can provoke an attack.

flag.note: FILE ANSWER vs STANDARD TEACHING — File says: c (EEG) Standard (Nelson; Oxford Handbook): c - correct Exam advice: File answer correct.
flag.source: Study guide flag (file key vs standard teaching)

questions.topic: Expressive (Broca) aphasia - left MCA territory stroke
questions.stem: A 65-YEAR-OLD HYPERTENSIVE and DIABETIC male presents with SUDDEN ONSET INABILITY TO SPEAK and RIGHT-SIDED WEAKNESS. EXAMINATION reveals RIGHT HEMIPLEGIA and EXPRESSIVE APHASIA. A NON-CONTRAST CT scan of the brain shows NO HEMORRHAGE. Q: What is the most likely diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: SAH presents with thunderclap headache and meningism; CT here shows no haemorrhage and the picture is a focal ischaemic syndrome.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Sudden right hemiplegia plus expressive (Broca) aphasia localizes to the dominant (left) hemisphere MCA territory; a normal early non-contrast CT (no haemorrhage) is typical of acute ischaemic stroke (early CT is often normal). This is ischaemic stroke until proven otherwise; consider thrombolysis if within window.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Abscess develops subacutely with fever and a ring-enhancing lesion, not sudden onset deficits with a normal CT.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Hypoglycaemia can mimic stroke but typically resolves with glucose and is non-localizing; check glucose, but the sustained focal syndrome fits a stroke.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Todd's paralysis follows a witnessed seizure and resolves over hours; no seizure is described here.

questions.keyPoint: Right hemiplegia + expressive aphasia + normal/no-haemorrhage CT = left (dominant) MCA ischaemic stroke; early CT is often normal in ischaemia.

rows: Sudden right hemiplegia, expressive aphasia, vascular risk factors, no haemorrhage on CT
Non-contrast CT, then consider CT angiography/MRI, glucose
IV thrombolysis (tPA) if <4.5h and no contraindication; thrombectomy for LVO; antiplatelets later
Early non-contrast CT is often normal in acute ischaemic stroke

flag.note: FILE ANSWER vs STANDARD TEACHING — File says: b (Ischemic stroke left MCA territory) Standard (Davidson's; Oxford Handbook): b - correct Exam advice: File answer correct.
flag.source: Study guide flag (file key vs standard teaching)

questions.stem: Same 65-year-old with left MCA ischaemic stroke; presents acutely. Q: What is the best immediate management?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Mannitol is for raised intracranial pressure/cerebral oedema, not the immediate management of an acute uncomplicated ischaemic stroke.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Aspirin/statin are important but are started AFTER excluding haemorrhage and AFTER thrombolysis decision (aspirin delayed 24h if tPA given); not the best immediate step when within the thrombolysis window.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Administer IV tPA if within 4.5 hours
choices.explanation: If the patient presents within 4.5 hours of onset, haemorrhage is excluded on CT, and there are no contraindications, IV alteplase (tPA) is the best immediate treatment to restore perfusion. This is the highest-yield acute stroke intervention.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Anticonvulsants are only for seizures, which are not described.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: LP has no role in acute ischaemic stroke management.

questions.keyPoint: Acute ischaemic stroke within 4.5h with no haemorrhage and no contraindication: give IV thrombolysis (tPA) immediately.

rows: Within thrombolysis window, CT shows no bleed
Non-contrast CT, glucose, time of onset
IV alteplase (tPA) <4.5h; mechanical thrombectomy for large-vessel occlusion
tPA window is 4.5 hours from onset

flag.note: FILE ANSWER vs STANDARD TEACHING — File says: c (Administer IV tPA if within 4.5 hours) Standard (Davidson's; AHA stroke guidelines): c - correct Exam advice: File answer correct.
flag.source: Study guide flag (file key vs standard teaching)

questions.stem: A 28-YEAR-OLD WOMAN presents with DOUBLE VISION, FATIGUE WORSENED BY END OF THE DAY, and DIFFICULTY CHEWING. NEUROLOGICAL EXAM reveals BILATERAL PTOSIS and FATIGABLE WEAKNESS. TENSILON TEST IS POSITIVE. Q: What is the most likely diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: LEMS causes proximal weakness that IMPROVES with repeated effort, with autonomic features and association with small-cell lung cancer; ocular/bulbar fatigability and a positive Tensilon test favour myasthenia.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Fatigable weakness worse at end of day, bilateral ptosis, diplopia, bulbar (chewing) weakness, and a positive edrophonium (Tensilon) test are classic for myasthenia gravis (anti-AChR antibodies, NMJ disorder). Confirm with anti-AChR antibodies and check for thymoma.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: MS causes CNS demyelination (optic neuritis, sensory/motor signs disseminated in space/time), not the fatigable NMJ pattern with a positive Tensilon test.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: GBS is acute ascending areflexic paralysis post-infection, not chronic fatigable ocular/bulbar weakness.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: A tumour would cause fixed/progressive focal deficits, not diurnally fluctuating fatigable weakness with a positive Tensilon test.

questions.keyPoint: Fatigable ptosis/diplopia worse by evening + positive Tensilon test = myasthenia gravis (anti-AChR antibodies; screen for thymoma).

rows: Fatigable ptosis, diplopia, bulbar weakness worse with use/evening
Anti-AChR antibody, repetitive nerve stimulation/single-fibre EMG, CT thorax for thymoma
Pyridostigmine; immunosuppression; thymectomy; IVIG/plasmapheresis in crisis
Anti-AChR antibodies confirm MG; check for thymoma

flag.note: FILE ANSWER vs STANDARD TEACHING — File says: b (Myasthenia gravis) Standard (Davidson's; Oxford Handbook): b - correct Exam advice: File answer correct.
flag.source: Study guide flag (file key vs standard teaching)

questions.topic: Myasthenia gravis - antibody to confirm diagnosis
questions.stem: Same young woman with suspected myasthenia gravis. Q: Which test is most specific to confirm the diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Anti-acetylcholine receptor (anti-AChR) antibodies are the most specific confirmatory test for generalized myasthenia gravis (positive in ~85% of generalized cases). MuSK antibodies are tested if AChR negative.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Repetitive nerve stimulation (decrement) and single-fibre EMG are sensitive and supportive but not antibody-specific; the question asks the most specific confirmatory test.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Muscle biopsy is for myopathies, not myasthenia (a neuromuscular junction disorder).

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Brain MRI is normal in MG; CT thorax is used to look for thymoma, but neither confirms the diagnosis.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Anti-MOG antibodies are associated with optic neuritis/MOG-associated demyelination, not myasthenia gravis.

questions.keyPoint: Anti-AChR antibody is the most specific confirmatory test for myasthenia gravis; anti-MuSK if AChR negative.

rows: Need antibody confirmation of NMJ disorder
Anti-AChR, then anti-MuSK; EMG; CT thorax
Anti-AChR antibody = most specific MG test

flag.note: FILE ANSWER vs STANDARD TEACHING — File says: a (Anti-ACh receptor antibody) Standard (Davidson's): a - correct Exam advice: File answer correct.
flag.source: Study guide flag (file key vs standard teaching)

questions.stem: Same woman, confirmed myasthenia gravis, for first-line symptomatic treatment. Q: Preferred treatment?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Pyridostigmine, an acetylcholinesterase inhibitor, is the first-line symptomatic treatment for myasthenia gravis, increasing ACh at the neuromuscular junction. Immunosuppression (steroids/azathioprine) and thymectomy are added as needed.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Levodopa is for Parkinson's disease, not MG.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Interferon-beta is a disease-modifying therapy for relapsing MS, not MG.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Folinic acid (leucovorin) is unrelated to myasthenia treatment.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Carbamazepine is an anticonvulsant and can actually worsen myasthenia; not appropriate.

questions.keyPoint: Pyridostigmine (anticholinesterase) is the first-line symptomatic treatment for myasthenia gravis.

rows: Pyridostigmine first-line; add steroids/azathioprine; thymectomy; IVIG/plasma exchange in crisis
Pyridostigmine is first-line symptomatic therapy for MG

flag.note: FILE ANSWER vs STANDARD TEACHING — File says: a (Pyridostigmine) Standard (Katzung; Davidson's): a - correct Exam advice: File answer correct. (Option text 'Lecovorin' appears to be a misprint of leucovorin.)
flag.source: Study guide flag (file key vs standard teaching)

questions.stem: A 45-YEAR-OLD MALE presents with BURNING PAIN and NUMBNESS in BOTH FEET, especially AT NIGHT. He has a history of POORLY CONTROLLED TYPE 2 DIABETES. NEUROLOGICAL EXAM reveals DECREASED SENSATION and ANKLE REFLEXES. Q: What is the most likely diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Symmetrical distal 'glove-and-stocking' burning pain and numbness worse at night with reduced ankle reflexes in poorly controlled diabetes is classic diabetic peripheral (sensorimotor) polyneuropathy. Optimise glycaemic control; treat neuropathic pain with pregabalin/duloxetine.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: GBS is acute, ascending, motor-predominant areflexic paralysis post-infection, not a chronic length-dependent sensory neuropathy.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: MS is a CNS demyelinating disease with UMN signs and lesions disseminated in space/time, not a symmetric peripheral sensory neuropathy.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: B12 deficiency can cause neuropathy but typically with subacute combined degeneration (dorsal columns/UMN signs); diabetes here is the obvious cause. Worth checking B12, but DPN is most likely.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Cord compression gives a sensory level, UMN signs and bladder involvement, not a symmetric distal sensory pattern with absent ankle jerks.

questions.keyPoint: Symmetric distal burning feet worse at night + reduced ankle jerks in a diabetic = diabetic peripheral neuropathy.

rows: Distal symmetric glove-and-stocking sensory loss, burning, reduced ankle reflexes
Clinical in a diabetic; monofilament testing
Glycaemic control; pregabalin/gabapentin or duloxetine for pain HIGH-YIELD SUMMARY
First-line neuropathic pain agents: pregabalin/gabapentin or duloxetine

flag.note: FILE ANSWER vs STANDARD TEACHING — File says: a (Diabetic peripheral neuropathy) Standard (Davidson's; NICE neuropathic pain): a - correct Exam advice: File answer correct.
flag.source: Study guide flag (file key vs standard teaching)

questions.stem: Same diabetic patient with painful peripheral neuropathy. Q: Best initial treatment option?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: IVIG is for immune neuropathies (GBS, CIDP), not painful diabetic neuropathy.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: First-line agents for painful diabetic neuropathy are gabapentinoids (pregabalin/gabapentin) or duloxetine (an SNRI). They target neuropathic pain mechanisms and are recommended by NICE/ADA.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Steroids do not treat diabetic neuropathy and worsen glycaemic control.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Carbamazepine is first-line for trigeminal neuralgia, not the preferred agent for diabetic neuropathic pain.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Levodopa treats Parkinson's disease and has no role in neuropathic pain.

questions.keyPoint: First-line treatment for painful diabetic neuropathy: pregabalin/gabapentin or duloxetine (plus glycaemic control).

rows: Pregabalin/gabapentin or duloxetine; amitriptyline alternative; optimise glucose
Pregabalin/duloxetine are first-line for diabetic neuropathic pain

flag.note: FILE ANSWER vs STANDARD TEACHING — File says: b (Pregabalin or duloxetine) Standard (NICE neuropathic pain guideline): b - correct Exam advice: File answer correct.
flag.source: Study guide flag (file key vs standard teaching)

questions.stem: A 30-YEAR-OLD MAN presents with CONFUSION, FEVER, and SEIZURES. He is DROWSY and has NECK STIFFNESS. CSF shows ELEVATED PROTEIN, LYMPHOCYTIC PLEOCYTOSIS, and NORMAL GLUCOSE. BRAIN MRI reveals HYPERINTENSITY IN THE TEMPORAL LOBE. Q: What is the most likely diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: TB meningitis gives lymphocytic CSF but with LOW glucose and high protein, a subacute course, and basal meningeal involvement, not temporal lobe MRI changes.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Bacterial meningitis shows neutrophilic CSF with low glucose and high protein, not lymphocytic pleocytosis with normal glucose.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Fever, confusion, seizures, lymphocytic CSF with normal glucose, and temporal lobe MRI changes are classic for herpes simplex encephalitis. Start empirical IV aciclovir immediately (do not wait for PCR) as it is the most common treatable sporadic encephalitis.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: A brain abscess shows a ring-enhancing mass lesion with focal signs, not diffuse temporal encephalitic change with this CSF profile.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Subdural empyema is an extra-axial pus collection (often post-sinusitis), not temporal lobe parenchymal hyperintensity with lymphocytic CSF.

questions.keyPoint: Fever + seizures + temporal lobe MRI change + lymphocytic CSF with normal glucose = HSV encephalitis; start IV aciclovir immediately.

rows: Fever, confusion, seizures, temporal lobe involvement
CSF HSV PCR; MRI temporal lobe changes; lymphocytic CSF, normal glucose
MRI brain, CSF analysis + HSV PCR, EEG
Memory/seizure sequelae, high mortality if untreated
Start IV aciclovir before PCR confirmation in suspected HSV encephalitis

flag.note: FILE ANSWER vs STANDARD TEACHING — File says: c (Viral encephalitis HSV) Standard (Davidson's; Oxford Handbook): c - correct Exam advice: File answer correct.
flag.source: Study guide flag (file key vs standard teaching)

questions.stem: Same patient with suspected HSV encephalitis. Q: Best initial treatment?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Ceftriaxone treats bacterial meningitis; it does not cover HSV. (It may be co-administered until bacterial cause excluded, but aciclovir is the key treatment here.)

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Empirical IV aciclovir must be started immediately in any suspected viral (HSV) encephalitis because early treatment dramatically reduces mortality and morbidity; do not wait for PCR confirmation.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Doxycycline is for rickettsial/atypical infections, not HSV encephalitis.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Steroids alone do not treat the viral infection and are not the primary therapy.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Observation is dangerous; untreated HSV encephalitis has very high mortality.

questions.keyPoint: Suspected HSV encephalitis = start empirical IV aciclovir immediately.

rows: IV aciclovir is started empirically before confirmation

flag.note: FILE ANSWER vs STANDARD TEACHING — File says: b (IV acyclovir) Standard (Davidson's; Oxford Handbook): b - correct Exam advice: File answer correct.
flag.source: Study guide flag (file key vs standard teaching)

questions.stem: A 24-YEAR-OLD MAN presents with REPETITIVE, BRIEF EPISODES OF ELECTRIC-SHOCK PAIN on the RIGHT SIDE OF HIS FACE, TRIGGERED BY CHEWING AND COLD WIND. NEUROLOGICAL EXAM IS NORMAL. Q: What is the most likely diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Temporal arteritis occurs in patients >50 with scalp tenderness/jaw claudication and raised ESR, not brief shock-like trigger-evoked facial pain in a young man.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Migraine is a sustained throbbing headache (hours), not paroxysmal seconds-long electric-shock pains triggered by touch/chewing.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Paroxysmal, brief, electric-shock-like unilateral facial pain in the trigeminal distribution triggered by light touch, chewing or cold wind, with a normal exam, is classic trigeminal neuralgia. First-line treatment is carbamazepine. In a young patient, consider MS/vascular loop and image with MRI.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Tension headache is bilateral band-like pressure, not lancinating trigger-evoked facial pain.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Dental abscess gives constant localized throbbing pain with swelling/tenderness, not trigger-evoked electric-shock paroxysms.

questions.keyPoint: Brief electric-shock unilateral facial pain triggered by touch/chewing/cold wind = trigeminal neuralgia; first-line carbamazepine. In young patients consider MS.

rows: Paroxysmal lancinating unilateral facial pain, trigger zones, normal exam
Clinical; MRI to exclude MS/vascular compression (esp. if young)
Carbamazepine first-line; microvascular decompression if refractory
Severe disabling pain, weight loss from avoiding eating
Carbamazepine is first-line for trigeminal neuralgia

flag.note: FILE ANSWER vs STANDARD TEACHING — File says: c (Trigeminal neuralgia) Standard (Oxford Handbook; Davidson's): c - correct Exam advice: File answer correct.
flag.source: Study guide flag (file key vs standard teaching)

questions.stem: Same patient with trigeminal neuralgia. Q: First-line treatment?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Simple analgesics are ineffective for the neuropathic lancinating pain of trigeminal neuralgia.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Carbamazepine is the established first-line drug for trigeminal neuralgia, reducing ectopic neuronal firing; oxcarbazepine is an alternative. Microvascular decompression is considered if drug-refractory.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Steroids are used in giant cell arteritis, not trigeminal neuralgia.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Amitriptyline helps other neuropathic pains but is not first-line for trigeminal neuralgia.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Gabapentin is a second-line option; carbamazepine is the established first-line agent.

questions.keyPoint: Carbamazepine is first-line for trigeminal neuralgia.

rows: Neuropathic facial pain requiring anticonvulsant HIGH-YIELD SUMMARY
Carbamazepine first-line; oxcarbazepine/baclofen; surgery if refractory

flag.note: FILE ANSWER vs STANDARD TEACHING — File says: b (Carbamazepine) Standard (Oxford Handbook; Katzung): b - correct Exam advice: File answer correct.
flag.source: Study guide flag (file key vs standard teaching)

questions.stem: A 17-YEAR-OLD BOY is brought to the ER after experiencing a GENERALIZED TONIC-CLONIC SEIZURE lasting 7 MINUTES. He has a KNOWN HISTORY OF EPILEPSY but MISSED HIS MEDICATIONS for 2 DAYS. He is CONFUSED AND DROWSY AFTER THE EPISODE. Q: What is the most likely diagnosis (post-event state)?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Status epilepticus is continuous seizure activity >5 min or recurrent seizures without recovery between; here the seizure ended and he is now post-ictal, not still seizing.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Confusion and drowsiness following a self-terminated generalized tonic-clonic seizure in a known epileptic who missed medication describes the normal post-ictal state. Management is supportive observation and restart of antiepileptic therapy.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: PNES typically lacks a true post-ictal confusional state and has atypical, non-physiological features; this patient has documented epilepsy and a clear post-ictal phase.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Absence seizures are brief staring spells with immediate recovery, not a prolonged GTC followed by drowsiness.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Hypoglycaemia can cause seizures, but the clear precipitant here is missed antiepileptic medication; check glucose but the picture is post-ictal.

questions.keyPoint: Drowsiness/confusion after a self-terminated GTC seizure in a non-compliant epileptic = post-ictal state; observe and restart medication.

rows: Transient confusion/drowsiness after a generalized seizure
Check glucose, AED levels; ensure full recovery
Post-ictal confusion is self-limiting after a seizure resolves

flag.note: FILE ANSWER vs STANDARD TEACHING — File says: b (Post-ictal state after seizure) Standard (Oxford Handbook): b - correct Exam advice: File answer correct.
flag.source: Study guide flag (file key vs standard teaching)

questions.topic: Post-ictal state - most appropriate next step
questions.stem: Same 17-year-old, now post-ictal after a self-terminated GTC seizure, having missed his medications. Q: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: IV benzodiazepines are for ongoing/active seizures or status epilepticus; the seizure has already stopped, so lorazepam is not indicated now.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Restarting maintenance medication is appropriate, but giving a stat oral dose during the acute post-ictal drowsy phase risks aspiration and is not the immediate priority; observation and restarting his regular regimen is preferred.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Since the seizure self-terminated and he is simply post-ictal, the correct step is supportive observation until he recovers, then restart his maintenance antiepileptic therapy and address the cause (missed medication/compliance).

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Urgent MRI is unnecessary in a known epileptic with a clear precipitant (missed medication) and a typical post-ictal recovery.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: LP is not indicated without features of CNS infection; the cause here is medication non-adherence.

questions.keyPoint: After a self-terminated seizure in a known epileptic who missed medication: observe and restart maintenance antiepileptics; benzodiazepines only for ongoing seizures/status.

rows: Seizure stopped; post-ictal drowsiness; non-adherence the trigger
Observe and restart maintenance antiepileptic therapy; reinforce adherence
Status epilepticus if untreated and seizures recur
Benzodiazepines treat active seizures/status, not the resolved post-ictal state

flag.note: FILE ANSWER vs STANDARD TEACHING — File says: c (Observation and restart maintenance therapy) Standard (Oxford Handbook; NICE epilepsy): c - reasonable; if the patient were still actively seizing >5 min, IV lorazepam (status protocol) would apply Exam advice: File answer correct for the post-ictal scenario as written.
flag.source: Study guide flag (file key vs standard teaching)

questions.stem: A 35-YEAR-OLD WOMAN presents with WEAKNESS and NUMBNESS in her RIGHT LEG that developed over 2 DAYS. She also reports URINARY URGENCY. Two years ago, she had BLURRY VISION in her RIGHT EYE that improved spontaneously. MRI shows MULTIPLE PERIVENTRICULAR LESIONS. Q: What is the most likely diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Neurological episodes disseminated in TIME (optic neuritis 2 years ago, now myelopathic limb symptoms) and SPACE (optic nerve, spinal cord, periventricular MRI lesions) in a young woman is classic relapsing-remitting multiple sclerosis. Supported by oligoclonal bands in CSF.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: NMO (anti-AQP4) causes severe optic neuritis and longitudinally extensive transverse myelitis (>3 segments), usually without periventricular MS-pattern lesions; periventricular lesions favour MS.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: GBS is an acute ascending peripheral areflexic paralysis post-infection, not relapsing CNS lesions with optic neuritis.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Isolated transverse myelitis explains the cord symptoms but not the prior optic neuritis and periventricular lesions disseminated in time/space.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: MG causes fatigable NMJ weakness, not sensory symptoms, optic neuritis or periventricular white-matter lesions.

questions.keyPoint: Lesions disseminated in time and space (optic neuritis + myelopathy + periventricular MRI lesions) in a young woman = multiple sclerosis.

rows: CNS lesions disseminated in time and space; optic neuritis, myelopathy, urinary urgency
McDonald criteria: MRI dissemination in space/time + CSF oligoclonal bands
MRI brain/spine with gadolinium; CSF oligoclonal bands; VEPs
Acute relapse: high-dose IV methylprednisolone; long-term: disease-modifying therapy (e.g. interferon-beta)
MS = dissemination in time and space; CSF oligoclonal bands support it

flag.note: FILE ANSWER vs STANDARD TEACHING — File says: a (Multiple sclerosis) Standard (Davidson's; McDonald criteria): a - correct Exam advice: File answer correct.
flag.source: Study guide flag (file key vs standard teaching)

questions.topic: Multiple sclerosis - investigation to support diagnosis
questions.stem: Same 35-year-old woman with suspected multiple sclerosis. Q: Which test is used to support the diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: CSF examination showing unmatched oligoclonal IgG bands (present in CSF but not serum) reflects intrathecal antibody synthesis and supports the diagnosis of MS alongside MRI dissemination in space/time.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: ANA screens for connective tissue/autoimmune disease (e.g. SLE), not MS.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: EEG assesses seizure activity; it has no role in diagnosing MS.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Visual acuity is non-specific; visual evoked potentials (not plain acuity) can support optic nerve demyelination, but oligoclonal bands are the specific supporting test asked for.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: NCS assess peripheral nerves; MS is a CNS disease, so they are normal/unhelpful.

questions.keyPoint: CSF oligoclonal bands (unmatched in serum) support a diagnosis of MS.

rows: Oligoclonal bands in CSF (not serum) support MS

flag.note: FILE ANSWER vs STANDARD TEACHING — File says: a (CSF analysis for oligoclonal bands) Standard (Davidson's): a - correct Exam advice: File answer correct.
flag.source: Study guide flag (file key vs standard teaching)

questions.topic: Multiple sclerosis - treatment for acute episode
questions.stem: Same woman with an acute MS relapse. Q: Preferred treatment for acute episode?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Interferon-beta is a long-term disease-modifying therapy to reduce relapse frequency, not the treatment of an acute relapse.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Acute MS relapses are treated with high-dose corticosteroids (IV methylprednisolone, typically 1 g/day for 3-5 days) to shorten the duration of the relapse. It does not alter long-term disease course.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: IVIG is not standard for acute MS relapses; it is used in some other immune neuropathies.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Plasma exchange is reserved for severe steroid-refractory relapses, not first-line acute treatment.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Azathioprine is a steroid-sparing immunosuppressant, not acute relapse therapy.

questions.keyPoint: Acute MS relapse = high-dose IV methylprednisolone (shortens relapse, does not change long-term course).

rows: MRI to confirm active lesion; exclude infection
High-dose IV methylprednisolone; plasma exchange if refractory; DMT for prevention
IV methylprednisolone treats acute MS relapse; interferon-beta is for prevention

flag.note: FILE ANSWER vs STANDARD TEACHING — File says: b (High-dose IV methylprednisolone) Standard (Davidson's; NICE MS): b - correct Exam advice: File answer correct.
flag.source: Study guide flag (file key vs standard teaching)

questions.stem: A 72-YEAR-OLD MAN is brought in with SUDDEN ONSET LOSS OF CONSCIOUSNESS, followed by RIGHT-SIDED HEMIPLEGIA and LEFT EYE DEVIATION. He is HYPERTENSIVE and NOT on medication. CT BRAIN shows a LARGE INTRACEREBRAL HAEMORRHAGE with MIDLINE SHIFT. Q: What is the most likely diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: An ischaemic stroke would not show acute high-density haemorrhage with midline shift on CT; the CT here is diagnostic of bleeding.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Subdural haematoma is a crescentic extra-axial collection (usually trauma/elderly), not an intraparenchymal haemorrhage from hypertension.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Sudden loss of consciousness with hemiplegia in an uncontrolled hypertensive, plus CT showing a large intraparenchymal (intracerebral) haemorrhage with midline shift, is classic hypertensive intracerebral haemorrhage (often basal ganglia/thalamus). Manage BP, raised ICP, and refer to neurosurgery.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Epidural haematoma is a biconvex extra-axial collection following temporal trauma/middle meningeal artery injury, with a lucid interval, not a hypertensive intraparenchymal bleed.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: CVT causes headache, raised ICP and sometimes venous infarcts/haemorrhages, but the picture of a hypertensive patient with a large intraparenchymal bleed fits hypertensive ICH.

questions.keyPoint: Sudden coma + hemiplegia in an uncontrolled hypertensive with CT intraparenchymal bleed and midline shift = hypertensive intracerebral haemorrhage.

rows: Sudden deficit/coma, uncontrolled hypertension, intraparenchymal bleed with mass effect
Control BP, manage raised ICP, reverse coagulopathy, urgent neurosurgical referral
Hypertensive ICH commonly affects basal ganglia/thalamus; control BP and refer to neurosurgery

flag.note: FILE ANSWER vs STANDARD TEACHING — File says: c (Hypertensive intracerebral hemorrhage) Standard (Davidson's; Oxford Handbook): c - correct Exam advice: File answer correct.
flag.source: Study guide flag (file key vs standard teaching)

questions.topic: Intracerebral haemorrhage - most urgent next step
questions.stem: Same 72-year-old with a large hypertensive intracerebral haemorrhage and midline shift. Q: What is the most urgent next step in management?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Aspirin is contraindicated in acute intracerebral haemorrhage as it would worsen bleeding.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: A large intracerebral haemorrhage with midline shift (mass effect/herniation risk) requires urgent neurosurgical referral for possible evacuation/decompression, alongside BP control and ICP management. Surgery can be life-saving for large lobar/cerebellar bleeds with deterioration.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Anticoagulation is absolutely contraindicated in acute haemorrhage; if on warfarin, it must be reversed.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: There is no infection; antibiotics are irrelevant.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: LP is contraindicated with a mass lesion and midline shift due to risk of herniation.

questions.keyPoint: Large ICH with midline shift = urgent neurosurgical referral (plus BP/ICP control); avoid antiplatelets/anticoagulants and LP.

rows: Large bleed, midline shift, deterioration risk
Urgent neurosurgical referral; BP control; reverse coagulopathy; manage ICP
Do not give antiplatelets/anticoagulants or LP in acute ICH; refer to neurosurgery

flag.note: FILE ANSWER vs STANDARD TEACHING — File says: b (Refer to neurosurgery) Standard (Oxford Handbook; AHA ICH guidelines): b - correct Exam advice: File answer correct.
flag.source: Study guide flag (file key vs standard teaching)

questions.stem: A 4-YEAR-OLD boy is noted by his parents to have EPISODES OF SUDDEN ARM EXTENSION followed by STIFFENING that lasts a FEW SECONDS. These episodes occur IN CLUSTERS, especially UPON WAKING. EEG shows HYPSARRHYTHMIA. Q: What is the most likely diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Absence seizures are brief staring spells with 3 Hz spike-and-wave, not clusters of spasms with hypsarrhythmia.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Clusters of brief tonic/flexor-extensor spasms (often on waking) with the EEG finding of hypsarrhythmia define West syndrome (infantile spasms). It is treated with ACTH or vigabatrin (vigabatrin especially in tuberous sclerosis). Note: classically presents in infancy (3-12 months); the stem's stated age is atypical.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: There is no fever, and febrile seizures are generalized convulsions, not clustered spasms with hypsarrhythmia.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Myoclonic epilepsy shows brief shock-like jerks with polyspike-wave EEG, not hypsarrhythmia and the spasm clusters described.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Hypsarrhythmia on EEG is always pathological; this is not a normal variant.

questions.keyPoint: Clusters of infantile spasms + hypsarrhythmia on EEG = West syndrome; treat with ACTH or vigabatrin.

rows: Clustered flexor/extensor spasms, often on waking; developmental regression HIGH-YIELD SUMMARY
EEG; MRI; metabolic/genetic workup (e.g. tuberous sclerosis)
ACTH (or oral steroids) or vigabatrin (vigabatrin first if tuberous sclerosis)
West syndrome triad: spasms + hypsarrhythmia + developmental arrest; treat with ACTH/vigabatrin

flag.note: FILE ANSWER vs STANDARD TEACHING — File says: b (Infantile spasms - West syndrome) Standard (Nelson Paediatrics): b - correct diagnosis for hypsarrhythmia/spasms; classically infancy (3-12 months), so the stated age '4-year-old' is atypical/likely a typo for '4-month-old' Exam advice: Diagnosis correct; flag the age discrepancy (West syndrome is an infantile, not 4-year-old, condition).
flag.source: Study guide flag (file key vs standard teaching)

questions.stem: Same child with West syndrome (infantile spasms with hypsarrhythmia). Q: Best treatment choice?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Carbamazepine can worsen generalized/infantile epilepsies and is not used for infantile spasms.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Benzodiazepines are not the standard treatment for West syndrome.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: First-line therapy for infantile spasms is ACTH (or high-dose oral steroids) and/or vigabatrin; vigabatrin is preferred when the cause is tuberous sclerosis. Early treatment improves developmental outcome.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Levetiracetam is not first-line for infantile spasms (limited efficacy compared with ACTH/vigabatrin).

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Phenobarbital is not the recommended treatment for West syndrome.

questions.keyPoint: Infantile spasms are treated with ACTH (or steroids) or vigabatrin; vigabatrin first-line if tuberous sclerosis.

rows: Infantile spasms with hypsarrhythmia needing prompt treatment
ACTH/oral steroids or vigabatrin (vigabatrin first in tuberous sclerosis)
ACTH/vigabatrin are first-line for infantile spasms

flag.note: FILE ANSWER vs STANDARD TEACHING — File says: c (ACTH or vigabatrin) Standard (Nelson Paediatrics): c - correct Exam advice: File answer correct.
flag.source: Study guide flag (file key vs standard teaching)

questions.stem: A 48-YEAR-OLD presents with GRADUAL ONSET RIGHT-SIDED FACIAL WEAKNESS, including INABILITY TO WRINKLE HIS FOREHEAD. He also reports DECREASED TASTE on the ANTERIOR TONGUE and MILD EAR PAIN. There is NO LIMB WEAKNESS or other deficits. Q: What is the most likely diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: An upper motor neuron (central) facial palsy spares the forehead because of bilateral cortical innervation; here the forehead IS involved, indicating a lower motor neuron lesion.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Lower motor neuron facial weakness involving the forehead, with loss of taste on the anterior two-thirds of the tongue (chorda tympani) and ear pain, and no other deficits, is idiopathic Bell's palsy (CN VII palsy). Treat early with corticosteroids; protect the eye.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: MG causes bilateral fatigable weakness (ptosis, diplopia) rather than a unilateral fixed LMN facial palsy with taste loss.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Acoustic neuroma (vestibular schwannoma) presents with hearing loss, tinnitus and imbalance, with facial weakness only late; the picture here is isolated CN VII palsy.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Temporal arteritis causes headache, jaw claudication and visual loss in the elderly, not isolated facial nerve palsy.

questions.keyPoint: LMN facial palsy that involves the forehead (with taste loss/ear pain) = Bell's palsy; forehead sparing = central/UMN lesion.

rows: Unilateral facial weakness including forehead, loss of taste anterior tongue, ear pain
Usually clinical; exclude stroke/Ramsay Hunt if atypical
Early oral corticosteroids (prednisolone); eye protection; +/- antivirals
Forehead involvement distinguishes Bell's palsy (LMN) from a central facial palsy (UMN)

flag.note: FILE ANSWER vs STANDARD TEACHING — File says: b (Bell's palsy - peripheral facial nerve palsy) Standard (Oxford Handbook; Davidson's): b - correct Exam advice: File answer correct.
flag.source: Study guide flag (file key vs standard teaching)

questions.stem: Same 48-year-old with Bell's palsy. Q: What is the best initial treatment?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Early oral corticosteroids (e.g. prednisolone within 72 hours of onset) are the established first-line treatment for Bell's palsy and improve recovery; antivirals (aciclovir) are sometimes added in severe cases. Eye protection is essential. NOTE: the printed answer letter on this page is 'a', but option (a) is also labelled the answer in the file.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Carbamazepine treats trigeminal neuralgia, not Bell's palsy.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Imaging is not the initial treatment; reserved for atypical/progressive or non-resolving cases to exclude other causes.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Facial exercises help recovery but are adjunctive; steroids are the key initial pharmacological treatment.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Surgical decompression is rarely used and is not the initial treatment for Bell's palsy.

questions.keyPoint: Bell's palsy: start early oral corticosteroids (within 72h); add antivirals in severe cases; always protect the eye.

rows: Acute LMN facial palsy needing early steroids
Early corticosteroids improve Bell's palsy outcome; protect the eye Section 8 - Neurology

flag.note: FILE ANSWER vs STANDARD TEACHING — File says: a (High-dose steroids +/- acyclovir) Standard (Oxford Handbook; Cochrane): a - correct; steroids are first-line, antivirals adjunctive Exam advice: File answer correct.
flag.source: Study guide flag (file key vs standard teaching)

questions.stem: A 66-YEAR-OLD WOMAN complains of STIFFNESS AND WEAKNESS CLIMBING STAIRS. She has a history of HYPERTENSION and diet-controlled TYPE 2 DIABETES. On examination, there is MILD UPPER ARM WEAKNESS, HIP FLEXION 4-/5 BILATERALLY, with bilateral WASTING and FLICKERS OF FASCICULATIONS in the RIGHT QUADRICEPS. Knee extension is 4/5. DORSIFLEXION and PLANTAR FLEXION are STRONG. BRISK KNEE AND ANKLE REFLEXES are ELICITED, as well as a POSITIVE HOFFMANN'S and BABINSKI'S SIGN. SENSORY EXAMINATION and CRANIAL NERVES ARE NORMAL. Her BMI is 8.9, her pulse is regular and her BP is 178/97. Q: What is the most likely diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: MG causes fatigable weakness with ocular/bulbar involvement and no UMN signs; here there are fasciculations and brisk reflexes (mixed UMN/LMN), which MG never produces.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Diabetic neuropathy is predominantly sensory with reduced reflexes; this patient has preserved sensation with brisk reflexes and upgoing plantars, which excludes it.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Inflammatory myopathy causes proximal weakness but with normal/reduced reflexes and no UMN signs or fasciculations; the combination of fasciculations + brisk reflexes points elsewhere.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: The combination of mixed upper motor neuron signs (brisk reflexes, positive Hoffmann's and Babinski's) AND lower motor neuron signs (wasting, fasciculations, weakness) with normal sensation and normal cranial nerves is the hallmark of motor neurone disease (amyotrophic lateral sclerosis). Diagnosis is clinical + EMG; riluzole modestly prolongs survival. (The file ticks D.)

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: MS produces UMN signs but typically with sensory involvement, optic/cerebellar signs and lesions disseminated in time/space; pure mixed UMN+LMN signs with fasciculations and preserved sensation indicate MND, not MS.

questions.keyPoint: Mixed UMN signs (brisk reflexes, upgoing plantars, Hoffmann's) + LMN signs (wasting, fasciculations) with NO sensory loss = motor neurone disease (ALS).

rows: Combined UMN + LMN signs, fasciculations, wasting, no sensory loss, normal cranial sensation
EMG/nerve conduction; MRI to exclude cord pathology
MND = mixed UMN and LMN signs with preserved sensation

flag.note: FILE ANSWER vs STANDARD TEACHING — File says: D (Motor neurone disease) - marked with a tick Standard (Davidson's; Oxford Handbook): D - correct Exam advice: File answer correct. (Note: 'BMI 8.9' in the stem is implausibly low and likely a transcription error.)
flag.source: Study guide flag (file key vs standard teaching)

questions.stem: A 23-YEAR-OLD MAN IS STABBED IN THE NECK. Once stabilized, his MRI shows a RIGHT HEMISECTION OF THE CORD AT C6. Q: What is the expected result of this injury?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: The diaphragm is supplied by C3-5 (phrenic nerve); a lesion at C6 spares diaphragmatic function.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Absent sensation to temperature in the left hand
choices.explanation: In Brown-Sequard (cord hemisection), the spinothalamic tract (pain/temperature) decussates within a couple of segments of entry, so loss of pain/temperature is CONTRALATERAL and begins a few levels below the lesion. A right hemisection therefore causes loss of temperature sensation on the LEFT side. (The file ticks B.)

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Corticospinal (motor) fibres have already decussated in the medulla, so weakness is IPSILATERAL (right), not left, below the lesion.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Absent sensation to light touch in the left hand
choices.explanation: Dorsal column (fine touch/proprioception/vibration) loss is IPSILATERAL (right side), because these fibres ascend uncrossed until the medulla; left-sided light touch is preserved.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: At the level of the lesion there is an LMN pattern (the C6 biceps reflex would be reduced/absent ipsilaterally), not brisk; brisk reflexes appear below the lesion ipsilaterally.

questions.keyPoint: Brown-Sequard (cord hemisection): IPSILATERAL motor + dorsal column loss; CONTRALATERAL pain/temperature loss (spinothalamic crosses in the cord).

rows: Ipsilateral UMN weakness and dorsal-column loss; contralateral pain/temperature loss
Spinothalamic loss is contralateral; dorsal column and motor loss are ipsilateral

flag.note: FILE ANSWER vs STANDARD TEACHING — File says: B (Absent sensation to temperature in the left hand) - marked with a tick Standard (Oxford Handbook of Clinical Medicine; neuroanatomy): B - correct (contralateral spinothalamic loss) Exam advice: File answer correct.
flag.source: Study guide flag (file key vs standard teaching)

questions.stem: A 23-YEAR-OLD WOMAN complains that her RIGHT LEG HAS BECOME PROGRESSIVELY STIFF AND CLUMSY OVER THE LAST COUPLE OF WEEKS. She has been unable to go to work for 4 days. On examination, TONE IS INCREASED and there is a CATCH AT THE KNEE. Power is reduced to 3-4/5 in the right leg flexors. There is NO SENSORY INVOLVEMENT and the rest of the neurological exam is normal other than a PALE DISC ON OPHTHALMOSCOPY. On further questioning, she admits she has had TWO EPISODES OF BLURRED VISION in her right eye in the last two years. Each lasted a couple of weeks from which she FULLY RECOVERED. Q: What is the most appropriate initial treatment?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: NSAIDs do not treat an MS relapse; they have no effect on CNS demyelination.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Interferon-beta is a long-term disease-modifying therapy to reduce future relapses, not the treatment of the current acute relapse.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Rest alone does not shorten a relapse; active treatment with corticosteroids is indicated for a disabling relapse.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Methotrexate is not standard MS therapy and does not treat an acute relapse.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: This is a relapsing-remitting MS relapse (prior optic neuritis -> pale disc; now a corticospinal/myelopathic relapse). The appropriate INITIAL treatment of an acute relapse is a course of high-dose corticosteroids (oral high-dose methylprednisolone or IV) to shorten the relapse. Disease-modifying therapy is added for long-term prevention. (The file ticks E.)

questions.keyPoint: Acute MS relapse = high-dose corticosteroids (oral or IV methylprednisolone) to shorten the attack; interferon-beta/DMTs are for long-term prevention, not the acute relapse.

rows: RRMS with prior optic neuritis (pale disc) and new myelopathic relapse, no sensory loss
High-dose corticosteroids (oral/IV methylprednisolone) for the relapse; DMT (e.g. interferon-beta) for prevention
Treat acute MS relapse with steroids; prevent relapses with disease-modifying therapy

flag.note: FILE ANSWER vs STANDARD TEACHING — File says: E (A course of oral steroids) - marked with a tick Standard (Davidson's; NICE MS): E - correct for the acute relapse Exam advice: File answer correct.
flag.source: Study guide flag (file key vs standard teaching)

questions.topic: Q4 - Lesion localization (2): lacunar/internal capsule infarct
questions.stem: A 78-YEAR-OLD MAN COLLAPSES at a concert and is brought into accident and emergency. He seems to FOLLOW CLEAR ONE-STEP COMMANDS but gets very frustrated as he CANNOT ANSWER QUESTIONS. He is UNABLE TO LIFT HIS RIGHT HAND OR LEG. He has an IRREGULARLY IRREGULAR PULSE and his BLOOD PRESSURE is 149/87. He takes only ASPIRIN and FRUSEMIDE. Q: What is the most likely diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Right-sided hemiplegia (arm and leg) with expressive language disturbance (cannot answer questions but follows commands suggests dominant-hemisphere involvement) localizes to the LEFT hemisphere; the irregularly irregular pulse indicates atrial fibrillation as the embolic source. A left cortical (MCA-territory) infarct best fits a combined motor-plus-language cortical syndrome from a cardioembolic stroke. (The file ticks A.)

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: A right capsular lesion would cause LEFT-sided weakness and (in most people) no aphasia; this patient has right-sided weakness.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: An embolic source (AF) and the clinical picture favour ischaemic infarction; CT would be needed to confirm, but haemorrhage is less likely given AF and aspirin, and the question stem points to infarction.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: A pure capsular (lacunar) lesion typically causes pure motor hemiparesis WITHOUT cortical signs such as aphasia; the presence of language disturbance indicates cortical involvement, and AF favours infarct over haemorrhage.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Brainstem lesions cause crossed signs, cranial nerve palsies and reduced consciousness, not isolated right hemiplegia with aphasia.

questions.keyPoint: Right hemiplegia + language disturbance = LEFT (dominant) hemisphere cortical lesion; irregularly irregular pulse = AF, the likely cardioembolic source. Cortical signs (aphasia) point to cortex, not a pure lacunar/capsular lesion.

rows: Right hemiplegia, aphasia (cortical sign), atrial fibrillation
Thrombolysis/thrombectomy if eligible; later anticoagulation for AF
Aphasia indicates cortical (not pure lacunar) involvement; irregularly irregular pulse = AF embolic source

flag.note: FILE ANSWER vs STANDARD TEACHING — File says: A (Left cortical infarct) - marked with a tick Standard (Oxford Handbook; Davidson's): A - correct: dominant-hemisphere cortical infarct with AF as embolic source Exam advice: File answer correct.
flag.source: Study guide flag (file key vs standard teaching)

questions.stem: A 19-YEAR-OLD WOMAN COLLAPSES at a concert and is WITNESSED to have a TONIC-CLONIC SEIZURE lasting 2 MINUTES. When the paramedics arrive and ask her questions, she MUMBLES BUT NO-ONE CAN UNDERSTAND what she is saying. Only when the paramedics applies PRESSURE TO HER NAILBED does she OPEN HER EYES and REACH OUT WITH HER OTHER HAND TO RUB HER NAIL AND THEN PUSH HIM AWAY. Q: What is her Glasgow Coma Scale (GCS)?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: 8 would underestimate her responses (e.g. E2 V2 M4); her motor response of localizing to pain is M5 and verbal is incomprehensible sounds V2, eyes open to pain E2, giving 9.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: 11 overestimates; it would require, e.g., eyes open to speech (E3) and inappropriate words (V3) which exceed what is described.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: 10 is one above the correct score; it would need a higher verbal or eye component than the incomprehensible sounds (V2) and eye-opening to pain (E2) described.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Eyes open to pain (nailbed pressure) = E2; mumbling incomprehensible sounds = V2; reaching to the site of pain (localizing) = M5. Total = E2 + V2 + M5 = 9. (The file ticks D.)

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Duplicate of option A and still an underestimate; correct GCS is 9, not 8.

questions.keyPoint: GCS = Eyes (open to pain = 2) + Verbal (incomprehensible sounds = 2) + Motor (localizes to pain = 5) = 9.

rows: Eye 1-4, Verbal 1-5, Motor 1-6; localizing pain = M5
Airway protection if GCS <=8; treat underlying cause
Localizing to pain scores M5; eyes opening to pain E2; incomprehensible sounds V2

flag.note: FILE ANSWER vs STANDARD TEACHING — File says: D (9) - marked with a tick Standard (Glasgow Coma Scale): D - correct (E2 V2 M5 = 9) Exam advice: File answer correct. Note options A and E are both printed as '8' (printing error).
flag.source: Study guide flag (file key vs standard teaching)

questions.stem: A 79-YEAR-OLD MAN is admitted with LEFT HEMIPARESIS. CT reveals a MIDDLE CEREBRAL ARTERY INFARCT. Q: What is the most significant risk factor for stroke?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Hypertension is the single most important modifiable risk factor for stroke (both ischaemic and haemorrhagic); it underlies both large-vessel/embolic and small-vessel disease, and treating it gives the greatest reduction in stroke risk. (The file ticks A.) Annotation reads 'embolic ... AF'; note that for embolic stroke specifically AF is key, but the most significant overall risk factor remains hypertension.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Smoking is a significant modifiable risk factor but confers less attributable risk than hypertension.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Family history is a non-modifiable contributor but far less significant than hypertension.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Diabetes raises stroke risk but is less significant overall than hypertension.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Hyperlipidaemia contributes to atherosclerosis but is a weaker independent stroke risk factor than hypertension.

questions.keyPoint: Hypertension is the most important (modifiable) risk factor for stroke overall; atrial fibrillation is the key risk factor specifically for cardioembolic stroke.

rows: Modifiable: hypertension (greatest), smoking, diabetes, hyperlipidaemia, AF
BP, ECG (AF), glucose, lipids, carotid Doppler
Hypertension is the most important modifiable stroke risk factor

flag.note: FILE ANSWER vs STANDARD TEACHING — File says: A (Hypertension) - marked with a tick; margin annotation mentions embolic/AF Standard (Davidson's; Oxford Handbook): A - correct as the single most significant overall risk factor for stroke Exam advice: File answer correct. The handwritten margin note 'embolic/AF' refers to the embolic source in this AF-type case but does not change that hypertension is the leading risk factor.
flag.source: Study guide flag (file key vs standard teaching)

questions.stem: A 42-year-old WOMAN presents with ATAXIA. GADOLINIUM-ENHANCED MRI reveals MULTIPLE SUBCORTICAL WHITE MATTER LESIONS as well as ENHANCING LESIONS in the cerebellum and spinal cord. She is diagnosed with MS. Two months later she develops OPTIC NEURITIS. Q: What feature is associated with a milder disease course?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Older age at onset is generally associated with a worse, more progressive course, not a milder one.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Cerebellar/brainstem and motor presentations carry a worse prognosis than sensory or optic presentations.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Female sex is a recognised favourable prognostic factor in MS. Other good-prognosis features include young age at onset, relapsing-remitting pattern, sensory or optic neuritis presentation, complete recovery from attacks and long inter-attack intervals. Women tend to have a more benign relapsing-remitting course than men, who more often follow a primary progressive pattern.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The interval between the two episodes of two months
choices.explanation: A short interval between relapses indicates more active, aggressive disease and a worse prognosis; long inter-attack intervals are favourable.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: A high lesion load with multiple enhancing lesions on MRI predicts a worse, not milder, course.

questions.keyPoint: Favourable MS prognostic factors: female sex, young onset age, sensory/optic neuritis onset, relapsing-remitting course, full recovery from attacks, long inter-relapse intervals.

rows: Dissemination in space and time; optic neuritis, ataxia, white matter lesions
McDonald criteria - MRI lesions disseminated in space & time
MRI brain/cord with gadolinium; CSF oligoclonal bands; VEPs
Acute relapse: IV methylprednisolone; disease-modifying drugs (beta-interferon, etc.)
Progressive disability, optic atrophy, bladder dysfunction
Female sex and optic/sensory onset predict a milder course

flag.note: FILE ANSWER vs STANDARD TEACHING — File says: C (ticked) Standard (Davidson's Principles & Practice of Medicine): C - female sex is a good prognostic factor Exam advice: Agrees with standard teaching.
flag.source: Study guide flag (file key vs standard teaching)

questions.stem: A 71-year-old man with ATRIAL FIBRILLATION is seen following an episode of SYNCOPE. He describes getting a poor night's sleep and, on getting OUT OF BED IN THE MORNING, feeling DIZZY for a couple of seconds before the lights dimmed around him. He was a couple of seconds later by his wife who had WITNESSED THE EVENT. She says he WENT PALE AND FELL TO THE FLOOR AND HIS ARMS AND LEGS JERKED. AFTER WAKING HE WAS SHAKEN BUT WAS 'BACK TO NORMAL' A FEW MINUTES AFTER THE EVENT. His medication includes ASPIRIN, ATENOLOL and FRUSEMIDE. Q: What is the most likely diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Vasovagal (neurocardiogenic) syncope is typically provoked by prolonged standing, emotion, pain or heat with prodromal nausea/sweating, not by standing up from bed; the postural trigger here points elsewhere.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Syncope on standing up from bed in an elderly man taking a beta-blocker (atenolol) and a diuretic (frusemide) is classic postural/orthostatic hypotension. The brief limb jerks are a convulsive (anoxic) syncope, not true epilepsy, and rapid full recovery without post-ictal confusion supports a syncopal cause. Atenolol blunts the reflex tachycardia and frusemide causes volume depletion, both predisposing to orthostatic drops in BP.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Cardiogenic syncope (arrhythmia, outflow obstruction) usually occurs without warning and may be exertional; the clear postural trigger and culprit drugs make orthostatic hypotension more likely despite the AF.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: TIAs cause focal neurological deficits, not transient global loss of consciousness with rapid complete recovery.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: The brief limb jerks are reflex anoxic (convulsive syncope); the absence of post-ictal confusion, tongue-biting or incontinence and the clear postural trigger argue against primary epilepsy.

questions.keyPoint: Convulsive syncope (brief jerks) on standing in a patient on beta-blocker + diuretic is orthostatic hypotension, not epilepsy - rapid full recovery is the discriminator.

rows: LOC on standing; pallor; brief anoxic jerks; rapid full recovery
Fall in systolic BP >=20 mmHg (or diastolic >=10) on standing
Lying/standing BP; medication review; ECG to exclude arrhythmia
Review/stop offending drugs (atenolol, frusemide); rise slowly; fludrocortisone if refractory
Falls and fractures in the elderly
