Beta-blocker + diuretic + standing = orthostatic hypotension with convulsive syncope

questions.stem: A 41-year-old man complains of TERRIBLE HEADACHE. It started an hour ago, without warning, while stressed at work. It affects the RIGHT SIDE of his head. He scores it 11/10 in severity. When asked, he agrees that LIGHT DOES BOTHER HIM A LITTLE. He had a similar EPISODE SIX MONTHS AGO, experiencing very similar headaches OVER 2 WEEKS WHICH RESOLVED SPONTANEOUSLY. On observation, he looks quite DISTRESSED and prefers to PACE UP AND DOWN, UNABLE TO SIT STILL. Q: What is the diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: SAH gives a single thunderclap headache (worst-ever, sudden) often with neck stiffness; the recurrent stereotyped clustering over weeks six months ago does not fit.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Tension headache is bilateral, band-like, mild-moderate and not associated with restlessness or this severity.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Intracerebral haemorrhage usually produces focal neurological deficits and reduced consciousness, not recurrent self-limiting unilateral headaches.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Migraineurs typically prefer to lie still in a dark quiet room; the marked restlessness and pacing here are characteristic of cluster headache, not migraine.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Severe, strictly unilateral, abrupt-onset headache in a man who is agitated and pacing (unable to keep still), occurring in bouts/clusters over weeks then remitting for months, is classic cluster headache. Mild photophobia and autonomic features may occur. The restlessness is the key discriminator from migraine, where patients lie still.

questions.keyPoint: Cluster headache patients are restless and pace; migraineurs lie still in the dark. Bouts over weeks with months of remission = clusters.

rows: Severe unilateral periorbital pain, restlessness, clustering in bouts, autonomic features (lacrimation, rhinorrhoea, ptosis)
Clinical; MRI/MRA if atypical to exclude secondary cause
Acute: 100% O2 + SC sumatriptan; prevention: verapamil
Severe disability during bouts; suicidal ideation in chronic form

questions.stem: A 49-year-old man complains of SUDDEN ONSET, PAINLESS UNILATERAL VISUAL LOSS lasting about a minute. He describes 'a black curtain coming down'. His blood pressure is 158/90, heart rate 73 bpm. There is an AUDIBLE BRUIT ON AUSCULTATION of his RIGHT CAROTID artery. Q: What is the most likely diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Central retinal vein occlusion causes more gradual, often persistent visual loss with a 'stormy sunset' fundus, not transient curtain-like loss with carotid bruit.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Central retinal artery occlusion causes sudden but persistent (not transient one-minute) painless visual loss; the brief self-resolving nature here makes amaurosis fugax (a TIA) more likely.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Transient, painless, monocular visual loss described as a curtain descending and resolving within minutes, with an ipsilateral carotid bruit, is amaurosis fugax - a retinal TIA from carotid atheroembolism. It is an important warning sign of stroke and mandates carotid imaging and vascular risk-factor management.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Optic neuritis causes painful (pain on eye movement) subacute visual loss over hours-days with colour desaturation, typically in younger patients, not transient curtain-like loss.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Acute angle-closure glaucoma is intensely painful with a red eye, haloes and a fixed mid-dilated pupil, not painless transient loss with a carotid bruit.

questions.keyPoint: Transient painless monocular 'curtain' visual loss + carotid bruit = amaurosis fugax (retinal TIA); investigate carotids and treat vascular risk.

rows: Transient painless monocular visual loss, 'curtain', resolves in minutes; carotid bruit
Clinical; carotid Doppler to find stenosis
Antiplatelet (aspirin/clopidogrel), statin, BP control; carotid endarterectomy if >70% stenosis
Curtain over the eye + carotid bruit = amaurosis fugax

questions.stem: A 77-year-old woman is admitted to hospital with a URINARY TRACT INFECTION. She receives antibiotics and seems to be responding well. On the fourth day she is eating her lunch when she SUDDENLY DROPS HER FORK. She calls for the nurse who notices the LEFT SIDE OF HER FACE IS DROOPING. Q: What is the best next course of action?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: An acute focal neurological deficit (sudden facial droop and arm weakness) is a suspected stroke. An urgent non-contrast CT head is the immediate next step to distinguish ischaemic from haemorrhagic stroke before any antiplatelet or thrombolytic treatment is given. Imaging must precede thrombolysis or aspirin.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Thrombolysis can only be given after a CT has excluded haemorrhage and confirmed eligibility/time window; giving it before imaging is dangerous.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: MRI is more sensitive for early ischaemia but is slower and less available; CT is the standard first-line emergency imaging to exclude bleeding.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Aspirin 300 mg is given for ischaemic stroke but only after CT excludes haemorrhage.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Swallow assessment and keeping the patient nil by mouth are important supportive measures, but the immediate priority to guide treatment is urgent CT imaging.

questions.keyPoint: Suspected acute stroke: urgent non-contrast CT head FIRST to exclude haemorrhage before any aspirin or thrombolysis.

rows: Sudden focal deficit - facial droop, arm weakness, dropping objects
Non-contrast CT first; then ECG, glucose, bloods, swallow assessment
If ischaemic and within window: thrombolysis/thrombectomy; then aspirin 300 mg
Always CT before thrombolysis or aspirin

questions.stem: A 71-year-old RIGHT-HANDED MALE is brought in by ambulance at 17:50 having SUFFERED A COLLAPSE. His wife came home to find him on the floor unable to move his right arm or leg and unable to speak. Her call to the ambulance was logged at 17:30. He has a past medical history of well-controlled HYPERTENSION, ISCHAEMIC HEART DISEASE and ATRIAL FIBRILLATION for which he is on WARFARIN. He had a HERNIA REPAIR three months ago and his brother had a 'bleed in the brain' at the age of 67. Q: What is the absolute contraindication to thrombolysis in this patient?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: A family history of intracerebral haemorrhage is not a contraindication to thrombolysis.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Major surgery within ~14 days is a relative (not absolute) contraindication; a hernia repair three months ago is well outside the high-risk window.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: The onset is uncertain ('found on the floor', call logged 17:30) - he likely fell some time before being found, so the exact onset time is unknown and may be outside the 4.5-hour thrombolysis window. Unknown/unwitnessed onset (or onset >4.5 h) is an absolute contraindication to IV thrombolysis. Note: in this question's framing the time of onset cannot be confirmed within window, hence it is the bar to treatment. (Therapeutic anticoagulation with warfarin is also an absolute contraindication, but the option offered here is time of onset.)

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: He has not been shown to have a haemorrhagic stroke; that would require CT confirmation and the stem does not state it.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Therapeutic anticoagulation IS an absolute contraindication; however the answer selected on the paper is C (time of onset). See flag.

questions.keyPoint: Unknown or out-of-window onset time is an absolute contraindication to IV thrombolysis; therapeutic warfarin (raised INR) is also absolute.

rows: Confirm onset time; CT head; check INR
Alteplase only if onset confirmed <=4.5 h and no contraindication

flag.note: FILE ANSWER vs STANDARD TEACHING — File says: C (Time of onset, ticked) Standard (Oxford Handbook of Clinical Medicine; RCP stroke guidelines): Both C (uncertain/out-of-window onset) and E (therapeutic warfarin/raised INR) are absolute contraindications. The paper marks C. Exam advice: Accept C as marked, but be aware therapeutic warfarin is equally an absolute contraindication - confirm intended single best answer.
flag.source: Study guide flag (file key vs standard teaching)

questions.topic: Q13 - Back pain / Spinal stenosis (note: printed title 'Guillain-Barre syndrome' mismatches
questions.stem: A 69-year-old man presents with a SIX-MONTH HISTORY of PROGRESSIVE LOWER BACK PAIN WHICH RADIATES DOWN TO HIS BUTTOCK. He found the pain was EXACERBATED WHILE TAKING HIS DAILY MORNING WALK and noticed that it EASED GOING UPHILL but WORSENED DOWNHILL. He stopped his daily walks as a result and now WALKS ONLY SLOWLY TO THE SHOPS where he needs to, taking BREAKS TO SIT DOWN AND EASE THE PAIN. He has a history of HYPERTENSION, DIABETES and PROSTATIC HYPERPLASIA. Q: What is the diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Vascular (intermittent) claudication is brought on by walking and relieved by standing still; it is not relieved by walking uphill (which increases demand). Here pain eases uphill (spine flexed) and worsens downhill (spine extended), the reverse of vascular claudication.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Osteoporotic vertebral fracture causes acute focal back pain after minimal trauma, not a chronic posture-dependent claudication pattern.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Neurogenic (spinal) claudication from lumbar canal stenosis classically gives back/buttock/leg pain on walking that is RELIEVED BY SPINAL FLEXION - easier walking uphill and when leaning forward/sitting, and WORSE downhill (spine extended). Relief by sitting and the posture dependence distinguish it from vascular claudication. Common in older patients.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Simple sciatica from disc prolapse gives dermatomal leg pain often worse on sitting/bending and with a positive straight-leg raise, not the walking-uphill-better, downhill-worse claudication pattern.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Metastatic back pain is typically constant, nocturnal, progressive and unrelated to posture or walking direction.

questions.keyPoint: Neurogenic claudication (spinal stenosis): pain eases on flexion (uphill, sitting, leaning on trolley), worse on extension (downhill) - the opposite of vascular claudication.

rows: Back/leg pain on walking, relieved by flexion (sitting, uphill), worse downhill; older patient
MRI lumbar spine; exclude vascular claudication (pulses, ABPI)
Physiotherapy, analgesia; decompressive laminectomy if severe
Progressive disability; cauda equina if severe
Flexion relieves neurogenic claudication (walk uphill better)

flag.note: FILE ANSWER vs STANDARD TEACHING — File says: C (Spinal stenosis, ticked) Standard (Oxford Handbook of Clinical Medicine): C - the vignette describes posture-dependent neurogenic claudication. NOTE: the printed section title says 'Guillain-Barre syndrome' which does not match this back-pain vignette - likely a typesetting/labelling error. Exam advice: Answer C is correct for the vignette; ignore the mismatched printed title.
flag.source: Study guide flag (file key vs standard teaching)

questions.stem: A 31-year-old WOMAN presents to A&E with PROGRESSIVE DIFFICULTY WALKING associated with lower back pain. A few days ago she was TRIPPING OVER THINGS; now she has DIFFICULTY CLIMBING STAIRS. She describes TINGLING AND NUMBNESS IN BOTH HANDS WHICH MOVED UP TO HER ELBOWS; she is UNABLE TO WRITE. On examination, cranial nerves are intact but there is ABSENT SENSATION TO VIBRATION AND PIN PRICK in her UPPER LIMBS TO THE ELBOW and LOWER LIMBS TO THE HIP. POWER IS 3/5 in the ankles and 4-/5 at the hip with ABSENT REFLEXES and MUTE PLANTARS. Her BP is 124/85, pulse 68 and sats 98 per cent on air. She has a past medical history of TYPE 1 DIABETES and recently RECOVERED FROM AN EPISODE OF FOOD POISONING A MONTH OR TWO AGO. Q: What is the diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: MS produces UMN signs (brisk reflexes, upgoing plantars) and CNS lesions, whereas this patient has flaccid weakness with ABSENT reflexes and mute (down/absent) plantars - a peripheral/LMN picture.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Ascending symmetrical flaccid weakness with areflexia and sensory loss, a few weeks after a gastrointestinal infection (food poisoning, e.g. Campylobacter), is classic acute inflammatory demyelinating polyradiculoneuropathy (GBS). Absent reflexes and mute plantars confirm an LMN/peripheral process. Autonomic involvement and respiratory failure must be monitored (FVC).

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Myasthenia causes fatigable weakness with ocular/bulbar involvement and PRESERVED reflexes and sensation; this patient has sensory loss and areflexia.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Diabetic neuropathy is chronic and length-dependent (glove-and-stocking), not an acute ascending weakness over days following an infection.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: There is no active infection; rather a POST-infectious immune-mediated demyelination (GBS) is described.

questions.keyPoint: Ascending symmetrical flaccid weakness + areflexia weeks after GI/respiratory infection = GBS; monitor FVC for respiratory failure.

rows: Clinical; nerve conduction studies; CSF albuminocytological dissociation (high protein, normal cells)
IV immunoglobulin or plasma exchange; supportive ventilation if FVC falls
Areflexia + ascending weakness after Campylobacter = GBS

questions.topic: Q15 - Parkinson's: feature NOT commonly associated
questions.stem: A LEFT-HANDED 79-year-old man presents with a troublesome RESTING TREMOR of his LEFT hand. The tremor is evident in his WRITING. He has also noticed his WRITING IS SMALLER than it used to be (micrographia). He complains he has DIFFICULTY TURNING IN BED to get comfortable and his wife complains that he sometimes KICKS HER in the middle of the night. When he gets out of bed in the morning he feels a little WOOZY. On examination he BLINKS ABOUT THREE TIMES A MINUTE. His face does not show much emotion (HYPOMIMIA). GLABELLAR TAP IS POSITIVE. He has a SLOW, SHUFFLING GAIT, has difficulty STOPPING, STARTING AND TURNING. He holds his feet tightly apart to steady himself. When you pull him backwards he is unable to right himself and stumbles back (POSTURAL INSTABILITY). Q: Which of the signs and symptoms is NOT commonly associated with parkinsonism?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Postural instability is one of the cardinal features of Parkinson's disease, so it IS associated - not the answer.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: REM sleep behaviour disorder (acting out dreams, kicking the bed partner) is a well-recognised feature/prodrome of Parkinson's - it IS associated.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Hypomimia (mask-like reduced facial expression) is typical of parkinsonism - it IS associated.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: A broad-based gait is a feature of CEREBELLAR ataxia, NOT of Parkinson's disease. The parkinsonian gait is narrow-based, shuffling and festinant with reduced arm swing and difficulty starting/stopping/turning. Therefore broad-based gait is the feature NOT commonly associated with parkinsonism. (The stem's phrase about holding feet apart is to mislead; true parkinsonian gait is narrow-based.)

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Autonomic features (postural hypotension - the morning wooziness, constipation, urinary symptoms) are common in Parkinson's - it IS associated.

questions.keyPoint: Parkinsonian gait is narrow-based, shuffling and festinant; a BROAD-based gait indicates cerebellar disease, not parkinsonism.

rows: Resting tremor, bradykinesia, rigidity, postural instability, micrographia, hypomimia, REM sleep behaviour disorder, autonomic dysfunction
Clinical (UK PDS Brain Bank criteria); DaTscan if uncertain
Dementia, falls, autonomic failure, levodopa dyskinesias
Parkinson gait is narrow-based shuffling; broad-based = cerebellar

questions.stem: A 33-year-old WOMAN attends her six-month follow-up appointment for headache. They are MIGRAINOUS in nature but whereas she used to have them every couple of months, over the last THREE MONTHS she has experienced a CHRONIC DAILY HEADACHE which VARIES IN LOCATION AND CAN BE ANYWHERE FROM 3-7/10 SEVERITY. Her last migraine with aura was two months ago. She takes CO-CODAMOL QDS and IBUPROFEN regularly. Q: What is the best medical management?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: She has developed MEDICATION-OVERUSE (analgesic rebound) HEADACHE from regular daily co-codamol and ibuprofen - a chronic daily headache varying in location and intensity. The correct management is to WITHDRAW the overused analgesics (stop all the regular acute medication); the headache typically improves over the following weeks. Codeine-containing compounds are particularly likely to cause this.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Adding another regular simple analgesic perpetuates medication-overuse headache rather than treating it.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Triptans themselves cause medication-overuse headache if used frequently; starting a regular triptan would worsen the problem.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Propranolol is a migraine prophylactic but is futile while medication-overuse headache persists; the analgesics must be withdrawn first.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Continuing the overused analgesics maintains the rebound headache; the regular use must stop.

questions.keyPoint: Chronic daily headache in a migraineur who overuses analgesics/codeine = medication-overuse headache; treat by stopping the offending drugs.

rows: >=15 headache days/month with regular acute analgesic/triptan/opioid use
Clinical - headache on >=10-15 days/month with overused medication
Withdraw the overused analgesics; then optimise migraine prophylaxis
Daily codeine/analgesic use causes rebound headache - stop the drug

questions.stem: A 17-year-old girl is brought into A&E with GENERALIZED TONIC-CLONIC SEIZURE. Her mother had found her FITTING in her bedroom about 20 MINUTES AGO. The ambulance crew handover state that her SATS ARE 96 PER CENT ON 15 L OF OXYGEN and they have GIVEN HER TWO DOSES OF RECTAL DIAZEPAM but she HAS NOT STOPPED FITTING. Q: What is the most appropriate management?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: She has already received two doses of a benzodiazepine (rectal diazepam). The status epilepticus protocol allows a maximum of two benzodiazepine doses; a third risks respiratory depression, so the next step is a second-line agent, not more benzodiazepine.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Phenobarbital is a later/alternative second-line agent but in standard UK protocols IV phenytoin is the next step after benzodiazepines fail; phenobarbital is used if phenytoin is contraindicated or in specialist settings.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Rapid-sequence induction/intubation and general anaesthesia are reserved for refractory status after second-line treatment (phenytoin) has failed (usually after ~30 min), not before trying phenytoin.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: ITU involvement is appropriate if seizures continue, but the immediate pharmacological step now is to load with IV phenytoin while escalating.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: This is established status epilepticus that has not responded to two doses of benzodiazepine. The next step per the status epilepticus protocol is a second-line IV antiepileptic - IV phenytoin loading (~15-18 mg/kg with cardiac monitoring) (or levetiracetam/valproate in newer guidance). If this fails, proceed to RSI/anaesthesia and ITU.

questions.keyPoint: Status epilepticus: max TWO benzodiazepine doses, then IV phenytoin (second-line); if still fitting at ~30 min, RSI/anaesthesia and ITU.

rows: Clinical; check glucose, ABG, drug levels
Capillary glucose, bloods, ABG, antiepileptic levels, consider CT/EEG
ABC + O2; benzodiazepine x2; then IV phenytoin; then RSI/anaesthesia + ITU
After 2 benzo doses, give IV phenytoin - not a third benzo

questions.stem: A 72-year-old man with known EPILEPSY and HYPERTENSION is admitted with PNEUMONIA. His drug history includes ASPIRIN, PHENYTOIN, BENDROFLUMETHIAZIDE and AMLODIPINE. His heart rate is 67, blood pressure 170/93, sats 94 per cent on 1 L of oxygen. NEUROLOGICAL EXAMINATION IS NORMAL. His doctor requests blood tests including PHENYTOIN LEVEL. Q: What is the correct indication for this test?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Phenytoin levels are not measured routinely in a patient who is seizure-free and clinically well; therapeutic drug monitoring is done for specific indications.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: He has no clinical features of toxicity (nystagmus, ataxia, dysarthria, drowsiness) and neuro exam is normal, so checking purely for toxicity is not the prime indication.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Phenytoin has a narrow therapeutic index and zero-order (saturable) kinetics, and the main legitimate uses of measuring its level are to check ADHERENCE/COMPLIANCE, to guide dose adjustment, and to assess suspected toxicity. In a stable, seizure-free, neurologically normal patient the appropriate indication offered here is confirming compliance with therapy. (Phenytoin is highly protein-bound, so interpret with albumin.)

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: He is seizure-free so the therapeutic effect is being achieved clinically; routinely confirming a 'therapeutic level' is not the prime indication, especially as efficacy is judged clinically.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Reassurance is not a valid laboratory-test indication.

questions.keyPoint: Phenytoin therapeutic drug monitoring is indicated to assess compliance, guide dosing, and check for toxicity - not as a routine check.

rows: Narrow therapeutic index, zero-order kinetics, highly protein-bound
Adjust dose carefully (small changes cause large level changes)
Toxicity: nystagmus, ataxia, dysarthria; gum hypertrophy long-term
Phenytoin levels checked for compliance, toxicity and dose adjustment

questions.stem: A 23-year-old WOMAN is seen in clinic for RECURRENT FUNNY TURNS. She is NOT AWARE of them, but her family and friends have noticed them. They say she LOOKS AROUND THE ROOM, PICKING AT HER CLOTHES AND SOMETIMES YAWNS, then she COMES BACK AFTER A MINUTE. She can GET DROWSY AFTER THESE EPISODES. Q: What seizure type does this patient describe?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Absence seizures (typically childhood) are brief (<10 s) blank stares with abrupt onset/offset and NO post-ictal drowsiness or complex automatisms; the minute-long event with automatisms and post-ictal drowsiness here is not absence.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Generalised tonic-clonic seizures involve loss of consciousness with stiffening then jerking of all limbs, not retained semipurposeful automatisms.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Simple partial (focal aware) seizures occur with PRESERVED awareness; here awareness is impaired (she is unaware and others notice), making it complex partial.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Focal seizures with impaired awareness (complex partial), usually temporal lobe, present with altered awareness, automatisms (lip-smacking, picking at clothes, looking around, yawning) lasting around a minute, followed by post-ictal confusion/drowsiness. The unawareness plus automatisms plus post-ictal drowsiness are classic.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: 'Generalised' is too non-specific and implies bilateral involvement from onset; this is a focal (partial) seizure with automatisms arising from one (temporal) region.

questions.keyPoint: Impaired awareness + automatisms (picking at clothes, lip-smacking) + post-ictal drowsiness = complex partial (focal impaired-awareness) seizure, usually temporal lobe.

rows: Complex partial (focal impaired-awareness) seizure - temporal lobe
Altered awareness, automatisms, ~1 min, post-ictal confusion
Automatisms + impaired awareness = complex partial seizure

questions.stem: You are asked to perform a lower limb peripheral neurological examination on a 45-year-old DIABETIC MALE. The patient has NORMAL TONE, 5/5 power, normal plantars and proprioception. However, you notice that the patient DOES NOT RESPOND TO ANY SENSORY STIMULUS ON THE MEDIAL SIDE OF THE RIGHT LOWER LEG. Q: Which dermatome is affected?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: L1 supplies the inguinal/groin region, not the medial lower leg.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: L2 covers the anterior/upper thigh, not the medial lower leg.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: L3 covers the lower medial thigh and area around the knee, above the affected region.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: The L4 dermatome covers the MEDIAL aspect of the LOWER LEG (and the medial malleolus/medial foot). Loss of sensation over the medial lower leg therefore localises to L4. (L4 also supplies the knee jerk.)

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: L5 supplies the LATERAL lower leg and dorsum of the foot (big toe), not the medial lower leg.

questions.keyPoint: L4 dermatome = medial lower leg and medial malleolus; L5 = lateral leg/dorsum of foot/big toe; S1 = sole/lateral foot.

rows: Numbness over medial lower leg/medial malleolus
Nerve conduction studies; MRI lumbar spine if radiculopathy suspected
Treat underlying cause (e.g. diabetes, L4 root compression)
L4 = medial lower leg; L5 = lateral leg/big toe; S1 = little toe/sole

questions.topic: Q21 - Lesion pattern location (1) - spinal cord
questions.stem: On examination, a patient has 5/5 POWER IN ALL MUSCLE GROUPS OF HIS UPPER LIMBS, 0/5 POWER IN ALL THE MUSCLE GROUPS OF HIS LOWER LIMBS. CRANIAL NERVES ARE INTACT. Q: Where is the lesion?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: A primary muscle (myopathic) disorder would not produce a sharp level between fully normal upper limbs and totally paralysed lower limbs; myopathy is usually proximal and symmetrical without a sensory/motor level.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: NMJ disease (e.g. myasthenia) causes fatigable, often ocular/bulbar weakness, not a clear-cut paraplegia with normal upper limbs.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: A peripheral neuropathy would give a length-dependent glove-and-stocking pattern, not selective complete paralysis of all lower-limb groups with completely normal arms.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: A discrete motor level - completely normal upper limbs (5/5) with complete lower-limb paralysis (0/5) and intact cranial nerves - localises to a thoracic SPINAL CORD lesion. A cord lesion produces a sensory/motor level, with everything below the lesion affected and everything above (including arms and cranial nerves) spared.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: A single brain (cerebral) lesion would more typically cause hemiparesis with possible cranial nerve/face involvement, not symmetrical paraplegia with completely spared arms; bilateral leg paralysis with a clear level points to the cord.

questions.keyPoint: A clear motor 'level' (normal above, paralysed below, cranial nerves spared) localises to the spinal cord (thoracic cord here).

rows: Motor/sensory level; normal arms, paralysed legs, intact cranial nerves
Urgent MRI spine; bloods for cause
Treat cause (decompression for compression, steroids for MS, etc.)
A sensory/motor level = spinal cord lesion

questions.stem: On examination, a patient has 5/5 POWER IN HIS UPPER LIMBS, 0/5 POWER IN HIS LOWER LIMBS. Further examination reveals a SENSORY LEVEL AT THE UMBILICUS. CRANIAL NERVES ARE INTACT. Q: Where is the lesion?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: C4 supplies the shoulder/clavicle region; a C4 cord level would also affect the upper limbs, which are normal here.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: T4 corresponds to the nipple line, which is above the umbilicus; the sensory level here is at the umbilicus (T10).

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: The UMBILICUS corresponds to the T10 dermatome, so a sensory level at the umbilicus localises the cord lesion to T10. The combination of normal arms, paraplegia and a T10 sensory level confirms a thoracic cord lesion at this level.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: L1 corresponds to the inguinal/groin region (below the umbilicus), not the umbilical level.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: L3 corresponds to the lower medial thigh/knee, well below the umbilicus.

questions.keyPoint: Key sensory landmarks: T4 = nipple, T10 = umbilicus, L1 = inguinal ligament/groin.

rows: Paraplegia with sensory level at the umbilicus

questions.topic: Q23 - Lesion pattern location (2) - left motor cortex
questions.stem: A patient is unable to move his RIGHT ARM OR LEG. When asked to SMILE, the LEFT SIDE OF HIS MOUTH DROOPS. Q: Where is the lesion?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: A left motor cortex (UMN) lesion would cause right-sided weakness AND a right-sided (contralateral) lower facial droop - the face and limb weakness would be on the SAME (right) side. Here the limb and facial weakness are on OPPOSITE sides, so this single-cortex option does not fit.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: A right cortical lesion would weaken the LEFT arm and leg, but this patient's arm/leg weakness is on the RIGHT.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: A LEFT BRAINSTEM (pontine) lesion produces a CROSSED picture: it interrupts the corticospinal tract before decussation (causing CONTRALATERAL right arm/leg weakness) and damages the ipsilateral lower motor neurone of the LEFT facial nerve nucleus (causing IPSILATERAL left facial weakness). Right body + left face weakness = crossed signs = brainstem lesion on the left.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: A right brainstem lesion would give left limb weakness with a right facial palsy - the opposite crossed pattern to that described.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: A cervical cord lesion cannot cause a facial droop, as the facial nerve nucleus is in the pons; the facial involvement localises the lesion to the brainstem.

questions.keyPoint: Crossed signs (one side face, opposite side body) localise to the brainstem; an LMN facial palsy plus contralateral hemiparesis = ipsilateral brainstem lesion.

rows: Ipsilateral LMN cranial nerve palsy + contralateral limb weakness

questions.topic: Q24 - Visual pathway lesion (RAPD / relative afferent lesion)
questions.stem: A LIGHT IS SHONE INTO A PATIENT'S RIGHT EYE AND IT CONSTRICTS. When MOVED TO THE LEFT EYE, THE LEFT EYE CONSTRICTS. When moved BACK TO THE RIGHT EYE, THE RIGHT EYE DILATES (swinging light test). Q: What is the diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: A complete afferent (optic nerve) lesion would give NO direct or consensual response from that eye at all; here the right eye does constrict initially, so the defect is relative/partial, not a complete afferent lesion.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: An efferent (oculomotor/parasympathetic) lesion would cause a fixed dilated pupil that fails to constrict to BOTH direct and consensual light; here the pupils do constrict, so the efferent pathway is intact.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: This is the swinging-light test demonstrating a RELATIVE AFFERENT PUPILLARY DEFECT (RAPD / Marcus Gunn pupil) of the RIGHT eye. When light swings back to the affected right eye, the reduced afferent (optic nerve) signal means the pupil DILATES rather than maintaining constriction. It indicates a partial/relative defect of the right optic nerve (e.g. optic neuritis) relative to the normal left eye.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: There is no such recognised entity producing this swinging-light finding; the abnormality is afferent (optic nerve), demonstrated by paradoxical dilatation on the affected side.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Paradoxical dilatation of a pupil when light swings to it is abnormal - a positive RAPD - not a normal response.

questions.keyPoint: Swinging-light test: affected pupil DILATES when light returns to it = relative afferent pupillary defect (Marcus Gunn pupil), e.g. optic neuritis.

rows: Relative afferent pupillary defect (RAPD / Marcus Gunn pupil) - right optic nerve
Affected pupil dilates on the swinging-light test; reduced afferent input
Visual acuity, fundoscopy, VEPs, MRI optic nerves
Treat cause (e.g. IV steroids for optic neuritis) HIGH-YIELD SUMMARY
RAPD = affected pupil paradoxically dilates to the swinging light

questions.stem: A 55-year-old WOMAN complains of DOUBLE VISION. She finds that she is more TIRED than usual and has DIFFICULTY CLIMBING STAIRS, especially when they are very long. She has difficulty getting items off high shelves at work and lately even brushing her hair is a problem. During the consultation, her VOICE FADES AWAY during conversation. REFLEXES ARE PRESENT and equal throughout. Q: Which sign or symptom is most indicative of myasthenia gravis?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Proximal weakness occurs in myasthenia but is non-specific (also seen in myopathies); it is not the hallmark feature.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Reflexes are typically preserved in myasthenia, but normal reflexes are non-specific and do not by themselves indicate myasthenia.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Diplopia is a common presenting feature but is not specific to myasthenia; the defining characteristic is its fatigability.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: FATIGABILITY - weakness that WORSENS WITH REPEATED OR SUSTAINED ACTIVITY and improves with rest - is the hallmark of myasthenia gravis. The progressive tiring (worse climbing long stairs, voice fading during conversation, worse later in the day) reflects depletion of acetylcholine receptors at the neuromuscular junction. It is the most specific/indicative feature.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Bulbar features (voice fading, dysphagia) are common in myasthenia but, like diplopia, are a manifestation rather than the defining hallmark, which is fatigability.

questions.keyPoint: Fatigability (weakness worsening with use, improving with rest) is the hallmark of myasthenia gravis.

rows: Fatigable weakness, diplopia, ptosis, bulbar/proximal weakness; worse with use
Anti-AChR antibodies (anti-MuSK if negative); repetitive nerve stimulation (decrement); CT thymus
Anti-AChR antibodies, EMG, CT chest for thymoma
Fatigability worsening through the day = myasthenia

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man complains of DOUBLE VISION. He finds that he is tired all the time and has DIFFICULTY CLIMBING STAIRS. He says difficulty getting items off high shelves at work and lately even BRUSHING HIS HAIR is a problem. REFLEXES ARE ABSENT BUT ELICITED AFTER EXERCISE (post-exercise facilitation). There is SHOULDER ABDUCTION WEAKNESS which is INITIALLY 4-/5 BUT ON REPEATED TESTING IS 4+/5 (improves with repetition). Q: What pathology is associated with this female's diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Thyrotoxic myopathy causes proximal weakness but does not show post-exercise facilitation or absent reflexes that reappear with exercise, and is not the classic associated pathology here.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Peptic ulcer disease has no recognised association with Lambert-Eaton syndrome.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Diabetes causes peripheral neuropathy, not the presynaptic NMJ facilitation pattern of Lambert-Eaton.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Stroke causes focal UMN deficits, not fatigable weakness that improves with repetition.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: The picture - weakness that IMPROVES with repeated activity (post-exercise facilitation) and absent reflexes that return after exercise - is LAMBERT-EATON MYASTHENIC SYNDROME, a presynaptic disorder caused by antibodies to voltage-gated calcium channels. It is a paraneoplastic syndrome most strongly associated with SMALL-CELL LUNG CARCINOMA. (Contrast with myasthenia, where strength fatigues with use.)

questions.keyPoint: Lambert-Eaton: weakness and reflexes IMPROVE with repeated use (facilitation); strongly associated with small-cell lung cancer (anti-VGCC antibodies).

rows: Proximal weakness improving with use, absent reflexes that return after exercise, autonomic features
Anti-voltage-gated calcium channel antibodies; EMG incremental response on repetitive stimulation
Anti-VGCC antibodies, EMG, CT chest for small-cell lung cancer
LEMS = improves with use + small-cell lung cancer

questions.topic: Q27 - Cranial nerve palsy (UMN facial / stroke)
questions.stem: On observation, a patient has a LEFT FACIAL DROOP. On closer examination his NASOLABIAL FOLD IS FLATTENED. When asked to smile, the LEFT CORNER OF HIS MOUTH DROOPS. He is unable to keep his CHEEKS PUFFED OUT. Eye closure is ONLY SLIGHTLY WEAKER COMPARED TO THE RIGHT and HIS FOREHEAD WRINKLES when he is asked to look up. Q: What is the diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Forehead SPARING (forehead still wrinkles, eye closure only slightly weak) with lower-face weakness indicates an UPPER MOTOR NEURONE facial palsy. Because the forehead has bilateral cortical innervation, a UMN lesion spares it. The LEFT lower-face weakness localises to the contralateral RIGHT hemisphere - a right middle cerebral artery (cortical) stroke.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: A parotid tumour compresses the facial nerve trunk causing a LMN palsy affecting the whole side of the face including the forehead - here the forehead is spared, so it is UMN.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: A LEFT-sided lesion would cause RIGHT-sided facial weakness; this patient's weakness is on the LEFT, so the lesion is right-sided.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Bell's palsy is a LMN facial nerve palsy that involves the FOREHEAD (cannot wrinkle/raise eyebrow, cannot close eye fully) - forehead sparing here excludes it.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: A cerebellopontine angle lesion (e.g. acoustic neuroma) damages the facial nerve causing a LMN palsy with forehead involvement, plus hearing/balance signs - not a forehead-sparing UMN pattern.

questions.keyPoint: Forehead SPARED = UMN facial palsy (contralateral cortical/stroke); forehead INVOLVED = LMN palsy (Bell's, parotid, CPA lesion).

rows: Upper motor neurone facial palsy from right MCA stroke
Contralateral lower-face weakness with forehead sparing
Forehead sparing = UMN; forehead involved = LMN (Bell's)

questions.stem: A FEMALE presents with DIPLOPIA. On closer examination, when asked to LOOK RIGHT, her LEFT EYE STAYS IN THE MIDLINE but HER RIGHT EYE MOVES RIGHT AND STARTS JERKING (nystagmus of the abducting eye, failure of adduction of the other eye). Q: What is the diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Myasthenia can mimic many ophthalmoplegias but the specific pattern described (failure of adduction of one eye with nystagmus of the abducting eye on lateral gaze) is the classic sign of an internuclear ophthalmoplegia from an MLF lesion, characteristically MS.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Vertigo is a symptom of a spinning sensation, not an eye-movement disorder, and does not explain the failure of adduction.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Cerebellar disease causes gaze-evoked nystagmus and dysmetria but not the dissociated adduction failure of internuclear ophthalmoplegia.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: This is an INTERNUCLEAR OPHTHALMOPLEGIA (INO): on looking to one side the adducting eye fails to cross the midline while the abducting eye develops nystagmus, due to a lesion of the MEDIAL LONGITUDINAL FASCICULUS. In a young woman, bilateral or unilateral INO is classically caused by MULTIPLE SCLEROSIS (the most common cause in the young).

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Peripheral neuropathy affects limb sensation/motor function, not the central MLF pathway responsible for conjugate gaze.

questions.keyPoint: Internuclear ophthalmoplegia (failed adduction + nystagmus of abducting eye) from an MLF lesion is classically MS in a young patient.

rows: Internuclear ophthalmoplegia (MLF lesion) due to MS
Failure of adduction one eye + nystagmus of abducting eye on lateral gaze
INO in a young woman = MS (MLF lesion)

questions.stem: A neurologist is examining a patient. She takes the patient's MIDDLE FINGER and FLICKS THE DISTAL PHALANX, her THUMB CONTRACTS IN RESPONSE. Q: What sign has been elicited?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Chvostek's sign is twitching of the facial muscles when tapping over the facial nerve, a sign of HYPOCALCAEMIA, not a finger-flick test.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: The glabellar tap (Myerson's sign) is persistent blinking when tapping the forehead, seen in Parkinsonism - it involves the face, not the finger.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Flicking the distal phalanx of the MIDDLE finger and observing reflex flexion/adduction of the THUMB (and index finger) is HOFFMANN'S SIGN, the upper-limb equivalent of an upgoing plantar. A positive Hoffmann's indicates an UPPER MOTOR NEURONE (corticospinal) lesion, e.g. cervical myelopathy.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Tinel's sign is tingling in the median nerve distribution when tapping over the carpal tunnel (carpal tunnel syndrome), not a thumb contraction on finger flick.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Babinski's sign is dorsiflexion of the great toe on stroking the sole - a foot test, not a finger flick (though it is the lower-limb UMN equivalent of Hoffmann's).

questions.keyPoint: Hoffmann's sign (thumb flexes when middle-finger nail is flicked) indicates an upper motor neurone lesion - the arm equivalent of an upgoing plantar.

rows: Hoffmann's sign - upper motor neurone (corticospinal) lesion
Reflex thumb flexion when distal phalanx of middle finger flicked
Clinical UMN sign; MRI cervical spine/brain for cause
Treat underlying UMN pathology (e.g. cervical myelopathy decompression)

questions.stem: A 69-year-old man is taken to his GP by his concerned wife. She complains that he has NOT BEEN HIMSELF for the last year. He has slowly become WITHDRAWN and STOPPED WORKING ON HIS HOBBIES. Now she is concerned that he often FORGETS TO BRUSH HIS TEETH. She has noticed he sometimes STRUGGLES TO FIND THE RIGHT WORD and this has gradually become more noticeable over the last couple of months. She presented today because she was surprised to come home to find him NAKED AND URINATING IN THE LIVING ROOM (DISINHIBITION). He has a history of HYPERTENSION and is an EX-SMOKER. Q: The most likely diagnosis is:

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Depression can cause pseudodementia with withdrawal, but socially inappropriate disinhibition (undressing/urinating in public) and prominent personality/behaviour change with word-finding difficulty point to a frontotemporal dementia.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Early personality and behavioural change - social DISINHIBITION (undressing and urinating in the living room), apathy/withdrawal, loss of interest in hobbies, neglect of personal care, and language/word-finding difficulty - in a relatively younger patient are characteristic of FRONTOTEMPORAL DEMENTIA. Memory may be relatively preserved early, with behaviour/personality and language affected first, distinguishing it from Alzheimer's.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Alzheimer's typically begins with prominent SHORT-TERM MEMORY loss rather than early disinhibited behaviour and personality change; the striking behavioural disinhibition here favours frontotemporal dementia.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Vascular dementia (suggested by his vascular risk factors) typically follows a stepwise course with focal neurological signs; the gradual personality/behavioural decline with disinhibition fits frontotemporal dementia better.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Dementia with Lewy bodies features fluctuating cognition, visual hallucinations and parkinsonism, none of which are described here.

questions.keyPoint: Early personality/behaviour change with disinhibition and language difficulty (memory relatively spared) = frontotemporal (Pick's) dementia.

rows: Early personality/behaviour change, disinhibition, apathy, language difficulty, relatively preserved memory early
MRI brain, neuropsychometry; rule out reversible causes
Supportive; behavioural management (cholinesterase inhibitors not helpful)
Disinhibition + personality change first = frontotemporal dementia

questions.topic: Q31 - Upgoing plantars / absent ankle jerks (mixed UMN+LMN) - cause NOT producing this
questions.stem: A clinical scenario asking which condition does NOT cause the combination of ABSENT ANKLE JERKS (a lower motor neurone sign) AND UP-GOING PLANTARS (an upper motor neurone sign). [Annotation on page notes hyporeflexia is the LMN sign and upgoing toes the UMN sign.] Q: Which of the following is NOT a cause of absent ankle jerks and up-going plantars?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Friedreich's ataxia classically causes absent ankle jerks with extensor plantars (combined LMN + UMN signs), so it IS a cause - not the answer.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Subacute combined degeneration of the cord from B12 deficiency causes absent ankle reflexes (dorsal column/peripheral) with upgoing plantars (corticospinal), so it IS a recognised cause.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Multiple sclerosis is a purely CENTRAL (CNS) demyelinating disease and characteristically produces UPPER MOTOR NEURONE signs - BRISK reflexes and upgoing plantars. It does NOT typically cause absent (lower motor neurone) ankle jerks, because it does not affect the peripheral nerves/anterior horn. Therefore MS is NOT a cause of the mixed absent-ankle-jerk + extensor-plantar pattern.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Cord compression at the conus/cauda level (or compression damaging both the local roots and the corticospinal tracts) can give absent ankle jerks with upgoing plantars, so it IS a recognised cause.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Motor neurone disease combines LMN signs (wasting, absent reflexes) and UMN signs (upgoing plantars) in the same patient, classically producing this mixed pattern - so it IS a cause.

questions.keyPoint: Causes of absent ankle jerks + upgoing plantars (mixed LMN+UMN): Friedreich's ataxia, subacute combined degeneration (B12), MND, taboparesis, conus lesions. MS gives pure UMN signs (brisk reflexes).

rows: Mixed UMN + LMN signs (absent ankle jerks + extensor plantars)
Lost ankle reflexes with upgoing toes
Identify cause: Friedreich's, B12 deficiency, MND, cord/conus lesion, taboparesis
B12, MRI spine, nerve conduction, genetic tests HIGH-YIELD SUMMARY
MS gives BRISK reflexes (pure UMN) - not absent ankle jerks

questions.stem: A patient has DIFFICULTY WALKING. His gait is unsteady. He seems to have difficulty RAISING HIS RIGHT LEG and SWINGS IT ROUND IN AN ARC AS HE WALKS. He HOLDS HIS RIGHT ARM AND WRIST FLEXED. Q: What type of gait does this patient exhibit?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: A HEMIPLEGIC GAIT follows an upper motor neurone (e.g. post-stroke) lesion: the affected leg is held stiff/extended and is swung outward in a CIRCUMDUCTING ARC, while the ipsilateral arm is held FLEXED and adducted. The unilateral flexed arm plus circumduction of the leg is classic.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: A scissoring (spastic diplegic) gait is BILATERAL, with both legs adducted and crossing over each other - typical of cerebral palsy - not a unilateral circumducting leg with a flexed arm.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: A high-stepping gait results from foot drop (LMN, e.g. common peroneal palsy); the patient lifts the foot high to clear the toes, without the flexed arm posture.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: 'Spastic' (paraparetic) gait usually implies bilateral stiff legs; the description here is unilateral with a flexed arm, i.e. specifically hemiplegic.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: A stomping (sensory ataxic) gait occurs with proprioceptive loss - the patient slaps the feet down and watches the ground - not the circumducting hemiplegic pattern.

questions.keyPoint: Hemiplegic gait = leg circumduction (swung in an arc) with a flexed adducted arm, typically after a stroke (UMN lesion).

rows: Circumducting stiff leg, flexed arm/wrist, unilateral

questions.stem: A patient is admitted with a STROKE. On examination of her visual fields, she is UNABLE TO SEE IN THE RIGHT LOWER QUADRANT of her field. Q: Where is the lesion?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: An optic chiasm lesion causes a bitemporal hemianopia (loss of both temporal fields), not an isolated single lower quadrant defect.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: A right lower homonymous quadrantanopia results from damage to the contralateral (LEFT) optic radiation fibres passing through the PARIETAL lobe (the superior radiation, carrying the inferior visual field). Mnemonic PITS: Parietal lesions cause Inferior field defects; Temporal lesions cause Superior field defects. A left parietal stroke therefore causes a right inferior quadrantanopia.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: A temporal lobe lesion causes a SUPERIOR quadrantanopia ('pie in the sky'), and a right-sided lesion would affect the LEFT field - the opposite side and quadrant to that described.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: A right-sided radiation lesion produces a LEFT-sided field defect; this patient's defect is on the RIGHT, so the lesion is on the left.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: An optic nerve lesion causes monocular visual loss in that one eye, not a homonymous quadrantic defect affecting the same side of both eyes' fields.

questions.keyPoint: PITS: Parietal radiation lesions cause Inferior field loss; Temporal lesions cause Superior ('pie in the sky') loss. Right inferior quadrantanopia = left parietal lesion.

rows: Loss of right lower quadrant in both eyes after left parietal stroke

questions.stem: A 43-year-old WOMAN presents with DIZZINESS to A&E. It STARTED SUDDENLY this morning; she AWOKE WITH A HEADACHE and the dizziness started when she SAT UP IN BED. She describes the ROOM SPINNING for a couple of minutes. IT SETTLES IF SHE KEEPS STILL, but RETURNS ON MOVEMENT. There is NO TINNITUS OR DEAFNESS, but some NAUSEA AND NO VOMITING. Q: The most likely diagnosis is:

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: A brainstem stroke would usually produce additional neurological signs (diplopia, dysarthria, ataxia, crossed sensory/motor signs) and persistent symptoms, not brief positional vertigo that settles when still.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: BPPV gives brief episodes (seconds to a couple of minutes) of rotational vertigo TRIGGERED BY HEAD MOVEMENT/POSITION CHANGE (sitting up, rolling over), settling when still, with NO hearing loss or tinnitus. It is caused by displaced otoconia in the semicircular canals and is confirmed by the Dix-Hallpike manoeuvre and treated with the Epley manoeuvre.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Meniere's disease features the triad of vertigo, fluctuating sensorineural HEARING LOSS and TINNITUS (with aural fullness), and attacks last longer (minutes-hours); the absence of tinnitus/deafness makes it unlikely.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Vestibular neuronitis causes a single prolonged episode of CONSTANT vertigo lasting days (often post-viral), not brief recurrent positional vertigo that settles when still.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Vestibular migraine causes vertigo with migrainous features but is not characteristically triggered purely by positional change settling on keeping still; the positional pattern here is classic for BPPV.

questions.keyPoint: BPPV: brief positional vertigo on head movement, settles when still, NO hearing loss/tinnitus; diagnose with Dix-Hallpike, treat with Epley.

rows: Brief positional rotational vertigo, no hearing loss/tinnitus, triggered by movement
Positional, brief, no hearing loss = BPPV (Dix-Hallpike/Epley)

questions.stem: A 40-year-old WOMAN seen in clinic has MULTIPLE FLESHY NODULES and several LIGHT BROWN, ROUND MACULES with a smooth border on her back, arms and legs (CAFE-AU-LAIT SPOTS). There are also FRECKLES under her arms (axillary freckling). Q: What is the underlying disorder?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Multiple cutaneous neurofibromas (fleshy nodules), CAFE-AU-LAIT macules and AXILLARY (or inguinal) FRECKLING are diagnostic features of NEUROFIBROMATOSIS TYPE 1 (von Recklinghausen disease). Other features include Lisch nodules (iris hamartomas), optic gliomas and bony lesions. NF1 is autosomal dominant (chromosome 17).

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: NF2 is dominated by BILATERAL VESTIBULAR SCHWANNOMAS (acoustic neuromas) and meningiomas with few skin lesions; the prominent cafe-au-lait spots, neurofibromas and axillary freckling here are typical of NF1, not NF2.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Tuberous sclerosis features adenoma sebaceum (facial angiofibromas), ash-leaf macules (hypopigmented), shagreen patches and epilepsy/learning difficulty, not multiple neurofibromas with cafe-au-lait spots and axillary freckling.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: HHT (Osler-Weber-Rendu) presents with mucocutaneous telangiectasia and recurrent epistaxis/AVMs, not neurofibromas and cafe-au-lait macules.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Sturge-Weber features a facial port-wine stain (capillary malformation) in the trigeminal distribution with leptomeningeal angioma and seizures, not multiple neurofibromas and cafe-au-lait spots.

questions.keyPoint: Cafe-au-lait macules + cutaneous neurofibromas + axillary/inguinal freckling = neurofibromatosis type 1 (also Lisch nodules, optic glioma).

rows: Cafe-au-lait spots, neurofibromas, axillary freckling, Lisch nodules, optic glioma
Clinical (>=2 NIH criteria); genetic NF1 mutation (chr 17)
Slit-lamp (Lisch nodules), MRI for optic glioma; genetics
Surveillance, surgical removal of symptomatic tumours
Malignant peripheral nerve sheath tumour, optic glioma, scoliosis, phaeochromocytoma

questions.stem: A 19-year-old man is admitted with a GCS OF 12. He was DOING PUSH-UPS when he complained of a SUDDEN-ONSET, SEVERE HEADACHE and COLLAPSED. Q: What would you expect on his CT?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: A CONVEX (biconvex/lens-shaped, lentiform) haematoma that does not cross suture lines is the classic CT appearance of an EXTRADURAL (epidural) haematoma, typically from a middle meningeal artery bleed in a young patient, often with a lucid interval. (Note: this stem describes thunderclap headache on exertion, which clinically suggests subarachnoid haemorrhage; however, of the options offered the named CT shapes point to the convex/extradural pattern as the marked answer.)

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Midline shift is a secondary mass effect that can accompany many large bleeds; it is not itself the characteristic primary CT description being sought.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: A crescent-shaped (concave) haematoma that crosses suture lines is the appearance of a SUBDURAL haematoma (often venous, in the elderly/alcoholics), not the lesion expected here.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Blood along the sulci and fissures
choices.explanation: Blood tracking in the sulci, fissures and basal cisterns is the appearance of SUBARACHNOID haemorrhage; while the thunderclap-on-exertion history fits SAH clinically, the option marked on the paper is the convex haematoma (A).

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Intraventricular blood may accompany severe haemorrhage but is not the expected primary finding for the scenario.

questions.keyPoint: CT haematoma shapes: extradural = biconvex/lens (does NOT cross sutures); subdural = crescent (crosses sutures); SAH = blood in sulci/cisterns.

rows: Young patient, lucid interval, biconvex lens-shaped clot not crossing sutures (middle meningeal artery)

flag.note: FILE ANSWER vs STANDARD TEACHING — File says: A (Convex haematoma, ticked) Standard (Oxford Handbook of Clinical Medicine; Radiology teaching): The CLINICAL history (thunderclap headache on exertion, collapse) is classic for SUBARACHNOID haemorrhage, which on CT shows blood in the sulci/fissures (option D). However the paper marks A (convex/extradural). There is a stem-vs-answer mismatch. Exam advice: Record A as marked, but note the vignette fits SAH (option D) better - confirm intended answer with tutor.
flag.source: Study guide flag (file key vs standard teaching)

questions.stem: A 60-year-old man presents with VISUAL PROBLEMS and DIZZINESS. The DIZZINESS STARTED SUDDENLY, he sees the ROOM SPINNING AROUND and he has noticed he keeps BUMPING INTO THINGS on his right. His BP is 159/91, heart rate 72. On examination there is NYSTAGMUS and DYSDIADOCHOKINESIS. Q: Where is his stroke?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Temporal lobe (MCA territory) strokes cause superior quadrantanopia and dysphasia, not vertigo with nystagmus and cerebellar dysdiadochokinesis.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: A parietal stroke causes contralateral sensory/visual field deficits and neglect, not the brainstem/cerebellar signs (vertigo, nystagmus, incoordination) seen here.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Likewise a right parietal lesion gives contralateral neglect/sensory loss, not vertigo with cerebellar signs.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Anterior (carotid/ACA/MCA) circulation strokes cause hemiparesis, hemisensory loss, dysphasia and hemianopia - not the vertigo, nystagmus and cerebellar incoordination of this patient.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Vertigo with the room spinning, nystagmus, dysdiadochokinesis (cerebellar incoordination) and visual disturbance indicate a POSTERIOR (vertebrobasilar) CIRCULATION stroke affecting the brainstem and cerebellum. Posterior circulation strokes typically cause the 5 'D's plus cerebellar and cranial nerve signs.

questions.keyPoint: Vertigo + nystagmus + cerebellar signs (dysdiadochokinesis) = posterior (vertebrobasilar) circulation stroke.

rows: Vertigo, nystagmus, ataxia/dysdiadochokinesis, visual disturbance, cranial nerve signs
MRI brain (better for posterior fossa), vascular imaging
Vertigo + cerebellar signs = posterior circulation stroke

questions.topic: Q38 - Paraesthesia (Chronic inflammatory demyelinating polyneuropathy, CIDP)
questions.stem: A 45-year-old man presents with a 5-DAY HISTORY OF PROGRESSIVE TINGLING AND NUMBNESS OF HIS HANDS AND FEET. He insists he has never had this problem before and that he was perfectly fine a week ago. Over the last 2 days he has had SOME DIFFICULTY WALKING but mostly he complains ABOUT DIFFICULTY ROLLING UP CIGARETTES. On examination there is MILD SYMMETRICAL DISTAL WEAKNESS, MILD GAIT ATAXIA and DYSDIADOCHOKINESIS. He smokes 30 cigarettes a day and drinks 1-2 bottles of wine. He has a family history of HYPERTENSION and his 63-year-old mother has TYPE 2 DIABETES, whom over the last year has complained of NUMBNESS and BURNING IN HER FEET. He self-discharges. A week later, his symptoms have PEAKED. He displays moderate distal weakness and numbness to his knees, after which he TURNS A CORNER and his symptoms start to slowly RESOLVE. Q: What is the diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Miller Fisher syndrome (a GBS variant) presents with the triad of ophthalmoplegia, ataxia and areflexia, not a relapsing/remitting distal sensorimotor neuropathy over weeks.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Alcoholic neuropathy is a chronic, slowly progressive length-dependent sensory neuropathy from sustained heavy drinking, not an acute/subacute relapsing course over days to weeks.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: CIDP is the chronic (relapsing-remitting or progressive over >8 weeks) demyelinating counterpart of GBS, with symmetrical proximal AND distal weakness plus sensory involvement. The relapsing course described - symptoms progressing, peaking, then slowly resolving (and the implication of recurrence) - fits chronic inflammatory demyelinating polyneuropathy. It responds to steroids/IVIg/plasma exchange (unlike GBS, which does not respond to steroids).

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Charcot-Marie-Tooth is an inherited, very slowly progressive neuropathy (pes cavus, distal wasting/'inverted champagne bottle' legs) from childhood, not an acute relapsing illness in adulthood.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Acute GBS reaches its nadir within ~4 weeks and is monophasic; the relapsing/remitting evolution and slow resolution described here point to the chronic form (CIDP) rather than acute GBS.

questions.keyPoint: CIDP = chronic/relapsing demyelinating polyneuropathy (>8 weeks), proximal + distal weakness, responds to steroids/IVIg (GBS does not respond to steroids).

rows: Relapsing/progressive symmetrical proximal+distal sensorimotor neuropathy over >8 weeks HIGH-YIELD SUMMARY
Nerve conduction studies (demyelination); CSF high protein; clinical course
CIDP = chronic GBS-like neuropathy that DOES respond to steroids

questions.stem: A 28-year-old JUNIOR DOCTOR has been complaining of a HEADACHE for the LAST 24 HOURS. It started GRADUALLY, slowly involving the ENTIRE CRANIUM, but over the last couple of hours has noticed that turning her head is UNCOMFORTABLE. She feels GENERALLY UNWELL and prefers to LIE IN A DARK ROOM. Her boyfriend has noticed that she SEEMS IRRITABLE. On examination she exhibits PHOTOPHOBIA and there is NECK STIFFNESS. There is NO PAPILLOEDEMA. Close examination of her skin reveals NO RASHES. KERNIG'S SIGN IS NEGATIVE. A lumbar puncture (LP) reveals LOW PROTEIN, NORMAL GLUCOSE AND LYMPHOCYTOSIS. Q: What is the diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: The CSF picture is the key: a LYMPHOCYTOSIS with NORMAL GLUCOSE and LOW/normal PROTEIN is characteristic of VIRAL (aseptic) MENINGITIS. Clinically she has headache, neck stiffness, photophobia and irritability but no rash and is not severely septic; the CSF (lymphocytes, normal glucose, normal protein) confirms a viral rather than bacterial cause. (Bacterial meningitis would show neutrophils, LOW glucose and HIGH protein.)

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Migraine causes photophobia and headache but not meningism (neck stiffness) with an abnormal CSF lymphocytosis; the LP findings exclude a simple migraine.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: 'Aseptic meningitis' overlaps with viral meningitis, but as a single best answer the specific term 'viral meningitis' is the one ticked; aseptic is a broader umbrella also including drug-induced and other non-pyogenic causes.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Bacterial meningitis produces a CSF with high neutrophils, LOW glucose and HIGH protein - the opposite of this patient's lymphocytosis with normal glucose and low protein.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: TB meningitis classically gives a lymphocytic CSF but with HIGH protein and LOW glucose and a more subacute course; here protein is low and glucose normal, fitting viral rather than TB meningitis.

questions.keyPoint: CSF lymphocytosis + NORMAL glucose + low/normal protein = viral meningitis. (Bacterial: neutrophils, low glucose, high protein.)

rows: Headache, neck stiffness, photophobia, no rash, generally less toxic; lymphocytic CSF
Lumbar puncture - lymphocytes, normal glucose, normal/low protein; viral PCR
LP with CSF analysis and viral PCR (enterovirus, HSV)
Supportive; aciclovir if HSV suspected HIGH-YIELD SUMMARY

questions.topic: Q40 - Loss of balance (positive Romberg / proprioceptive sensory ataxia)
questions.stem: A 36-year-old WOMAN presents to clinic with NEUROLOGICAL SYMPTOMS. On examination, she is ABLE TO STAND WITH HER FEET TOGETHER (steady with eyes open). Upon CLOSING HER EYES, however, she is UNABLE TO KEEP HER BALANCE (POSITIVE ROMBERG'S SIGN). Q: What is the diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Although diabetic peripheral neuropathy can impair proprioception, 'diabetes' alone is not the mechanism of the sign; the sign itself points to a proprioceptive (sensory) deficit, captured by option D rather than naming diabetes.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: A cerebellar lesion causes a broad-based ataxia that is present with the eyes OPEN and does NOT significantly worsen on eye closure - so Romberg's is NEGATIVE in cerebellar disease. This is the classic distractor and is incorrect.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Alcohol typically causes a cerebellar (vermis) ataxia, which is unsteady with eyes open and Romberg-negative; it does not produce the eyes-closed-specific instability described.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: A POSITIVE ROMBERG'S SIGN - steady with eyes open but losing balance only when the eyes are CLOSED - indicates a PROPRIOCEPTIVE (dorsal column / sensory) ataxia. With the eyes open, vision compensates for the lost joint-position sense; removing visual input unmasks the deficit. Causes include dorsal column disease (B12 deficiency/subacute combined degeneration, tabes dorsalis) and sensory peripheral neuropathy. This is the sensory/dorsal-column option, NOT cerebellar.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: A primary visual problem would impair balance with the eyes OPEN; here she is steady with eyes open and only unsteady on closing them, which is the opposite pattern and indicates loss of proprioception, not vision.

questions.keyPoint: Positive Romberg (steady eyes open, falls eyes closed) = PROPRIOCEPTIVE/dorsal-column sensory ataxia, NOT cerebellar (cerebellar ataxia is Romberg-negative, unsteady even with eyes open).

rows: Sensory (proprioceptive / dorsal column) ataxia - positive Romberg
Steady with eyes open, unsteady with eyes closed (positive Romberg)
Romberg's test positive; identify cause (B12, tabes, sensory neuropathy)
B12/folate, NCS, MRI cord, syphilis serology
Positive Romberg = proprioceptive (dorsal column) loss, not cerebellar RAPID REVISION — All High-Yield Summaries Sensory ataxia
Stamping gait worse in dark, positive Romberg
Loss of proprioception/vibration, absent ankle jerk
B12, nerve conduction, dorsal column imaging
